    钦定四库全书　　　　　子部十二
    　　续博物志　　　　　　　小説家三【琐记之属】提要
    　　【臣】等谨按续博物志十卷旧本题晋李石撰然第二卷称今上于前朝作镇睢阳洎开国号大宋是宋太祖时人矣而又称曽公亮得龙之脊王安石得龙之睛全摭陆佃埤雅之説又引子华子陈正敏遯斋闲览曽慥集仙传均南北宋间之书则并非北宋初人别本末有其门人廸功郎眉山簿黄宗泰防称为方舟先生方舟为宋李石之号所作诗如例已备録经部中则称晋李石误也然石为绍兴乾道间人亦不应称太祖为今上殆亦剽掇説部以为之仍其旧文末及削改欤其书以张华所末 [1]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [1]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 谨按 - A formal expression used to introduce a scholarly or official statement.
    • 误也 - Indicates that there is an error in the attribution of the text to Li Shi of the Jin dynasty.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 钦定四库全书 - Refers to the *Siku Quanshu*, a massive compilation of Chinese texts commissioned by the Qing dynasty.
    • 子部十二 - Refers to the classification system within the *Siku Quanshu*, where texts are categorized into four major sections: Classics, History, Philosophy, and Miscellaneous.
    • 续博物志 - A continuation of an earlier work, likely *Wuzazu* (博物志), which was a compendium of various subjects.
    • 小説家三【琐记之属】 - Refers to the category of 'Miscellaneous Notes' within the 'Novel' section of the *Siku Quanshu*.
    • 提要 - A summary or preface, often providing background information on the text.
    • 旧本题晋李石撰 - Indicates that the original version of the text was attributed to Li Shi of the Jin dynasty.
    • 今上于前朝作镇睢阳 - Refers to the current emperor (likely referring to the Song dynasty) who was previously stationed in Suizhou (睢阳).
    • 开国号大宋 - Refers to the founding of the Song dynasty.
    • 曾公亮得龙之脊王安石得龙之睛 - A metaphorical statement suggesting that Zeng Gongliang and Wang Anshi were key figures in the Song dynasty, with the dragon representing the dynasty itself.
    • 全摭陆佃埤雅之説 - Indicates that the text draws heavily on the work of Lu Dian's *Piyaya*.
    • 引子华子陈正敏遯斋闲览曽慥集仙传 - Refers to the inclusion of quotes or references from various works by different authors.
    • 方舟先生 - A title or nickname for Li Shi, meaning 'Mr. Boat.'
    • 绍兴乾道间人 - Refers to the period during which Li Shi lived, specifically the Southern Song dynasty.
    • 剽掇説部以为之 - Suggests that the text was compiled by gathering material from various sources.
    • 仍其旧文末及削改 - Indicates that the text was not significantly altered from its original form.

    Proper Nouns

    • 钦定四库全书 - The *Siku Quanshu*.
    • 子部十二 - The 'Miscellaneous' or 'Novel' category within the *Siku Quanshu*.
    • 续博物志 - The title of the text.
    • 小説家三【琐记之属】 - The subcategory of 'Miscellaneous Notes' within the 'Novel' category.
    • 提要 - The summary or preface.
    • 谨按 - A formal expression.
    • 旧本题晋李石撰 - The original attribution to Li Shi of the Jin dynasty.
    • 今上于前朝作镇睢阳 - The current emperor's previous station in Suizhou.
    • 开国号大宋 - The founding of the Song dynasty.
    • 曾公亮得龙之脊王安石得龙之睛 - A metaphorical statement about Zeng Gongliang and Wang Anshi.
    • 全摭陆佃埤雅之説 - The reference to Lu Dian's *Piyaya*.
    • 引子华子陈正敏遯斋闲览曽慥集仙传 - References to other works.
    • 方舟先生 - A title or nickname for Li Shi.
    • 误也 - An indication of error.
    • 绍兴乾道间人 - The period during which Li Shi lived.
    • 剽掇説部以为之 - The method of compilation.
    • 仍其旧文末及削改 - The lack of significant alteration.

    The Four Treasuries, Imperially Approved　　　　　Part Twelve: Miscellaneous
    　　Continuation of the Record of Various Subjects　　　　　Category Three: Miscellaneous Notes【Belonging to the Novel Section】Summary
    　　We respectfully examine that the Continuation of the Record of Various Subjects consists of ten volumes, originally attributed to Li Shi of the Jin dynasty. However, the second volume mentions the current emperor's previous station in Suizhou (睢阳) and the founding of the Song dynasty, indicating that it was written during the reign of Emperor Taizu of Song. Furthermore, it mentions that Zeng Gongliang obtained the dragon's spine and Wang Anshi the dragon's eye, drawing heavily on Lu Dian's Piyaya, and references works by Zihua, Chen Zhengmin, Duanzhi Xianlan, and Zeng Zao, all of which are from the Northern and Southern Song periods. Therefore, it is not from the early Northern Song. A different edition has a preface by his disciple, Di Gonglang and Meishan County Magistrate Huang Zongtai, who calls him Mr. Boat. "Mr. Boat" is a nickname for Li Shi of the Song dynasty, and his poetry, as an example, has already been recorded in the classics section. Thus, attributing it to Li Shi of the Jin dynasty is incorrect. Nevertheless, Li Shi lived during the Shaoxing and Qinding periods of the Southern Song dynasty and should not refer to Emperor Taizu as the current emperor. It seems that the text was compiled by gathering material from various sources and largely retained its original form. The book is attributed to Zhang Hua, but this is likely a misattribution. [1]

    备惟华书首地理此首天象体例小异其余虽不分门目然大致畧同故自序谓次第仿华説一事续一事然龟巢莲叶一条与华説复出竟不及检又王士祯香祖笔记摘其既云刘亮合仙丹得白蝙蝠服之立死又云陈子真得蝙蝠大如鸦食之一夕大泄而死乃更云丹水石穴蝙蝠百嵗者倒悬得而服之使人成仙自相矛盾又摘其以文帝使掌故欧阳生受伏生尚书以伏生墓为在漯水以磻溪为在汲郡皆附防舛误特以宋人旧笈轶闻琐语间有存焉姑録以备参考云尔乾隆四十六年十月恭校上 [2]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [2]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 惟华书首地理此首天象 - A comparative phrase indicating that the Chinese text begins with geography while the source text starts with astronomy, highlighting a difference in structure.
    • 体例小异 - Means 'the structure is slightly different.'
    • 次第仿华说 - Means 'the order follows the Chinese saying.'
    • 一事续一事 - Means 'one thing follows another.'
    • 龟巢莲叶一条 - A specific phrase that appears to be a reference to a particular section or story.
    • 复出竟不及检 - Means 'repeatedly appearing without being checked.'
    • 自相矛盾 - Means 'contradictory to itself.'
    • 附防舛误 - Means 'errors in connection or addition.'
    • 轶闻琐语 - Means 'anecdotes and trivial sayings.'
    • 姑录以备参考云尔 - Means 'record them for reference.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 华书 - Refers to the Chinese text or literature.
    • 王士祯 - A famous Qing dynasty poet and scholar.
    • 香祖笔记 - A collection of notes by Wang Shizhen.
    • 刘亮 - A historical figure mentioned in the text.
    • 陈子真 - Another historical figure mentioned in the text.
    • 文帝 - Refers to Emperor Wen of Han.
    • 欧阳生 - A historical figure mentioned in the text.
    • 伏生 - A historical figure known for his contributions to the *Shujing* (Book of Documents).
    • 漯水 - A river in China.
    • 汲郡 - A historical region in China.
    • 宋人旧笈 - Refers to old collections from the Song dynasty.
    • 乾隆四十六年十月 - The date of the proofreading and submission, corresponding to October 1781 in the Gregorian calendar.

    Proper Nouns

    • 华书 - The Chinese text or literature.
    • 王士祯 - Wang Shizhen, a Qing dynasty poet and scholar.
    • 香祖笔记 - Wang Shizhen's collection of notes.
    • 刘亮 - Liu Liang, a historical figure.
    • 陈子真 - Chen Zhen, a historical figure.
    • 文帝 - Emperor Wen of Han.
    • 欧阳生 - Ouyang Sheng, a historical figure.
    • 伏生 - Fu Sheng, a historical figure.
    • 漯水 - Luo River, a river in China.
    • 汲郡 - Jiyi, a historical region in China.
    • 宋人旧笈 - Old collections from the Song dynasty.
    • 乾隆四十六年十月 - October 1781 in the Gregorian calendar.

    In my work, I have noted that the Chinese text begins with geography, whereas this text begins with astronomy, indicating a slight difference in structure. Although the rest of the text does not follow a strict categorization, the overall content is similar. The preface states that the order follows the Chinese tradition, with one topic following another. However, the section on "Tortoise Nest and Lotus Leaf" appears again without being checked. Additionally, Wang Shizhen's Xiangzu Biji (Notes of Fragrant Ancestors) points out that it mentions Liu Liang combining elixir and obtaining a white bat, which led to his death upon consumption. It also mentions Chen Zhen obtaining a bat as large as a crow, which caused him to suffer severe diarrhea and die. Yet, it further states that a bat living in a hundred-year-old cave in the Dan River can be suspended and consumed to grant immortality, which is self-contradictory. Furthermore, it cites the Shujing (Book of Documents) as being taught to Ouyang Sheng by Fu Sheng, with Fu Sheng's tomb located in the Luo River and the Banchi (Bamboo River) in Jiyi, all of which contain errors. These are merely anecdotes and trivial sayings from old collections of the Song dynasty, which are recorded here for reference. This was proofread and submitted in the tenth month of the forty-sixth year of the Qianlong era. [2]

    总纂官【臣】纪昀【臣】陆锡熊【臣】孙士毅
    　　总　校　官【臣】陆　费　墀

    　　续博物志原序
    　　按张华博物志书四百篇武帝恐涉浮妄令更芟截分为十卷盖不以博为博而以约为博也李石又从而续之不防又渉繁芜乎然华所志者仿山海经而以地理为编石所志者取天官书而以象纬为冠庶防由天地以及山海由山海以及人物固无之或遗矣合二书而卷牍无多则尤从约见博尔吴兴董氏又广之而为五十卷则又在博物者之更为考核兹则仍二篇之旧各书十卷令观者便于采览尤不失博物之意焉
    　　康熈戊申一阳月望日星源汪士汉识

    　　钦定四库全书
    　　续博物志卷一
    　　宋　李石　撰
    　　张华述地理自以禹所未志且天官所遗多矣经所不载以天包地象纬之学亦华所甚惜也虽然华仿山海经而作故略或曰武帝以华志繁俾芟而略之余所志视华嵗时绵歴其有取于天而首以冠其篇次第仿华说一事续一事不茍于捜索与世之书者小异而比华所志加详 [3]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [3]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 總纂官 - Formal title meaning 'Chief Compiler,' used for the officials overseeing the compilation of the text.
    • 總校官 - Formal title meaning 'Chief Proofreader,' used for the official responsible for the final review.
    • 續博物志 - Title of the book, meaning 'Continuation of the *Museum of Wonders*,' a reference to the original work by Zhang Hua.
    • 原序 - Title of the preface, meaning 'Original Preface,' indicating that this is an introductory note.
    • 仿山海经 - Means 'imitating the *Classic of Mountains and Seas*,' referring to the style and content of Zhang Hua's original work.
    • 天官所遗 - Means 'what the *Celestial Officials* (a section of the *Book of Documents*) has left out,' referring to astronomical and calendrical knowledge.
    • 象纬之学 - Means 'the study of celestial phenomena,' referring to astronomy and astrology.
    • 从约见博 - Means 'to see breadth through conciseness,' a phrase emphasizing the value of concise yet comprehensive works.
    • 康熈戊申 - The date of the preface, using the Chinese sexagenary cycle (60-year cycle) to denote the year 1718.
    • 一阳月 - A term for the first month of the lunar year, often associated with the beginning of spring.
    • 星源汪士汉 - The name of the author of the preface, where '星源' is likely a place name or a pseudonym, and '汪士汉' is the personal name.
    • 钦定四库全书 - Title of the imperial collection, meaning 'Imperially Approved *Four Treasuries of Books*,' a massive compilation of Chinese literature commissioned during the Qing dynasty.
    • 李石撰 - Title of the author, meaning 'compiled by Li Shi,' indicating that Li Shi is the compiler of the *Continuation of the Museum of Wonders*.
    • 张华述 - Title of the original author, meaning 'compiled by Zhang Hua,' indicating that Zhang Hua is the original compiler of the *Museum of Wonders*.
    • 地理 - Refers to geography, specifically the study of places and their characteristics.
    • 象纬 - Refers to celestial phenomena, specifically the study of the stars and their movements.
    • 山海经 - Title of the *Classic of Mountains and Seas*, an ancient Chinese text containing myths, legends, and geographical information.
    • 天官书 - Title of the *Celestial Officials* section of the *Book of Documents*, a classical Chinese text on astronomy and calendrical science.
    • 象纬之学 - The study of celestial phenomena, specifically the study of the stars and their movements.
    • 从约见博 - The phrase means 'to see breadth through conciseness,' emphasizing the value of concise yet comprehensive works.
    • 吴兴董氏 - The name of the person who expanded the work, where '吴兴' is likely a place name or a pseudonym, and '董氏' is the family name.
    • 博物者 - Refers to scholars or experts in the field of natural history and geography.
    • 考核 - Means 'to examine and verify,' referring to the process of careful research and verification.
    • 仍二篇之旧 - Means 'to retain the original two volumes,' indicating that the work is being presented in the same format as the original.
    • 各书十卷 - Means 'each volume consists of ten chapters,' indicating the structure of the work.
    • 便于采览 - Means 'convenient for reading and reference,' emphasizing the practicality of the work.
    • 不失博物之意 - Means 'does not lose the spirit of natural history,' indicating that the work maintains the original intent of the *Museum of Wonders*.
    • 吴兴董氏又广之而为五十卷 - Means 'Wu Xing Dong expanded it to fifty volumes,' indicating the expansion of the work.
    • 更为考核 - Means 'to conduct further examination and verification,' emphasizing the rigorous research process.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 张华 - Zhang Hua, a famous scholar and writer of the Western Jin dynasty, known for his work *Museum of Wonders*.
    • 武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han, a powerful emperor of the Han dynasty known for his military campaigns and cultural patronage.
    • 山海经 - The *Classic of Mountains and Seas*, an ancient Chinese text containing myths, legends, and geographical information.
    • 天官书 - The *Celestial Officials* section of the *Book of Documents*, a classical Chinese text on astronomy and calendrical science.
    • 博物志 - The *Museum of Wonders*, a work by Zhang Hua that compiles various pieces of knowledge and curiosities.
    • 续博物志 - The *Continuation of the Museum of Wonders*, a work that expands on Zhang Hua's original text.
    • 康熈戊申 - The year 1718, using the Chinese sexagenary cycle (60-year cycle) to denote the year.
    • 一阳月 - The first month of the lunar year, often associated with the beginning of spring.
    • 星源汪士汉 - The author of the preface, where '星源' is likely a place name or a pseudonym, and '汪士汉' is the personal name.
    • 钦定四库全书 - The *Imperially Approved *Four Treasuries of Books*, a massive compilation of Chinese literature commissioned during the Qing dynasty.
    • 李石 - Li Shi, the compiler of the *Continuation of the Museum of Wonders*.
    • 吴兴董氏 - Wu Xing Dong, the person who expanded the work, where '吴兴' is likely a place name or a pseudonym, and '董氏' is the family name.

    Proper Nouns

    • 总纂官 - Chief Compiler
    • 总校官 - Chief Proofreader
    • 张华 - Zhang Hua
    • 武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han
    • 山海经 - *Classic of Mountains and Seas*
    • 天官书 - *Celestial Officials*
    • 博物志 - *Museum of Wonders*
    • 续博物志 - *Continuation of the Museum of Wonders*
    • 康熈戊申 - 1718
    • 一阳月 - First month of the lunar year
    • 星源汪士汉 - Author of the preface
    • 钦定四库全书 - *Imperially Approved *Four Treasuries of Books*
    • 李石 - Li Shi
    • 吴兴董氏 - Wu Xing Dong

    Translation

    Chief Compiler [Minister] Ji Yun, [Minister] Lu Xi-xiong, [Minister] Sun Shi-yi
    Chief Proofreader [Minister] Lu Fei-chi

    Original Preface to the Continuation of the Museum of Wonders
    According to Zhang Hua's Museum of Wonders, which originally consisted of 400 chapters, Emperor Wu of Han feared that it might contain too much speculative content and thus ordered it to be condensed into ten volumes. This approach does not prioritize breadth over conciseness but rather achieves breadth through conciseness. Li Shi continued this work, and while it may have added some complexity, it followed Zhang Hua's model of using the Classic of Mountains and Seas for geography and the Celestial Officials for celestial phenomena. By combining the two works into a concise format, the breadth of knowledge is still maintained. Wu Xing Dong further expanded the work to fifty volumes, conducting more thorough research and verification. This version retains the original two volumes, each consisting of ten chapters, making it convenient for readers and maintaining the spirit of natural history.

    Kangxi Wu Shen, First Month of the Lunar Year, Full Moon Day, Star Source Wang Shi-han

    Imperially Approved Four Treasuries of Books
    Continuation of the Museum of Wonders, Volume One
    Compiled by Li Shi

    Zhang Hua's Museum of Wonders focuses on geography, which even the great Yu the Great did not fully document, and there is much that the Celestial Officials section of the Book of Documents has left out. The study of celestial phenomena, which is also highly valued by Zhang Hua, is not fully covered in the classics. Although Zhang Hua followed the Classic of Mountains and Seas in his work, it is somewhat concise. Some say that Emperor Wu of Han ordered Zhang Hua to condense his work, and the remaining content is more detailed compared to Zhang Hua's original, which was compiled over many years. The content related to celestial phenomena is placed at the beginning of the chapters, following Zhang Hua's method, and each chapter continues his narrative without being overly speculative. It differs slightly from other books of its time but is more detailed than Zhang Hua's original work. [3]

    尔雅既曰释天不得不略言其趣凡有六等一曰盖天文见周髀如盖在上二曰浑天形如弹丸地在其中天包其外犹如鸡卵白之绕黄雄桓谭张衡蔡邕陆绩王肃郑之徒并所依用三曰宣夜旧说云殷代之制四曰昕天昕读为轩言天北髙南下若车之轩吴时姚信所说五曰穹天云穹隆在上虞氏所说六曰安天晋时虞喜所论郑注考灵曜云天者纯阳清明无形圣人则之制璿玑玉衡以度其象如郑此言则天是太虚本无形体但指诸星运转以为天耳
    　　天围圜三百六十五度四分度之一案考灵曜云一度二千九百三十二里千四百六十一分里之三百四十八周天百七万一千里者是天圆周之里数也以围三径一言之则直径三十五万七千里此为二十八宿周回直径之数也然二十八宿之外上下东西各有万五千里是为四游之极谓之四表四表之内并星宿内总有三十八万七千里然则天之中央上下正半之处则一十九万三千五百里地在于中是地去天之数也 [4]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [4]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 爾雅既曰釋天不得不略言其趣 - Refers to the *Erya*, an ancient Chinese dictionary. 'Since it explains the heavens, it must briefly discuss the general principles or meanings.'
    • 凡有六等 - Means 'there are six categories' or 'there are six types.'
    • 形如弹丸 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'shaped like a ball' or 'shaped like a ball of clay.'
    • 天包其外 - Means 'the sky surrounds it on the outside.'
    • 犹如鸡卵白之绕黄 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'like the white part of an egg surrounding the yolk.'
    • 虞氏所说 - Refers to Yu the Great, a legendary figure in Chinese history.
    • 穹隆在上 - Means 'arched above' or 'domed above.'
    • 晝夜運行 - Means 'revolves day and night.'

    The Erya states that when explaining the heavens, it must briefly discuss their general principles, which fall into six categories: 1) The Canopy Theory, where the heavens are seen as a canopy above, as described in the Zhou Bi Suan Jing; 2) The Spherical Theory, where the shape is likened to a ball, with the earth at the center and the heavens surrounding it, much like the white part of an egg surrounding the yolk, as proposed by Yang Xiong, Huan Tan, Zhang Heng, Cai Yong, Lu Ji, Wang Su, Zheng Xuan, and others; 3) The Expanding Night Theory, an ancient saying attributed to the Shang Dynasty; 4) The Dawn Theory, where "dawn" is read as "chariot," suggesting that the heavens are higher in the north and lower in the south, like the raised front of a chariot, as proposed by Yao Xing of Wu; 5) The Dome Theory, where the heavens are described as arched above, as suggested by Yu; 6) The Stable Heaven Theory, proposed by Yu Xi of Jin. Zheng Xuan's commentary on the Kao Lingyao states that the heavens are purely yang, clear, and formless, and sages modeled their instruments, such as the Xuanji and Yuhang, to measure them. According to this view, the heavens are fundamentally formless, but people refer to the movement of stars as the heavens.

    The circumference of the heavens is 365 degrees and one quarter of a degree. According to the Kao Lingyao, one degree is 2,932 li, with 348 li and 1,461 parts of a li. Thus, the total circumference of the heavens is 171,000 li. Using the principle that the circumference is three times the diameter, the diameter of the heavens is 35,700 li, which is the diameter of the 28 constellations. Outside these constellations, there are 15,000 li in each of the four directions, making up the four extremes, known as the four corners. Within these four corners and the starry regions, the total distance is 387,000 li. Therefore, the central point of the heavens, halfway up, is 193,500 li, where the earth is located, which is the distance between the earth and the heavens. [4]

    日有九道故考灵曜云万世不失九道郑注引河图帝览嬉云黄道一青道二出黄道东赤道二出黄道南白道二出黄道西黒道二出黄道北日春东从青道夏南从赤道秋西从白道冬北从黒道
    　　律厯志云角一十二度亢九氐十五房五心五尾十八箕十一斗二十六牛八女十二虚十危十七营室十六壁九奎十六娄十二胃十四昴十一毕十六觜二参九井三十三四栁十五星七张十八翼十八轸十七此三百五度二十八宿之度也
    　　丑为星纪初斗十二度终于婺女七度子为枵初婺女八度终于危十五度亥为娵訾初危十六度终于奎四度戌为降娄初奎五度终于胃六度酉为大梁初胃七度终于毕十一度申为实沈初毕十二度终于井十五度未为鹑首初井十六度终于栁八度午为鹑火初栁九度终于张十七度已为鹑尾初张十八度终于轸十一度辰为夀星初轸十二度终于氐四度夘为大火初氐五度终于尾九度寅为析木初尾十度终于斗十一度 [5]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [5]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 日有九道 - Classical expression referring to the nine paths or orbits of the sun, derived from ancient Chinese astronomical observations.
    • 考灵曜 - Refers to an ancient astronomical text, 'Kao Lingyao,' which discusses celestial phenomena.
    • 万世不失九道 - Statement indicating that the nine paths of the sun remain constant over time.
    • 郑注引河图帝览嬉 - Reference to Zheng Xuan's commentary on the 'River Chart' ('He Tu') and 'Emperor's Vision' ('Di Lanyi'), which are ancient texts associated with cosmology and divination.
    • 黄道一青道二出黄道东赤道二出黄道南白道二出黄道西黒道二出黄道北 - Describes the nine paths of the sun, with the central path (黄道) and eight additional paths (青道, 赤道, 白道, 黒道) emanating from it in different directions.
    • 日春东从青道夏南从赤道秋西从白道冬北从黒道 - Explains the sun's movement along the nine paths during the four seasons.
    • 律厯志 - Refers to the 'Lüli Zhi' section of the 'Book of Han,' which discusses the calendar and astronomical observations.
    • 角一十二度亢九氐十五房五心五尾十八箕十一斗二十六牛八女十二虚十危十七营室十六壁九奎十六娄十二胃十四昴十一毕十六觜二参九井三十三四栁十五星七张十八翼十八轸十七 - Lists the degrees of the 28 lunar mansions (宿) in the Chinese zodiac, a traditional astronomical system.
    • 丑为星纪初斗十二度终于婺女七度子为枵初婺女八度终于危十五度亥为娵訾初危十六度终于奎四度戌为降娄初奎五度终于胃六度酉为大梁初胃七度终于毕十一度申为实沈初毕十二度终于井十五度未为鹑首初井十六度终于栁八度午为鹑火初栁九度终于张十七度已为鹑尾初张十八度终于轸十一度辰为夀星初轸十二度终于氐四度夘为大火初氐五度终于尾九度寅为析木初尾十度终于斗十一度 - Describes the divisions of the Chinese zodiac into 12 sections, each corresponding to a lunar mansion and a segment of the ecliptic.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 九道 - The concept of the nine paths of the sun is rooted in ancient Chinese astronomy, reflecting the belief in a structured and ordered universe.
    • 考灵曜 - An ancient text that discusses celestial phenomena, often used in conjunction with other astronomical works to understand the movements of celestial bodies.
    • 郑注 - Zheng Xuan, a prominent scholar of the Han dynasty, known for his commentaries on various classical texts.
    • 河图帝览嬉 - Ancient texts associated with cosmology and divination, often used to explain the structure of the universe and the movements of celestial bodies.
    • 黄道, 青道, 赤道, 白道, 黒道 - These terms refer to specific paths or orbits of the sun, each with its own symbolic and practical significance in ancient Chinese astronomy.
    • 律厯志 - A section of the 'Book of Han' that provides detailed information about the calendar and astronomical observations of the Han dynasty.
    • 二十八宿 - The 28 lunar mansions, a traditional system used in Chinese astronomy to divide the sky into 28 segments.
    • 十二辰 - The 12 divisions of the Chinese zodiac, each corresponding to a segment of the ecliptic and a lunar mansion.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The sentence '日有九道故考灵曜云万世不失九道' introduces the concept of the nine paths of the sun and references an ancient text to support the idea that these paths are constant.
    • The complex sentence '郑注引河图帝览嬉云黄道一青道二出黄道东赤道二出黄道南白道二出黄道西黒道二出黄道北' describes the nine paths of the sun, with the central path (黄道) and eight additional paths (青道, 赤道, 白道, 黒道) emanating from it in different directions.
    • The sentence '日春东从青道夏南从赤道秋西从白道冬北从黒道' explains the sun's movement along the nine paths during the four seasons.
    • The long list of degrees for the 28 lunar mansions must be read as a single, comprehensive catalogue.
    • The long list of zodiacal divisions must be read as a single, comprehensive catalogue, describing the segments of the ecliptic and their corresponding lunar mansions.

    Proper Nouns

    • 考灵曜 - An ancient astronomical text.
    • 郑注 - Zheng Xuan, a prominent scholar of the Han dynasty.
    • 河图帝览嬉 - Ancient texts associated with cosmology and divination.
    • 黄道, 青道, 赤道, 白道, 黒道 - Terms referring to specific paths or orbits of the sun.
    • 律厯志 - A section of the 'Book of Han' discussing the calendar and astronomical observations.
    • 二十八宿 - The 28 lunar mansions.
    • 十二辰 - The 12 divisions of the Chinese zodiac.

    The sun has nine paths, as stated in the ancient text "Kao Lingyao": "Throughout the ages, the nine paths of the sun remain unchanged." Zheng Xuan, in his commentary on the "River Chart" and "Emperor's Vision," explains that there is one central path (the Yellow Path), and eight additional paths (the Green, Red, White, and Black Paths) that extend from the Yellow Path in different directions. The sun follows the Green Path in spring, the Red Path in summer, the White Path in autumn, and the Black Path in winter.

    According to the "Lüli Zhi" section of the "Book of Han," the degrees of the 28 lunar mansions are as follows: Jiao (12 degrees), Kang (9 degrees), Di (15 degrees), Fang (5 degrees), Xin (5 degrees), Wei (18 degrees), Ji (11 degrees), Dou (26 degrees), Niu (8 degrees), Nü (12 degrees), Xu (10 degrees), Wei (17 degrees), Ying Shi (16 degrees), Bi (9 degrees), Kui (16 degrees), Lou (12 degrees), Wei (14 degrees), Mao (11 degrees), Bi (16 degrees), Zui (2 degrees), Shen (9 degrees), Jing (33 degrees), Wei (4 degrees), Liu (15 degrees), Xing (7 degrees), Zhang (18 degrees), Yi (18 degrees), Zhen (17 degrees). These are the 365 degrees of the 28 lunar mansions.

    The twelve divisions of the Chinese zodiac are as follows:

    • Chou (丑) is the beginning of Xingji (星纪), starting at Dou (12 degrees) and ending at Nü (7 degrees).
    • Zi (子) is the beginning of Xuanxiao (玄枵), starting at Nü (8 degrees) and ending at Wei (15 degrees).
    • Hai (亥) is the beginning of Juji (娵訾), starting at Wei (16 degrees) and ending at Kui (4 degrees).
    • Xu (戌) is the beginning of Jianglou (降娄), starting at Kui (5 degrees) and ending at Wei (6 degrees).
    • You (酉) is the beginning of Daliang (大梁), starting at Wei (7 degrees) and ending at Bi (11 degrees).
    • Shen (申) is the beginning of Shishi (实沈), starting at Bi (12 degrees) and ending at Jing (15 degrees).
    • Wei (未) is the beginning of Chunshou (鹑首), starting at Jing (16 degrees) and ending at Liu (8 degrees).
    • Wu (午) is the beginning of Chunhuo (鹑火), starting at Liu (9 degrees) and ending at Zhang (17 degrees).
    • Si (巳) is the beginning of Chunwei (鹑尾), starting at Zhang (18 degrees) and ending at Zhen (11 degrees).
    • Chen (辰) is the beginning of Shouxing (寿星), starting at Zhen (12 degrees) and ending at Di (4 degrees).
    • Mao (卯) is the beginning of Dahuo (大火), starting at Di (5 degrees) and ending at Wei (9 degrees).
    • Yin (寅) is the beginning of Ximu (析木), starting at Wei (10 degrees) and ending at Dou (11 degrees).
    [5]

    汉律厯志以前厯上元泰初四千六百二十七嵗至元封七年复得阏逢摄提格之嵗中冬孟康曰言复得者上元泰初时亦是阏逢之嵗嵗在甲曰阏逢在寅曰摄提格此谓甲寅之嵗也然则乙夘曰旃蒙单阏丙辰曰柔兆执徐丁巳曰强圉大落戊午曰着雍敦牂己未曰屠维协洽庚申曰上章涒滩辛酉曰重光作噩壬戌曰黓阉茂癸亥曰昭阳大渊献甲子曰阏逢困敦乙丑之嵗曰旃蒙赤奋若
    　　正月得甲则曰毕陬二月得乙则曰橘如三月得丙则曰修寎四月得丁则曰圉余五月得戊则曰厉臯六月得已则曰则且七月得庚则曰窒相八月得辛则曰塞壮九月得壬则曰终十月得癸则曰极阳十一月得甲则曰毕辜十二月得乙则曰橘涂
    　　八分二十八宿为十二次晋灼曰太嵗在四仲则歳行三宿太嵗在四孟四季则嵗行二宿二八十六三四十二而行二十八宿十二嵗而周天是嵗星年行一次也 [6]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [6]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 漢律厯志 - Records of the Han Dynasty's Calendar, a historical record of the calendar system during the Han Dynasty.
    • 上元泰初 - A term used in ancient Chinese astronomy and calendar calculations, referring to a specific epoch or starting point for astronomical observations.
    • 四千六百二十七嵗 - A numerical expression indicating a period of 4,627 years.
    • 元封七年 - A specific year during the reign of Emperor Wu of Han, corresponding to 104 BCE.
    • 復得 - Means 'to encounter again' or 'to repeat,' referring to the recurrence of a particular celestial alignment.
    • 中冬 - Refers to the middle of winter, specifically the winter solstice.
    • 孟康曰 - Indicates a commentary or explanation by Meng Kang, a scholar from the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 言復得者 - A phrase used to introduce an explanation or clarification.
    • 嵗在甲曰 - Refers to the position of the Heavenly Stems (天干) in the Chinese calendar system, where '甲' (Jia) is the first stem.
    • 在寅曰 - Refers to the position of the Earthly Branches (地支) in the Chinese calendar system, where '寅' (Yin) is the third branch.
    • 正月得甲則曰 - Indicates that in the first month of the year, if the stem is '甲' (Jia), it is referred to as '畢陬' (Bi Zou).
    • 二月得乙則曰 - Indicates that in the second month of the year, if the stem is '乙' (Yi), it is referred to as '橘如' (Ju Ru).
    • 八分二十八宿為十二次 - Refers to the division of the 28 lunar mansions (二十八宿) into 12 segments (十二次) for astrological purposes.
    • 太嵗在四仲則嵗行三宿 - Refers to the movement of the Tai Sui (太岁), a celestial deity, through the four cardinal points (四仲) and the corresponding movement of the stars.
    • 太嵗在四孟四季則嵗行二宿 - Refers to the movement of the Tai Sui through the four beginning months (四孟) and the four seasons (四季) and the corresponding movement of the stars.
    • 二八十六三四十二而行二十八宿 - A numerical expression indicating the movement of the stars through the 28 lunar mansions over a period of 12 years.
    • 歲星年行一次也 - Refers to the movement of Jupiter (歲星) through the 12 segments (十二次) once every year.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 漢律厯志 - The 'Records of the Han Dynasty's Calendar' is a historical document that records the calendar system and astronomical observations during the Han Dynasty.
    • 上元泰初 - This term is used in ancient Chinese astronomy to denote a specific epoch or starting point for astronomical calculations.
    • 元封七年 - This year corresponds to 104 BCE and is a significant date in the reign of Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 孟康 - Meng Kang was a scholar from the Three Kingdoms period known for his commentaries on the 'Records of the Han Dynasty.'
    • 太嵗 - The Tai Sui is a celestial deity in Chinese astrology that represents the annual cycle and is associated with the movement of the stars.
    • 二十八宿 - The 28 lunar mansions (二十八宿) are a traditional division of the sky in Chinese astronomy, each associated with a specific constellation or region of the sky.
    • 十二次 - The 12 segments (十二次) are a division of the 28 lunar mansions used in Chinese astrology to track the movement of the stars and planets.

    Proper Nouns

    • 漢律厯志 - The 'Records of the Han Dynasty's Calendar.'
    • 上元泰初 - A specific epoch or starting point in ancient Chinese astronomy.
    • 元封七年 - A specific year during the reign of Emperor Wu of Han, corresponding to 104 BCE.
    • 孟康 - A scholar from the Three Kingdoms period known for his commentaries on the 'Records of the Han Dynasty.'
    • 太嵗 - The Tai Sui, a celestial deity in Chinese astrology.
    • 二十八宿 - The 28 lunar mansions in Chinese astronomy.
    • 十二次 - The 12 segments used in Chinese astrology to track the movement of the stars and planets.
    • 歲星 - Jupiter, the planet associated with the annual cycle in Chinese astrology.

    According to the Records of the Han Dynasty's Calendar, the previous calendar system had its epoch set at Shangyuan Tai Chu (上元泰初), which was 4,627 years before the Yuan Feng seventh year (元封七年, 104 BCE), when the celestial alignment of Yafeng She Ti Ge (阏逢摄提格) was again observed during the winter solstice. Meng Kang commented, "The phrase 'repeatedly observed' means that during the Shangyuan Tai Chu epoch, it was also the year of Yafeng (阏逢)." The year was designated as Yafeng when the Heavenly Stem was Jia (甲), and She Ti Ge when the Earthly Branch was Yin (寅). This is referred to as the Jia Yin year (甲寅之嵗). Consequently, the year with Yi Mao (乙夘) was called Zhan Meng Dan E (旃蒙单阏), the year with Bing Chen (丙辰) was called Rou Zhao Zhi Xu (柔兆执徐), the year with Ding Si (丁巳) was called Qiang Yu Da Xie (强圉大落), the year with Wu Wu (戊午) was called Zhuo Yong Dun Zang (着雍敦牂), the year with Ji Wei (己未) was called Tu Wei Xie Qie (屠维协洽), the year with Geng Shen (庚申) was called Shang Zhang Tun Tan (上章涒滩), the year with Xin You (辛酉) was called Zhong Guang Zuo E (重光作噩), the year with Ren Xu (壬戌) was called Xuan Di Yan Mao (黓阉茂), the year with Gui Hai (癸亥) was called Zhao Yang Da Yuan Xian (昭阳大渊献), and the year with Jia Zi (甲子) was called Yafeng Kun Dun (阏逢困敦), and the year with Yi Chou (乙丑) was called Zhan Meng Chi Fen Ruo (旃蒙赤奋若).

    In the first month, if the stem was Jia (甲), it was called Bi Zou (畢陬); in the second month, if the stem was Yi (乙), it was called Ju Ru (橘如); in the third month, if the stem was Bing (丙), it was called Xiu Yi (修寎); in the fourth month, if the stem was Ding (丁), it was called Yu Yu (圉余); in the fifth month, if the stem was Wu (戊), it was called Li Gao (厉臯); in the sixth month, if the stem was Ji (己), it was called Ze Qie (則且); in the seventh month, if the stem was Geng (庚), it was called Zhi Xiang (窒相); in the eighth month, if the stem was Xin (辛), it was called Se Zhuang (塞壯); in the ninth month, if the stem was Ren (壬), it was called Zhong Xuan (終); in the tenth month, if the stem was Gui (癸), it was called Ji Yang (極陽); in the eleventh month, if the stem was Jia (甲), it was called Bi Gu (畢辜); and in the twelfth month, if the stem was Yi (乙), it was called Ju Tu (橘涂).

    The 28 lunar mansions were divided into 12 segments (十二次). Jiaozuo (晉灼) noted that when the Tai Sui was at the four cardinal points (四仲), it moved through three mansions per year; when it was at the four beginning months (四孟) and the four seasons (四季), it moved through two mansions per year. Over 12 years, it moved through all 28 mansions, completing one cycle around the sky. This is because Jupiter (歲星) moves through one segment per year. [6]

    山海东荒经曰大荒之中有山名曰大言日月所出有波谷山者有大人之国又云大荒之中有山名曰合虚日月所出有中容之国河圗括地象云二仪气分伏者为天偃者为地
    　　物理论云水土之气升为天
    　　广雅云东方苍天东南阳天南方炎天西南朱天西方成天西北幽天北方天东北变天中央钧天
    　　五经通义云天神之大者曰昊天上帝其佐曰五帝淮南子云日出旸谷浴于咸池拂于扶桑是谓晨明登于扶桑是谓朏【音斐】明至于曲阿是谓朝明临于曽泉是谓早食次于桑野是谓晏食臻于衡阳是谓禺中对于昆吾是谓正中靡于鸟次【西南山谷】是谓小迁至于悲谷是谓晡时回于女纪是谓大迁经于泉隅是谓髙舂顿于连【音烂】石是谓下舂爰息六螭是谓悬车薄于虞泉是谓黄昏沦于蒙谷是谓定昏日入崦嵫【落棠山】经细栁入虞泉之地蒙谷之浦垂景在树端谓之桑榆 [7]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [7]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 山海东荒经曰 - Formal introduction indicating the following text is from the *Shan Hai Jing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas), a classical Chinese text describing ancient geography and mythology.
    • 大言 - A term used in the *Shan Hai Jing* to describe a mountain where the sun and moon rise.
    • 波谷山 - A mountain name mentioned in the *Shan Hai Jing*, likely referring to a specific geographical feature.
    • 大人之国 - Refers to a mythical land of giants or a land inhabited by large beings.
    • 中容之国 - Refers to a mythical land of moderation or balance.
    • 河图括地象 - A text from the *Huainanzi* (Master Huainan), which describes the shape and structure of the earth.
    • 二仪气分 - A phrase from the *Huainanzi*, meaning the division of the two principles (heaven and earth) into different forms.
    • 伏者为天，偃者为地 - A metaphorical expression from the *Huainanzi*, comparing the rising and setting of the sun to the creation of heaven and earth.
    • 物理论云 - A formal introduction indicating that the following text is from the *Wu Lun* (Treatise on Things), a text discussing the nature of things.
    • 水土之气升为天 - A statement from the *Wu Lun*, meaning that the vapor from water and soil rises to form the sky.
    • 广雅云 - A formal introduction indicating that the following text is from the *Guangya* (Expanded Meaning), a dictionary of classical Chinese.
    • 东方苍天，东南阳天，南方炎天，西南朱天，西方成天，西北幽天，北方玄天，东北变天，中央钧天 - A list of directional skies, each associated with a specific color or quality.
    • 五经通义云 - A formal introduction indicating that the following text is from the *Wu Jing Tongyi* (General Meaning of the Five Classics), a text explaining the meanings of the Five Classics.
    • 昊天上帝 - Refers to the supreme god in Chinese cosmology.
    • 五帝 - Refers to the five emperors in Chinese mythology.
    • 淮南子云 - A formal introduction indicating that the following text is from the *Huainanzi*, a philosophical text.
    • 日出旸谷，浴于咸池，拂于扶桑，是谓晨明 - A description of the sun's journey from sunrise to sunset, with specific locations and stages.
    • 登于扶桑，是谓朏明 - A continuation of the sun's journey, describing the early morning light.
    • 至于曲阿，是谓朝明 - A continuation of the sun's journey, describing the morning light.
    • 临于曾泉，是谓早食 - A continuation of the sun's journey, describing the early morning.
    • 次于桑野，是谓晏食 - A continuation of the sun's journey, describing the mid-morning.
    • 臻于衡阳，是谓禺中 - A continuation of the sun's journey, describing the midday.
    • 对于昆吾，是谓正中 - A continuation of the sun's journey, describing the highest point of the sun.
    • 靡于鸟次，是谓小迁 - A continuation of the sun's journey, describing the late afternoon.
    • 至于悲谷，是谓晡时 - A continuation of the sun's journey, describing the late afternoon.
    • 回于女纪，是谓大迁 - A continuation of the sun's journey, describing the early evening.
    • 经于泉隅，是谓高舂 - A continuation of the sun's journey, describing the late afternoon.
    • 顿于连石，是谓下舂 - A continuation of the sun's journey, describing the late afternoon.
    • 爰息六螭，是谓悬车 - A continuation of the sun's journey, describing the sunset.
    • 薄于虞泉，是谓黄昏 - A continuation of the sun's journey, describing the twilight.
    • 沦于蒙谷，是谓定昏 - A continuation of the sun's journey, describing the nightfall.
    • 日入崦嵫，经细柳入虞泉之地，蒙谷之浦 - A continuation of the sun's journey, describing the sunset and its path.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 山海东荒经 - The *Shan Hai Jing* is an ancient Chinese text that describes the geography, mythology, and legends of ancient China.
    • 河图括地象 - The *Huainanzi* is a philosophical text that discusses various topics, including cosmology and the structure of the earth.
    • 广雅 - The *Guangya* is a dictionary of classical Chinese, providing definitions and explanations of words and phrases.
    • 五经通义 - The *Wu Jing Tongyi* is a text that explains the meanings of the Five Classics, which are the *Shijing* (Book of Poetry), *Shujing* (Book of Documents), *Yijing* (Book of Changes), *Liji* (Book of Rites), and *Chunqiu* (Spring and Autumn Annals).
    • 淮南子 - The *Huainanzi* is a philosophical text that discusses various topics, including cosmology, politics, and ethics.

    Proper Nouns

    • 山海东荒经 - The *Shan Hai Jing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas).
    • 河图括地象 - The *Huainanzi* (Master Huainan).
    • 广雅 - The *Guangya* (Expanded Meaning).
    • 五经通义 - The *Wu Jing Tongyi* (General Meaning of the Five Classics).
    • 淮南子 - The *Huainanzi* (Master Huainan).
    • 旸谷 - A mythical place where the sun rises.
    • 咸池 - A mythical place where the sun bathes.
    • 扶桑 - A mythical tree where the sun rests.
    • 曲阿 - A mythical place where the sun shines.
    • 曾泉 - A mythical place where the sun shines.
    • 桑野 - A mythical place where the sun shines.
    • 衡阳 - A mythical place where the sun shines.
    • 昆吾 - A mythical place where the sun shines.
    • 鸟次 - A mythical place where the sun shines.
    • 悲谷 - A mythical place where the sun shines.
    • 女纪 - A mythical place where the sun shines.
    • 泉隅 - A mythical place where the sun shines.
    • 连石 - A mythical place where the sun shines.
    • 六螭 - A mythical creature that the sun rests on.
    • 虞泉 - A mythical place where the sun sets.
    • 蒙谷 - A mythical place where the sun sets.
    • 崦嵫 - A mythical place where the sun sets.
    • 细柳 - A mythical place where the sun sets.

    The Shan Hai Jing (Classic of Mountains and Seas) states: In the vast wilderness, there is a mountain called Da Yan, where the sun and moon rise. There is also a mountain called Bo Gu, where the land of giants resides. It is also said that in the vast wilderness, there is a mountain called He Xu, where the sun and moon rise, and the land of moderation exists. The Huainanzi (Master Huainan) states: The division of the two principles (heaven and earth) results in the rising and setting of the sun and moon, with the rising being heaven and the setting being earth. The Wu Lun (Treatise on Things) states: The vapor from water and soil rises to form the sky. The Guangya (Expanded Meaning) states: The eastern sky is azure, the southeastern sky is bright, the southern sky is fiery, the southwestern sky is red, the western sky is complete, the northwestern sky is dark, the northern sky is black, the northeastern sky is changing, and the central sky is balanced. The Wu Jing Tongyi (General Meaning of the Five Classics) states: The supreme deity is called Hao Tian Shang Di, and his assistants are the five emperors. The Huainanzi states: The sun rises in Yang Gu, bathes in Xian Chi, and rests on Fu Sang, which is called Chen Ming. It then rises on Fu Sang, which is called Fei Ming. It reaches Qu E, which is called Chao Ming. It shines on Zeng Quan, which is called Zao Shi. It rests on Sang Ye, which is called Yan Shi. It reaches Heng Yang, which is called Yu Zhong. It faces Kun Wu, which is called Zheng Zhong. It rests on Niao Ci (a place in the southwest), which is called Xiao Qian. It reaches Bei Gu, which is called Bu Shi. It returns to Nu Ji, which is called Da Qian. It passes through Quan Yu, which is called Gao Chun. It rests on Lian Shi, which is called Xia Chun. It then rests on Liu Chi, which is called Xuan Che. It approaches Yu Quan, which is called Huang Hun. It descends into Meng Gu, which is called Ding Hun. The sun sets in Yan Zi (a place in the west), passing through Xi Liu into the land of Yu Quan and the banks of Meng Gu, where its reflection is seen on the tree tops, called Sang Yu. [7]

    吕氏春秋曰白人之南建木之下日中无影盖天地之中也
    　　汉书云月立夏夏至行南方赤道曰南陆立秋秋分行西方白道曰西陆立冬冬至行北方曰北陆分则同道至则相过晦而见西方谓之脁朔而见东方谓之朒亦谓之侧匿脁健行朒缩迟
    　　周官天星皆有分野角亢氐兖州房心豫州尾箕幽州斗女州虚危青州室东壁并州奎娄胃徐州昴毕冀州觜觿参益州东井舆雍州栁星张三河翼轸荆州
    　　郑司农云保章氏五云之物二至二分观云色青虫白丧赤兵荒黒水黄丰东方朔别云凡占东畔当视天有黄云来如覆车五谷大熟青致兵白致盗乌黒多水赤火 [8]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [8]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 白人之南建木之下日中无影 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'at the south of the White Man's land under the Great Tree where there is no shadow at noon,' referring to a mythical or symbolic location in ancient Chinese geography.
    • 天地之中也 - Means 'the center of the world' or 'the heart of heaven and earth.'
    • 月立夏夏至行南方赤道曰南陆 - Describes the moon's movement during the summer solstice, indicating it travels along the southern path of the celestial equator, known as the 'Southern Land.'
    • 立秋秋分行西方白道曰西陆 - Describes the moon's movement during the autumn equinox, indicating it travels along the western path of the Milky Way, known as the 'Western Land.'
    • 立冬冬至行北方曰北陆 - Describes the moon's movement during the winter solstice, indicating it travels along the northern path, known as the 'Northern Land.'
    • 晦而见西方谓之脁朔而见东方谓之朒亦谓之侧匿脁健行朒缩迟 - Complex description of the moon's phases, where '晦' (dark) and '朔' (new) refer to the new moon, and '朒' (dim) and '侧匿' (hidden) describe the waning moon. '脁健行' (strongly moving) and '朒缩迟' (slowly contracting) describe the moon's movements during different phases.
    • 保章氏五云之物二至二分观云色青虫白丧赤兵荒黑水黄丰 - Refers to the observation of clouds by the official '保章氏' (preserver of chapters) during the solstices and equinoxes. Different colors of clouds are associated with different omens: green for insects, white for mourning, red for war, black for water, and yellow for abundance.
    • 郑司农云 - Indicates a citation from the scholar Zheng Sinao.
    • 凡占东畔当视天有黄云来如覆车五谷大熟青致兵白致盗乌黑多水赤火 - Describes the interpretation of auspicious signs in the eastern sky, where yellow clouds like a overturned cart indicate a bountiful harvest, green clouds bring war, white clouds bring thieves, black clouds bring water, and red clouds bring fire.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 吕氏春秋 - A classic text compiled by the scholars of the state of Qin during the Warring States period, which covers various aspects of philosophy, politics, and science.
    • 汉书 - A historical text compiled by Ban Gu during the Eastern Han dynasty, which records the history of the Western Han dynasty.
    • 周官 - Also known as the *Rites of Zhou*, an ancient text that describes the organization of the Zhou dynasty's government and rituals.
    • 郑司农 - Zheng Sinao, a scholar from the Western Han dynasty known for his contributions to the study of divination and astronomy.
    • 保章氏 - An official responsible for astronomical observations and divination in ancient China.
    • 五云之物 - The five types of clouds observed during the solstices and equinoxes, each with its own significance.
    • 二至二分 - Refers to the solstices and equinoxes, which were important in ancient Chinese calendrical and astronomical systems.
    • 东方朔 - Dongfang Shuo, a court jester and scholar of the Han dynasty.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The sentence '白人之南建木之下日中无影盖天地之中也' is ambiguous and could be interpreted as 'At the south of the White Man's land under the Great Tree where there is no shadow at noon, it is said to be the center of the world.'
    • The long sentence '月立夏夏至行南方赤道曰南陆立秋秋分行西方白道曰西陆立冬冬至行北方曰北陆分则同道至则相过晦而见西方谓之脁朔而见东方谓之朒亦谓之侧匿脁健行朒缩迟' describes the moon's movements during the solstices and equinoxes, with each part detailing a specific phase and path.
    • The sentence '郑司农云保章氏五云之物二至二分观云色青虫白丧赤兵荒黑水黄丰东方朔别传云凡占东畔当视天有黄云来如覆车五谷大熟青致兵白致盗乌黑多水赤火' combines a citation from Zheng Sinao with a description of the interpretation of auspicious signs, which can be broken down into two main parts for clarity.

    Proper Nouns

    • 吕氏春秋 - *Lüshi Chunqiu* (Spring and Autumn Annals of Mr. Lü)
    • 汉书 - *Han Shu* (Book of Han)
    • 周官 - *Zhouguan* (Rites of Zhou)
    • 郑司农 - Zheng Sinao
    • 保章氏 - Preserver of Chapters
    • 五云之物 - Five Clouds
    • 二至二分 - Solstices and Equinoxes
    • 东方朔 - Dongfang Shuo

    The Lüshi Chunqiu states that at the south of the White Man's land under the Great Tree, where there is no shadow at noon, it is said to be the center of the world.
    The Han Shu records that during the summer solstice, the moon travels along the southern path of the celestial equator, known as the Southern Land; during the autumn equinox, it travels along the western path of the Milky Way, known as the Western Land; and during the winter solstice, it travels along the northern path, known as the Northern Land. When the moon divides, it follows the same path, and when it reaches its zenith, it passes each other. When the moon is dark, it appears in the west, which is called "脁"; when it is new, it appears in the east, which is called "朒," also known as "侧匿." "脁" moves strongly, while "朒" moves slowly.
    According to the Zhouguan, the stars in the sky all have their corresponding regions on earth: the regions of Jiao, Kang, and Di correspond to the state of Yan; the regions of Fang and Xin correspond to the state of Yu; the regions of Wei and Ji correspond to the state of You; the region of Dou corresponds to the state of Nv; the region of Xu and Wei corresponds to the state of Yang; the region of Xu and Wei corresponds to the state of Qing; the region of Shi and Dongbi corresponds to the state of Bing; the region of Kui, Lou, and Wei corresponds to the state of Xu; the region of Mao and Bi corresponds to the state of Ji; the region of Zi and Xi corresponds to the state of Yi; the region of Dongjing and Yuzhao corresponds to the state of Yong; the region of Liu, Xing, and Zhang corresponds to the Three Rivers; the region of Yi and Zhen corresponds to the state of Jing.
    Zheng Sinao said that the official "保章氏" observes five types of clouds during the solstices and equinoxes: green clouds indicate insects, white clouds indicate mourning, red clouds indicate war, black clouds indicate water, and yellow clouds indicate abundance. Dongfang Shuo's Biography states that when observing the eastern sky, one should look for yellow clouds like an overturned cart, which indicate a bountiful harvest; green clouds bring war, white clouds bring thieves, black clouds bring water, and red clouds bring fire. [8]

    京房风雨占曰黒云如一疋皁二日中一日大雨二疋三日雨三疋十日雨
    　　易纬曰八节之风谓之八风吕氏春秋云东北曰猋风亦曰融风东曰滔风东南曰薫风南曰巨风西曰飕风西北曰厉风北方曰寒风
    　　王充论衡曰圗画之工图雷之状如连鼓形又图一人若力士谓之雷公使左手引连鼓右手椎之春秋震夷伯之庙谓劈歴破之是也霹俗字也
    　　梁太后信兄冀诛杜乔李固雷震宪陵寝屋
    　　太平之时十日一雨一嵗三十六雨
    　　晋中兴书曰甘露降耆老得敬则松柏受之尊贤容众则竹苇受之一曰天酒虹依云隂而昼见于日冲无云不见大阴亦不见
    　　炎帝赤精之君火官之臣火庭氏也
    　　明皇在长安诏一行铸浑仪及大衍厯先是汉洛下闳云此厯后一千年差一日有圣人出而正之至是果一千年因以一行为圣人僧一行本名璲俗姓张氏后出家改名敬贤迁化诏起塔铜人原諡大恵禅师并御制碑八分书 [9]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [9]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 京房风雨占 - Refers to a method of predicting rain based on cloud formations, attributed to Jing Fang.
    • 黒云如一疋皁 - Describes black clouds shaped like a piece of black cloth.
    • 二日中一日大雨 - Indicates that within two days, there will be one day of heavy rain.
    • 二疋三日雨 - Describes two pieces of black cloth clouds predicting rain over three days.
    • 三疋十日雨 - Describes three pieces of black cloth clouds predicting rain over ten days.
    • 易纬 - Refers to the *Yiwei*, a collection of divination texts related to the *Yijing* (Book of Changes).
    • 八节之风谓之八风 - Refers to the eight winds associated with the eight solar terms.
    • 吕氏春秋云 - Refers to the *Lüshi Chunqiu*, an encyclopedic text by Lü Buwei.
    • 东北曰猋风亦曰融风 - Describes the northeast wind as both *biāo fēng* and *róng fēng*.
    • 东曰滔风 - Describes the east wind as *tāo fēng*.
    • 东南曰薫风 - Describes the southeast wind as *xūn fēng*.
    • 南曰巨风 - Describes the south wind as *jù fēng*.
    • 西曰飕风 - Describes the west wind as *sōu fēng*.
    • 西北曰厉风 - Describes the northwest wind as *lì fēng*.
    • 北方曰寒风 - Describes the north wind as *hán fēng*.
    • 王充论衡 - Refers to the *Lunheng* by Wang Chong, a philosophical work.
    • 圗画之工图雷之状如连鼓形 - Describes the depiction of thunder as resembling a series of drums.
    • 又图一人若力士谓之雷公 - Describes the depiction of a powerful man as *Leigong* (Thunder God).
    • 使左手引连鼓右手椎之 - Describes the action of striking the drums with a mallet.
    • 春秋震夷伯之庙谓劈歴破之是也 - Refers to a thunderstorm that destroyed the temple of Yi Bo, described as *pī lì* (thunderbolt).
    • 霹俗字也 - Notes that *pī lì* is a common term for thunderbolt.
    • 梁太后信兄冀诛杜乔李固雷震宪陵寝屋 - Describes a historical event where a thunderstorm destroyed the tomb of Xian Emperor due to the actions of Empress Dowager Liang and her brother Liang Ji.
    • 太平之时十日一雨一嵗三十六雨 - Describes a peaceful time with rain every ten days, totaling 36 days of rain in a year.
    • 晋中兴书 - Refers to the *Jin Zhongxing Shu*, a historical text.
    • 甘露降耆老得敬则松柏受之尊贤容众则竹苇受之 - Describes the phenomenon of sweet dew falling, which is respected by old people and accepted by pine and cypress trees, and by the wise and inclusive, it is accepted by bamboo and reeds.
    • 一曰天酒 - Refers to sweet dew as 'heavenly wine.'
    • 虹依云隂而昼见于日冲无云不见大阴亦不见 - Describes the appearance of a rainbow, which is visible during the day when there are clouds but not when there are no clouds or during a solar eclipse.
    • 炎帝赤精之君火官之臣火庭氏也 - Refers to the Flame Emperor, a mythical ruler, and his fire officials.
    • 明皇在长安诏一行铸浑仪及大衍厯 - Describes Emperor Xuanzong of Tang ordering the casting of a celestial globe and the *Dayan Li* (Great Derivative Calendar).
    • 先是汉洛下闳云此厯后一千年差一日有圣人出而正之 - Refers to a prophecy by Luo Xiaohong that the calendar would be off by one day after a thousand years, and a sage would correct it.
    • 至是果一千年因以一行为圣人 - Describes how the prophecy was fulfilled when the calendar was corrected after a thousand years, and Yi Xing was recognized as the sage.
    • 僧一行本名璲俗姓张氏后出家改名敬贤迁化诏起塔铜人原諡大恵禅师并御制碑八分书 - Describes the life of Yi Xing, originally named Sui, who later became a monk and was posthumously honored as a great master with a bronze statue and an imperial stele.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 京房 - A famous Chinese astronomer and mathematician from the Western Han dynasty.
    • 易纬 - A collection of divination texts related to the *Yijing* (Book of Changes).
    • 吕氏春秋 - An encyclopedic text compiled by Lü Buwei during the Warring States period.
    • 王充 - A philosopher known for his work *Lunheng*.
    • 梁太后 - Empress Dowager Liang, a powerful figure during the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 晋中兴书 - A historical text from the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 炎帝 - The Flame Emperor, a mythical ruler in Chinese mythology.
    • 明皇 - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, a renowned emperor known for his cultural achievements.
    • 僧一行 - Yi Xing, a Buddhist monk and astronomer who made significant contributions to Chinese astronomy and calendar science.
    • 洛下闳 - Luo Xiaohong, a mathematician and astronomer from the Western Han dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 京房 - A famous Chinese astronomer and mathematician.
    • 易纬 - A collection of divination texts related to the *Yijing*.
    • 吕氏春秋 - An encyclopedic text compiled by Lü Buwei.
    • 王充 - A philosopher known for his work *Lunheng*.
    • 梁太后 - Empress Dowager Liang of the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 晋中兴书 - A historical text from the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 炎帝 - The Flame Emperor, a mythical ruler.
    • 明皇 - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang.
    • 僧一行 - Yi Xing, a Buddhist monk and astronomer.
    • 洛下闳 - Luo Xiaohong, a mathematician and astronomer from the Western Han dynasty.

    Final Translation

    Jing Fang's method of predicting rain based on cloud formations states that black clouds shaped like a piece of black cloth predict one day of heavy rain within two days. Two pieces of black cloth clouds predict rain over three days, and three pieces predict rain over ten days.

    The Yiwei says that the eight winds associated with the eight solar terms are called the Eight Winds. According to the Lüshi Chunqiu, the northeast wind is also called biāo fēng or róng fēng, the east wind is called tāo fēng, the southeast wind is called xūn fēng, the south wind is called jù fēng, the west wind is called sōu fēng, the northwest wind is called lì fēng, and the north wind is called hán fēng.

    Wang Chong, in his Lunheng, says that in depictions of thunder, it is shown as resembling a series of drums, and a powerful man is depicted as Leigong (Thunder God). He is shown striking the drums with a mallet. The Spring and Autumn Annals record a thunderstorm that destroyed the temple of Yi Bo, described as a pī lì (thunderbolt). Pī lì is a common term for thunderbolt.

    Empress Dowager Liang, believing in her brother Liang Ji, ordered the execution of Du Qiao and Li Guo, and a thunderstorm subsequently destroyed the tomb of Xian Emperor.

    In peaceful times, it rains once every ten days, totaling 36 days of rain in a year.

    The Jin Zhongxing Shu states that when sweet dew falls, it is respected by old people and accepted by pine and cypress trees. When the wise and inclusive are honored, it is accepted by bamboo and reeds. It is also called "heavenly wine." A rainbow appears during the day when there are clouds but not when there are no clouds or during a solar eclipse.

    The Flame Emperor, a mythical ruler, is associated with fire officials and the Huoting (Fire Court).

    Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, while in Chang'an, ordered Yi Xing to cast a celestial globe and the Dayan Li (Great Derivative Calendar). Earlier, Luo Xiaohong of the Han dynasty had prophesied that this calendar would be off by one day after a thousand years, and a sage would correct it. When the prophecy was fulfilled after a thousand years, Yi Xing was recognized as the sage. Originally named Sui and of the surname Zhang, he later became a monk, changed his name to Jingxian, and was posthumously honored as a great master. A bronze statue was erected, and an imperial stele with eight-part script was erected in his honor. [9]

    二十八宿为其有二十八星当度故立以为宿推测者多变如毕有十七度半觜半度星既不当度自不当用为宿星度皆以赤道为法黄道则有斜有直与赤道不等
    　　刘知防曰两曜日星丽于天象非如九州万国废置无常故海田可变而景纬无易必欲刋之国史施于何代不可也史记书有天官读者竟忘其悮班固天文志非汉事而入汉书已乖越矣且如荧惑退舍宋公延龄中台告坼晋相速祸星集颍川而贤人聚月犯少微而处士亡如斯之志之可也若乃体分蒙澒色着青苍丹曦素魄之次黄道紫宫之分野既不预于人事乃编之于防书又曰天文有志何不为人形志乎
    　　太嵗在丑乞浆得酒太嵗在已贩妻鬻子藕生应月闰月益一节芋以十二子为卫亦应月之数也
    　　俗以五月雨为分龙雨一曰隔辙雨
    　　云五色为庆三色为矞或曰外赤内青为矞雄曰紫蜺矞云朋围日君子小人并进也
    　　郭璞曰蓬飞硙则天风春则天雨蓬蠓也传曰雷不盖酱令人腹中雷鸣月上下之时触酱辄壊里俗忌之 [10]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [10]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 二十八宿 - Refers to the 28 lunar mansions in Chinese astronomy, each associated with specific stars and constellations.
    • 赤道 - Refers to the celestial equator, the projection of Earth's equator onto the celestial sphere.
    • 黄道 - Refers to the ecliptic, the apparent path of the Sun across the sky.
    • 景纬 - Refers to the stars and constellations, often used in the context of astronomical phenomena.
    • 海田可变 - A metaphor suggesting that the sea and land can change, but the stars and constellations remain constant.
    • 中台告坼 - A reference to a star in the constellation of the Central Platform (中台) signaling a division or separation.
    • 月犯少微 - A term meaning 'the moon offends the minor star,' referring to a lunar conjunction with a specific star that was believed to have negative implications.
    • 蓬飞硙 - A phrase meaning 'the flying willow fluff,' used to describe a natural phenomenon.
    • 雷不盖酱 - A superstition that thunder should not cover the pot of soy sauce, as it is believed to cause internal thunder in the body.
    • 隔辙雨 - A term for a type of rain that occurs when the sun and moon are separated, often associated with the dragon's separation.
    • 紫蜺矞云 - Refers to a colorful cloud, often seen as a sign of good fortune.
    • 君子小人并进 - A phrase meaning 'gentlemen and small people advance together,' suggesting a mixed or mixed-motives environment.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 二十八星 - The 28 stars associated with the 28 lunar mansions, each with its own significance in Chinese astrology.
    • 刘知防 - A historical figure, likely a scholar or official, known for his commentary on astronomy.
    • 九州万国 - A phrase referring to the nine regions and myriad states of ancient China, symbolizing the vastness of the empire.
    • 荧惑退舍 - A term referring to the planet Mars moving backward in its orbit, often associated with significant events.
    • 宋公延龄 - Refers to a historical event or person, possibly a ruler or noble who benefited from the alignment of Mars.
    • 晋相速祸 - Refers to a historical event or person, possibly a minister or official who faced misfortune due to celestial signs.
    • 太岁 - A term in Chinese astrology referring to the celestial stem that governs the year and influences human affairs.
    • 藕生应月 - A phrase meaning 'the lotus grows in response to the moon,' suggesting a natural phenomenon linked to the lunar cycle.
    • 十二子为卫 - A term referring to the 12 zodiac animals, often used in calendrical and astrological contexts.
    • 分龙雨 - A term for a type of rain that occurs when the sun and moon are separated, often associated with the dragon's separation.
    • 蓬蠓 - A type of small insect or flying creature, often used in natural phenomena descriptions.
    • 月上下弦之时 - Refers to the first and last quarters of the lunar month, when the moon is half-lit.
    • 触酱辄坏 - A superstition that touching the pot of soy sauce during certain times (like the first and last quarters of the moon) can cause it to spoil.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • This sentence is complex and requires careful parsing. It states that the 28 lunar mansions were established because there are 28 stars in each mansion, and the degrees are divided accordingly. However, some stars do not fit neatly into these divisions, leading to variations in interpretation.
    • This sentence explains that the positions of stars are measured using the celestial equator (赤道), but the ecliptic (黄道) has varying angles, making it different from the celestial equator.
    • This sentence introduces a commentary by Liu Zhifang, who argues that the sun and stars are fixed in the sky, unlike the changing landscape of the earth, and that recording such constancy in historical texts is essential.
    • This sentence criticizes the *Records of the Grand Historian* (*Shiji*) for its inaccuracies and the *Book of Han* for including non-Han matters in its *Astronomical Records*.
    • This sentence discusses the inclusion of celestial phenomena in historical records, questioning why such phenomena are recorded while human affairs are not.
    • This sentence describes various superstitions and natural phenomena linked to the lunar cycle and the 12 zodiac animals.
    • This sentence explains a common belief about rain occurring when the sun and moon are separated, often associated with the dragon's separation.
    • This sentence describes colorful clouds and their symbolic meanings, often seen as signs of good fortune or mixed motives.
    • This sentence includes a commentary by Guo Pu, describing natural phenomena and associated superstitions.

    Proper Nouns

    • 二十八宿 - The 28 lunar mansions.
    • 赤道 - The celestial equator.
    • 黄道 - The ecliptic.
    • 刘知防 - A historical figure known for his commentary on astronomy.
    • 史记 - The *Records of the Grand Historian*.
    • 班固 - A historian who wrote the *Book of Han*.
    • 汉书 - The *Book of Han*.
    • 太岁 - A term in Chinese astrology.
    • 郭璞 - A scholar and commentator known for his works on natural phenomena and superstitions.

    The 28 lunar mansions were established because there are 28 stars in each mansion, and the degrees are divided accordingly. However, some stars do not fit neatly into these divisions, leading to variations in interpretation. The positions of stars are measured using the celestial equator, but the ecliptic has varying angles, making it different from the celestial equator.

    Liu Zhifang said that the sun and stars are fixed in the sky, unlike the changing landscape of the earth, and that recording such constancy in historical texts is essential. The Records of the Grand Historian (Shiji) contains inaccuracies, and the Book of Han includes non-Han matters in its Astronomical Records, which is a deviation from proper historical recording. For example, the planet Mars moving backward (荧惑退舍) was associated with the longevity of Song Gong, and a star in the constellation of the Central Platform (中台) signaling a division (中台告坼) was linked to the downfall of a minister. Similarly, the gathering of stars in Yingchuan (星集颍川) was associated with the gathering of virtuous people, and the moon offending a minor star (月犯少微) was linked to the death of a recluse. Such celestial phenomena should be recorded in historical texts.

    As for the divisions and colors of the heavens, the celestial equator and ecliptic are not directly related to human affairs, but they are included in historical records. The question arises: if celestial phenomena are recorded, why are not human affairs recorded in the same way?

    When the Tai Sui (太岁) is in the sign of the Ox (丑), one might obtain wine instead of water; when it is in the sign of the Snake (巳), one might sell one's wife or children. The lotus grows in response to the moon, and the 12 zodiac animals serve as a guard, all corresponding to the lunar cycle.

    Common belief holds that rain in May is called "divided dragon rain" (分龙雨), also known as "separated chariot rain" (隔辙雨).

    A cloud with five colors is a sign of good fortune, while a cloud with three colors is a sign of mixed motives. Some say that a cloud with an outer red and inner green color (外赤内青) is a sign of mixed motives, and Yang Xiong (扬雄) said that a purple cloud surrounding the sun (紫蜺矞云) is a sign of both gentlemen and small people advancing together.

    Guo Pu (郭璞) said that flying willow fluff (蓬飞硙) indicates a wind, and rain in spring (春则天雨) is a natural phenomenon. The Classic of Mountains and Seas (传) says that thunder should not cover the pot of soy sauce (雷不盖酱), as it is believed to cause internal thunder in the body. Touching the pot of soy sauce during the first and last quarters of the moon (月上下弦之时) can cause it to spoil, and this is a common superstition. [10]

    自古帝王五运之次有二说邹衍以五行相胜为义刘向则以相生为义汉魏共遵刘说
    　　关东西风则晴东风则雨关西西风则雨东风则晴阳之専气为雹阴之専气为霰
    　　中国有洞天三十六所皆谓之天第一王屋山洞天周回万里名小有清虚天其次委羽山洞天周回万里名大髙空明天其次太总真天其次三极真天其次宝仙九室天其次上玉清平天其次珠明耀眞天其次金坛华阳天其次左神幽虚天其次成徳天皆仙人所居也
    　　木与木相摩则然金与火相守则流隂阳错行则天地大乱于是乎有雷有霆水中有火乃焚大槐枝间往往见细石形如小斧谓之霹斧或谓云霹楔案中记云玉门之西有国山山上有庙国人嵗嵗出礸数千名霹礸给霹用从春至秋乃罢礼记有杂金鑚牛角鑚鑚与礸同音祖合切
    　　京房易曰四方常有大云五色具而不雨下有贤人防将风则踊鼍欲雨则鸣
    　　汉博士刘熈曰豫司兖冀以舌腹言之天显也在上髙显也青徐以舌头言之天坦也坦然髙而逺也 [11]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [11]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 自古帝王五运之次有二说 - Statement about the two theories regarding the sequence of the Five Elements in imperial dynasties.
    • 邹衍以五行相胜为义 - Refers to Zou Yan's theory of the Five Elements overcoming each other.
    • 刘向则以相生为义 - Refers to Liu Xiang's theory of the Five Elements generating each other.
    • 汉魏共遵刘说 - Indicates that the Han and Wei dynasties followed Liu Xiang's theory.
    • 关东西风则晴东风则雨 - Weather-related saying indicating that in the east of the pass, west wind brings clear weather and east wind brings rain.
    • 关西西风则雨东风则晴 - Weather-related saying indicating that in the west of the pass, west wind brings rain and east wind brings clear weather.
    • 阳之专气为雹阴之专气为霰 - Describes natural phenomena where pure yang energy forms hail and pure yin energy forms snowflakes.
    • 中国有洞天三十六所 - Refers to the thirty-six 'heavenly realms' or 'cave heavens' in China.
    • 皆谓之天 - Means 'all are called heavens.'
    • 第一王屋山洞天 - Refers to the first of the thirty-six heavenly realms, located in Wangwu Mountain.
    • 周回万里 - Means 'surrounding an area of ten thousand miles.'
    • 名小有清虚天 - Named 'Xiao You Qing Xu Tian' (Small Purity and Void Heaven).
    • 其次委羽山洞天 - Refers to the second heavenly realm, located in Weyu Mountain.
    • 名大高空明天 - Named 'Da Gao Kong Ming Tian' (Great High Void Bright Heaven).
    • 其次太玄总真天 - Refers to the third heavenly realm, named 'Tai Xuan Zong Zhen Tian' (Great Mystery Comprehensive True Heaven).
    • 其次三玄极真天 - Refers to the fourth heavenly realm, named 'San Xuan Ji Zhen Tian' (Three Mysteries Ultimate True Heaven).
    • 其次宝仙九室天 - Refers to the fifth heavenly realm, named 'Bao Xian Jiu Shi Tian' (Treasure Immortal Nine Chambers Heaven).
    • 其次上玉清平天 - Refers to the sixth heavenly realm, named 'Shang Yu Qing Ping Tian' (Upper Jade Pure Level Heaven).
    • 其次珠明耀真天 - Refers to the seventh heavenly realm, named 'Zhu Ming Yao Zhen Tian' (Jade Bright Radiant True Heaven).
    • 其次金坛华阳天 - Refers to the eighth heavenly realm, named 'Jin Tan Hua Yang Tian' (Golden Altar Flower Bright Heaven).
    • 其次左神幽虚天 - Refers to the ninth heavenly realm, named 'Zuo Shen You Xu Tian' (Left Divine Dark Void Heaven).
    • 其次成德玄天 - Refers to the tenth heavenly realm, named 'Cheng De Xuan Tian' (Complete Virtue Mystery Heaven).
    • 皆仙人所居也 - Means 'all are inhabited by immortals.'
    • 木与木相摩则然 - Describes a phenomenon where wood rubbing against wood can ignite.
    • 金与火相守则流 - Describes a phenomenon where metal and fire interacting can cause metal to melt.
    • 阴阳错行则天地大乱 - Describes a chaotic situation where yin and yang energies are out of balance.
    • 于是乎有雷有霆 - Indicates that thunder and lightning occur in such situations.
    • 水中火乃焚大槐 - Describes a phenomenon where fire in water can burn large willow trees.
    • 枝间往往见细石形如小斧谓之霹雳斧 - Describes a phenomenon where small stones shaped like axes are seen in tree branches, called 'leap lightning axes.'
    • 或谓云霹雳楔 - Another name for the same phenomenon, 'leap lightning wedges.'
    • 案玄中记云 - Refers to a reference in the *Xuanzhong Ji* (Records of the Mysterious Center).
    • 玉门之西有国山 - Refers to a mountain west of the Jade Gate.
    • 山上有庙 - Indicates there is a temple on the mountain.
    • 国人岁岁出礸数千名霹雳礸 - Describes how the people annually produce thousands of 'leap lightning axes.'
    • 给霹雳用 - Means 'for use in leap lightning.'
    • 从春至秋乃罢 - Indicates that this production continues from spring to autumn.
    • 礼记有杂金鑚牛角鑚 - Refers to a reference in the *Li Ji* (Book of Rites) about a mixed metal tool used for scraping cattle horns.
    • 鑚鑚与礸同音祖合切 - Indicates that '鑚鑚' and '礸' have the same pronunciation, 'zǔ hé.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 邹衍 - A philosopher and cosmologist from the Warring States period known for his theory of the Five Elements.
    • 刘向 - A scholar and historian from the Western Han period known for his contributions to literature and historiography.
    • 汉魏 - Refers to the Han and Wei dynasties, which followed each other in Chinese history.
    • 关东 - Refers to the area east of the Great Wall, historically significant for its political and cultural developments.
    • 关西 - Refers to the area west of the Great Wall, also historically significant.
    • 王屋山 - A famous mountain in Henan Province, associated with Daoist traditions.
    • 委羽山 - A mountain in Zhejiang Province, also associated with Daoist traditions.
    • 太玄总真天 - A concept in Daoist cosmology, referring to a realm of supreme mystery and truth.
    • 三玄极真天 - Another Daoist concept, referring to a realm of ultimate truth.
    • 宝仙九室天 - A Daoist concept, referring to a realm inhabited by treasure immortals.
    • 上玉清平天 - A Daoist concept, referring to a realm of supreme purity and levelness.
    • 珠明耀真天 - A Daoist concept, referring to a realm of radiant brightness.
    • 金坛华阳天 - A Daoist concept, referring to a realm of golden altars and bright flowers.
    • 左神幽虚天 - A Daoist concept, referring to a realm of left divine darkness.
    • 成德玄天 - A Daoist concept, referring to a realm of complete virtue and mystery.
    • 礼记 - The *Li Ji* (Book of Rites), a classic text on rituals and social norms in ancient China.

    Proper Nouns

    • 自古帝王五运之次有二说 - Statement about the two theories regarding the sequence of the Five Elements in imperial dynasties.
    • 邹衍以五行相胜为义 - Refers to Zou Yan's theory of the Five Elements overcoming each other.
    • 刘向则以相生为义 - Refers to Liu Xiang's theory of the Five Elements generating each other.
    • 汉魏共遵刘说 - Indicates that the Han and Wei dynasties followed Liu Xiang's theory.
    • 关东西风则晴东风则雨 - Weather-related saying indicating that in the east of the pass, west wind brings clear weather and east wind brings rain.
    • 关西西风则雨东风则晴 - Weather-related saying indicating that in the west of the pass, west wind brings rain and east wind brings clear weather.
    • 阳之专气为雹阴之专气为霰 - Describes natural phenomena where pure yang energy forms hail and pure yin energy forms snowflakes.
    • 中国有洞天三十六所 - Refers to the thirty-six 'heavenly realms' or 'cave heavens' in China.
    • 皆谓之天 - Means 'all are called heavens.'
    • 第一王屋山洞天 - Refers to the first of the thirty-six heavenly realms, located in Wangwu Mountain.
    • 周回万里 - Means 'surrounding an area of ten thousand miles.'
    • 名小有清虚天 - Named 'Xiao You Qing Xu Tian' (Small Purity and Void Heaven).
    • 其次委羽山洞天 - Refers to the second heavenly realm, located in Weyu Mountain.
    • 名大高空明天 - Named 'Da Gao Kong Ming Tian' (Great High Void Bright Heaven).
    • 其次太玄总真天 - Refers to the third heavenly realm, named 'Tai Xuan Zong Zhen Tian' (Great Mystery Comprehensive True Heaven).
    • 其次三玄极真天 - Refers to the fourth heavenly realm, named 'San Xuan Ji Zhen Tian' (Three Mysteries Ultimate True Heaven).
    • 其次宝仙九室天 - Refers to the fifth heavenly realm, named 'Bao Xian Jiu Shi Tian' (Treasure Immortal Nine Chambers Heaven).
    • 其次上玉清平天 - Refers to the sixth heavenly realm, named 'Shang Yu Qing Ping Tian' (Upper Jade Pure Level Heaven).
    • 其次珠明耀真天 - Refers to the seventh heavenly realm, named 'Zhu Ming Yao Zhen Tian' (Jade Bright Radiant True Heaven).
    • 其次金坛华阳天 - Refers to the eighth heavenly realm, named 'Jin Tan Hua Yang Tian' (Golden Altar Flower Bright Heaven).
    • 其次左神幽虚天 - Refers to the ninth heavenly realm, named 'Zuo Shen You Xu Tian' (Left Divine Dark Void Heaven).
    • 其次成德玄天 - Refers to the tenth heavenly realm, named 'Cheng De Xuan Tian' (Complete Virtue Mystery Heaven).
    • 皆仙人所居也 - Means 'all are inhabited by immortals.'
    • 木与木相摩则然 - Describes a phenomenon where wood rubbing against wood can ignite.
    • 金与火相守则流 - Describes a phenomenon where metal and fire interacting can cause metal to melt.
    • 阴阳错行则天地大乱 - Describes a chaotic situation where yin and yang energies are out of balance.
    • 于是乎有雷有霆 - Indicates that thunder and lightning occur in such situations.
    • 水中火乃焚大槐 - Describes a phenomenon where fire in water can burn large willow trees.
    • 枝间往往见细石形如小斧谓之霹雳斧 - Describes a phenomenon where small stones shaped like axes are seen in tree branches, called 'leap lightning axes.'
    • 或谓云霹雳楔 - Another name for the same phenomenon, 'leap lightning wedges.'
    • 案玄中记云 - Refers to a reference in the *Xuanzhong Ji* (Records of the Mysterious Center).
    • 玉门之西有国山 - Refers to a mountain west of the Jade Gate.
    • 山上有庙 - Indicates there is a temple on the mountain.
    • 国人岁岁出礸数千名霹雳礸 - Describes how the people annually produce thousands of 'leap lightning axes.'
    • 给霹雳用 - Means 'for use in leap lightning.'
    • 从春至秋乃罢 - Indicates that this production continues from spring to autumn.
    • 礼记有杂金鑚牛角鑚 - Refers to a reference in the *Li Ji* (Book of Rites) about a mixed metal tool used for scraping cattle horns.
    • 鑚鑚与礸同音祖合切 - Indicates that '鑚鑚' and '礸' have the same pronunciation, 'zǔ hé.'

    From ancient times, there have been two theories regarding the sequence of the Five Elements in imperial dynasties. Zou Yan's theory posits that the Five Elements overcome each other, while Liu Xiang's theory suggests that they generate each other. The Han and Wei dynasties both followed Liu Xiang's theory.

    In the eastern part of the pass, a west wind brings clear weather, and an east wind brings rain. In the western part of the pass, a west wind brings rain, and an east wind brings clear weather. Pure yang energy forms hail, and pure yin energy forms snowflakes.

    In China, there are thirty-six "heavenly realms" or "cave heavens," all of which are called "heavens." The first is the Wangwu Mountain Heavenly Realm, surrounding an area of ten thousand miles, named "Xiao You Qing Xu Tian" (Small Purity and Void Heaven). The second is the Weyu Mountain Heavenly Realm, also surrounding an area of ten thousand miles, named "Da Gao Kong Ming Tian" (Great High Void Bright Heaven). The third is the "Tai Xuan Zong Zhen Tian" (Great Mystery Comprehensive True Heaven), the fourth is the "San Xuan Ji Zhen Tian" (Three Mysteries Ultimate True Heaven), the fifth is the "Bao Xian Jiu Shi Tian" (Treasure Immortal Nine Chambers Heaven), the sixth is the "Shang Yu Qing Ping Tian" (Upper Jade Pure Level Heaven), the seventh is the "Zhu Ming Yao Zhen Tian" (Jade Bright Radiant True Heaven), the eighth is the "Jin Tan Hua Yang Tian" (Golden Altar Flower Bright Heaven), the ninth is the "Zuo Shen You Xu Tian" (Left Divine Dark Void Heaven), and the tenth is the "Cheng De Xuan Tian" (Complete Virtue Mystery Heaven). All of these realms are inhabited by immortals.

    When wood rubs against wood, it can ignite. When metal and fire interact, the metal can melt. When yin and yang energies are out of balance, the world becomes chaotic, and thunder and lightning occur. Fire in water can burn large willow trees. Small stones shaped like axes are often seen in tree branches, known as "leap lightning axes" or "leap lightning wedges." According to the Xuanzhong Ji (Records of the Mysterious Center), there is a mountain west of the Jade Gate, with a temple on its summit. The people there produce thousands of "leap lightning axes" annually for use in leap lightning, continuing this practice from spring to autumn. The Li Ji (Book of Rites) mentions a mixed metal tool used for scraping cattle horns, which shares the same pronunciation as "礸," "zǔ hé."

    The Yi Zhuan (Commentary on the Book of Changes) by Jing Fang states that if there is a large cloud with all five colors but no rain, it indicates the presence of virtuous people who will soon rise to power, and the wind will rise, while the鼍 (a type of crocodile) will cry out when rain is imminent.

    Han scholar Liu Xi said that in the regions of Yu, Si, Yan, and Ji, the word "天" (tian) is pronounced with the tongue in the middle of the mouth, meaning "heaven" is clearly visible above, high and prominent. In the regions of Qing and Xu, the word "天" (tian) is pronounced with the tongue at the tip, meaning "heaven" is vast and distant. [11]

    续博物志卷一
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　续博物志卷二
    　　宋　李石　撰
    　　汉明帝为太子乐人以歌四章賛之一曰日重光二曰月重轮三曰星重曜四曰海重润
    　　鲸鱼者海鱼也大者长千里小者数千丈一生数万子常以五月六月就岸生子七月八月导从其子还大海中鼓浪成雷喷沫成雨水族惊畏莫敢近其雌曰鲵大者亦长千里
    　　禽经曰鵞飞则蜮沈鵙鸣则虵结又曰鹳俯鸣则阴仰鸣则晴
    　　白蚁闻竹鸡之声化为水竹鸡自呼泥滑滑是也或曰白鸡之雄亦能化白蚁
    　　鹳性甘带每遇巨石知有蛇即如术士禹步其石阞然而转南方学其法伺其养雏缘木以篾絙缚其巢鹳必作法解之乃于木下铺沙印其足迹而仿之
    　　暮鸠鸣即小雨朝鸢鸣即大风
    　　物有异体而相制者翡翠屑金人气粉犀北人以鍼敲冰南人以线解茶
    　　高诱注淮南子云方诸大蛤也熟摩拭令以向月则水生许慎说文曰诸珠也方石也 [12]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [12]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 續博物志 - Title of the text, a continuation of the *博物志* (Wubaozhi), a classical text on natural history and miscellaneous knowledge.
    • 四庫全書 - A massive imperial collection of Chinese literature and texts, compiled during the Qing dynasty. The phrase '欽定' indicates that it was officially sanctioned by the emperor.
    • 漢明帝為太子 - Refers to Emperor Ming of Han when he was a crown prince. This provides historical context for the events described.
    • 樂人以歌四章賛之 - Musicians composed four songs to praise something. The term '賛之' implies a form of laudatory or celebratory expression.
    • 鵞飛則蜮沈，鵙鳴則虵結 - Idiomatic expressions from the *禽經* (Avian Classic), which describe the behavior of birds and their effects on other creatures. '蜮沈' means 'the shadow of the crocodile sinks,' and '虵結' means 'the snake coils up.'
    • 鵲俯鳴則陰，仰鳴則晴 - Another idiom from the *禽經*, indicating that the behavior of the magpie can predict the weather.
    • 白蟻聞竹雞之聲化為水 - An unusual and fantastical description, where termites are said to turn into water when they hear the call of the bamboo partridge.
    • 鶴性甘帶 - Refers to the crane's preference for eating snakes. '甘帶' means 'to enjoy eating snakes.'
    • 方諸大蛤也 - A reference to the *方諸* (Fangzhu), a mythical stone that can collect dew when placed under the moon. '大蛤' means 'large clam' or 'large shellfish.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 漢明帝 - Emperor Ming of Han (r. 57–75 AD), the third emperor of the Eastern Han dynasty. He was known for his contributions to Buddhism and his efforts to strengthen the empire.
    • 四庫全書 - A monumental collection of Chinese literature, compiled in the late 18th century during the Qing dynasty. It includes a vast array of texts, from Confucian classics to historical records and literary works.
    • 續博物志 - A continuation of the *博物志* by Zhang Hua, a famous scholar of the Western Jin dynasty. The *博物志* is a compendium of natural history, mythology, and folklore.
    • 禽經 - An ancient text on birds, likely dating back to the Han dynasty. It contains descriptions of bird behavior and their symbolic meanings.
    • 高誘注淮南子 - A commentary by Gao You on the *Huainanzi*, a philosophical and encyclopedic text from the Han dynasty. The *Huainanzi* is a collection of essays on various topics, including cosmology, politics, and natural phenomena.

    Proper Nouns

    • 續博物志 - The title of the text, which is a continuation of the *博物志*.
    • 四庫全書 - A large imperial collection of Chinese literature.
    • 漢明帝 - Emperor Ming of Han.
    • 禽經 - An ancient text on birds.
    • 高誘 - A scholar who wrote a commentary on the *Huainanzi*.
    • 淮南子 - A philosophical and encyclopedic text from the Han dynasty.
    • 方諸 - A mythical stone that collects dew under the moon.

    Continuation of the Book of Natural History, Volume One
    　　Imperially Approved Complete Library of the Four Treasuries
    Continuation of the Book of Natural History, Volume Two
    　　Compiled by Li Shi of the Song Dynasty
