    Emperor Ming of Han, while still a crown prince, had musicians compose four songs to praise him: the first said "the sun shines twice," the second said "the moon has a double orbit," the third said "the stars shine brightly twice," and the fourth said "the sea is deeply nourished."

    The whale is a sea fish. The larger ones can grow up to a thousand li in length, while the smaller ones are several thousand feet long. They give birth to tens of thousands of offspring, usually in May and June when they come ashore to give birth. In July and August, they guide their young back to the vast ocean, where they create waves that sound like thunder and spray water that resembles rain, causing other sea creatures to tremble in fear and dare not approach. The female whale is called a "nai," and the larger ones can also grow up to a thousand li in length.

    The Avian Classic states: "When the goose flies, the shadow of the crocodile sinks; when the cuckoo sings, the snake coils up." It also says: "When the crane caws with its head down, it foretells rain; when it caws with its head up, it foretells clear skies."

    When termites hear the call of the bamboo partridge, they turn into water. The bamboo partridge calls out "mud-slip-slip." It is said that the rooster of the white chicken can also transform termites.

    The crane has a preference for eating snakes. Whenever it encounters a large stone and senses the presence of a snake, it moves like a sorcerer, causing the stone to tremble and turn. People in the south learned this method and would tie a nest with bamboo ropes on a tree, waiting for the crane to hatch its young. When the crane tried to free its nest, they would place sand beneath the tree and trace the crane's footprints, imitating its movements.

    When the evening jay caws, it foretells light rain; when the morning kite cries, it foretells strong winds.

    There are objects with unusual forms that control each other: the jadeite can cut gold, and the powder of the rhinoceros can dissolve human breath. In the north, people use needles to chip ice, while in the south, people use threads to cut tea.

    Gao You, in his commentary on the Huainanzi, said: "The fangzhu is a large clam. When polished and rubbed, it becomes smooth, and when placed under the moon, it can collect dew." Xu Shen, in his Shuowen Jiezi, said: "The fangzhu is a pearl; it is also a flat stone." [12]

    有一国王小夫人生一肉团大夫人妬之作木函弃之常河水后河边人得之肉破生千小儿勇健欲伐父王国小夫人以乳五百道射小儿口遂弛弓仗号为贤刼千佛
    　　帝喾有四妃一生帝挚一生帝尧一生商之先一生周之先商后封于宋都商丘
    　　今上于前朝作鎭睢阳洎开国号大宋又建都在大火之下宋为火正按天文心星为帝王实宋分野天地人之冥契自古罕有
    　　孔子生于鲁襄公二十二年
    　　老君其母曽见日精下落如流星飞入口中有娠七十二嵗而生于陈国涡水李树下剖左腋而生长一十二尺
    　　佛者本号释迦文即天竺释迦卫国王之子于四月八日夜从母右脇而生有三十二相当周庄王九年鲁庄公七年夏四月常星不见夜明是也
    　　鼋脂得火可以燃铁
    　　佛以三月十五日夜出家二十九出家三十五得道宁国论云蜀中本无獠犍为徳阳山谷洞中攘攘而出转转渐大自为夫妇而益多夫土干则生蚤地湿则生蚊积谷则生蠧腐肉则生蛆蛆化为蝇蝇又自生蛆蛆又生蝇岂有穷乎 [13]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [13]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 有一国王小夫人生一肉团 - Metaphorical description of an unusual birth, symbolizing a significant event or figure.
    • 妬之作木函弃之常河水 - Describes the action of the jealous wife, throwing a wooden box into the river.
    • 河边人得之肉破生千小儿勇健欲伐父王国 - Fantastical narrative element describing the birth of a thousand brave and strong children who wanted to attack their father's kingdom.
    • 小夫人以乳五百道射小儿口遂弛弓仗号为贤刼千佛 - Symbolic act of blessing and transformation, where the minor wife shoots the children's mouths with 500 streams of milk.
    • 帝喾有四妃一生帝挚一生帝尧一生商之先一生周之先 - Historical reference to the founding of dynasties, listing the consorts of Emperor Ku and their descendants.
    • 商后封于宋都商丘 - Historical fact about the state of Song being enfeoffed to the descendants of Shang.
    • 今上于前朝作鎭睢阳洎开国号大宋又建都在大火之下宋为火正 - Combination of historical and astrological references, describing the founding of the state of Da Song and its capital.
    • 孔子生于鲁襄公二十二年 - Specific historical date of Confucius's birth.
    • 老君其母曽见日精下落如流星飞入口中有娠七十二嵗而生于陈国涡水李树下剖左腋而生长一十二尺 - Mythological account of Laozi's birth, describing his mother's encounter with the essence of the sun and the miraculous birth.
    • 佛者本号释迦文即天竺释迦卫国王之子于四月八日夜从母右脇而生有三十二相当周庄王九年鲁庄公七年夏四月常星不见夜明是也 - Detailed historical and astrological description of the Buddha's birth, including the date and the 32 marks of perfection.
    • 鼋脂得火可以燃铁 - Scientific statement about the properties of turtle fat, stating that it can burn iron when ignited.
    • 佛以三月十五日夜出家二十九出家三十五得道宁国论云蜀中本无獠犍为徳阳山谷洞中攘攘而出转转渐大自为夫妇而益多夫土干则生蚤地湿则生蚊积谷则生蠧腐肉则生蛆蛆化为蝇蝇又自生蛆蛆又生蝇岂有穷乎 - Mix of religious, historical, and biological content, describing the Buddha's life and a metaphorical account of the emergence of life from various conditions.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 帝喾 - Emperor Ku, a legendary ruler of ancient China.
    • 帝挚 - Di Zhi, son of Emperor Ku, considered an ancestor of the Xia Dynasty.
    • 帝尧 - Di Yao, son of Emperor Ku, considered an ancestor of the Xia Dynasty.
    • 商之先 - Ancestor of the Shang Dynasty.
    • 周之先 - Ancestor of the Zhou Dynasty.
    • 宋 - The state of Song, enfeoffed to the descendants of Shang.
    • 孔子 - Confucius, a famous philosopher and teacher.
    • 老君 - Laozi, the founder of Daoism.
    • 佛 - The Buddha, the founder of Buddhism.
    • 周庄王 - King Zhuang of Zhou, a historical figure.
    • 鲁庄公 - Duke Zhuang of Lu, a historical figure.
    • 宁国论 - A historical or philosophical text.
    • 蜀中 - The region of Shu, in modern-day Sichuan province.
    • 犍为徳阳 - A place in Sichuan province.
    • 陈国 - Chen state, a historical region.
    • 涡水 - Wei River, a historical location.
    • 天竺 - India, the birthplace of Buddhism.
    • 释迦卫 - Shakya, the kingdom of the Buddha's birth.
    • 蚤 - Flea, a type of insect.
    • 蚊 - Mosquito, a type of insect.
    • 蠧 - Worm, a type of insect.
    • 蛆 - Maggot, a type of insect.
    • 蝇 - Fly, a type of insect.

    Proper Nouns

    • 帝喾 - Emperor Ku
    • 帝挚 - Di Zhi
    • 帝尧 - Di Yao
    • 商 - Shang
    • 周 - Zhou
    • 宋 - Song
    • 孔子 - Confucius
    • 老君 - Laozi
    • 佛 - Buddha
    • 周庄王 - King Zhuang of Zhou
    • 鲁庄公 - Duke Zhuang of Lu
    • 宁国论 - Ningguo theory
    • 蜀中 - Shu region
    • 犍为徳阳 - Deyang Valley
    • 陈国 - Chen state
    • 涡水 - Wei River
    • 天竺 - India
    • 释迦卫 - Shakya
    • 鼋 - Turtle
    • 蚤 - Flea
    • 蚊 - Mosquito
    • 蠧 - Worm
    • 蛆 - Maggot
    • 蝇 - Fly

    A king's minor wife gave birth to a lump of flesh. The jealous senior wife made a wooden box and threw it into the common river. Later, people by the river found it, the flesh broke, and a thousand brave and strong children were born who wanted to attack their father's kingdom. The minor wife shot the children's mouths with 500 streams of milk, then relaxed her bow and staff, calling them virtuous and seizing a thousand Buddhas.

    Emperor Ku had four consorts. One gave birth to Di Zhi, one to Di Yao, one to the ancestor of Shang, and one to the ancestor of Zhou. The descendants of Shang were later enfeoffed in Song, with their capital at Shangqiu.

    In the previous dynasty, the current emperor established a garrison at Suizhou and later founded the state of Da Song, building its capital under the constellation of the Fire Star, as Song is the Fire Official. According to the celestial chart, the Heart Star represents the emperor, and the region of Song aligns with the heavens, a rare cosmic harmony.

    Confucius was born in the 22nd year of Duke Xiang of Lu.

    Laozi's mother once saw the essence of the sun fall like a shooting star, entering her mouth. She was pregnant for 72 years and gave birth to Laozi at a plum tree by the Wei River in Chen, cutting open her left armpit to give birth to a child twelve feet long.

    The Buddha, originally named Shakyamuni, was the son of the king of Shakyas in India. He was born on the eighth day of the fourth month at night, emerging from his mother's right side, with 32 marks of perfection, corresponding to the ninth year of King Zhuang of Zhou and the seventh year of Duke Zhuang of Lu, when the stars were not visible in the sky but the night was bright.

    The fat of a turtle can burn iron when ignited.

    The Buddha left home on the night of the 15th day of the third month, at the age of 29, and attained enlightenment at the age of 35. According to the Ningguo theory, there were originally no barbarians in Shu, but they emerged from the caves in Deyang Valley, growing larger and larger, forming couples and increasing in number. When the soil is dry, fleas are born; when the ground is wet, mosquitoes are born; when grain is stored, worms are born; when meat rots, maggots are born; maggots turn into flies, and flies give birth to more maggots, and maggots give birth to more flies, endlessly. [13]

    日月蚀而私者生儿则多疾日月晦朔望而私者生儿则愚痴瘖痖钩绞丫戾逐阵而私者生儿多凶暴无礼亦犹木日造麴而酸水日造酱则生虫九焦日种谷则不生芽六合日遣不去火日安蜂则苦土日种麻则不生
    　　麈尾能留红扫毡毡不蠧
    　　駞粪烟杀蚊壁虱
    　　李卫公言鵞惊防防猒火孔雀辟恶
    　　水母生儿无目鼈生儿无耳
    　　桐庐人尝伐竹遗竹见一宿竿雉头蛇身犹未变乃知竹为蛇蛇为雉头先化曽于青城山小路上见之
    　　所以夀者无死气于中也
    　　河水东北有石室室中若有积卷因谓之积书岩每见神人往还世人不悟其为仙者又谓之神彼土目曰唐述因名其山曰唐述山堂曰唐述窟道术之士多往栖焉
    　　巫咸山在安县东南巫咸右手操青蛇左手操赤蛇羣巫所从上下大荒西经曰巫咸即巫朌巫彭巫姑巫眞巫孔巫抵巫谢巫罗 [14]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [14]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 日月蚀而私者生儿则多疾 - Superstitious belief that conceiving during a solar or lunar eclipse results in a child prone to illness.
    • 日月晦朔望而私者生儿则愚痴瘖痖 - Superstitious belief that conceiving during the new moon, full moon, or quarter moon results in a child with intellectual disabilities or speech impediments.
    • 钩绞丫戾逐阵而私者生儿多凶暴无礼 - Belief that conceiving during certain celestial alignments or patterns leads to a child with aggressive and unruly behavior.
    • 亦犹木日造麴而酸水日造酱则生虫九焦日种谷则不生芽六合日遣不去火日安蜂则苦土日种麻则不生 - Analogies comparing various activities performed on specific days to undesirable outcomes, such as spoiled food, pests, and failed crops.
    • 麈尾能留红扫毡毡不蠧 - Belief that using a dust broom (麈尾) can prevent redness on the carpet and keep it from rotting.
    • 駝粪烟杀蚊壁虱 - Belief that camel dung smoke can repel mosquitoes and lice.
    • 李卫公言鵞惊防防猒火孔雀辟恶 - Statement attributed to Li Weigong that geese can scare away ghosts, and peacocks can ward off evil.
    • 水母生儿无目鼈生儿无耳 - Belief that jellyfish give birth to blind offspring, and turtles and softshell turtles give birth to deaf offspring.
    • 桐庐人尝伐竹遗竹见一宿竿雉头蛇身犹未变乃知竹为蛇蛇为雉头先化曽于青城山小路上见之 - Story about a person from Tonglu who, while cutting bamboo, saw a snake that had transformed into a pheasant's head and body, indicating that bamboo can turn into snakes and vice versa.
    • 所以夀者无死气于中也 - Belief that longevity is due to the absence of death energy within.
    • 河水东北有石室室中若有积卷因谓之积书岩每见神人往还世人不悟其为仙者又谓之神彼土目曰唐述因名其山曰唐述山堂曰唐述窟道术之士多往栖焉 - Description of a stone chamber in the northeast of the Yellow River, where scrolls are stored, and where immortals are said to visit. The locals call these spirits 'Tangshu,' and the mountain and cave are named accordingly.
    • 巫咸山在安县东南巫咸右手操青蛇左手操赤蛇羣巫所从上下大荒西经曰巫咸即巫朌巫彭巫姑巫眞巫孔巫抵巫谢巫罗 - Description of Wuxian Mountain in the southeast of An County, where the spirit Wuxian holds a green snake in his right hand and a red snake in his left, and where other spirits follow him. The *Classic of Mountains and Seas* mentions Wuxian as one of several spirits.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 日月蚀 - Refers to solar and lunar eclipses, which were often seen as omens in ancient Chinese culture.
    • 晦朔望 - Refers to the new moon, full moon, and quarter moon, which were significant in ancient Chinese astrology and superstitions.
    • 钩绞丫戾逐阵 - Refers to celestial patterns and alignments, which were believed to influence human affairs.
    • 麈尾 - A traditional Chinese dust broom made from the tail of a deer, used for sweeping and believed to have protective properties.
    • 駝粪烟 - Camel dung smoke, which was used for various purposes, including repelling insects.
    • 李卫公 - Refers to Li Weigong, a historical figure known for his military and political achievements.
    • 鵞惊防防猒火孔雀辟恶 - References to geese, fire, and peacocks, which were believed to have protective or repelling properties.
    • 水母生儿无目鼈生儿无耳 - References to jellyfish, turtles, and softshell turtles, which were believed to have unusual reproductive characteristics.
    • 桐庐人 - Refers to people from Tonglu, a place in Zhejiang Province.
    • 青城山 - A famous mountain in Sichuan Province, known for its Taoist temples and natural beauty.
    • 河水 - Refers to the Yellow River, one of the most important rivers in Chinese history.
    • 积书岩 - A stone chamber in the northeast of the Yellow River, where scrolls are stored.
    • 神人 - Refers to immortals or divine beings, who were believed to visit certain places.
    • 唐述 - A term used by the locals to describe spirits or ghosts.
    • 巫咸山 - A mountain in the southeast of An County, where the spirit Wuxian is said to reside.
    • 巫咸 - A spirit or deity mentioned in the *Classic of Mountains and Seas*.
    • 巫朌巫彭巫姑巫眞巫孔巫抵巫谢巫罗 - Other spirits or deities mentioned in the *Classic of Mountains and Seas*.

    Proper Nouns

    • 李卫公 - Li Weigong, a historical figure.
    • 青城山 - Mount Qingcheng, a famous mountain in Sichuan Province.
    • 河水 - The Yellow River.
    • 积书岩 - A stone chamber in the northeast of the Yellow River.
    • 唐述 - A term used by the locals to describe spirits or ghosts.
    • 巫咸山 - Wuxian Mountain, a mountain in the southeast of An County.
    • 巫咸 - A spirit or deity mentioned in the *Classic of Mountains and Seas*.
    • 巫朌巫彭巫姑巫眞巫孔巫抵巫谢巫罗 - Other spirits or deities mentioned in the *Classic of Mountains and Seas*.

    A solar or lunar eclipse during conception can result in a child prone to illness. Conception during the new moon, full moon, or quarter moon can lead to a child with intellectual disabilities or speech impediments. Conceiving during certain celestial alignments can result in a child with aggressive and unruly behavior. Just as making wine on a certain day can spoil it, making soy sauce on another day can attract pests, and planting seeds on yet another day can prevent them from sprouting. Using a dust broom (麈尾) can prevent redness on the carpet and keep it from rotting. Camel dung smoke can repel mosquitoes and lice. Li Weigong said that geese can scare away ghosts, and peacocks can ward off evil. Jellyfish give birth to blind offspring, and turtles and softshell turtles give birth to deaf offspring. A person from Tonglu, while cutting bamboo, saw a snake that had transformed into a pheasant's head and body, indicating that bamboo can turn into snakes and vice versa. Longevity is due to the absence of death energy within. In the northeast of the Yellow River, there is a stone chamber where scrolls are stored, and where immortals are said to visit. The locals call these spirits "Tangshu," and the mountain and cave are named accordingly. Wuxian Mountain, in the southeast of An County, is where the spirit Wuxian holds a green snake in his right hand and a red snake in his left, and where other spirits follow him. The Classic of Mountains and Seas mentions Wuxian as one of several spirits. [14]

    先提山有钩蛇长七八丈尾末有岐蛇在山涧水中以尾钩岸上人牛食之水傍瘴气特恶气中有物不见其形其作有声中木则折中人则害名曰弹郡有罪人徙之禁防不过十日死也
    　　眞菊可以延龄野菊可以防人张华云黄精益夀钩吻杀人是矣
    　　子程子曰昔洛阳北部有母既生子病不能自举乳求他妇负哺之子婴及孩母疾不间子长不识所育负哺者盗其爱二母忿鬬于庭子右负哺者而反疎其生母
    　　有人为山魈所祟或教以爆竹如除夕可弭人用其言获安问之则曰此荆楚嵗时记以辟山魈隂冷之气胜则声阳以攻之
    　　莆者其状如蓬枝多叶少根如丝叶如扇不摇自动风生主庖厨清凉驱杀虫蝇以助供养尧时生于庖厨为帝王去恶孙柔王充白虎皆云太平时生于庖厨中熯一炬火爨一镬水终日不能倚一尺冰置之厨终夜不能寒
    　　师旷奏清角有集廊门延颈而鸣饰翼而舞今世有驯养之闻歌曲拊掌而舞者习之也 [15]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [15]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 先提山 - Likely refers to a specific mountain, possibly 'Xianti Mountain' (仙提山), but the exact meaning is uncertain.
    • 钩蛇 - A mythical or legendary creature, possibly a snake with a hook-like tail.
    • 尾末有岐 - The tail has a forked end, which may be a physical description or a metaphor.
    • 以尾钩岸上人牛食之 - The snake uses its tail to hook and eat people and cattle on the shore.
    • 水傍瘴气 - Miasma or瘴气 (zhàng qì) near the water, referring to poisonous or harmful air.
    • 特恶 - Especially evil or harmful.
    • 弹 - Likely a mythical creature or a term for something unseen but harmful.
    • 郡有罪人徙之 - People with crimes are exiled to this place.
    • 禁防不过十日死也 - Even with strict precautions, one cannot survive more than ten days.
    • 眞菊 - True chrysanthemum, believed to have medicinal properties.
    • 延龄 - Extend one's lifespan.
    • 野菊 - Wild chrysanthemum, which may be harmful.
    • 张华云 - A reference to Zhang Hua, a historical figure.
    • 黄精益夀 - Yellow essence (likely referring to a substance) prolongs life.
    • 钩吻杀人 - A poisonous plant that kills.
    • 子程子 - Likely a reference to Cheng Hao or Cheng Yi, Confucian scholars.
    • 负哺之 - Carrying and feeding the child.
    • 盗其爱 - The nurse steals the child's affection.
    • 二母忿鬬于庭 - The two mothers fight in the courtyard.
    • 子右负哺者而反疎其生母 - The child favors the nurse and distances himself from his biological mother.
    • 山魈 - A mythical creature or mountain demon.
    • 爆竹 - Firecrackers, used to ward off evil spirits.
    • 荆楚岁时记 - A historical text about the customs of the Jingchu region.
    • 辟山魈 - Ward off the mountain demon.
    • 阴冷之气胜则声阳以攻之 - When the cold and dark energy is strong, sound (firecrackers) is used to counteract it.
    • 莆 - Likely a plant or herb, possibly 'luo pu' (罗布), but the exact meaning is uncertain.
    • 主庖厨清凉 - Used in the kitchen to provide coolness.
    • 驱杀虫蝇 - Repels insects and flies.
    • 尧时 - The time of Emperor Yao, a legendary ruler.
    • 为帝王去恶 - Helps the emperor eliminate evil.
    • 孙柔王充白虎 - References to historical or philosophical texts.
    • 太平时 - A time of peace and prosperity.
    • 熯一炬火爨一镬水终日不能 - Burning a torch and cooking water in a pot all day cannot heat it.
    • 倚一尺冰置之厨终夜不能寒 - Placing a foot of ice in the kitchen all night cannot cool it.
    • 师旷 - A historical musician.
    • 清角 - A type of music.
    •  - Likely a bird or mythical creature.
    • 延颈而鸣饰翼而舞 - Stretches its neck to sing and adorns its wings to dance.
    • 今世有驯养之 - In modern times, there are tamed creatures.
    • 闻歌曲拊掌而舞者习之也 - They hear songs, clap their hands, and dance, having been trained to do so.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 先提山 - Likely a mythical or legendary mountain, possibly 'Xianti Mountain' (仙提山).
    • 钩蛇 - A mythical creature, possibly a snake with a hook-like tail.
    • 弹 - A mythical or legendary creature, possibly a ghost or demon.
    • 子程子 - Likely a reference to Cheng Hao or Cheng Yi, Confucian scholars.
    • 山魈 - A mythical creature or mountain demon.
    • 荆楚岁时记 - A historical text about the customs of the Jingchu region.
    • 师旷 - A historical musician.
    • 清角 - A type of music.
    • 莆 - Likely a plant or herb, possibly 'luo pu' (罗布).

    Proper Nouns

    • 先提山 - Likely a mythical or legendary mountain.
    • 钩蛇 - A mythical creature.
    • 弹 - A mythical or legendary creature.

    Xianti Mountain is home to a hook snake, which is several dozen feet long, with a forked tail. This snake resides in the mountain stream, using its tail to hook and consume people and cattle on the shore. Near the water, there is a particularly noxious miasma, within which lurks an unseen creature. This creature makes a sound, and when it strikes, it can break trees or harm humans. It is known as the "Ghost Dart." Those with crimes are exiled to this place, but even with strict precautions, they cannot survive more than ten days.

    True chrysanthemum can prolong life, while wild chrysanthemum can be harmful. Zhang Hua once said that the "Yellow Essence" prolongs life, while the "Hook-Wort" is deadly.

    Master Cheng said: "In the northern part of Luoyang, there was a woman who, after giving birth, was too ill to nurse her child. She sought the help of another woman to carry and feed the child. As the child grew into an infant, the mother's illness persisted, and the child did not recognize her. The nurse, who had taken on the role of mother, stole the child's affection. The two women fought in the courtyard, and the child favored the nurse, distancing himself from his biological mother."

    There was a man who was tormented by a mountain demon. Someone advised him to use firecrackers, like those used on New Year's Eve, to ward it off. He followed the advice and found safety. When asked about it, he said it was recorded in the Jingchu Annals of Seasons as a method to repel the mountain demon. When the cold and dark energy is strong, sound (firecrackers) is used to counteract it.

    The "Luo Pu" plant resembles a thorny bush, with many leaves and few roots, like silk. Its leaves are fan-like and move without being touched, creating a breeze. It is used in the kitchen to provide coolness, repel insects and flies, and assist in the preparation of offerings. In the time of Emperor Yao, it was said to have grown in the kitchen to help the emperor eliminate evil. Both Sun Ruo and Wang Chong, in their works, mentioned that during times of peace, the Luo Pu plant grew in the kitchen. If one burns a torch and cooks water in a pot all day, it cannot be heated, and if one places a foot of ice in the kitchen all night, it cannot be cooled.

    Master Shi Kuang played the "Qingjiao" music, and a creature gathered at the door of the hall, stretching its neck to sing and adorning its wings to dance. In modern times, there are tamed creatures that hear songs, clap their hands, and dance, having been trained to do so. [15]

    正月上辰出水边盥濯食蓬饵以祓邪汉仪也
    　　雏乌形鸦乌纯黒而觜红亦曰阿雏乌乌鸦反哺为慈乌腹下白不反哺为鸦乌白头羣飞为燕乌大而白头为仓乌卑居为楚乌又鸦乌为贾乌庄子曰鸦贾也
    　　相家说人臣得龙之一体当至公相曽公亮得龙之脊王安石得龙之睛
    　　鱼劳则尾赤人劳则髪白
    　　鳣黄鱼口在颔下无鳞长鼻软骨俗谓玉板长二三丈江东呼为黄鱼
    　　网鱼得鱮不如啖茹
    　　南越志曰乌鲗懐墨而知礼江东人或取其墨书契以绐人物书迹如淡墨逾年墨消空纸耳
    　　抱朴子蟾蜍夀至千嵗者头上有角颔下有丹书八字裴頠崇有论曰鸟无胃肺蛤蜃无脏蛭以空中而生以无胃而育
    　　聒抱者鸺鹠鹳鹊也影抱者鼈鼋也
    　　视防制柂观鱼制帆
    　　神和子姓屈突名无为字无不为张咏布衣时遇之海蟾子姓刘名昭逺华山陈抟馆之道院与种放往来广徳先生杜光庭处州人仕唐为内供奉避乱入蜀王氏爵为师臣 [16]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [16]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 正月上辰 - The first day of the first lunar month, traditionally a time for rituals and celebrations.
    • 出水边盥濯食蓬饵以祓邪 - A ritual involving washing in water, eating *pom* (a type of cake), and performing purification rites to ward off evil.
    • 汉仪也 - Indicates that this is a Han Dynasty ritual or custom.
    • 雏乌形似鸦乌 - Describes the appearance of young crows, comparing them to adult crows.
    • 纯黑而觜红 - Describes the coloration of the young crows, with a black body and a red beak.
    • 亦曰阿雏乌 - Another name for the young crows.
    • 乌鸦反哺为慈乌 - Describes the behavior of crows feeding their parents, symbolizing filial piety.
    • 腹下白不反哺为鸦乌 - Describes a variant of the crow that does not feed its parents and has a white belly.
    • 白头羣飞为燕乌 - Describes crows with white heads that fly in groups.
    • 大而白头为仓乌 - Describes large crows with white heads.
    • 卑居为楚乌 - Describes crows that live in low-lying areas.
    • 又鸦乌为贾乌 - Another name for crows, possibly related to commerce or trade.
    • 庄子曰鸦贾也 - A reference to the philosopher Zhuangzi, who described crows as 'traders.'
    • 相家说人臣得龙之一体当至公相 - A statement from the *Xiang* (divination) tradition, suggesting that a minister who possesses a part of the dragon will achieve high office.
    • 曽公亮得龙之脊王安石得龙之睛 - Specific examples of ministers who achieved high office, with 曾公亮 (Zeng Gongliang) having the dragon's spine and 王安石 (Wang Anshi) having the dragon's eye.
    • 鱼劳则尾赤人劳则髪白 - A proverb comparing the signs of hard work in fish (reddened tails) and humans (gray hair).
    • 鳣黄鱼口在颔下无鳞长鼻软骨俗谓玉板长二三丈江东呼为黄鱼 - Describes a type of fish, the *zhān* (a kind of yellow croaker), with specific physical characteristics and regional names.
    • 网鱼得鱮不如啖茹 - A proverb suggesting that catching fish is less rewarding than eating vegetables.
    • 南越志曰乌鲗懐墨而知礼江东人或取其墨书契以绐人物书迹如淡墨逾年墨消空纸耳 - A reference to the *Nanyue* (Southern Yue) records, describing the *wèi* (cuttlefish) and its use in writing and deception.
    • 抱朴子蟾蜍夀至千嵗者头上有角颔下有丹书八字 - A reference to the *Baopu Zi* (Master Who Embraces Simplicity), describing the longevity and characteristics of toads.
    • 裴頠崇有论曰鸟无胃肺蛤蜃无脏蛭以空中而生以无胃而育 - A reference to the *Chong You Lun* (Treatise on the Importance of Existence) by Pei Wei, discussing the unique biological features of birds and shellfish.
    • 聒抱者鸺鹠鹳鹊也影抱者鼈鼋也 - Describes animals that make noise when held (owls, storks, and crows) and those that cast shadows (turtles, tortoises, and softshell turtles).
    • 视防制柂观鱼制帆 - A metaphor comparing vigilance to steering a ship and observing fish to adjusting a sail.
    • 神和子姓屈突名无为字无不为张咏布衣时遇之海蟾子姓刘名昭逺华山陈抟馆之道院与种放往来广徳先生杜光庭处州人仕唐为内供奉避乱入蜀王氏爵为师臣 - Describes several historical figures, including their names, titles, and significant events in their lives.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 正月上辰 - The first day of the first lunar month, a significant date in the Chinese calendar.
    • 汉仪也 - Indicates that the described practices are part of Han Dynasty customs.
    • 雏乌 - Young crows, often associated with filial piety in Chinese culture.
    • 乌鸦反哺 - The act of young crows feeding their parents, symbolizing filial piety.
    • 曾公亮 - A high-ranking official during the Song Dynasty.
    • 王安石 - A prominent statesman and reformer during the Song Dynasty.
    • 鱼劳则尾赤人劳则髪白 - A proverb reflecting the idea that hard work leaves physical marks.
    • 鳣黄鱼 - A type of fish with specific physical characteristics and regional names.
    • 南越志 - Records from the Southern Yue region, providing cultural and biological information.
    • 抱朴子 - A text by Ge Hong, a famous alchemist and writer.
    • 裴頠崇有论 - A philosophical work by Pei Wei, discussing the nature of existence.
    • 神和子 - A historical figure with a specific name and title.
    • 海蟾子 - Another historical figure with a specific name and title.
    • 广徳先生 - A historical figure with a specific name and title.
    • 杜光庭 - A Taoist scholar and official during the Tang Dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 正月上辰 - The first day of the first lunar month.
    • 汉仪 - Han Dynasty customs.
    • 雏乌 - Young crows.
    • 乌鸦 - Crows.
    • 慈乌 - Filial crows.
    • 鸦乌 - Crows.
    • 燕乌 - Crows with white heads.
    • 仓乌 - Large crows with white heads.
    • 楚乌 - Crows that live in low-lying areas.
    • 贾乌 - Another name for crows, possibly related to commerce or trade.
    • 曽公亮 - A high-ranking official during the Song Dynasty.
    • 王安石 - A prominent statesman and reformer during the Song Dynasty.
    • 鳣黄鱼 - A type of fish with specific physical characteristics and regional names.
    • 南越志 - Records from the Southern Yue region, providing cultural and biological information.
    • 抱朴子 - A text by Ge Hong, a famous alchemist and writer.
    • 裴頠崇有论 - A philosophical work by Pei Wei, discussing the nature of existence.
    • 神和子 - A historical figure with a specific name and title.
    • 海蟾子 - Another historical figure with a specific name and title.
    • 广徳先生 - A historical figure with a specific name and title.
    • 杜光庭 - A Taoist scholar and official during the Tang Dynasty.

    On the first day of the first lunar month, people would go to the water's edge to wash themselves, eat pom cakes, and perform purification rites to ward off evil, following Han Dynasty customs.

    Young crows resemble adult crows, with a black body and a red beak. They are also called a chu crows. Crows that feed their parents are known as ci crows, while those that do not have a white belly. Crows with white heads that fly in groups are called yan crows. Large crows with white heads are called cang crows, and those that live in low-lying areas are called chu crows. Another name for crows is jia crows, as Zhuangzi once said, "Crows are traders."

    According to the Xiang (divination) tradition, a minister who possesses a part of the dragon will achieve high office. Zeng Gongliang had the dragon's spine, and Wang Anshi had the dragon's eye.

    When fish are overworked, their tails turn red; when humans are overworked, their hair turns white.

    The zhan yellow fish has its mouth under the chin, no scales, a long nose, and soft bones. It is commonly known as yu ban and can grow to two or three zhang long. In the Jiangdong region, it is called huang yu.

    Catching fish is less rewarding than eating vegetables.

    According to the Nanyue records, the wei (cuttlefish) carries ink and knows propriety. People in the Jiangdong region sometimes use its ink to write contracts and deceive others. The writing fades like light ink over time, leaving only a faint trace on the paper.

    In the Baopu Zi, it is said that toads can live for a thousand years, with horns on their heads and eight characters written in red on their chins. Pei Wei, in his Chong You Lun, noted that birds lack stomachs and lungs, and shellfish lack organs, as they are born from the void and nourished without a stomach.

    The animals that make noise when held are owls, storks, and crows. Those that cast shadows are turtles, tortoises, and softshell turtles.

    Vigilance is like steering a ship, and observing fish is like adjusting a sail.

    Shen Hezi, whose surname is Qutu and given name is Wuwei, with the courtesy name of Wubei, met Zhang Yong, a scholar, during his time as a commoner. Hai Chanzhi, whose surname is Liu and given name is Zhao Yuan, was a guest at the Daoist temple on Mount Hua, where he associated with Zhong Fang. Guangde Xiansheng, Du Guangting, a native of Chuzhou, served as an imperial attendant during the Tang Dynasty and fled to Shu during the chaos, where he was enfeoffed as a teacher by the Wang family. [16]

    尔朱先生伪蜀广政中饮酒食猪臓渝州刺史谓其幻惑以竹笼沈之江至防为渔人所得上升严补阙得青金丹方朱桃椎薛稷图其形传于世
    　　吕洞賔谓矦生曰蛇蝎天地淘以自然之气使之生以遂性杀此救彼有损无益取纸语三口共一室室畔水偏清乃吕洞宾字
    　　陈希夷先生一睡或半嵗或三数月近亦不下月余留蔵真息饮纳玉液吾睡真睡也曽与毛女游
    　　鲤鱼大小并三十六鳞
    　　猬能跳入虎耳中见鹊便自仰腹受啄
    　　水蛭亦如鱼子经三年得水犹活
    　　蛇蜕塞两耳治疟疾覆盆子是苺子笮取汁合成膏涂发不白麞鹿非八卦辰属不荤羶能温补于人生死无尤道家听用为脯
    　　天宝中河南缑氏县太子陵仙观每年九月三日夜有道士一人得仙已有旧例至日具姓名申府张竭忠为令不之信阴令二勇士执兵觇之至三更有一黒虎入观来衔一道士射之不中弃道士而去令于是申府请弓矢大猎石穴中格杀数虎或金简玉録冠帔髪骨甚多其观遂废为陵使之居 [17]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [17]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 伪蜀广政中 - Refers to the period during the reign of Emperor Guangzheng of the Later Shu state, a brief kingdom in Chinese history.
    • 饮酒食猪臓 - Literally 'drinking wine and eating pig intestines,' likely an idiom or euphemism for a specific ritual or practice.
    • 渝州刺史 - Refers to the governor of Yuzhou, an administrative region in ancient China.
    • 幻惑 - Means 'illusion' or 'deception,' often used to describe supernatural or mystical phenomena.
    • 竹笼沈之江 - Literally 'put in a bamboo cage and submerged in the river,' a method of execution or disposal.
    • 防为渔人所得 - Means 'was later found by a fisherman,' indicating a miraculous or unexpected outcome.
    • 上升严补阙 - Refers to ascending to a higher spiritual or official position, specifically the role of '补阙' (a type of official in the imperial court).
    • 青金丹方 - Refers to a formula for an elixir of immortality, often associated with alchemy.
    • 朱桃椎薛稷图其形 - Refers to the artists Zhu Taozhui and Xue Ji, who painted the figure of the subject.
    • 传于世 - Means 'passed down to future generations,' indicating the enduring legacy of the figure.
    • 吕洞賔谓矦生曰 - Refers to Lu Dongbin, a legendary Taoist immortal, speaking to someone named Hou Sheng.
    • 蛇蝎天地淘以自然之气 - Literally 'snakes and scorpions are refined by the natural qi of heaven and earth,' a metaphor for the refinement of natural elements.
    • 使生以遂性杀此救彼 - Means 'to make them live to fulfill their nature, killing one to save another,' a paradoxical statement about the balance of life and death.
    • 有损无益 - Means 'there is harm but no benefit,' a conclusion about the above statement.
    • 取纸语三口共一室 - Literally 'take a piece of paper, write three mouths in one room,' a riddle or metaphor.
    • 室畔水偏清 - Means 'the water beside the room is unusually clear,' a clue in the riddle.
    • 吕洞宾字 - Refers to Lu Dongbin's name or nickname, often associated with his legendary status.
    • 陈希夷先生 - Refers to Chen Xiyi, a legendary Taoist figure known for his longevity and spiritual practices.
    • 一睡或半嵗或三数月 - Means 'sleeping for half a year or several months,' a description of his unique sleeping habits.
    • 近亦不下月余 - Means 'recently not less than a month or more,' indicating a change in his sleeping pattern.
    • 留蔵真息 - Means 'preserving true breath,' a reference to Taoist practices of breath control.
    • 饮纳玉液 - Means 'drinking the jade liquid,' a metaphor for consuming elixirs of immortality.
    • 吾睡真睡也 - Means 'my sleep is true sleep,' a statement about the depth of his sleep.
    • 曾与毛女游 - Means 'I have traveled with the hair girl,' referring to a legendary Taoist companion.
    • 鲤鱼大小并三十六鳞 - Literally 'carp, large or small, all have thirty-six scales,' a description of carp.
    • 猬能跳入虎耳中 - Means 'the hedgehog can jump into the tiger's ear,' a metaphor for the hedgehog's agility.
    • 见鹊便自仰腹受啄 - Means 'when it sees a magpie, it lies on its belly and lets it peck,' a description of the hedgehog's behavior.
    • 水蛭亦如鱼子 - Means 'water leeches are also like fish eggs,' a comparison between the two.
    • 经三年得水犹活 - Means 'after three years, if they get water, they still live,' indicating the leeches' resilience.
    • 蛇蜕塞两耳 - Means 'snake skin is used to plug both ears,' a traditional remedy.
    • 治疟疾 - Means 'to treat malaria,' indicating the purpose of the remedy.
    • 覆盆子是苺子笮取汁 - Means 'goji berries are made from strawberry juice,' a description of a food preparation method.
    • 合成膏涂发不白 - Means 'mixed into a paste to prevent white hair,' indicating a cosmetic use.
    • 麞鹿非八卦辰属不荤羶 - Means 'musk deer and deer are not impure or stinky unless they belong to the eight trigrams and the twelve animals,' a statement about their purity.
    • 能温补于人生死无尤 - Means 'they can warm and supplement the body, with no blame in life or death,' indicating their medicinal value.
    • 道家听用为脯 - Means 'Taoists use them as dried meat,' indicating their use in Taoist practices.
    • 天宝中 - Refers to the Tianbao era, a period in the Tang dynasty.
    • 河南缑氏县太子陵仙观 - Refers to the Xianyao Temple in Taizi陵, a location in Henan province.
    • 每年九月三日夜 - Means 'every September 3rd night,' indicating a specific date.
    • 有道士一人得仙 - Means 'there is a Taoist who becomes an immortal,' indicating a miraculous event.
    • 已有旧例 - Means 'there is an old precedent,' indicating that this event is not new.
    • 至日具姓名申府 - Means 'on the day, report the name to the prefecture,' indicating a formal procedure.
    • 张竭忠为令不之信 - Means 'Zhang Jiezhong, the prefect, did not believe it,' indicating skepticism.
    • 阴令二勇士执兵觇之 - Means 'secretly ordered two brave men to carry weapons and spy on it,' indicating surveillance.
    • 至三更有一黒虎入观来衔一道士 - Means 'at three o'clock at night, a black tiger entered the temple and carried away a Taoist,' indicating a supernatural event.
    • 射之不中 - Means 'shot at it but missed,' indicating an unsuccessful attempt.
    • 弃道士而去 - Means 'left the Taoist and went away,' indicating the tiger's departure.
    • 令于是申府请弓矢 - Means 'the prefect then reported to the prefecture and requested bows and arrows,' indicating a response.
    • 大猎石穴中格杀数虎 - Means 'a large hunt in the stone cave killed several tigers,' indicating a successful hunt.
    • 或金简玉録冠帔髪骨甚多 - Means 'some golden tablets and jade records, crowns, cloaks, hair, and bones were found in great numbers,' indicating the discovery of artifacts.
    • 其观遂废为陵使之居 - Means 'the temple was then abandoned and became a tomb for him to reside,' indicating a transformation of the site.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 伪蜀广政中 - Refers to the Later Shu state, a kingdom established by Meng Chang in the late 10th century.
    • 渝州刺史 - Refers to the governor of Yuzhou, an administrative region in ancient China.
    • 幻惑 - Often used in Taoist and Buddhist texts to describe supernatural phenomena.
    • 竹笼沈之江 - A method of execution or disposal, indicating the severity of the situation.
    • 防为渔人所得 - Indicates a miraculous or unexpected outcome, often associated with immortals or deities.
    • 上升严补阙 - Refers to a high-ranking official in the imperial court, indicating the subject's elevated status.
    • 青金丹方 - Refers to an elixir of immortality, often associated with Taoist alchemy.
    • 朱桃椎薛稷图其形 - Refers to the artists Zhu Taozhui and Xue Ji, who painted the figure of the subject.
    • 传于世 - Indicates the enduring legacy of the figure, often associated with immortals or deities.
    • 吕洞賔谓矦生曰 - Refers to Lu Dongbin, a legendary Taoist immortal, speaking to someone named Hou Sheng.
    • 蛇蝎天地淘以自然之气 - A metaphor for the refinement of natural elements, often associated with Taoist cosmology.
    • 使生以遂性杀此救彼 - A paradoxical statement about the balance of life and death, often associated with Taoist philosophy.
    • 有损无益 - A conclusion about the above statement, often associated with Taoist ethics.
    • 取纸语三口共一室 - A riddle or metaphor, often associated with Taoist wisdom.
    • 室畔水偏清 - A clue in the riddle, often associated with Taoist symbolism.
    • 吕洞宾字 - Refers to Lu Dongbin's name or nickname, often associated with his legendary status.
    • 陈希夷先生 - Refers to Chen Xiyi, a legendary Taoist figure known for his longevity and spiritual practices.
    • 一睡或半嵗或三数月 - Means 'sleeping for half a year or several months,' a description of his unique sleeping habits.
    • 近亦不下月余 - Means 'recently not less than a month or more,' indicating a change in his sleeping pattern.
    • 留蔵真息 - Means 'preserving true breath,' a reference to Taoist practices of breath control.
    • 饮纳玉液 - Means 'drinking the jade liquid,' a metaphor for consuming elixirs of immortality.
    • 吾睡真睡也 - Means 'my sleep is true sleep,' a statement about the depth of his sleep.
    • 曾与毛女游 - Means 'I have traveled with the hair girl,' referring to a legendary Taoist companion.
    • 鲤鱼大小并三十六鳞 - Literally 'carp, large or small, all have thirty-six scales,' a description of carp.
    • 猬能跳入虎耳中 - Means 'the hedgehog can jump into the tiger's ear,' a metaphor for the hedgehog's agility.
    • 见鹊便自仰腹受啄 - Means 'when it sees a magpie, it lies on its belly and lets it peck,' a description of the hedgehog's behavior.
    • 水蛭亦如鱼子 - Means 'water leeches are also like fish eggs,' a comparison between the two.
    • 经三年得水犹活 - Means 'after three years, if they get water, they still live,' indicating the leeches' resilience.
    • 蛇蜕塞两耳 - Means 'snake skin is used to plug both ears,' a traditional remedy.
    • 治疟疾 - Means 'to treat malaria,' indicating the purpose of the remedy.
    • 覆盆子是苺子笮取汁 - Means 'goji berries are made from strawberry juice,' a description of a food preparation method.
    • 合成膏涂发不白 - Means 'mixed into a paste to prevent white hair,' indicating a cosmetic use.
    • 麞鹿非八卦辰属不荤羶 - Means 'musk deer and deer are not impure or stinky unless they belong to the eight trigrams and the twelve animals,' a statement about their purity.
    • 能温补于人生死无尤 - Means 'they can warm and supplement the body, with no blame in life or death,' indicating their medicinal value.
    • 道家听用为脯 - Means 'Taoists use them as dried meat,' indicating their use in Taoist practices.
    • 天宝中 - Refers to the Tianbao era, a period in the Tang dynasty.
    • 河南缑氏县太子陵仙观 - Refers to the Xianyao Temple in Taizi陵, a location in Henan province.
    • 每年九月三日夜 - Means 'every September 3rd night,' indicating a specific date.
    • 有道士一人得仙 - Means 'there is a Taoist who becomes an immortal,' indicating a miraculous event.
    • 已有旧例 - Means 'there is an old precedent,' indicating that this event is not new.
    • 至日具姓名申府 - Means 'on the day, report the name to the prefecture,' indicating a formal procedure.
    • 张竭忠为令不之信 - Means 'Zhang Jiezhong, the prefect, did not believe it,' indicating skepticism.
    • 阴令二勇士执兵觇之 - Means 'secretly ordered two brave men to carry weapons and spy on it,' indicating surveillance.
    • 至三更有一黒虎入观来衔一道士 - Means 'at three o'clock at night, a black tiger entered the temple and carried away a Taoist,' indicating a supernatural event.
    • 射之不中 - Means 'shot at it but missed,' indicating an unsuccessful attempt.
    • 弃道士而去 - Means 'left the Taoist and went away,' indicating the tiger's departure.
    • 令于是申府请弓矢 - Means 'the prefect then reported to the prefecture and requested bows and arrows,' indicating a response.
    • 大猎石穴中格杀数虎 - Means 'a large hunt in the stone cave killed several tigers,' indicating a successful hunt.
    • 或金简玉録冠帔髪骨甚多 - Means 'some golden tablets and jade records, crowns, cloaks, hair, and bones were found in great numbers,' indicating the discovery of artifacts.
    • 其观遂废为陵使之居 - Means 'the temple was then abandoned and became a tomb for him to reside,' indicating a transformation of the site.

    Er Zhu Mr. in the Guangzheng period of Pseudo-Shu drank wine and ate pig intestines. The governor of Yuzhou considered him to be under an illusion and had him put in a bamboo cage and submerged in the river. Later, he was found by a fisherman and ascended to the position of Yanzhu Qu. He obtained the formula for the Qingjin Dan and Zhu Taozhui and Xue Ji painted his figure, which was passed down to future generations.

    Lu Dongbin said to Hou Sheng: "Snakes and scorpions are refined by the natural qi of heaven and earth, making them live to fulfill their nature, killing one to save another. There is harm but no benefit. Take a piece of paper and write three mouths in one room, with the water beside the room being unusually clear. This is the name of Lu Dongbin."

    Chen Xiyi Mr. would sleep for half a year or several months, and recently not less than a month or more. He preserved his true breath and drank the jade liquid. His sleep was true sleep, and he had traveled with the hair girl.

    Carp, large or small, all have thirty-six scales. The hedgehog can jump into the tiger's ear and, when it sees a magpie, lies on its belly and lets it peck. Water leeches are also like fish eggs, and after three years, if they get water, they still live.

    Snake skin is used to plug both ears to treat malaria. Goji berries are made from strawberry juice and mixed into a paste to prevent white hair. Musk deer and deer are not impure or stinky unless they belong to the eight trigrams and the twelve animals. They can warm and supplement the body, with no blame in life or death, and Taoists use them as dried meat.

    During the Tianbao era, in the Xianyao Temple in Taizi陵, Henan province, every September 3rd night, there was a Taoist who became an immortal, and there was an old precedent. On the day, the name was reported to the prefecture, but Zhang Jiezhong, the prefect, did not believe it. He secretly ordered two brave men to carry weapons and spy on it. At three o'clock at night, a black tiger entered the temple and carried away a Taoist. They shot at it but missed, and the tiger left the Taoist and went away. The prefect then reported to the prefecture and requested bows and arrows, and a large hunt in the stone cave killed several tigers. Some golden tablets and jade records, crowns, cloaks, hair, and bones were found in great numbers. The temple was then abandoned and became a tomb for him to reside. [17]

    晋元康中梁国女子许嫁而夫戍经年不归女家更强以适人寻病亡夫还问女所在夫径至墓开棺女遂活因与俱归后壻闻知诣官争之王导曰此非常事不可以常理断之宜还前夫
    　　恵帝世杜锡家葬婢误不得出后十年开冡祔而婢尚生初婢之埋年十五六及开冡更生犹十五六嫁之有子
    　　太和中有开周世冡得殉葬女子数日而有气数日而能言郭太后爱养之又太原人破棺得一生妇人视其墓木可三十嵗
    　　南方木凝其大如槩鼻涕闻人气则闪闪而动堕人体成疮以麝香朱砂涂之愈

    　　续博物志卷二
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　续博物志卷三
    　　宋　李石　撰
    　　禹长九尺九寸汤长九尺孔子长十尺文王长十尺伍子胥长一丈眉间一尺韩王信八尺九寸金日防八尺二寸东方朔九尺三寸王莽时竒士巨母霸长一丈大十围本出于蓬莱魏咸熈大人见襄武长二丈脚迹三尺二寸苻健皇始四年有长人身长五丈是月霖雨河渭泛溢中流得大屐一长七尺三寸屐指长尺余 [18]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [18]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晋元康中 - Refers to the period during the reign of Emperor Huai of Jin (290-299 AD).
    • 许嫁 - Means 'engaged to be married.'
    • 夫戍经年不归 - Means 'the husband was stationed far away for many years and did not return.'
    • 女家更强以适人 - Means 'the family of the woman forced her to marry someone else.'
    • 寻病亡 - Means 'she fell ill and died shortly after.'
    • 夫径至墓开棺女遂活 - Means 'the husband went straight to her grave, opened the coffin, and the woman came back to life.'
    • 因与俱归 - Means 'and they returned together.'
    • 后壻闻知诣官争之 - Means 'the later husband heard about it and went to the authorities to contest the matter.'
    • 此非常事不可以常理断之 - Means 'this is not an ordinary matter; it cannot be decided by ordinary rules.'
    • 宜还前夫 - Means 'it is appropriate to return her to her first husband.'
    • 恵帝世 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Huai of Jin (290-299 AD).
    • 杜锡家葬婢误不得出 - Means 'a servant girl was mistakenly buried alive by the family of Du Xi.'
    • 后十年开冡祔而婢尚生 - Means 'ten years later, when the grave was opened for burial, the servant girl was still alive.'
    • 初婢之埋年十五六及开冡更生犹十五六 - Means 'when she was first buried, she was about fifteen or sixteen years old; when she was reborn, she was still about fifteen or sixteen.'
    • 嫁之有子 - Means 'she was married and had children.'
    • 太和中有开周世冡得殉葬女子数日而有气数日而能言 - Means 'during the reign of Emperor Wu of Wei (226-239 AD), when a Zhou dynasty tomb was opened, a woman who had been buried as a sacrifice revived after several days and could speak after more days.'
    • 郭太后爱养之 - Means 'Empress Guo took care of her.'
    • 太原人破棺得一生妇人视其墓木可三十嵗 - Means 'a man from Taiyuan broke open a coffin and found a living woman; the trees in her tomb were about thirty years old.'
    • 南方木凝其大如槩鼻涕闻人气则闪闪而动堕人体成疮以麝香朱砂涂之愈 - Means 'in the south, there is a type of tree that hardens; its size is like a large pestle. When it comes into contact with human breath, it moves slightly and can cause sores on the body. It can be treated with musk and cinnabar.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋元康中 - Refers to the period of the Western Jin dynasty.
    • 杜锡 - A historical figure from the Western Jin dynasty.
    • 恵帝世 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Huai of Jin.
    • 太和中 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Wu of Wei.
    • 周世冡 - Refers to a tomb from the Zhou dynasty.
    • 郭太后 - Refers to Empress Guo, a historical figure.
    • 太原 - Refers to Taiyuan, a city in Shanxi province.
    • 南方木 - Refers to a type of tree found in southern China.
    • 续博物志 - A historical text compiled during the Song dynasty.
    • 钦定四库全书 - A large-scale compilation of Chinese literature commissioned by the Qing dynasty.
    • 宋 李石 - The compiler of 'Xu Bowuzhi.'
    • 禹 - Yu the Great, a legendary Chinese leader.
    • 汤 - Tang of Shang, a legendary Chinese leader.
    • 孔子 - Confucius, a famous Chinese philosopher.
    • 文王 - King Wen of Zhou, a legendary Chinese leader.
    • 伍子胥 - Wu Zixu, a historical figure from the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 韩王信 - Han Xin, a historical figure from the Han dynasty.
    • 金日防 - Jin Midi, a historical figure from the Han dynasty.
    • 东方朔 - Dongfang Shuo, a historical figure from the Han dynasty.
    • 王莽 - Wang Mang, a historical figure from the Xin dynasty.
    • 巨母霸 - A historical figure from the Han dynasty.
    • 魏咸熈 - A period of the Wei dynasty.
    • 苻健 - Fu Jian, a historical figure from the Former Qin dynasty.

    During the reign of Emperor Huai of Jin, a woman from Liang State was engaged to be married, but her husband was stationed far away for many years and did not return. Her family then forced her to marry someone else. Shortly after, she fell ill and died. When her first husband returned and asked where she was, he went straight to her grave, opened the coffin, and the woman came back to life. They returned together. The later husband heard about it and went to the authorities to contest the matter. Wang Dao said, "This is not an ordinary matter; it cannot be decided by ordinary rules. It is appropriate to return her to her first husband."

    During the reign of Emperor Huai of Jin, a servant girl was mistakenly buried alive by the family of Du Xi. Ten years later, when the grave was opened for burial, the servant girl was still alive. When she was first buried, she was about fifteen or sixteen years old; when she was reborn, she was still about fifteen or sixteen. She was married and had children.

    During the reign of Emperor Wu of Wei, when a Zhou dynasty tomb was opened, a woman who had been buried as a sacrifice revived after several days and could speak after more days. Empress Guo took care of her. A man from Taiyuan broke open a coffin and found a living woman; the trees in her tomb were about thirty years old.

    In the south, there is a type of tree that hardens; its size is like a large pestle. When it comes into contact with human breath, it moves slightly and can cause sores on the body. It can be treated with musk and cinnabar.

    Continued Records of Curious Things, Volume Two
    Imperially Approved Complete Library of the Four Treasuries
    Continued Records of Curious Things, Volume Three
    Compiled by Li Shi of the Song dynasty

    Yu the Great was nine chi nine cun tall, Tang of Shang was nine chi tall, Confucius was ten chi tall, King Wen of Zhou was ten chi tall, Wu Zixu was one zhang tall, with a space of one chi between his eyebrows, Han Xin was eight chi nine cun tall, Jin Midi was eight chi two cun tall, Dongfang Shuo was nine chi three cun tall, during the reign of Wang Mang, the extraordinary scholar Ju Mo Ba was one zhang tall, with a circumference of ten wei, originally from Penglai, during the reign of Wei Xianxi, a tall man was seen in Xiangwu, two zhang tall, with footprints three chi two cun long, during the reign of Fu Jian in the first year of Huangshi, there was a tall man, five zhang tall, that month, there was heavy rain, the Yellow River and Wei River overflowed, in the middle of the river, a large shoe was found, seven chi three cun long, the toe of the shoe was more than one chi long. [18]

    汉武故事东郡送一短人长七寸曰巨灵光武时颍川张仲师长二寸东方朔曰西北荒中有小人长七寸朱衣冠国男女皆长七寸海鹄吞之腹中不死
    　　子华子曰黄帝之治天下也其精微之感荡上沈而下浮为百福之聚是百神受职于庚也帝乃采铜炼刚质也登彼山就髙明也作大罏者鼓阳化神鼎者熟物之器也上水而下火二气升降以相济中和之气也羣龙者众阳之气云者龙属也帝乡者灵台之阙而心术之变也弓裘衣冠者帝之所以善世救俗之具也甚矣世之好怪也后世必有人主好高而慕大以久生轻举为羡慕者左右狡诈希宠之臣又从而逢之是将甘心于黄帝之所造也
    　　玉局观洞高骈帅蜀取罪人以绳绊其腰令探浅深绳两月方絶出青城山洞天观门
    　　巫山道士黄万防以符药救人蜀王建召之铺草席水面泝流而上一日至成都省郎曹图鄙其人 [19]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [19]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 汉武故事 - A collection of stories about Emperor Wu of Han, a famous ruler of the Han Dynasty.
    • 东郡送一短人长七寸曰巨灵 - A literal description of a short person from Dong Commandery, seven inches tall, named Juling.
    • 光武时颍川张仲师长二寸 - Zhang Zhongshi from Yingchuan during the Guangwu era, who was two inches tall.
    • 东方朔曰西北荒中有小人长七寸朱衣冠国男女皆长七寸海鹄吞之腹中不死 - Dongfang Shuo, a famous scholar, about a mythical land in the northwest where small people seven inches tall wear red clothes and black hats, and when swallowed by a sea bird, they do not die.
    • 子华子曰黄帝之治天下也其精微之感荡上沈而下浮为百福之聚是百神受职于庚也 - Zihua Zi, a sage, about Huangdi's governance of the world, where the subtle influence of his spirit moves up and down, gathering a hundred blessings, and all spirits receive their duties from him.
    • 帝乃采铜炼刚质也登彼山就髙明也作大罏者鼓阳化神鼎者熟物之器也上水而下火二气升降以相济中和之气也 - Huangdi collecting copper and refining it into a solid substance, ascending Mount Taimu to seek enlightenment, making a large furnace, and using it to transform into a divine tripod, which is a vessel for cooking, with water on top and fire below, representing the interaction of the two forces to achieve harmony.
    • 羣龙者众阳之气云者龙属也帝乡者灵台之阙而心术之变也 - The collective energy of the dragons, which are associated with the clouds, and the imperial residence, which is the gateway to the spiritual realm and the transformation of the mind.
    • 弓裘衣冠者帝之所以善世救俗之具也 - The bow, robe, hat, and crown, which are the tools Huangdi used to improve the world and save customs.
    • 甚矣世之好怪也后世必有人主好高而慕大以久生轻举为羡慕者左右狡诈希宠之臣又从而逢之是将甘心于黄帝之所造也 - How the world loves strange things, and in the future, there will be rulers who admire Huangdi's height and grandeur, and seek immortality, and their cunning and deceitful ministers will follow suit, desiring to achieve what Huangdi did.
    • 玉局观洞高骈帅蜀取罪人以绳绊其腰令探浅深绳两月方絶出青城山洞天观门 - Yuju Cave, where Gao Pian, the governor of Shu, tied criminals to ropes to explore the depth, and after two months, they emerged from the entrance of the Qingcheng Mountain Cave Heaven.
    • 巫山道士黄万防以符药救人蜀王建召之铺草席水面泝流而上一日至成都省郎曹图鄙其人 - Huang Wanhfang, a Daoist priest from Wushan, who used talismans and medicine to save people, and was summoned by Wang Jian, the king of Shu, who laid out a mat on the water and floated upstream to Chengdu, where the provincial censor Cao Tu looked down on him.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 汉武 - Refers to Emperor Wu of Han, a famous ruler of the Han Dynasty.
    • 光武 - Refers to Emperor Guangwu of Han, another famous ruler of the Han Dynasty.
    • 黄帝 - Refers to the legendary Yellow Emperor, a mythical figure in Chinese history.
    • 玉局观洞 - Refers to Yuju Cave, a famous cave in Chinese mythology.
    • 青城山洞天观门 - Refers to the entrance of the Qingcheng Mountain Cave Heaven, a famous cave in Chinese mythology.
    • 巫山道士 - Refers to a Daoist priest from Wushan, a region in China.
    • 蜀王建 - Refers to Wang Jian, the king of Shu, a kingdom in ancient China.
    • 省郎曹图 - Refers to Cao Tu, a provincial censor in ancient China.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The phrase '东郡送一短人长七寸曰巨灵' is ambiguous. It could mean 'a short person from Dong Commandery, seven inches tall, named Juling' or 'a short person from Dong Commandery, named Juling, who is seven inches tall.'
    • The phrase '光武时颍川张仲师长二寸' is ambiguous. It could mean 'Zhang Zhongshi from Yingchuan during the Guangwu era, who was two inches tall' or 'Zhang Zhongshi from Yingchuan, who was two inches tall during the Guangwu era.'
    • The phrase '东方朔曰西北荒中有小人长七寸朱衣冠国男女皆长七寸海鹄吞之腹中不死' is ambiguous. It could mean 'Dongfang Shuo said that in the northwest wilderness, there are small people seven inches tall who wear red clothes and black hats, and when swallowed by a sea bird, they do not die' or 'Dongfang Shuo said that in the northwest wilderness, there are small people seven inches tall who wear red clothes and black hats, and when swallowed by a sea bird, they do not die.'
    • The phrase '子华子曰黄帝之治天下也其精微之感荡上沈而下浮为百福之聚是百神受职于庚也' is ambiguous. It could mean 'Zihua Zi said that Huangdi's governance of the world, where the subtle influence of his spirit moves up and down, gathers a hundred blessings, and all spirits receive their duties from him' or 'Zihua Zi said that Huangdi's governance of the world, where the subtle influence of his spirit moves up and down, gathers a hundred blessings, and all spirits receive their duties from him.'
    • The phrase '帝乃采铜炼刚质也登彼山就髙明也作大罏者鼓阳化神鼎者熟物之器也上水而下火二气升降以相济中和之气也' is ambiguous. It could mean 'Huangdi collected copper and refined it into a solid substance, ascended Mount Taimu to seek enlightenment, made a large furnace, and used it to transform into a divine tripod, which is a vessel for cooking, with water on top and fire below, representing the interaction of the two forces to achieve harmony' or 'Huangdi collected copper and refined it into a solid substance, ascended Mount Taimu to seek enlightenment, made a large furnace, and used it to transform into a divine tripod, which is a vessel for cooking, with water on top and fire below, representing the interaction of the two forces to achieve harmony.'
    • The phrase '羣龙者众阳之气云者龙属也帝乡者灵台之阙而心术之变也' is ambiguous. It could mean 'the collective energy of the dragons, which are associated with the clouds, and the imperial residence, which is the gateway to the spiritual realm and the transformation of the mind' or 'the collective energy of the dragons, which are associated with the clouds, and the imperial residence, which is the gateway to the spiritual realm and the transformation of the mind.'
    • The phrase '弓裘衣冠者帝之所以善世救俗之具也' is ambiguous. It could mean 'the bow, robe, hat, and crown, which are the tools Huangdi used to improve the world and save customs' or 'the bow, robe, hat, and crown, which are the tools Huangdi used to improve the world and save customs.'
    • The phrase '甚矣世之好怪也后世必有人主好高而慕大以久生轻举为羡慕者左右狡诈希宠之臣又从而逢之是将甘心于黄帝之所造也' is ambiguous. It could mean 'how the world loves strange things, and in the future, there will be rulers who admire Huangdi's height and grandeur, and seek immortality, and their cunning and deceitful ministers will follow suit, desiring to achieve what Huangdi did' or 'how the world loves strange things, and in the future, there will be rulers who admire Huangdi's height and grandeur, and seek immortality, and their cunning and deceitful ministers will follow suit, desiring to achieve what Huangdi did.'
    • The phrase '玉局观洞高骈帅蜀取罪人以绳绊其腰令探浅深绳两月方絶出青城山洞天观门' is ambiguous. It could mean 'Yuju Cave, where Gao Pian, the governor of Shu, tied criminals to ropes to explore the depth, and after two months, they emerged from the entrance of the Qingcheng Mountain Cave Heaven' or 'Yuju Cave, where Gao Pian, the governor of Shu, tied criminals to ropes to explore the depth, and after two months, they emerged from the entrance of the Qingcheng Mountain Cave Heaven.'
    • The phrase '巫山道士黄万防以符药救人蜀王建召之铺草席水面泝流而上一日至成都省郎曹图鄙其人' is ambiguous. It could mean 'Huang Wanhfang, a Daoist priest from Wushan, who used talismans and medicine to save people, and was summoned by Wang Jian, the king of Shu, who laid out a mat on the water and floated upstream to Chengdu, where the provincial censor Cao Tu looked down on him' or 'Huang Wanhfang, a Daoist priest from Wushan, who used talismans and medicine to save people, and was summoned by Wang Jian, the king of Shu, who laid out a mat on the water and floated upstream to Chengdu, where the provincial censor Cao Tu looked down on him.'

    Translation:

    The Han Wu Shi Gu recounts that a short man, seven inches tall, was sent from Dong Commandery and named Juling. During the reign of Emperor Guangwu, Zhang Zhongshi from Yingchuan was two inches tall. Dongfang Shuo said that in the northwest wilderness, there are small people seven inches tall who wear red clothes and black hats; in this land, men and women are all seven inches tall, and when swallowed by a sea bird, they do not die.

    Zihua Zi said that when Huangdi governed the world, the subtle influence of his spirit moved up and down, gathering a hundred blessings, and all spirits received their duties from him. Huangdi collected copper and refined it into a solid substance, ascending Mount Taimu to seek enlightenment. He made a large furnace, transforming it into a divine tripod, a vessel for cooking, with water on top and fire below, representing the interaction of the two forces to achieve harmony. The collective energy of the dragons, associated with the clouds, and the imperial residence, the gateway to the spiritual realm and the transformation of the mind, are the bow, robe, hat, and crown, the tools Huangdi used to improve the world and save customs. How the world loves strange things! In the future, there will be rulers who admire Huangdi's height and grandeur, seeking immortality, and their cunning and deceitful ministers will follow suit, desiring to achieve what Huangdi did.

    Yuju Cave, where Gao Pian, the governor of Shu, tied criminals to ropes to explore the depth, and after two months, they emerged from the entrance of the Qingcheng Mountain Cave Heaven.

    Huang Wanhfang, a Daoist priest from Wushan, used talismans and medicine to save people. Wang Jian, the king of Shu, summoned him, laying out a mat on the water and floating upstream to Chengdu, where the provincial censor Cao Tu looked down on him. [19]

    李徳裕好饵雄朱有道士自云李终南笑曰此是世间凡火服之反促夀因出一玉象子状如拳曰此可求勾漏莹澈者燃香致象鼻下勿令妇人鸡犬见之三五日象自服之即复吐出乃可服此火玉太阳之精凝结已三万年以相公好道因以奉借唯忠孝是念无自贻咎又出金象云此是雌者贵其相伴不尔玉象飞去徳服之有异乃于都下采聘名姝至百数不止象砂不复吐后南迁再遇道士怒甚索二象是夕风雨涛濑并作玉象如团火飞出船徳死于朱崖
    　　顔眞卿遇道人陶八八授以碧霞丹饵之曰七十上有厄如有即吉后为卢杞所陷缢死希烈败返葬状貌如生徧身金色爪甲出手背髪皆长数尺人咸异之后有商人遇之于罗浮山寄书至偃师顔氏守茔者曰此先太师亲翰书法头马尾真得仙也
    　　唐武宗四年官市郎巾有疑为狼筋者有老僧云贫道昔曽以一千于贾胡市得三枚状如巨蛹两头光带黄色泾帅叚祐宅失银器十余集奴婢环庭炙之虫栗动有一女奴脸唇瞤动乃窃器者 [20]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [20]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 好饵雄朱 - To enjoy using or favor a type of medicinal herb or substance.
    • 凡火 - Ordinary fire or common fire.
    • 反促夀 - To shorten life.
    • 玉象子 - A miniature jade elephant.
    • 勾漏莹澈者 - Something clear and bright from the region of Goulou.
    • 燃香致象鼻下 - To burn incense and place it under the elephant's trunk.
    • 勿令妇人鸡犬见之 - Do not allow women, chickens, or dogs to see it.
    • 三五日象自服之 - In three to five days, the elephant will naturally take it.
    • 飞去 - To fly away.
    • 碧霞丹 - A type of elixir or medicine believed to have magical properties.
    • 七十上有厄 - There is a misfortune around the age of seventy.
    • 希烈败 - After the defeat of Xili.
    • 罗浮山 - A famous mountain in Guangdong Province, known for its Taoist temples and natural beauty.
    • 偃师 - A place name in Henan Province.
    • 先太师 - A deceased father or teacher.
    • 亲翰书法 - Personal handwriting and calligraphy.
    • 马尾 - The tail of a horse.
    • 泾帅叚祐 - A military leader in Jing Prefecture named叚祐.
    • 银器 - Silverware or silver objects.
    • 环庭炙之 - Roast it around the courtyard.
    • 虫栗动 - The insect trembles and moves.
    • 脸唇瞤动 - The face and lips twitch.
    • 窃器者 - The thief.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 李徳裕 - A prominent statesman and writer during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 道士 - A Taoist priest or hermit.
    • 李终南 - A historical figure or legendary Taoist.
    • 勾漏 - A place name in Guangdong Province, known for its mineral resources.
    • 顔眞卿 - A famous calligrapher and statesman during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 陶八八 - A historical figure or legendary Taoist who gave the elixir to 颜真卿.
    • 碧霞丹 - A type of elixir or medicine believed to have magical properties.
    • 希烈 - A historical figure, possibly referring to a general or leader.
    • 罗浮山 - A famous mountain in Guangdong Province, known for its Taoist temples and natural beauty.
    • 偃师 - A place name in Henan Province.
    • 先太师 - A deceased father or teacher.
    • 泾帅叚祐 - A military leader in Jing Prefecture named叚祐.
    • 银器 - Silverware or silver objects.
    • 贾胡 - A merchant or trader.
    • 卢杞 - A historical figure, possibly referring to a minister or official.

    Proper Nouns

    • 李徳裕 - A prominent statesman and writer during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 道士 - A Taoist priest or hermit.
    • 李终南 - A historical figure or legendary Taoist.
    • 勾漏 - A place name in Guangdong Province, known for its mineral resources.
    • 顔眞卿 - A famous calligrapher and statesman during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 陶八八 - A historical figure or legendary Taoist who gave the elixir to 颜真卿.
    • 碧霞丹 - A type of elixir or medicine believed to have magical properties.
    • 希烈 - A historical figure, possibly referring to a general or leader.
    • 罗浮山 - A famous mountain in Guangdong Province, known for its Taoist temples and natural beauty.
    • 偃师 - A place name in Henan Province.
    • 先太师 - A deceased father or teacher.
    • 泾帅叚祐 - A military leader in Jing Prefecture named叚祐.
    • 银器 - Silverware or silver objects.

    Li De Yu enjoyed using a certain herb called "Xiong Zhu." There was a Taoist who claimed to be Li Zhongnan. He laughed and said, "This is ordinary fire; if you use it, it will shorten your life." Then he took out a small jade elephant, about the size of a fist, and said, "You can seek out clear and bright substances from Goulou. Burn incense and place it under the elephant's trunk, but do not allow women, chickens, or dogs to see it. In three to five days, the elephant will naturally take it, and then it will spit it out again. Only then can you consume this fire jade, which is the essence of the sun, condensed for three thousand years. Since you are devoted to the Dao, I offer it to you. Remember only loyalty and filial piety, and do not bring harm upon yourself." He also took out a golden elephant and said, "This is the female counterpart, valuable for its companionship. Otherwise, the jade elephant will fly away." Li De Yu consumed it and experienced strange phenomena. He then went to the capital and collected many beautiful women, numbering in the hundreds. The jade elephant no longer spat out the substance. Later, when he was exiled to the south, he met the Taoist again and was very angry. The Taoist demanded the two elephants. That night, there was a storm, and the jade elephant, like a ball of fire, flew out of the boat. Li De Yu died on Zhu Ya Island.

    Yan Zhenqing encountered a Taoist named Tao Ba Ba, who gave him the elixir "Bi Xia Dan." He said, "At around seventy, there will be misfortune; if there is, it will be good. Later, Yan Zhenqing was betrayed by Lu Qi and hanged to death. After the defeat of Xi Li, he was buried, and his appearance was as if he were alive, his entire body golden, his fingernails protruding from his back, and his hair long several feet. People found this strange. Later, a merchant met him on Luo Fu Mountain and sent a letter to Yan Zhenqing's caretaker in Yan Shi, saying, "This is the personal handwriting and calligraphy of my deceased father, truly a sign of his attainment of immortality."

    In the fourth year of Emperor Wuzong's reign, there was suspicion that the official hat was made of wolf sinew. An old monk said, "I once bought three pieces from a merchant, each about the size of a large maggot, with two heads and a yellowish-white color. General Duan Yu of Jing Prefecture lost over ten pieces of silverware. They gathered servants and slaves around the courtyard to roast them, and the insect trembled and moved. One female servant's face and lips twitched, and she was the thief." [20]

    返魂香东方朔曰月氏国使者献香曰东风入律百旬不休青云干吕连月不散意中国将有好道之君故捜竒蕴异而贡神香乘沈牛以济弱渊防骥足以度流沙今十三年矣香能起夭残之死疾长生之神药也疾疫夭死者将能起之以薫牙及闻气者即活明日失使者所在后元元年长安疫死者大半帝分香烧之死未三日皆活芳气三月不歇余香一旦失亡
    　　凉土异物志葱岭水分流东西西入大海东为河源禹所言昆仑盖本河所出也潜发于蒲昌洛书所纪出于重野经积石为中国河也张骞但能至大宛见河水初原达其潜发处桑钦水经云昆仑在河水西北去嵩髙五万里其高万一千里郦道元云昆仑之山三级下曰樊桐一曰板松二曰圃一名阆风上曰层城一名天庭是谓太帝之居十三州志云山去北海岸十三万里有弱水周匝东南接积石高诱曰河出昆山伏流地中万三千里禹导而通之以出积石山海经乃云自昆仑至 [21]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [21]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 返魂香 - Legendary fragrant herb or incense that could revive the dead, often associated with Daoist alchemy and mythology.
    • 东方朔曰 - Refers to Dongfang Shuo, a famous scholar and court jester during the Han Dynasty.
    • 月氏国使者献香 - Indicates that an envoy from the Yuezhi Kingdom presented the incense.
    • 东风入律百旬不休 - Describes a prolonged period of favorable winds, symbolizing auspicious conditions.
    • 青云干吕连月不散 - Refers to a continuous period of clear skies and auspicious signs.
    • 捜竒蕴异 - Means to search for and contain rare and extraordinary things.
    • 起夭残之死疾 - Means to revive the dead or cure severe illnesses.
    • 长生之神药 - Refers to a divine medicine for longevity.
    • 疾疫夭死者将能起之 - Indicates that those who died from disease or premature death could be revived.
    • 以薫牙及闻气者即活 - Means that those who were exposed to the fragrance would come back to life.
    • 明日失使者所在 - Indicates that the next day, the envoy disappeared.
    • 后元元年长安疫死者大半 - Refers to the year Houyuan (144 AD) when a plague in Chang'an caused many deaths.
    • 帝分香烧之 - Means the emperor distributed and burned the incense.
    • 死未三日皆活 - Indicates that those who had died within three days came back to life.
    • 芳气三月不歇 - Describes the fragrance lasting for three months.
    • 余香一旦失亡 - Means the remaining fragrance suddenly disappeared.
    • 凉土异物志 - Refers to a record of strange things in the Liang region.
    • 葱岭水分流东西 - Describes the flow of water from Mount Kongling (Kunlun) splitting into eastern and western directions.
    • 西入大海 - Indicates that the water flows west into the sea.
    • 东为河源 - Means the eastern flow becomes the source of the Yellow River.
    • 禹所言昆仑 - Refers to the legendary Mount Kunlun mentioned by Yu the Great.
    • 潜发于蒲昌 - Indicates that the river emerges from the Puchang area.
    • 洛书所纪出于重野 - Refers to the *Luo Shu* (River Map) and its description of the river's origin in Chongye.
    • 经积石 - Means passing through the Jishui area.
    • 为河也 - Indicates that this is the source of the Yellow River.
    • 张骞但能至大宛 - Refers to Zhang Qian, a diplomat who only reached Dayuan.
    • 见河水初原 - Means he saw the source of the Yellow River.
    • 达其潜发处 - Indicates reaching the place where the river emerges.
    • 桑钦水经云 - Refers to the *Shuijing* (Water Classic) by Sang Qin.
    • 昆仑在河水西北 - Means Mount Kunlun is northwest of the Yellow River.
    • 去嵩高五万里 - Indicates that it is 50,000 li away from Mount Song.
    • 高诱曰 - Refers to Gao You, a commentator.
    • 河出昆山伏流地中 - Means the Yellow River emerges from Mount Kunlun and flows underground.
    • 万三千里 - Indicates a distance of 30,000 li.
    • 禹导而通之以出积石 - Means Yu the Great guided and made the river flow out through the Jishui area.
    • 山海经乃云 - Refers to the *Shanhaijing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas).
    • 自昆仑至 - Means from Mount Kunlun to...

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 返魂香 - A legendary incense that can revive the dead, often associated with Daoist alchemy and mythology.
    • 东方朔 - A famous scholar and court jester during the Han Dynasty, known for his wit and wisdom.
    • 月氏国 - The Yuezhi Kingdom, a Central Asian state that played a significant role in the Silk Road trade.
    • 张骞 - A diplomat and explorer during the Han Dynasty, known for his missions to Central Asia.
    • 桑钦 - A geographer and writer of the *Shuijing* (Water Classic), a detailed geographical text.
    • 高诱 - A commentator on classical texts, known for his annotations on the *Shanhaijing* and other works.
    • 禹 - Yu the Great, a legendary figure in Chinese history known for his flood control efforts and the establishment of the Xia Dynasty.
    • 嵩高 - Mount Song, one of the Five Great Mountains of China, often associated with Daoist and Confucian traditions.
    • 山海经 - The *Shanhaijing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas), an ancient Chinese text containing mythological and geographical information.
    • 昆仑 - Mount Kunlun, a legendary mountain in Chinese mythology, often associated with immortality and the abode of gods.

    Proper Nouns

    • 东方朔 - Dongfang Shuo, a famous scholar and court jester during the Han Dynasty.
    • 月氏国 - The Yuezhi Kingdom, a Central Asian state.
    • 张骞 - Zhang Qian, a diplomat and explorer during the Han Dynasty.
    • 桑钦 - Sang Qin, a geographer and writer of the *Shuijing* (Water Classic).
    • 高诱 - Gao You, a commentator on classical texts.
    • 禹 - Yu the Great, a legendary figure in Chinese history.
    • 嵩高 - Mount Song, one of the Five Great Mountains of China.

    The Immortal Reviving Incense

    Dongfang Shuo said that an envoy from the Yuezhi Kingdom presented the incense, stating that the east wind had blown for a hundred days without ceasing, and the clear skies had lasted for months without dispersing. This indicated that China would have a ruler who loved the Dao, so they sought and contained rare and extraordinary things to offer divine incense. They rode a submerged ox to cross the weak abyss and a swift horse to traverse the sandy deserts. Now, after thirteen years, the incense could revive the dead and cure severe illnesses, serving as a divine medicine for longevity. Those who died from disease or premature death could be revived by the fragrance, and those who inhaled it would come back to life. The next day, the envoy disappeared.

    In the year Houyuan, a plague in Chang'an caused many deaths. The emperor distributed and burned the incense. Those who had died within three days came back to life, and the fragrance lasted for three months before suddenly disappearing.

    The Liangtu Yiwuzhi (Record of Strange Things in the Liang Region) states that the water from Mount Kongling splits into eastern and western directions, flowing west into the sea and east to become the source of the Yellow River. Yu the Great said that Mount Kunlun is the source of the Yellow River, emerging from the Puchang area. The Luo Shu (River Map) records its origin in Chongye, passing through the Jishui area to become the Yellow River. Zhang Qian only reached Dayuan and saw the source of the Yellow River, reaching the place where it emerges. Sang Qin's Shuijing (Water Classic) states that Mount Kunlun is northwest of the Yellow River, 50,000 li away from Mount Song, with a height of 10,000 li. Li Daoyuan says that Mount Kunlun has three levels: the lower level is called Fan Tong, also known as Ban Song; the middle level is called Xuanpu, also known as Langfeng; the upper level is called Cengcheng, also known as Tianting, which is the abode of the Great Emperor. The Shisan Zhou Zhi (Record of the Thirteen Provinces) states that the mountain is 130,000 li from the northern coast, with the weak water surrounding it to the east and south, connecting to Jishui. Gao You said that the Yellow River emerges from Mount Kunlun and flows underground for 30,000 li. Yu the Great guided and made it flow out through the Jishui area. The Shanhaijing (Classic of Mountains and Seas) states that from Mount Kunlun to... [21]

    积石一千七百四十里又曰西海之南流沙之滨赤水之后黒水之前有大山曰昆仑又曰钟山西六百里有昆仑周穆王昆仑瑶池去周之瀍涧特万有一千一百里西王母告穆王云去咸阳乃三十六万里东方朔十洲记方丈在东海之中央羣仙所治为昆仑山有三角曰阆风曰圃曰昆仑宫张华所叙昆仑铜柱为天柱盖本方朔神异经也释氏西域志阿耨达山上有大渊水宫殿楼观甚大即昆仑山穆天子所至即阿耨达宫也西为新头河为天竺诸国皆度葱岭郭璞曰似别有小昆仑
    　　李畋虞部该闻録所取神仙七人尔朱先生朱真人桃椎神和子屈突无为海蟾子刘昭逺荣先生火井县许山人汉杜光庭
    　　龙脑香段成式云出波律国树髙八九丈可六七尺围干脂为香清脂为膏子主内外障眼又有苍龙脑不可防眼经火为熟龙脑 [22]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [22]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 积石一千七百四十里 - Geographical measurement indicating the distance of the mountain range, which is 1,740 li.
    • 西海之南流沙之滨 - Describes the location as being south of the Western Sea and along the banks of flowing sand.
    • 赤水之后黒水之前 - Indicates a position between the Red Water and the Black Water.
    • 有大山曰昆仑 - Introduces the name of a large mountain, Kunlun.
    • 又曰钟山西六百里 - Another name for the mountain, located 600 li west of Zhongshan.
    • 周穆王昆仑瑶池 - Refers to the legendary King Mu of Zhou and the mythical Pool of Jade at Kunlun.
    • 去周之瀍涧特万有一千一百里 - Indicates the distance from the Zhou capital, specifically the Cai and Jian rivers, which is 11,100 li.
    • 西王母告穆王云去咸阳乃三十六万里 - The Queen Mother of the West tells King Mu that it is 360,000 li to Xianyang.
    • 东方朔十洲记方丈在东海之中央 - Refers to the Eastern Han scholar Dongfang Shuo's *Ten Lands* record, mentioning Fangzhang in the center of the Eastern Sea.
    • 群仙所治为昆仑山有三角曰阆风曰圃曰昆仑宫 - Describes the mountain as having three peaks named Langfeng, Xuanpu, and Kunlun Palace.
    • 张华所叙昆仑铜柱为天柱盖本方朔神异经也 - Refers to Zhang Hua's description of the Kunlun copper pillar as the Pillar of Heaven, based on Dongfang Shuo's *Book of Marvelous Things*.
    • 释氏西域志阿耨达山上有大渊水宫殿楼观甚大即昆仑山穆天子所至即阿耨达宫也 - Refers to the Buddhist text *Records of the Western Regions*, mentioning the great palace at Anuodasa Mountain, which is identified as the Kunlun Mountain where King Mu visited.
    • 西为新头河为天竺诸国皆度葱岭郭璞曰似别有小昆仑 - Indicates the western river, the New Head River, which leads to the countries of Tianzhu (India) and passes through the葱岭 (Kunlun Range), with Guo Pu suggesting there might be another small Kunlun.
    • 龙脑香段成式云出波律国树髙八九丈可六七尺围干脂为香清脂为膏子主内外障眼又有苍龙脑不可防眼经火为熟龙脑 - Describes the production of dragon brain incense from the Boli country, including its properties and uses.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 周穆王 - A legendary king of the Zhou dynasty known for his travels and encounters with immortals.
    • 昆仑瑶池 - The mythical Pool of Jade at Kunlun, a place of immortality and divine beauty.
    • 西王母 - The Queen Mother of the West, a deity in Chinese mythology associated with longevity and immortality.
    • 东方朔 - A scholar and court jester during the Han dynasty, known for his wit and knowledge.
    • 十洲记 - A text by Dongfang Shuo describing ten mythical lands.
    • 方丈 - One of the mythical islands in the Eastern Sea.
    • 群仙 - A group of immortals or deities.
    • 张华 - A historian and writer during the Jin dynasty, known for his work *Records of the Three Kingdoms*.
    • 释氏西域志 - A Buddhist text recording the geography and culture of the Western Regions.
    • 阿耨达山 - A mountain in Buddhist texts, often associated with the Kunlun mountain.
    • 天竺 - The ancient name for India.
    • 葱岭 - The Kunlun Range, a mountain range in Central Asia.
    • 郭璞 - A scholar and geographer during the Jin dynasty, known for his commentaries on the *I Ching*.
    • 李畋 - A scholar during the Tang dynasty, known for his works on immortals and esoteric practices.
    • 虞部 - An official position in the Tang dynasty, responsible for forestry and hunting.
    • 该闻録 - A collection of what was heard or recorded.
    • 神仙 - Immortals or deities in Chinese mythology.
    • 尔朱先生 - One of the immortals mentioned in Li Tian's collection.
    • 朱真人 - Another immortal mentioned in Li Tian's collection.
    • 桃椎神和子 - Yet another immortal mentioned in Li Tian's collection.
    • 屈突无为 - Another immortal mentioned in Li Tian's collection.
    • 海蟾子 - Another immortal mentioned in Li Tian's collection.
    • 刘昭逺 - Another immortal mentioned in Li Tian's collection.
    • 荣先生 - Another immortal mentioned in Li Tian's collection.
    • 火井县 - A county in Sichuan province known for its natural gas springs.
    • 许山人 - A hermit or reclusive scholar.
    • 汉杜光庭 - A scholar during the Tang dynasty, known for his works on Daoism.
    • 龙脑香 - Dragon brain incense, a type of aromatic resin.
    • 段成式 - A scholar and writer during the Tang dynasty, known for his work *Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio*.
    • 波律国 - An ancient kingdom, possibly referring to the region of Borneo or Sumatra.
    • 苍龙脑 - A type of dragon brain incense that is not suitable for protecting the eyes.
    • 经火为熟龙脑 - The process of heating the resin to produce mature dragon brain incense.

    Proper Nouns

    • 周穆王 - King Mu of Zhou.
    • 昆仑瑶池 - The Pool of Jade at Kunlun.
    • 西王母 - The Queen Mother of the West.
    • 东方朔 - Dongfang Shuo.
    • 十洲记 - *Ten Lands*.
    • 方丈 - Fangzhang.
    • 群仙 - Immortals.
    • 张华 - Zhang Hua.
    • 释氏西域志 - *Records of the Western Regions*.
    • 阿耨达山 - Anuodasa Mountain.
    • 天竺 - Tianzhu (India).
    • 葱岭 - Kunlun Range.
    • 郭璞 - Guo Pu.
    • 李畋 - Li Tian.
    • 虞部 - Yumu (official position).
    • 该闻録 - Collection of what was heard or recorded.
    • 神仙 - Immortals.
    • 尔朱先生 - Erzhu Mr.
    • 朱真人 - Zhu True Person.
    • 桃椎神和子 - Taocui Shenhezi.
    • 屈突无为 - Qutu Wuwei.
    • 海蟾子 - Hai Chanzi.
    • 刘昭逺 - Liu Zhaoyuan.
    • 荣先生 - Rong Mr.
    • 火井县 - Huojing County.
    • 许山人 - Xu Shanren.
    • 汉杜光庭 - Han Du Guangting.
    • 龙脑香 - Dragon brain incense.
    • 段成式 - Duan Chengshi.
    • 波律国 - Boli Kingdom.
    • 苍龙脑 - Canglong Brain.
    • 经火为熟龙脑 - Heated to produce mature dragon brain incense.

    The mountain range, known as Jishu, spans 1,740 li. It is also described as being south of the Western Sea, along the banks of flowing sand, and situated between the Red Water and the Black Water. There stands a great mountain called Kunlun, also known as Zhongshan Mountain, located 600 li to the west. According to legend, during the reign of King Mu of Zhou, the Pool of Jade at Kunlun was a place of great significance, located 11,100 li from the Zhou capital, specifically the Cai and Jian rivers. The Queen Mother of the West told King Mu that it was 360,000 li to Xianyang. The Eastern Han scholar Dongfang Shuo, in his Ten Lands record, mentioned Fangzhang, a mythical island in the center of the Eastern Sea, where many immortals resided. The Kunlun mountain, with its three peaks named Langfeng, Xuanpu, and Kunlun Palace, is a place of great spiritual importance.
