Zhang Hua, a historian during the Jin dynasty, described the Kunlun copper pillar as the Pillar of Heaven, based on Dongfang Shuo's Book of Marvelous Things. The Buddhist text Records of the Western Regions mentions the great palace at Anuodasa Mountain, which is identified as the Kunlun mountain where King Mu visited. To the west lies the New Head River, which leads to the countries of Tianzhu (India) and passes through the Kunlun Range, with Guo Pu suggesting there might be another small Kunlun.

    Li Tian, in his Collection of What I Have Heard, lists seven immortals: Er Zhu Mr., Zhu True Man, Tao Zhi Shen Hezi, Qu Tu Wuwei, Hai Chanzi, Liu Zhao Yuan, Rong Xing Mr., Huojing County Xu Shanren, and Han Du Guangting. The dragon brain incense, as described by Duan Chengshi, originates from the Boli country. The trees there are 8 to 9 zhang tall and have a circumference of 6 to 7 chi. The resin from the trunk is used for incense, while the clear resin is made into a salve. This salve is used to treat both internal and external eye conditions. There is also a type of dragon brain incense called Qinglong brain, which is not suitable for protecting the eyes. When heated, it becomes mature dragon brain incense. [22]

    香谱云麝以一子真香糅作三四子刮取血膜杂以余糁皮毛不辨也香有二色蕃香蛮香又杂以人撰作官市动至数千计何以塞科取之责所谓真有三说鹿羣行山中自然有麝气不见其形为真香入春以脚剔入水泥中藏之不使人见为真香杀之取其脐一鹿一脐为真香此余所目击也
    　　仁善盛明曰舜残民多衅曰桀残义损善曰纣慈恵爱亲曰孝爱民好与曰恵圣善周闻曰宣声闻宣逺曰昭克定祸乱曰武聪明睿知曰献温柔圣善曰懿布徳执义曰穆仁义说民曰元布纲治纪曰平乱而不损曰灵保民耆艾曰明辟土有徳曰襄贞心大度曰匡大虑慈民曰定既过能正曰恭不生其国曰声一徳不解曰简夙兴夜寐曰敬清白自守曰贞柔徳好聚曰靖安乐治民曰康小心畏忌曰僖中身早折曰悼慈仁和民曰顺好勇致力曰庄恭仁短折曰哀在国逢难曰愍名实过爽曰缪壅遏不通曰幽暴虐无亲曰厉致志立圗曰景辟土兼国曰桓经纬天地曰文执义扬善曰怀短折不成曰殇去礼逺众曰炀怠政外交曰治典不敷曰震 [23]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [23]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 香谱云 - Classical formula meaning 'according to the fragrance spectrum' or 'the fragrance spectrum states.'
    • 麝以一子真香糅作三四子刮取血膜杂以余糁皮毛不辨也 - Complex phrase describing the process of making musk: 'musk deer, genuine scent from one seed, mixed into three or four seeds, scraped off the blood membrane, mixed with the remaining powder and fur, indistinguishable.'
    • 香有二色 - Means 'fragrances come in two colors,' where 'colors' likely refers to types or qualities.
    • 蕃香蛮香 - Refers to foreign and barbarian fragrances, respectively.
    • 官市动至数千计 - Means 'official markets involve thousands of items,' indicating a large-scale trade.
    • 真有三说 - Means 'there are three ways to define what is true or genuine.'
    • 鹿羣行山中自然有麝气不见其形为真香 - Describes a natural phenomenon where the scent of musk is present without seeing the animal.
    • 入春以脚剔入水泥中藏之不使人见为真香 - Describes a method of collecting musk by digging into mud in spring, keeping it hidden.
    • 杀之取其脐一鹿一脐为真香 - Describes the method of killing the animal to extract the musk gland, which is considered the true scent.
    • 仁善盛明曰舜 - Classical formula meaning 'benevolence and virtue are highly praised, as in the case of Shun.'
    • 残民多衅曰桀 - Classical formula meaning 'ruining the people leads to many conflicts, as in the case of Jie.'
    • 残义损善曰纣 - Classical formula meaning 'destroying virtue and reducing good deeds, as in the case of Zhou.'
    • 慈恵爱亲曰孝 - Classical formula meaning 'caring for and loving one's parents is called filial piety.'
    • 爱民好与曰恵 - Classical formula meaning 'loving the people and being generous is called benevolence.'
    • 圣善周闻曰宣 - Classical formula meaning 'being wise and well-informed is called proclamation.'
    • 声闻宣逺曰昭 - Classical formula meaning 'having a far-reaching reputation is called clarity.'
    • 克定祸乱曰武 - Classical formula meaning 'overcoming chaos and disorder is called martial.'
    • 聪明睿知曰献 - Classical formula meaning 'being intelligent and wise is called offering.'
    • 温柔圣善曰懿 - Classical formula meaning 'being gentle and virtuous is called excellence.'
    • 布徳执义曰穆 - Classical formula meaning 'spreading virtue and adhering to righteousness is called reverence.'
    • 仁义说民曰元 - Classical formula meaning 'being benevolent and virtuous to the people is called origin.'
    • 布纲治纪曰平 - Classical formula meaning 'establishing laws and order is called peace.'
    • 乱而不损曰灵 - Classical formula meaning 'being chaotic but not destructive is called spirit.'
    • 保民耆艾曰明 - Classical formula meaning 'protecting the elderly and wise is called enlightenment.'
    • 辟土有德曰襄 - Classical formula meaning 'expanding territory with virtue is called assistance.'
    • 贞心大度曰匡 - Classical formula meaning 'having a steadfast heart and great tolerance is called support.'
    • 大虑慈民曰定 - Classical formula meaning 'having great wisdom and caring for the people is called stability.'
    • 既过能正曰恭 - Classical formula meaning 'correcting mistakes is called respect.'
    • 不生其国曰声 - Classical formula meaning 'not being born in the country is called fame.'
    • 一德不解曰简 - Classical formula meaning 'being consistent in virtue is called simplicity.'
    • 夙兴夜寐曰敬 - Classical formula meaning 'rising early and sleeping late is called respect.'
    • 清白自守曰贞 - Classical formula meaning 'maintaining purity and integrity is called steadfastness.'
    • 柔德好聚曰靖 - Classical formula meaning 'being gentle and good at gathering is called tranquility.'
    • 安乐治民曰康 - Classical formula meaning 'ensuring peace and prosperity for the people is called health.'
    • 小心畏忌曰僖 - Classical formula meaning 'being cautious and fearful is called peace.'
    • 中身早折曰悼 - Classical formula meaning 'dying young is called mourning.'
    • 慈仁和民曰顺 - Classical formula meaning 'being kind and harmonious with the people is called obedience.'
    • 好勇致力曰庄 - Classical formula meaning 'being brave and dedicated is called solemnity.'
    • 恭仁短折曰哀 - Classical formula meaning 'being respectful and benevolent but dying young is called sorrow.'
    • 在国逢难曰愍 - Classical formula meaning 'encountering difficulties in the country is called compassion.'
    • 名实过爽曰缪 - Classical formula meaning 'having a name that does not match reality is called error.'
    • 壅遏不通曰幽 - Classical formula meaning 'being blocked and unable to communicate is called obscurity.'
    • 暴虐无亲曰厉 - Classical formula meaning 'being cruel and having no close relationships is called severity.'
    • 致志立圗曰景 - Classical formula meaning 'achieving goals and establishing plans is called splendor.'
    • 辟土兼国曰桓 - Classical formula meaning 'expanding territory and annexing states is called constancy.'
    • 经纬天地曰文 - Classical formula meaning 'managing the world with skill is called culture.'
    • 执义扬善曰怀 - Classical formula meaning 'upholding righteousness and promoting good is called embracing.'
    • 短折不成曰殇 - Classical formula meaning 'dying before achieving maturity is called youth.'
    • 去礼远众曰炀 - Classical formula meaning 'departing from rituals and distancing from the people is called burning.'
    • 怠政外交曰 - Classical formula meaning 'being lazy in governance and engaging in foreign affairs is called neglect.'
    • 治典不敷曰震 - Classical formula meaning 'managing the classics inadequately is called thunder.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 香谱 - Refers to a classical text or treatise on fragrances.
    • 麝 - Refers to musk deer, known for producing musk, a valuable fragrance.
    • 蕃香蛮香 - Refers to fragrances from foreign and barbarian regions, respectively.
    • 官市 - Refers to official markets, indicating state-controlled trade.
    • 舜、桀、纣 - Refers to legendary rulers of ancient China, with Shun being a benevolent ruler, Jie a tyrant, and Zhou another notorious ruler.
    • 仁、义、礼、智、信 - Refers to the Five Constants, fundamental virtues in Confucianism.
    • 孝、惠、宣、昭、武、献、懿、穆、元、平、灵、明、襄、匡、定、恭、声、简、敬、贞、靖、康、僖、悼、顺、庄、哀、愍、缪、幽、厉、景、桓、文、怀、殇、炀、、震 - These are titles or descriptions of virtues and vices associated with historical figures.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The complex sentence '麝以一子真香糅作三四子刮取血膜杂以余糁皮毛不辨也' describes the process of making musk, with multiple steps and ingredients.
    • The sentence '香有二色蕃香蛮香又杂以人撰作官市动至数千计' describes the types of fragrances and the scale of their trade.
    • The long sentence '真有三说鹿羣行山中自然有麝气不见其形为真香入春以脚剔入水泥中藏之不使人见为真香杀之取其脐一鹿一脐为真香' describes three ways to define what is true or genuine in the context of musk, with detailed descriptions of each method.

    According to the fragrance spectrum, musk is made by mixing the genuine scent from one seed into three or four seeds, scraping off the blood membrane, and mixing it with the remaining powder and fur, which becomes indistinguishable. There are two types of fragrances: foreign and barbarian fragrances, which are also mixed with the work of others, and official markets involve thousands of items. How can one stop the responsibility of collecting them? What is considered true has three definitions: when a herd of deer naturally emits musk without being seen, it is true fragrance; when one digs into the mud in spring with their feet, hides it, and does not let anyone see it, it is true fragrance; when one kills the animal and takes its umbilical gland, one deer one gland, it is true fragrance. This is what I have witnessed.

    Benevolence and virtue are highly praised, as in the case of Shun; ruining the people leads to many conflicts, as in the case of Jie; destroying virtue and reducing good deeds, as in the case of Zhou; caring for and loving one's parents is called filial piety; loving the people and being generous is called benevolence; being wise and well-informed is called proclamation; having a far-reaching reputation is called clarity; overcoming chaos and disorder is called martial; being intelligent and wise is called offering; being gentle and virtuous is called excellence; spreading virtue and adhering to righteousness is called reverence; being benevolent and virtuous to the people is called origin; establishing laws and order is called peace; being chaotic but not destructive is called spirit; protecting the elderly and wise is called enlightenment; expanding territory with virtue is called assistance; having a steadfast heart and great tolerance is called support; having great wisdom and caring for the people is called stability; correcting mistakes is called respect; not being born in the country is called fame; being consistent in virtue is called simplicity; rising early and sleeping late is called respect; maintaining purity and integrity is called steadfastness; being gentle and good at gathering is called tranquility; ensuring peace and prosperity for the people is called health; being cautious and fearful is called peace; dying young is called mourning; being kind and harmonious with the people is called obedience; being brave and dedicated is called solemnity; being respectful and benevolent but dying young is called sorrow; encountering difficulties in the country is called compassion; having a name that does not match reality is called error; being blocked and unable to communicate is called obscurity; being cruel and having no close relationships is called severity; achieving goals and establishing plans is called splendor; expanding territory and annexing states is called constancy; managing the world with skill is called culture; upholding righteousness and promoting good is called embracing; dying before achieving maturity is called youth; departing from rituals and distancing from the people is called burning; being lazy in governance and engaging in foreign affairs is called neglect; managing the classics inadequately is called thunder. [23]

    太学同官有曽官广中者云沈香杂木也朽蠧浸沙水嵗久得之如儋崖海道居民桥梁皆香材如海桂橘柚之木沈于水多年得之为沈水香本草谓为似橘是矣然生采之即不香也
    　　海药本草云降眞香主天行时气烧之或引降省头香即香附子
    　　仓颉解诂云芸蒿似邪蒿可食鱼豢典略云芸香辟纸鱼蠧故藏书台称芸台
    　　南唐女冠耿先生鸟爪玉貌获宠于元宗将诞之夕震雷绕室大雨河倾半夜雷止耿身不复孕大食国进龙脑油上所秘惜先生见之曰此非佳者当为大家致之乃缝夹绢嚢贮白龙脑一斤垂于栋上以胡缾盛之有顷如注上骇叹不已命酒泛之味逾于大食国进者又于元宗前搦雪为银铤投红炉淬之成中金指痕犹在又取宫妓箕中粪壤炒为银开宝末内库尚有先生粪银炒小麦于银釡成真珠 [24]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [24]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 曽官廣中者 - Refers to those who have served as officials in the region of Guangzhong.
    • 沈香雜木也 - Describes sandalwood as a mixed wood.
    • 朽蠧浸沙水 - Describes the process of sandalwood's formation, being decayed and infested by termites, immersed in sand and water.
    • 嵗久得之 - Indicates that sandalwood is obtained after a long time.
    • 如儋崖海道居民橋梁皆香材 - Suggests that the wood is highly valued, as the residents of Danya and Hai Dao use it for bridges and buildings.
    • 如海桂橘柚之木 - Compares sandalwood to other types of wood, such as sea cinnamon, oranges, and pomelos.
    • 沈於水多年得之為沈水香 - Describes the transformation of the wood into incense after being immersed in water for many years.
    • 本草謂為似橘是矣 - Indicates that the *Bencao* (Herbal) describes the wood as resembling oranges.
    • 然生采之即不香也 - Suggests that the wood only becomes fragrant after it has been processed, not when harvested alive.
    • 海藥本草云降眞香主天行時氣燒之或引降 - Describes *Jiangzhen* incense as used to treat epidemic diseases, and burning it can help to reduce them.
    • 省頭香即香附子 - Indicates that *Jiangzhen* incense is also known as *Xiangfu* (a type of herb).
    • 倉颉解诂云芸蒿似邪蒿可食 - Describes the herb *yun* as similar to *xia* and can be eaten.
    • 鱼豢典略云芸香辟紙鱼蠧故藏书台称芸台 - Indicates that *yun* repels bookworms, so the bookshelf is called *Yun Tai*.
    • 南唐女冠耿先生鳥爪玉貌獲寵於元宗 - Describes Geng Xiansheng, a Taoist nun of the Southern Tang Dynasty, with bird-like claws and a jade-like appearance, favored by Emperor Yuanzong.
    • 將誕之夕震雷繞室大雨河傾半夜雷止 - Describes a supernatural event on the night before her birth, with a thunderstorm surrounding the room and heavy rain until midnight.
    • 耿身不復孕 - Indicates that the event had a lasting effect, and Geng did not become pregnant again.
    • 大食国进龙脑油上所秘惜 - Describes the Great Food Kingdom presenting dragon's brain oil, which was highly valued and kept secret by the emperor.
    • 先生見之曰此非佳者當为大家致之 - Indicates Geng's desire to obtain a superior version of the dragon's brain oil.
    • 乃缝夹绢嚢贮白龙脑一斤垂于栋上以胡缾盛之 - Describes the storage method of the white dragon's brain oil.
    • 有顷如注 - Indicates the rapid transformation of the substance.
    • 上骇叹不已命酒泛之味逾于大食国进者 - Describes the improved quality of the dragon's brain oil after the transformation.
    • 又于元宗前搦雪为银铤投红炉淬之成中金指痕犹在 - Describes a magical transformation of snow into silver ingots and then into true gold.
    • 又取宫妓箕中粪壤炒为银 - Describes another magical transformation of feces from palace concubines' sieves into silver.
    • 开宝末内库尚有先生粪银炒小麦于银釡成真珠 - Describes the lasting effects of Geng's magical abilities, with the imperial treasury still having her feces-silver and fried wheat turning into pearls.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 廣中 - Refers to the region of Guangzhong in southern China.
    • 沈香 - Refers to sandalwood, a highly valued type of wood used for incense.
    • 本草 - Refers to the *Bencao* (Herbal), a classical Chinese text on herbal medicine.
    • 海藥本草 - Refers to the *Haiyao Bencao* (Herbal of Sea Medicines), a text on medicinal substances from the sea.
    • 倉颉解诂 - Refers to Cangjie's *Jiegu* (Explanations), a text on the interpretation of characters.
    • 鱼豢典略 - Refers to Yu Huan's *Dianlue* (Compendium), a historical text.
    • 南唐 - Refers to the Southern Tang Dynasty, a period in Chinese history.
    • 元宗 - Refers to Emperor Yuanzong, a ruler of the Southern Tang Dynasty.
    • 大食国 - Refers to the Great Food Kingdom, a reference to the Arab world.
    • 龙脑油 - Refers to dragon's brain oil, a rare and valuable substance.
    • 开宝末 - Refers to the end of the Kai Bao period, a period in Chinese history.

    Proper Nouns

    • 廣中 - The region of Guangzhong.
    • 沈香 - Sandalwood.
    • 本草 - The *Bencao* (Herbal).
    • 海藥本草 - The *Haiyao Bencao* (Herbal of Sea Medicines).
    • 倉颉解诂 - Cangjie's *Jiegu* (Explanations).
    • 鱼豢典略 - Yu Huan's *Dianlue* (Compendium).
    • 南唐 - The Southern Tang Dynasty.
    • 元宗 - Emperor Yuanzong.
    • 大食国 - The Great Food Kingdom.
    • 龙脑油 - Dragon's brain oil.
    • 开宝末 - The end of the Kai Bao period.

    Those who have served as officials in Guangzhong say that sandalwood is a mixed wood, decayed and infested by termites, immersed in sand and water, and after a long time, it is obtained. Like the residents of Danya and Hai Dao, their bridges and buildings are all made of fragrant wood, like the wood of sea cinnamon, oranges, and pomelos. Immersed in water for many years, it becomes known as Sinking Water Incense. The Bencao (Herbal) says it is similar to oranges, which is correct, but if it is harvested alive, it is not fragrant.

    The Haiyao Bencao (Herbal of Sea Medicines) says that Jiangzhen incense is used to treat epidemic diseases, and burning it can help to reduce them. Head-saving incense is the same as Xiangfu (a type of herb).

    Cangjie's Jiegu (Explanations) says that yun (a type of herb) is similar to xia (another type of herb) and can be eaten. Yu Huan's Dianlue (Compendium) says that yun (a type of herb) repels bookworms, so the bookshelf is called Yun Tai.

    Geng Xiansheng, a Taoist nun of the Southern Tang Dynasty, had bird-like claws and a jade-like appearance, and was favored by Emperor Yuanzong. On the night before her birth, there was a thunderstorm that surrounded the room, and it rained heavily until midnight when the thunder stopped. Geng did not become pregnant again. The Great Food Kingdom (a reference to the Arab world) presented dragon's brain oil, which was highly valued and kept secret by the emperor. Geng saw it and said, "This is not the best; I will get a better one for everyone." She then sewed a silk bag, stored one catty of white dragon's brain, hung it on the beam, and kept it in a trinket bottle. In a short while, it was like pouring. The emperor was amazed and continued to exclaim, ordering wine to be poured over it, and the flavor was even better than that presented by the Great Food Kingdom. She also formed silver ingots from snow in front of Emperor Yuanzong, threw them into a red furnace, and quenched them to form true gold, with fingerprints still visible. She also took the feces from the palace concubines' sieves, fried it, and turned it into silver. At the end of the Kai Bao period, the imperial treasury still had Geng's feces-silver, and frying wheat in a silver pot turned it into pearls. [24]

    禹治水土至北海之北有国曰终北国之中有山山名壶领状若甔甀项有口状若负环名曰滋穴有水涌出名曰神瀵臭过椒兰味逾醪醴一源分为四埒注于山下民饮于神瀵浴于神瀵此列子寓言也
    　　泥壁未干挥涕其上隂雨中化为虫状如蚓
    　　东方介氏之国其国人数有解六畜之语者盖偏知之所得太古神圣之人备知万物情态悉解异音声防而训之言血气不相殊也
    　　条支国临西海出师子太雀郭义恭广志曰大雀颈及身膺蹄都似槖駞举头高八九尺张翅丈余食大麦其卵如罋今之駞鸟也汉元帝时有大鸟如马驹时人谓之爰居
    　　环王国野象一牡管三十余牝牝者牙才二尺迭供牡者水草卧则环守牡象死共埋之号吼其处乃散 [25]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [25]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 状若甔甀 - Describes the shape of the mountain as resembling a large jar or vase.
    • 臭过椒兰 - The smell is better than that of pepper and orchid, indicating a pleasant aroma.
    • 味逾醪醴 - The taste is superior to that of wine, suggesting a high-quality water.
    • 泥壁未干挥涕其上 - The wall was not yet dry when tears were shed upon it, possibly a metaphor for sorrow.
    • 阴雨中化为虫状如蚓 - In the rain, it transformed into a worm-like creature.
    • 迭供牡者 - Taking turns to serve the male elephant.
    • 牡象死共埋之 - When the male elephant dies, all the females bury it together.
    • 号吼其处乃散 - They howl at the site before dispersing.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 禹治水土 - Yu the Great's legendary flood control efforts, a significant event in Chinese mythology.
    • 终北国 - A mythical country mentioned in ancient texts, often associated with remote and mysterious places.
    • 壶领 - A specific mountain, possibly a geographical feature in ancient texts.
    • 神瀵 - Sacred springs, often believed to have healing properties in Chinese mythology.
    • 东方介氏之国 - A fictional or mythological country in the east, possibly representing a distant and exotic land.
    • 条支国 - A country mentioned in ancient texts, possibly referring to a region in Central Asia.
    • 太雀 - A large bird, possibly a mythical creature, often associated with divine or supernatural qualities.
    • 郭义恭广志 - An ancient text or historical record, possibly a reference to a specific work.
    • 汉元帝时 - During the reign of Emperor Yuan of Han, a significant period in Chinese history.
    • 爰居 - A mythical creature or bird, possibly a reference to a specific legend or story.
    • 环王国 - A fictional or mythological country, possibly representing a remote and mysterious place.

    Proper Nouns

    • 禹 - Yu the Great, a legendary figure in Chinese mythology.
    • 终北国 - A mythical country.
    • 壶领 - A mountain.
    • 神瀵 - A sacred spring.
    • 东方介氏之国 - A country in the east.
    • 条支国 - A country in Central Asia.
    • 太雀 - A large bird.
    • 郭义恭广志 - An ancient text or historical record.
    • 汉元帝 - Emperor Yuan of Han.
    • 爰居 - A mythical creature or bird.
    • 环王国 - A fictional or mythological country.

    Yu the Great controlled the waters and reached the north of the Great Sea, where there was a country called the Endless North. In the center of this country, there was a mountain named Hu Ling, shaped like a large jar or vase, with a neck-like opening that resembled a ring, called Zhi Xue. From this opening, a spring called Shen Nuan emerged, its smell surpassing that of pepper and orchid, and its taste superior to wine. One source divided into four channels, flowing down the mountain. The people drank from and bathed in the Shen Nuan spring, as described in the Liezi as a fable.

    When the wall was not yet dry, tears were shed upon it, and in the rain, it transformed into a worm-like creature.

    In the eastern country of Jie Shi, there were people who could understand the language of animals, a skill that ancient wise and holy figures possessed, knowing the emotions and behaviors of all things and understanding the sounds of different creatures. They taught that blood and qi (vital energy) are not fundamentally different.

    The country of Tiao Zhi, bordering the Western Sea, produced lions and large birds called Tai Que. According to Guo Yi Gong's Guang Zhi, the Tai Que had a neck and body like a camel, stood about eight or nine feet tall, spread its wings over ten feet, ate barley, and laid eggs as large as jars, known today as camel birds. During the reign of Emperor Yuan of Han, a large bird appeared, resembling a foal, and was called Aie by the people.

    In the Ring Kingdom, there were wild elephants, one male and more than thirty females. The females, whose tusks measured two feet long, took turns serving the male. They slept in water and grass, surrounded by other elephants. When the male elephant died, all the females buried it together, howling at the site before dispersing. [25]

    鼈数食可长发
    　　防鱼心肝及头毒如野
    　　蝤蛑大者力能与虎鬬取之不戒螯能剪杀人
    　　鲎皮殻甚锐然性畏蚊蚊小螫之辄毙燕山録曰煮羊以防煮鼈以蚊物有相感鹈鹕饮水数升而不足鳣鲔入口若露而死猨有手可以捕鼠而制于鼠鹿有角可以触犬而制于犬

    　　续博物志卷三
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　续博物志卷四
    　　宋　李石　撰
    　　燧人四佐明由晓升级【政也】必育受税俗【赋也】成博受古诸【侯也】陨丘受延嬉【録也】
    　　伏牺六佐金提主化俗【民也】鸟明主建福【利也】视黙主灾恶【害也】纪通为中职【内也】仲起为海陆【土也】阳侯为江海【水也】
    　　黄帝七辅风后受金法天老受天箓五圣受道级知命受纠俗窥纪受变复地典受州络力墨受淮斥【力墨或作力牧】陶渊明云事见论语摘辅象
    　　孔长彦孔季彦兄弟聚徒数百故时人为之语曰鲁国孔氏好读经兄弟讲诵皆可聴学士来者有声名不过孔氏那得成 [26]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [26]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 燧人四佐 - Refers to the four assistants of the legendary figure Suiren, who is credited with the invention of fire.
    • 明由晓升级【政也】 - Means 'bright and clear, rising to a higher level of governance.'
    • 必育受税俗【赋也】 - Means 'necessarily nurtured, receiving taxes and customs [as a form of tribute].'
    • 成博受古诸【侯也】 - Means 'becoming broad and receiving ancient lords [as a form of tribute].'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 燧人 - Legendary figure credited with the invention of fire.
    • 孔长彦 - Brother of Kong Jiyuan, both were scholars who gathered many students.
    • 孔季彦 - Brother of Kong Changyan, both were scholars who gathered many students.
    • 鲁国 - Ancient state in China, now part of Shandong province.

    Proper Nouns

    • 燧人四佐 - Four assistants of Suiren.
    • 明由晓升级 - Bright and clear, rising to a higher level of governance.
    • 必育受税俗 - Necessarily nurtured, receiving taxes and customs.
    • 成博受古诸 - Becoming broad and receiving ancient lords.
    • 孔长彦 - Kong Changyan, scholar.
    • 孔季彦 - Kong Jiyuan, scholar.
    • 鲁国 - Lu state.

    The Chinese softshell turtle; frequent consumption can promote hair growth. The heart, liver, and head of the pufferfish are as toxic as a wild plant (the exact plant is unclear due to the non-standard character ). The larger crabs have the strength to fight a tiger; if not careful, their claws can cut and kill people. The horseshoe crab has a very sharp shell, but it is afraid of mosquitoes, and even a small sting can kill it. According to the Yan Mountain Record, to cook sheep to prevent cooking turtles with mosquitoes. Things have mutual influences. The pelican drinks several liters of water but still feels thirsty; if the sturgeon enters the mouth, it dies like dew. The monkey has hands to catch mice but is controlled by mice; the deer has horns to touch dogs but is controlled by dogs.

    Continuation of the Text

    续博物志卷三
    钦定四库全书
    续博物志卷四
    宋 李石 撰

    燧人四佐明由晓升级【政也】必育受税俗【赋也】成博受古诸【侯也】陨丘受延嬉【录也】

    伏牺六佐金提主化俗【民也】鸟明主建福【利也】视黙主灾恶【害也】纪通为中职【内也】仲起为海陆【土也】阳侯为江海【水也】

    黄帝七辅风后受金法天老受天箓五圣受道级知命受纠俗窥纪受变复地典受州络力墨受淮斥【力墨或作力牧】陶渊明云事见论语摘辅象

    孔长彦孔季彦兄弟聚徒数百故时人为之语曰鲁国孔氏好读经兄弟讲诵皆可聴学士来者有声名不过孔氏那得成

    The Four Assistants of Suiren, Bright and Clear, Rising to a Higher Level of Governance; Necessarily Nurtured, Receiving Taxes and Customs as a Form of Tribute; Becoming Broadly Informed, Receiving Ancient Lords as a Record; Descending to the Hill, Receiving Extended Play as a Record.

    The Six Assistants of Fuxi, Gold and Bronze, Leading the Transformation of Customs; Birds and Beasts, Building Fortune; Vision and Silence, Mastering Disasters and Evils; Records and Communication, Serving as Central Officials; Middle Rising, Serving as Sea and Land; Yang Hou, Serving as Rivers and Seas.

    The Seven Assistants of Huangdi, Wind and Rain, Receiving the Laws of Heaven; Ancient Elders, Receiving the Celestial Script; Five Sages, Receiving the Levels of the Way; Knowing Fate, Receiving the Correction of Customs; Observing Records, Receiving Changes; Revisiting the Earth, Receiving the Network of States; Force and Control, Receiving the Expulsion of the Huai; Tao Yuanming said, "This is mentioned in the Analects of Confucius and the Auxiliary Images."

    Brothers Kong Changyan and Kong Jiyan gathered hundreds of disciples, and at that time people said, "The Kong family of Lu likes to read the classics; their brothers' lectures and recitations are all worth listening to. Scholars who come to them gain fame, but they cannot surpass the Kong family; how could they achieve it?"

    Final Translation

    The Chinese softshell turtle; frequent consumption can promote hair growth. The heart, liver, and head of the pufferfish are as toxic as a wild plant (the exact plant is unclear due to the non-standard character ). The larger crabs have the strength to fight a tiger; if not careful, their claws can cut and kill people. The horseshoe crab has a very sharp shell, but it is afraid of mosquitoes, and even a small sting can kill it. According to the Yan Mountain Record, to cook sheep to prevent cooking turtles with mosquitoes. Things have mutual influences. The pelican drinks several liters of water but still feels thirsty; if the sturgeon enters the mouth, it dies like dew. The monkey has hands to catch mice but is controlled by mice; the deer has horns to touch dogs but is controlled by dogs.

    The Four Assistants of Suiren, Bright and Clear, Rising to a Higher Level of Governance; Necessarily Nurtured, Receiving Taxes and Customs as a Form of Tribute; Becoming Broadly Informed, Receiving Ancient Lords as a Record; Descending to the Hill, Receiving Extended Play as a Record.

    The Six Assistants of Fuxi, Gold and Bronze, Leading the Transformation of Customs; Birds and Beasts, Building Fortune; Vision and Silence, Mastering Disasters and Evils; Records and Communication, Serving as Central Officials; Middle Rising, Serving as Sea and Land; Yang Hou, Serving as Rivers and Seas.

    The Seven Assistants of Huangdi, Wind and Rain, Receiving the Laws of Heaven; Ancient Elders, Receiving the Celestial Script; Five Sages, Receiving the Levels of the Way; Knowing Fate, Receiving the Correction of Customs; Observing Records, Receiving Changes; Revisiting the Earth, Receiving the Network of States; Force and Control, Receiving the Expulsion of the Huai; Tao Yuanming said, "This is mentioned in the Analects of Confucius and the Auxiliary Images."

    Brothers Kong Changyan and Kong Jiyan gathered hundreds of disciples, and at that time people said, "The Kong family of Lu likes to read the classics; their brothers' lectures and recitations are all worth listening to. Scholars who come to them gain fame, but they cannot surpass the Kong family; how could they achieve it?" [26]

    孔子生鲤字伯鱼鲤生伋字子思伋生白字子上白生求字子家求生箕字子京箕生穿字子高穿生子顺相魏自叔梁纥至子顺九世徙居大梁
    　　毛虫之精曰麟羽虫之精曰鳯介虫之精曰龟鳞虫之精曰龙裸虫之精曰圣人
    　　刘向校勘以来子歆为七略大凢万三千二百六十九卷王莽焚烧之后王允而西者仅七十余乘道逺复弃其半荀勗分经史子集为四部大凢九千九百四十五卷西晋李兑以勗旧部校之在者三千一十四卷宋谢灵运造四部目凡四千五百八十二卷王俭造目録万五千七十四卷齐王亮造书目万八千一十卷梁任昉文徳殿所藏二万三千一百六卷元帝克平侯景文徳殿书归江陵凡十余万卷魏师入焚之宋武所四千卷后周书目八千增至万卷周武平齐仅至五千卷唐灭隋魏郑公尽图书载以船经砥柱没十之七八大凢八万六千九百六十六卷见在三万六千七百八卷开元中四部目録凡五万一千八百五十二卷 [27]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [27]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 孔子生鲤字伯鱼 - Biographical formula meaning 'Confucius gave birth to Qiu, who was named Boyu.'
    • 鲤生伋字子思 - Biographical formula meaning 'Qiu gave birth to Ji, who was named Zisi.'
    • 伋生白字子上 - 'Ji gave birth to Bai, who was named Zishang.'
    • 白生求字子家 - 'Bai gave birth to Qiu, who was named Zijia.'
    • 求生箕字子京 - 'Qiu gave birth to Ji, who was named Zijing.'
    • 箕生穿字子高 - 'Ji gave birth to Chuan, who was named Zigao.'
    • 穿生子顺相魏 - 'Chuan gave birth to Zishun, who served as chancellor in Wei.'
    • 自叔梁纥至子顺九世徙居大梁 - From Shuliang He to Zishun, nine generations later, they moved to Daliang.
    • 毛虫之精曰麟 - Classical formula meaning 'the essence of insects with fur is the Qilin.'
    • 羽虫之精曰凤 - 'The essence of feathered creatures is the phoenix.'
    • 介虫之精曰龟 - 'The essence of shelled creatures is the turtle.'
    • 鳞虫之精曰龙 - 'The essence of scaled creatures is the dragon.'
    • 裸虫之精曰圣人 - 'The essence of naked creatures is the sage.'
    • 刘向校勘以来子歆为七略 - Since Liu Xiang's collation, his son Liu Xin compiled the Seven Categories.
    • 大凡万三千二百六十九卷 - In total, there were 13,269 volumes.
    • 王莽焚烧之后王允遗而西者仅七十余乘 - After Wang Mang's burning, only about seventy carts were left behind by Wang Yun.
    • 道远复弃其半 - On the way, half of them were abandoned.
    • 荀勖分经史子集为四部 - Xun Xu divided the classics, histories, philosophies, and literature into four categories.
    • 大凡九千九百四十五卷 - In total, there were 9,945 volumes.
    • 西晋李兑以勖旧部校之在者三千一十四卷 - During the Western Jin, Li Dui checked Xun Xu's old classification and found 3,114 volumes remaining.
    • 宋谢灵运造四部目 - During the Song Dynasty, Xie Lingyun created the Four Categories Catalogue.
    • 凡四千五百八十二卷 - In total, there were 4,582 volumes.
    • 王俭造目录万五千七十四卷 - Wang Jian created the catalogue with 15,744 volumes.
    • 齐王亮造书目万八千一百十卷 - During the Qi Dynasty, Wang Liang created the book catalogue with 18,110 volumes.
    • 梁任昉文德殿所藏二万三千一百六卷 - During the Liang Dynasty, Ren Fang's collection at Wendu Hall had 23,106 volumes.
    • 元帝克平侯景遗文德殿书归江陵凡十余万卷 - Emperor Yuan conquered Hou Jing and returned the books from Wendu Hall to Jiangling, totaling over 100,000 volumes.
    • 魏师入焚之 - The Wei army burned them.
    • 宋武所遗四千卷 - Emperor Wu of Song left behind 4,000 volumes.
    • 后周书目八千增至万卷 - During the Later Zhou, the book catalogue increased from 8,000 to 10,000 volumes.
    • 周武平齐仅至五千卷 - Emperor Wu of Zhou, after conquering Qi, had only 5,000 volumes.
    • 唐灭隋魏郑公尽遗图书载以船经砥柱没十之七 - During the Tang Dynasty, after the fall of Sui, Wei Zheng collected all the books, loaded them onto ships, and they sank seven-tenths at the Dishi Rapids.
    • 八大凡八万六千九百六十六卷 - In total, there were 86,966 volumes.
    • 见在三万六千七百八卷 - Currently, there are 36,708 volumes.
    • 开元中四部目录凡五万一千八百五十二卷 - During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, the Four Categories Catalogue had 51,852 volumes.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 孔子 - Confucius, the great Chinese philosopher and teacher.
    • 叔梁纥 - The father of Confucius.
    • 子顺 - A descendant of Confucius who served as chancellor in the state of Wei.
    • 大梁 - A city in ancient China, known for its cultural and political significance.
    • 刘向 - A prominent Chinese scholar and historian during the Han Dynasty.
    • 刘歆 - The son of Liu Xiang, who compiled the Seven Categories.
    • 王莽 - A usurper who briefly established the Xin Dynasty, known for his reforms and destruction of books.
    • 王允 - A high-ranking official during the late Han Dynasty.
    • 荀勖 - A scholar and official during the Western Jin Dynasty.
    • 李兑 - A scholar during the Western Jin Dynasty.
    • 谢灵运 - A poet and scholar during the Song Dynasty.
    • 王俭 - A scholar and official during the Southern Qi Dynasty.
    • 王亮 - An official during the Qi Dynasty.
    • 任昉 - A scholar and official during the Liang Dynasty.
    • 元帝 - Emperor Yuan of Liang, a ruler during the Liang Dynasty.
    • 侯景 - A general who rebelled against the Liang Dynasty.
    • 魏师 - The army of the Northern Wei Dynasty.
    • 宋武 - Emperor Wu of Song, a ruler during the Southern Song Dynasty.
    • 后周 - The Later Zhou Dynasty, a short-lived dynasty in Chinese history.
    • 周武 - Emperor Wu of Zhou, a ruler during the Later Zhou Dynasty.
    • 唐灭隋 - The Tang Dynasty's conquest of the Sui Dynasty.
    • 魏郑公 - Wei Zheng, a prominent official and historian during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 开元 - The reign period of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, known for its prosperity and cultural achievements.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • This long sequence must be read as a series of biographical statements, each introducing a new generation and their name.
    • This parallel structure lists the essences of different types of creatures, each paired with a classical symbol or figure.
    • This sentence combines a historical event with a numerical count, requiring careful parsing.
    • This sentence describes a series of events, including burning, leaving, and abandoning, which must be read in sequence.
    • This sentence combines a historical action with a numerical count, similar to the previous example.
    • This sentence describes a verification process and a resulting count.
    • This sentence combines a historical action with a numerical count.
    • This sentence combines a historical action with a numerical count.
    • This sentence combines a historical action with a numerical count.
    • This sentence combines a historical location with a numerical count.
    • This sentence combines a historical event with a numerical count.
    • This sentence describes a single event.
    • This sentence combines a historical figure with a numerical count.
    • This sentence describes a change in the number of books.
    • This sentence describes a reduction in the number of books.
    • This sentence describes a series of events, including collecting, loading, and sinking, which must be read in sequence.
    • This sentence combines a historical event with a numerical count.

    Confucius gave birth to Qiu, who was named Boyu; Qiu gave birth to Ji, who was named Zisi; Ji gave birth to Bai, who was named Zishang; Bai gave birth to Qiu, who was named Zijia; Qiu gave birth to Ji, who was named Zijing; Ji gave birth to Chuan, who was named Zigao; Chuan gave birth to Zishun, who served as chancellor in Wei. From Shuliang He to Zishun, nine generations later, they moved to Daliang.

    The essence of insects with fur is the Qilin; the essence of feathered creatures is the phoenix; the essence of shelled creatures is the turtle; the essence of scaled creatures is the dragon; the essence of naked creatures is the sage.

    Since Liu Xiang's collation, his son Liu Xin compiled the Seven Categories, totaling 13,269 volumes. After Wang Mang's burning, only about seventy carts were left behind by Wang Yun; on the way, half of them were abandoned. Xun Xu divided the classics, histories, philosophies, and literature into four categories, totaling 9,945 volumes. During the Western Jin, Li Dui checked Xun Xu's old classification and found 3,114 volumes remaining. During the Song Dynasty, Xie Lingyun created the Four Categories Catalogue, totaling 4,582 volumes. Wang Jian created the catalogue with 15,744 volumes. During the Qi Dynasty, Wang Liang created the book catalogue with 18,110 volumes. During the Liang Dynasty, Ren Fang's collection at Wendu Hall had 23,106 volumes. Emperor Yuan conquered Hou Jing and returned the books from Wendu Hall to Jiangling, totaling over 100,000 volumes. The Wei army burned them. Emperor Wu of Song left behind 4,000 volumes. During the Later Zhou, the book catalogue increased from 8,000 to 10,000 volumes. Emperor Wu of Zhou, after conquering Qi, had only 5,000 volumes. During the Tang Dynasty, after the fall of Sui, Wei Zheng collected all the books, loaded them onto ships, and they sank seven-tenths at the Dishi Rapids; in total, there were 86,966 volumes; currently, there are 36,708 volumes. During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, the Four Categories Catalogue had 51,852 volumes. [27]

    自秦同书文丞相斯作仓颉篇中车府令高作爰厯篇太史敬作博学篇文园令相如作凢将篇黄门令游作急就篇皆书文之林苑欲识字者不可不知惜哉凡将以上不可复见急就章存焉西京文气亦可见矣顔籕注此书尝得皇象钟繇卫夫人王防稽等篇世所唯张芝索靖二家为真章草书芝有鳯爵鸿鹄等数行靖所书乃三之二自母繜而下阙七百五十字 [28]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [28]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 皆书文之林苑欲识字者不可不知 - Classical statement emphasizing the importance of these texts for learning to read.
    • 惜哉凡将以上不可复见急就章存焉 - Classical lament about the loss of earlier texts, with 'Jiju章' still surviving.
    • 西京文气亦可见矣 - Classical reference to the literary style of the Western Han capital, Chang'an.
    • 顔籕注此书尝得皇象钟繇卫夫人王防稽等篇世所传唯张芝索靖二家为真章草书 - Classical reference to the commentaries and surviving works of various calligraphers.
    • 芝有凤爵鸿鹄等数行靖所书乃三之二 - Classical reference to specific works by Zhang Zhi and Suo Jing.
    • 自母繜而下阙七百五十字 - Classical reference to the missing text in the 'Jiju篇' starting from 'Mu Zuan'.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 秦同书文丞相斯 - Refers to Li Si, the Chancellor of Qin, who created the 'Cangjie篇'.
    • 中车府令高 - Refers to Zhao Gao, the Director of the Imperial Carriage, who created the 'Yuanli篇'.
    • 太史敬 - Refers to Sima Qian, the Grand Historian, who created the 'Boxue篇'.
    • 文园令相如 - Refers to Sima Xiangru, the Prefect of the Imperial Garden, who created the 'Fanjiang篇'.
    • 黄门令游 - Refers to Yang Xiong, the Palace Attendant, who created the 'Jiju篇'.
    • 西京 - Refers to Chang'an, the capital of the Western Han dynasty.
    • 顔籕 - Refers to Yan You, a calligrapher who commented on the 'Jiju篇'.
    • 皇象钟繇卫夫人王防稽 - Refers to various calligraphers whose works are mentioned.
    • 张芝索靖 - Refers to Zhang Zhi and Suo Jing, two famous calligraphers.
    • 凤爵鸿鹄 - Refers to specific works by Zhang Zhi.
    • 母繜 - A specific point in the 'Jiju篇' where the text is missing.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • This long sequence must be read as a single catalogue of texts and their creators.
    • This clause states that all these texts are essential for learning to read.
    • This clause laments the loss of earlier texts but notes that the 'Jiju篇' still exists.
    • This clause refers to the literary style of the Western Han capital.
    • This clause discusses the commentaries and surviving works of various calligraphers.
    • This clause refers to specific works by Zhang Zhi and Suo Jing.
    • This clause refers to the missing text in the 'Jiju篇' starting from 'Mu Zuan'.

    Proper Nouns

    • 秦同书文丞相斯 - Li Si, the Chancellor of Qin.
    • 中车府令高 - Zhao Gao, the Director of the Imperial Carriage.
    • 太史敬 - Sima Qian, the Grand Historian.
    • 文园令相如 - Sima Xiangru, the Prefect of the Imperial Garden.
    • 黄门令游 - Yang Xiong, the Palace Attendant.
    • 西京 - Chang'an, the capital of the Western Han dynasty.
    • 顔籕 - Yan You, a calligrapher.
    • 皇象钟繇卫夫人王防稽 - Various calligraphers.
    • 张芝索靖 - Zhang Zhi and Suo Jing, famous calligraphers.
    • 凤爵鸿鹄 - Specific works by Zhang Zhi.
    • 母繜 - A specific point in the 'Jiju篇' where the text is missing.

    From the Qin dynasty, the Chancellor of Literature, Li Si, created the "Cangjie篇"; the Director of the Imperial Carriage, Zhao Gao, created the "Yuanli篇"; the Grand Historian, Sima Qian, created the "Boxue篇"; the Prefect of the Imperial Garden, Sima Xiangru, created the "Fanjiang篇"; and the Palace Attendant, Yang Xiong, created the "Jiju篇." All these texts are essential for learning to read. Alas, the "Fanjiang篇" and earlier texts are no longer extant, but the "Jiju篇" still survives. The literary style of the Western Han capital, Chang'an, can also be seen in these works. Yan You, who commented on this book, once obtained the "Yuanli篇" by Huang Xiang, the "Yuanli篇" by Zhong Yao, and the "Yuanli篇" by Wei Shuo, among others. However, only the works of Zhang Zhi and Suo Jing are considered true examples of "Zhangcao" script. Zhang Zhi had several lines titled "Fengjue" and "Honghu," while Suo Jing's works amounted to two-thirds of the "Yuanli篇." Starting from "Mu Zuan," the text is missing for seven hundred and fifty characters. [28]

    前辈云于字必字无草书
    　　宋袁淑授庐山公九锡文荷槖致餐尔之功音随时兴尔之智用必应时尔之鸣长颊广额修尾后垂尔之形是用遣中大夫闾丘骡加尔使衔勒大鸿胪斑脚大将军以扬州之庐江吉之庐陵吴之桐庐封尔为庐山公大兰王九锡文大亥十年乙亥朔十三日丁亥燕伯使者豪豨册命大兰王曰咨资豢养于人主虽无爵而有秩君之纯封国豕氏君之美白蹢彰于周诗渉波应乎隆象君之徳长驱直突阵无全锋君之勇
    　　宣父因祷尼山得名丘字仲尼子名鲤字伯鱼史防亦字子鱼冉耕字伯牛如鬭谷于菟之黑臀杵臼之甚多
    　　宋齐丘乃字超回不自量如此孟轲庄周其字不传或云轲字子舆周字子休皆后人以意取之耳 [29]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [29]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 前辈云 - Colloquial phrase meaning 'elderly scholars say' or 'according to the ancients.'
    • 于字必字无草书 - Ambiguous phrase; could mean 'the character '于' must always be written in regular script and never in cursive script' or 'the character '于' is always written in regular script and never in cursive script.'
    • 荷槖致餐尔之功音随时兴尔之智用必应时尔之鸣长颊广额修尾后垂尔之形是用遣中大夫闾丘骡加尔使衔勒大鸿胪斑脚大将军以扬州之庐江吉之庐陵吴之桐庐封尔为庐山公大兰王九锡文大亥十年乙亥朔十三日丁亥燕伯使者豪豨册命大兰王曰咨资豢养于人主虽无爵而有秩君之纯封国豕氏君之美白蹢彰于周诗渉波应乎隆象君之德长驱直突阵无全锋君之勇 - Highly complex and elliptical passage; appears to be a formal investiture text but is difficult to parse due to lack of clear grammatical markers and use of archaic and specialized vocabulary.
    • 宋齐丘乃字超回不自量如此孟轲庄周其字不传或云轲字子舆周字子休皆后人以意取之耳 - Complex phrase; discusses naming conventions of historical figures and suggests some names were assigned posthumously or by later scholars.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 前辈 - Refers to elderly scholars or ancient sages.
    • 于字 - The character '于' (yú), a common character in Chinese.
    • 草书 - Cursive script, one of the traditional styles of Chinese calligraphy.
    • 中大夫闾丘骡 - Title or name of a high-ranking official.
    • 大鸿胪斑脚大将军 - Title or name of a high-ranking military official.
    • 扬州之庐江吉之庐陵吴之桐庐 - Geographical locations in ancient China.
    • 庐山公大兰王 - Titles of a noble or ruler.
    • 九锡文 - Formal investiture document granting nine special honors.
    • 大亥十年乙亥朔十三日丁亥 - Date in the ancient Chinese calendar.
    • 燕伯使者豪豨 - Title or name of a messenger or envoy.
    • 宋齐丘 - Historical figure, likely a scholar or official.
    • 孟轲 - Mencius, a famous Confucian philosopher.
    • 庄周 - Zhuangzi, a famous Daoist philosopher.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 于字必字无草书 - Ambiguous phrase; could be interpreted in multiple ways.
    • 荷槖致餐尔之功音随时兴尔之智用必应时尔之鸣长颊广额修尾后垂尔之形是用遣中大夫闾丘骡加尔使衔勒大鸿胪斑脚大将军以扬州之庐江吉之庐陵吴之桐庐封尔为庐山公大兰王九锡文大亥十年乙亥朔十三日丁亥燕伯使者豪豨册命大兰王曰咨资豢养于人主虽无爵而有秩君之纯封国豕氏君之美白蹢彰于周诗渉波应乎隆象君之德长驱直突阵无全锋君之勇 - Highly elliptical and lacks clear grammatical markers; difficult to parse.
    • 宋齐丘乃字超回不自量如此孟轲庄周其字不传或云轲字子舆周字子休皆后人以意取之耳 - Complex and lacks clear grammatical markers; discusses naming conventions of historical figures.

    Proper Nouns

    • 于字 - The character '于' (yú).
    • 中大夫闾丘骡 - Title or name of a high-ranking official.
    • 大鸿胪斑脚大将军 - Title or name of a high-ranking military official.
    • 扬州之庐江吉之庐陵吴之桐庐 - Geographical locations in ancient China.
    • 庐山公大兰王 - Titles of a noble or ruler.
    • 九锡文 - Formal investiture document granting nine special honors.
    • 大亥十年乙亥朔十三日丁亥 - Date in the ancient Chinese calendar.
    • 燕伯使者豪豨 - Title or name of a messenger or envoy.
    • 宋齐丘 - Historical figure, likely a scholar or official.
    • 孟轲 - Mencius, a famous Confucian philosopher.
    • 庄周 - Zhuangzi, a famous Daoist philosopher.

    Elderly scholars say that the character "于" must always be written in regular script and never in cursive script.

    In the ninth-year investiture text granted by Song Yuan Shu to the Lord of Mount Lu, it states: "Your merit in carrying the sack and providing food is commensurate with the times; your wisdom is timely, and your voice resounds with a long jaw and broad forehead, a long tail trailing behind, and a fine form. Therefore, I dispatch the Grand Doctor Lu Qiule to bestow upon you the title of Lord of Mount Lu and the Great Lan King, granting you nine special honors. In the tenth year of Da Hai, on the thirteenth day of the first month, the envoy from Yan, Hao Xi, was sent to invest the Great Lan King, saying: 'Your nourishment comes from the ruler, though you have no rank, you have a position of honor. Your purity is like a pure land, and your beauty is like a white hoof, which is praised in the Zhou poems. You are like a wave that responds to the grand image, your virtue is like a long drive through a sudden attack, and your courage is unmatched in battle.'

    Confucius, due to his prayers at Mount Ni, was given the name Qiu and the courtesy name Zhongni. His son, named Li, had the courtesy name Boyu. Shi Fang also had the courtesy name Ziyu. Ran Geng had the courtesy name Boni. Like Dou Gu Yu Tu, who was known as He Di Chu Jiu, there were many such cases.

    Song Qi Qiu was given the courtesy name Chaohui, which was not self-assessed like Mencius and Zhuangzi. Their courtesy names are not transmitted, and it is said that Mencius's courtesy name was Ziyu and Zhuangzi's was Zihou, both of which were chosen by later scholars.

    Please let me know if you need any further assistance or if there are any specific sections you would like to focus on. [29]

    东汉无复名或云王莽之禁沿袭所致
    　　不可为叵如是为尔而已为耳之乎为诸西域二合之音切字之原也
    　　绛州碧落观天尊像高丈余唐龙朔中刺史李谌为母氏太妃追荐所造上有篆文老黄冠云李使君高宗之子有二道士求篆此文闭户篆毕化为二白鸽飞去余一及字欠一画李阳冰于此学篆凡十二年终不得其妙
    　　明皇获二十七仙玉像于宁州罗川县金华洞奇伟异状各各不同内有心星一坐甚美手板复有明皇御名其地属王可思庄其人已百岁后置真宁观改罗州为真宁县今有玉象图传于世
    　　乾元末肃宗获十三寳于楚州改元寳应
    　　天寳中大宛进汗血马六匹一曰红叱拨二曰紫叱拨三曰青叱拨四曰黄叱拨五曰丁香叱拨六曰桃花叱拨上乃制名曰红【阙】辇曰紫玉辇曰平山辇曰凌云辇曰飞香辇曰百花辇后幸蜀以平山凌云为谶西京杂记汉文自代还有良马九匹皆天下骏也一浮云二赤电三絶羣四逸骠五紫燕六绿骊七龙子八驎驹九絶尘号九逸 [30]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [30]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 无复名 - Ambiguous phrase meaning 'no longer has a name' or 'does not have a name,' suggesting a loss of identity or significance.
    • 沿袭所致 - Means 'due to continued practice or tradition,' indicating that something has been maintained over time.
    • 不可为叵 - Unclear phrase, possibly meaning 'cannot be hidden' or 'cannot be doubted,' but the exact meaning is uncertain.
    • 如是为尔而已为耳之乎为诸 - Highly elliptical and difficult to interpret, possibly a series of rhetorical questions or exclamations.
    • 西域二合之音切字之原也 - Refers to the origin of the phonetic system in the Western Regions, where two consonants or vowels are combined to form a syllable.
    • 绛州碧落观天尊像高丈余 - Describes a statue of the Heavenly Lord at the Bixue Temple in Jizhou, which is over ten feet tall.
    • 唐龙朔中刺史李谌为母氏太妃追荐所造 - Indicates that the statue was created by the prefect Li Chen during the Longshuo period of the Tang dynasty in honor of his mother, the dowager.
    • 上有篆文老黄冠云李使君高宗之子 - The statue bears an inscription that reads, 'The old scholar Li, the son of Emperor Gaozong.'
    • 有二道士求篆此文闭户篆毕化为二白鸽飞去 - Two Taoist priests asked to inscribe this text, closed the door, completed the inscription, and then transformed into two white pigeons and flew away.
    • 余一及字欠一画 - One character is missing a stroke.
    • 李阳冰于此学篆凡十二年终不得其妙 - Li Yangbing studied calligraphy here for twelve years but never mastered it.
    • 明皇获二十七仙玉像于宁州罗川县金华洞 - The Ming Emperor obtained twenty-seven jade statues of immortals from the Jinhua Cave in Luochuan County, Ningzhou.
    • 奇伟异状各各不同 - Each statue has a unique and extraordinary appearance.
    • 内有心星一坐甚美手板复有明皇御名 - One of the statues, the Heart Star, is particularly beautiful, and the handboard bears the name of the Ming Emperor.
    • 其地属王可思庄其人已百岁 - The place belonged to Wang Kesi's estate, and the person was already a hundred years old.
    • 后置真宁观改罗州为真宁县 - Later, a temple called Zhenning Temple was built, and the state of Luo was renamed Zhenning County.
    • 今有玉象图传于世 - Today, there is a jade elephant diagram流传于世.
    • 乾元末肃宗获十三寳于楚州改元寳应 - At the end of the Qianyuan period, Emperor Suzong obtained thirteen treasures from Chu Prefecture and changed the era name to Baoying.
    • 天寳中大宛进汗血马六匹 - During the Tianbao period, the Great Yueh tribe presented six horses with flowing blood.
    • 一曰红叱拨二曰紫叱拨三曰青叱拨四曰黄叱拨五曰丁香叱拨六曰桃花叱拨 - The names of the horses are Red Chibao, Purple Chibao, Blue Chibao, Yellow Chibao, Clove Chibao, and Peach Blossom Chibao.
    • 上乃制名曰红【阙】辇曰紫玉辇曰平山辇曰凌云辇曰飞香辇曰百花辇 - The emperor named them Red Chariot, Purple Jade Chariot, Level Mountain Chariot, Cloud Piercing Chariot, Flying Fragrance Chariot, and Hundred Flowers Chariot.
    • 后幸蜀以平山凌云为谶 - Later, when the emperor visited Shu, the Level Mountain and Cloud Piercing Chariots were seen as omens.
    • 西京杂记汉文自代还有良马九匹 - According to the *Miscellaneous Records of the Western Capital*, Emperor Wen of Han had nine excellent horses when he returned from Dai.
    • 皆天下骏也 - All were the best horses in the world.
    • 一浮云二赤电三絶羣四逸骠五紫燕六绿骊七龙子八驎驹九絶尘 - The names of the horses are Cloud, Red Lightning, Exceptional, Swift Charger, Purple Swallow, Green Swallow, Dragon Child, Unicorn Foal, and Dust.
    • 号九逸 - They were called the Nine Elusive Horses.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 东汉无复名或云王莽之禁沿袭所致 - Refers to the Eastern Han dynasty, where something no longer has a name, possibly due to the continued influence of Wang Mang's policies.
    • 王莽 - A usurper who briefly ruled China during the Xin dynasty.
    • 唐龙朔中 - The Longshuo period of the Tang dynasty (661-663 AD).
    • 李谌 - A prefect during the Tang dynasty.
    • 李阳冰 - A famous calligrapher of the Tang dynasty.
    • 明皇 - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, also known as Ming Huang.
    • 乾元末 - The end of the Qianyuan period (758-760 AD) of the Tang dynasty.
    • 肃宗 - Emperor Suzong of Tang.
    • 天寳中 - The Tianbao period (742-756 AD) of the Tang dynasty.
    • 大宛 - An ancient kingdom in Central Asia known for its horses.
    • 汗血马 - Horses with flowing blood, a legendary breed.
    • 汉文 - Emperor Wen of Han, a ruler of the Western Han dynasty.
    • 代 - A region in northern China.
    • 西京杂记 - A historical text compiled during the Eastern Jin dynasty, containing various anecdotes and stories.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 东汉无复名或云王莽之禁沿袭所致 - This sentence is ambiguous and could be interpreted as 'The Eastern Han dynasty no longer has a name, or it is said that this is due to the continued influence of Wang Mang's policies.'
    • 不可为叵如是为尔而已为耳之乎为诸 - This phrase is highly elliptical and difficult to interpret. It could be a series of rhetorical questions or exclamations, but the exact meaning is unclear.
    • 西域二合之音切字之原也 - This phrase is a technical term referring to the origin of the phonetic system in the Western Regions, where two consonants or vowels are combined to form a syllable.
    • 绛州碧落观天尊像高丈余唐龙朔中刺史李谌为母氏太妃追荐所造 - This sentence is a long, complex statement that must be read as a single unit, describing the creation of a statue.
    • 上有篆文老黄冠云李使君高宗之子 - This phrase is a description of the inscription on the statue.
    • 有二道士求篆此文闭户篆毕化为二白鸽飞去 - This sentence describes a magical event involving two Taoist priests.
    • 余一及字欠一画 - This phrase is a technical description of a missing stroke in a character.
    • 李阳冰于此学篆凡十二年终不得其妙 - This sentence describes Li Yangbing's experience with calligraphy.
    • 明皇获二十七仙玉像于宁州罗川县金华洞 - This sentence describes the acquisition of twenty-seven jade statues of immortals.
    • 奇伟异状各各不同 - This phrase describes the unique and extraordinary appearances of the statues.
    • 内有心星一坐甚美手板复有明皇御名 - This phrase describes a specific statue and its inscription.
    • 其地属王可思庄其人已百岁 - This phrase provides information about the location and the person associated with it.
    • 后置真宁观改罗州为真宁县 - This sentence describes a later event involving the renaming of a place.
    • 今有玉象图传于世 - This phrase indicates the existence of a jade elephant diagram.
    • 乾元末肃宗获十三寳于楚州改元寳应 - This sentence describes the acquisition of thirteen treasures and the change in the era name.
    • 天寳中大宛进汗血马六匹 - This sentence describes the presentation of six horses with flowing blood.
    • 一曰红叱拨二曰紫叱拨三曰青叱拨四曰黄叱拨五曰丁香叱拨六曰桃花叱拨 - This phrase lists the names of the horses.
    • 上乃制名曰红【阙】辇曰紫玉辇曰平山辇曰凌云辇曰飞香辇曰百花辇 - This sentence describes the emperor's naming of the horses.
    • 后幸蜀以平山凌云为谶 - This sentence describes the emperor's visit to Shu and the omens associated with the Level Mountain and Cloud Piercing Chariots.
    • 西京杂记汉文自代还有良马九匹 - This sentence refers to Emperor Wen of Han having nine excellent horses when he returned from Dai.
    • 皆天下骏也 - This phrase indicates that all the horses were the best in the world.
    • 一浮云二赤电三絶羣四逸骠五紫燕六绿骊七龙子八驎驹九絶尘 - This phrase lists the names of the horses.
    • 号九逸 - This phrase indicates that the horses were called the Nine Elusive Horses.

    The Eastern Han dynasty no longer has a name, or it is said that this is due to the continued influence of Wang Mang's policies. It cannot be hidden, for this is the way it is, and that is all there is to it, or perhaps it is a matter of the Western Regions' phonetic system, where two consonants or vowels are combined to form a syllable, which is the origin of the characters.

    At the Bixue Temple in Jizhou, there is a statue of the Heavenly Lord that is over ten feet tall. During the Longshuo period of the Tang dynasty, the prefect Li Chen created it in honor of his mother, the dowager. The statue bears an inscription that reads, "The old scholar Li, the son of Emperor Gaozong." Two Taoist priests asked to inscribe this text, closed the door, completed the inscription, and then transformed into two white pigeons and flew away. One character is missing a stroke. Li Yangbing studied calligraphy here for twelve years but never mastered it.

    Emperor Xuanzong of Tang obtained twenty-seven jade statues of immortals from the Jinhua Cave in Luochuan County, Ningzhou. Each statue has a unique and extraordinary appearance. One of the statues, the Heart Star, is particularly beautiful, and the handboard bears the name of the Ming Emperor. The place belonged to Wang Kesi's estate, and the person was already a hundred years old. Later, a temple called Zhenning Temple was built, and the state of Luo was renamed Zhenning County. Today, there is a jade elephant diagram流传于世.

    At the end of the Qianyuan period, Emperor Suzong obtained thirteen treasures from Chu Prefecture and changed the era name to Baoying.

    During the Tianbao period, the Great Yueh tribe presented six horses with flowing blood. The names of the horses are Red Chibao, Purple Chibao, Blue Chibao, Yellow Chibao, Clove Chibao, and Peach Blossom Chibao. The emperor named them Red Chariot, Purple Jade Chariot, Level Mountain Chariot, Cloud Piercing Chariot, Flying Fragrance Chariot, and Hundred Flowers Chariot. Later, when the emperor visited Shu, the Level Mountain and Cloud Piercing Chariots were seen as omens.

    According to the Miscellaneous Records of the Western Capital, Emperor Wen of Han had nine excellent horses when he returned from Dai. All were the best horses in the world. The names of the horses are Cloud, Red Lightning, Exceptional, Swift Charger, Purple Swallow, Green Swallow, Dragon Child, Unicorn Foal, and Dust. They were called the Nine Elusive Horses. [30]

    易林曰防为鸡鸡鸣节时家乐无忧
    　　皇览曰尤冡在东郡寿张县阚鄊城中高七尺常十月祠之有赤气出如绛名为尤旗
    　　谷城西流泉西北入清水域西北三里有项羽冡石碣犹存题云项王之墓皇览谓去县十五里谬也彭城谷阳城西南又有项羽水经谓为非鲁为楚守汉王以羽首示鲁乃降遂以鲁公礼葬羽于谷城以谷阳城谷城二冡得非身首异处乎皇览曰山阳钜泽县有肩髀冡黄帝杀尤身首异处故别葬亦二冡也
    　　济水东北泺水出焉泺水出厯城旧县水涌若轮齐侯防于泺是也有舜妃庙对山有舜祠舜井舜耕歴山亦云在此蒲坂县西有歴观舜所耕歴山有舜井沩汭二水山上有舜庙周处风土记曰舜葬上虞耕处即始宁剡二县山下多柞木吴越之间名柞为枥故曰歴山二说一以泺为歴一以枥为歴未知孰是皆郦道元说
    　　牧靡县因草得名生牧靡可以解毒鸟多误食乌喙口中毒必急飞往牧靡山啄牧靡以解毒 [31]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [31]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 防为鸡鸡鸣节时家乐无忧 - Cryptic phrase; 'guarding chickens at the time when they crow, the family is happy and without worries.'
    • 黄帝杀防尤身首异处 - Mythological event where Yellow Emperor kills Fuxi, and their bodies are separated.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 易林 - Text related to the *Yijing* (Book of Changes), often used for divination.
    • 黄帝 - Yellow Emperor, a legendary figure in Chinese mythology, considered the first ruler of China.
    • 防尤 - Fuxi, a legendary figure in Chinese mythology, often associated with the creation of the *Yijing*.
    • 项羽 - Xiang Yu, a famous general and rival of Liu Bang during the Chu-Han Contention.
    • 乌喙 - A toxic plant, possibly referring to a specific species of poisonous plant.
    • 牧靡 - A type of grass, possibly with medicinal properties.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 防为鸡鸡鸣节时家乐无忧 - Elliptical phrase; 'Guarding chickens at the time when they crow, the family is happy and without worries.'
    • 黄帝杀防尤身首异处 - Describes a mythological event where Yellow Emperor kills Fuxi, and their bodies are separated. The exact sequence of events and implications are not fully clear.
    • 鸟多误食乌喙口中毒必急飞往牧靡山啄牧靡以解毒 - Complex sentence describing birds that are poisoned by eating toxic plants and instinctively seek out a specific grass to counteract the poison.

    Proper Nouns

    • 易林 - Text related to the *Yijing* (Book of Changes).
    • 黄帝 - Yellow Emperor.
    • 防尤 - Fuxi.
    • 项羽 - Xiang Yu.
    • 乌喙 - A toxic plant.
    • 牧靡 - A type of grass.
    • 东郡寿张县阚鄊城中 - Geographic location.
    • 山阳钜泽县 - Another geographic location.

    The Yijing (Book of Changes) says: "Guarding chickens at the time when they crow, the family is happy and without worries."

    The Imperial Records state: The tomb of Fuxi is located in the town of Kanzhou within the county of Shouzhang in Dongjun. It stands seven chi high and is regularly honored in the tenth month with a red vapor rising like a crimson flag, known as the "Fuxi Flag."

    West of Gucheng flows the Clear Water River, which enters the northwest into the Clear Water Domain. Three li northwest of this is the tomb of Xiang Yu, with a stone stele still standing, inscribed with "Tomb of King Xiang." The Imperial Records mention it is fifteen li from the county, which is incorrect. Southwest of Gucheng, near the city of Guyang, there is another tomb of Xiang Yu. The Waterways Classic states it is not the same as the one in Gucheng, as it was where Xiang Yu defended Chu and Han Wang showed his head to Lu, causing Lu to surrender. Thus, Xiang Yu was buried in Gucheng with the rites of a Marquis of Lu. The two tombs in Gucheng and Guyang suggest that his body and head were buried separately.

    The Imperial Records also state: In the county of Juzi in Shanyang, there is a tomb of the shoulder and thigh of Fuxi. After Yellow Emperor killed Fuxi, their bodies were separated, so they were buried in two different tombs.

    The Jishui River flows northeast, and the Lü River emerges from it. The Lü River originates from the old county of Licheng, where water surges up like a wheel. It is said that Duke Qi once met here. There is a temple dedicated to the consort of Emperor Shun, a shrine to Emperor Shun, a well known as Shun's Well, and a mountain called Shun's Mountain, where Emperor Shun is said to have cultivated land. It is also said to be in the western part of Puban County, with a pavilion called Lüguan. On Shun's Mountain, there are two streams named Wei and Re, and a shrine to Emperor Shun on the mountain. The Records of Local Customs by Zhou Chu state: Emperor Shun was buried in Shangyu, and his cultivation site is in the counties of Shin and Shan, where there are many oak trees. In Wu and Yue, oaks are called li. Hence, it is called Lü Mountain. There are two interpretations: one that Lü is derived from the Lü River, and another that it is derived from the li tree. Neither is certain, according to Li Daoyuan.

    The county of Mumo is named after a type of grass. The grass can be used to counteract poison. Birds often mistakenly eat toxic plants, leading to poisoning. Birds that are poisoned will quickly fly to the Mumo Mountain to eat the grass and counteract the poison. [31]

    马出越睒之西若羔纽莎縻之粉米饮之七岁可御日驰数百里
    　　东观汉记王阜为益州太守神马四出滇河中魏末有神马夜过官牧迹大如斗入河水中
    　　真诰金陵古名之为伏龙之地句曲山秦时为句金之坛以积金山得名山生黄金汉灵帝诏采句曲之金以充武库孙权遣宿卫人采金屯伏龙之地因改名金陵陶景云大茅山尚有数坑名金井山之近东诸处碎石往往皆有金砂真诰又云水色白都不学道饮此水亦令人寿亦金津液之所溉耶
    　　冈山有穴通大句曲山有石脑在方山下石脑故如石但小斑色而软形状圆小如甑色似钟乳李整昔有风疾先多房事服此乃愈
    　　豫章城南门曰松阳门门内有樟高七丈五尺大二十五围垂隂数亩汉官仪曰豫章郡树生庭中因以为名
    　　刘歆云湖汉等九水入彭蠡因言九江
    　　沫水出广柔徼外有石纽乡禹所生也
    　　沫水自蒙山至南安溷崖水脉漂疾歴代为患蜀守李氷发卒凿平溷崖河神赑怒冰操刀入水与神鬬遂平溷崖 [32]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [32]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 若羔紐莎縻之粉米飲之 - Metaphorical description of the horse's diet, likely referring to a special or luxurious feed.
    • 河神赑怒 - Mythological event where the river god was angry, leading to a confrontation with Li Bing.
    • 河神赑怒冰操刀入水与神鬬遂平溷崖 - Detailed mythological event where Li Bing fought the river god and leveled the Hunyan Cliff.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 神馬 - Refers to a divine or mythical horse, often associated with supernatural abilities.
    • 滇河 - The Dian River, a historical river in Yunnan, known for its significance in ancient Chinese history.
    • 魏末 - Refers to the end of the Wei dynasty, a period in Chinese history.
    • 金陵 - An ancient name for Nanjing, a city with significant historical and cultural importance.
    • 句曲山 - A mountain in Jiangsu, known for its ritual significance and natural resources.
    • 豫章 - An ancient name for Nanchang, a city in Jiangxi, known for its historical and cultural significance.
    • 彭蠡 - Another name for Poyang Lake, one of the largest freshwater lakes in China.
    • 沫水 - A river in Sichuan, known for its turbulent waters and historical significance.
    • 李氷 - Li Bing, a famous Chinese hydraulic engineer from the Warring States period, known for his work on the Dujiangyan Irrigation System.

    Proper Nouns

    • 越睒 - A place name, likely referring to a region in ancient China.
    • 滇河 - The Dian River.
    • 魏末 - The end of the Wei dynasty.
    • 金陵 - An ancient name for Nanjing.
    • 句曲山 - A mountain in Jiangsu.
    • 豫章 - An ancient name for Nanchang.
    • 彭蠡 - Another name for Poyang Lake.
    • 沫水 - A river in Sichuan.
    • 李氷 - Li Bing, a famous Chinese hydraulic engineer.

    Horses from the west of Yue Shan, if fed a diet of fine grains, can be ridden by the age of seven and travel hundreds of miles in a day.

    During the reign of Emperor Ling of Han, Wang Fu, the governor of Yizhou, reported that divine horses appeared in four places in the Dian River. At the end of the Wei dynasty, a divine horse passed by the official pasture at night, leaving footprints as large as a斗, and entered the river.

    The Zhengao states that Nanjing, an ancient name for the city, was known as the "Place Where the Dragon is Hidden." The Guiqu Mountain, during the Qin dynasty, was a ritual altar called the "Mountain of Accumulated Gold," named for its gold deposits. The mountain produces gold, and during the reign of Emperor Ling of Han, an edict was issued to extract gold from Guiqu Mountain to fill the armory. Sun Quan sent guards to extract gold and camp at the place where the dragon is hidden, which was then renamed Nanjing. Tao Hongjing's Dai Damao Mountain still has several pits named "Jinjing," and in the eastern parts of the mountain, there are often traces of gold ore in the small stones. The Zhengao also states that the water there is white, and drinking it without learning the Dao can also prolong one's life, as it is nourished by the golden elixir.

    Gang Mountain has a cave that connects to the large Guiqu Mountain, and there is a type of mineral called "shibao" at the base of Fang Mountain. This mineral resembles stone but is slightly spotted and soft, with a round shape as small as a steamer, and a color similar to that of stalactites. Li Zheng, a man from the past, suffered from wind disease and had engaged in excessive sexual activity. After taking this mineral, he was cured.

    The southern gate of Yuzhang City is called Songyang Gate, and inside the gate is a樟 tree that is seven zhang and five chi tall, with a circumference of twenty-five chi. Its branches and leaves cover several acres. The Han Official Rites states that the tree grew in the courtyard of Yuzhang County, and the city was named after it.

    Liu Xin stated that the nine rivers, including the Lake Han, flow into Poyang Lake, and this is why the region is called "Jiujiang."

    The Mo River originates beyond the borders of Guangrou and has the village of Shiniu, where Yu the Great was born.

    The Mo River flows from Meng Mountain to Nan'an and the Hunyan Cliff, where the water is turbulent and has been a problem for generations. The governor of Shu, Li Bing, ordered the construction of a canal to level the Hunyan Cliff. The river god was angry, and Li Bing took a knife and entered the water, fighting the god, and eventually leveled the cliff. [32]

    续博物志卷四
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　续博物志卷五
    　　宋　李石　撰
    　　女娲之功烈上际九天下契黄垆名声被后世光辉重万物乘雷车服应龙青虬席萝图震黄璐援絶瑞前白螭后奔虵
    　　项浚始学篇曰上古有天皇十三头洞防记曰一姓十三人徐整厯纪曰岁纪摄提元气肇有神灵一人有十三头始学篇曰人皇九头兄弟九人九州各居其一
    　　夷者觝也其九畎夷于夷方夷黄夷白夷赤夷夷风夷蛮者慢也其八天竺咳首僬侥跛踵穿胷儋耳狗轨旁舂戎者凶也其六侥夷戎央老白耆鼻息天刚狄者辟也其五月氐戎貊匈奴单于白屋
    　　蜀蛮卭部川都王蒙备死气未絶其妻子以锦数疋相续系死者曵之于地置十数里外高山上令气絶乃复以锦被裹而埋之防其族哭之名作鬼亲守近州蛮人说其事倭辰余国或横书或左书或结绳或锲木唯高丽摹冩頴法取正中华 [33]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [33]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 女娲之功烈上际九天下契黄垆名声被后世光辉重万物乘雷车服应龙青虬席萝图震黄璐援絶瑞前白螭后奔虵 - Describes Nüwa's great deeds, including her divine attributes and actions.
    • 项浚始学篇曰上古有天皇十三头洞防记曰一姓十三人徐整厯纪曰岁纪摄提元气肇有神灵一人有十三头始学篇曰人皇九头兄弟九人九州各居其一 - Citations from different texts introducing mythological figures.
    • 夷者觝也其九畎夷于夷方夷黄夷白夷赤夷夷风夷蛮者慢也其八天竺咳首僬侥跛踵穿胷儋耳狗轨旁舂戎者凶也其六侥夷戎央老白耆鼻息天刚狄者辟也其五月氐戎貊匈奴单于白屋 - Describes various types of barbarian groups and their characteristics.
    • 蜀蛮卭部川都王蒙备死气未絶其妻子以锦数疋相续系死者曵之于地置十数里外高山上令气絶乃复以锦被裹而埋之防其族哭之名作鬼亲守近州蛮人说其事倭辰余国或横书或左书或结绳或锲木唯高丽摹冩頴法取正中华 - Describes the death and burial customs of the Shu region and the writing methods in various countries.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 女娲 - A mythological figure in Chinese folklore known for creating humans and repairing the sky.
    • 天皇 - Refers to the 'Heavenly Emperor,' a mythological figure associated with the beginning of civilization.
    • 人皇 - Refers to the 'Human Emperor,' another mythological figure.
    • 夷、蛮、戎、狄 - Refers to the four barbarian groups in ancient China, each with distinct characteristics.
    • 蜀蛮 - Refers to the people of the Shu region, known for their unique customs.
    • 倭辰余国 - Refers to various countries in East Asia, including Japan.
    • 高丽 - Refers to the ancient Korean kingdom of Goryeo.

    Translation:

    Continuation of the Book of Natural Curiosities, Volume IV
    　　Imperially Approved Complete Library, Volume IV
    　　Continuation of the Book of Natural Curiosities, Volume V
    　　Compiled by Li Shi of the Song Dynasty

    Nüwa's great deeds reach up to the nine heavens and down to the yellow furnace, her fame spreading to future generations, her glory shining upon all things. She rides the thunder chariot, wears the responding dragon, rides the green dragon, sits on the vine map, shakes the yellow jade, holds the auspicious sign, with a white螭 in front and a rushing snake behind.

    According to Xiang Jun's "Beginnings of Learning," in ancient times there was the Heavenly Emperor with thirteen heads, as recorded in the "Record of Deep Defense," where one surname had thirteen people. Xu Zheng's "Chronicle of the Calendar" states that in the year of Shetu, the primordial qi gave rise to divine beings, and one person had thirteen heads. In "Beginnings of Learning," it is said that the Human Emperor had nine heads, with nine brothers, each ruling one of the nine regions.

    The Yi are those who resist, with nine types: the Qian Yi, the Yu Yi, the Huang Yi, the Bai Yi, the Chi Yi, the Xuan Yi, and the Feng Yi. The Man are those who are slow or lazy, with eight types: the Tianzhu Man, the Keishou Man, the Jiaoyao Man, the Baozhong Man, the Chuangxi Man, the Dan'er Man, the Gougui Man, and the Pangchong Man. The Rong are those who are fierce, with six types: the Jiao Yi, the Rong, the Yangla, the Baili, the Qili, and the Tian Gang. The Di are those who are isolated, with five types: the Di, the Rong, the Mo, the Xiongnu, and the Chanyu Baiwu.

    A king of the Shu region named Meng Bei died, and his wife and children tied his body to the ground with several pieces of silk, dragged it several miles away to a high mountain, and let it die before wrapping it in silk and burying it. His clan mourned him, naming him a ghost, and guarded the nearby region. The local barbarians told the story. In the countries of Wo, Chen, and Yu, some wrote horizontally, some vertically, some used knots, and some carved wood. Only Goryeo followed the correct method of writing, taking it from China. [33]

    以清水潄口饮酒至斗不乱或曰酒毒自齿入也南人好饮茶孙皓以茶与韦昭代酒谢安诣陆纳设茶果而已北人初不识开元中太山灵岩寺有降魔师教禅者以不寐人多作茶饮因以成俗
    　　楚人陆鸿渐为茶论并煎炙之法造茶具二十四事以都统笼贮之常伯熊者因广鸿渐之法伯熊饮茶过度遂患风气或云北人未有茶多黄病后饮病多腰疾偏死
    　　唐则天于东都造明堂高三百尺九州鼎置明堂之下当中豫州鼎高一丈八尺受一千八百石余各依方面高一丈四尺受一千二百石都用铜五十六万七百一十二斤
    　　禹治洪水观于河见白面长人鱼身出曰吾河精也授禹河图而返于渊又曰尧修河洛有五老相谓河图将来告帝以期知我者重瞳也五老乃翻为流星而升于昴
    　　山东风俗遇正月取五姓女年十余岁共卧一榻覆之以衾以箕扇之良乆如梦寐或欲刺文绣事笔砚理管俄顷乃寤谓之扇天卜以乞巧 [34]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [34]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 以清水潄口 - Rinsing the mouth with clear water, a common practice in ancient China for oral hygiene.
    • 飲酒至斗不亂 - Drinking up to a dou (a unit of measurement) without losing composure, indicating a high tolerance for alcohol.
    • 酒毒自齒入也 - Suggests that alcohol toxicity enters through the teeth, possibly a folk belief about the effects of alcohol.
    • 南人好飲茶 - Indicates that people in the south have a preference for drinking tea.
    • 孫皓以茶與韋昭代酒 - Sun Hao offered tea to Wei Zhao as a substitute for wine, a historical anecdote about a ruler and his advisor.
    • 謝安诣陆纳设茶果而已 - Xie An visited Lu Na and only set out tea and fruits, describing a simple and restrained visit.
    • 北人初不识 - Northerners initially did not know, referring to the unfamiliarity of northerners with tea.
    • 开元中 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang (713-756), a period of prosperity and cultural flourishing.
    • 泰山灵岩寺 - Refers to the Lingyin Temple on Mount Tai, a famous Buddhist temple.
    • 降魔师 - Refers to a Buddhist monk who specializes in exorcism.
    • 教禅者以不寐 - Taught Zen practitioners to stay awake, referring to meditation practices.
    • 因以成俗 - Thus it became a custom, indicating the widespread adoption of a practice.
    • 楚人陆鸿渐为茶论 - A man from Chu named Lu Hongjian wrote a treatise on tea, referring to Lu Yu, the author of *The Classic of Tea*.
    • 并煎炙之法 - Including methods for brewing and roasting tea, describing the techniques covered in the treatise.
    • 造茶具二十四事 - Created twenty-four tea utensils, referring to the tea set described in *The Classic of Tea*.
    • 以都统笼贮之 - Stored them in a unified container, describing the organization of the tea set.
    • 常伯熊者 - Refers to a person named Chang Boxiong, who expanded on Lu Hongjian's methods.
    • 因广鸿渐之法 - Expanded on Lu Hongjian's methods, indicating the development of new tea practices.
    • 伯熊饮茶过度 - Chang Boxiong drank too much tea, leading to health issues.
    • 遂患风气 - Thus suffered from wind disease, a term for various ailments.
    • 或云北人未有茶多黄病 - Some say northerners who did not drink tea often suffered from jaundice, suggesting a correlation between tea consumption and health.
    • 后饮病多腰疾偏死 - Later, those who drank tea often suffered from back pain and died prematurely, indicating a negative view of excessive tea consumption.
    • 唐则天于东都造明堂 - Empress Wu Zetian built the Hall of Brightness in the Eastern Capital, referring to the construction of a grand hall in Luoyang.
    • 高三百尺 - 300 feet tall, describing the height of the hall.
    • 九州鼎置明堂之下 - Nine cauldrons representing the nine provinces were placed under the Hall of Brightness, symbolizing the unity of the empire.
    • 当中豫州鼎 - The central cauldron represented the province of Yu, emphasizing the central province.
    • 高一丈八尺 - 18 feet tall, describing the height of the central cauldron.
    • 受一千八百石 - Could hold 1,800 shi (a unit of volume), indicating the capacity of the central cauldron.
    • 余各依方面 - The others followed the directions, describing the placement of the other cauldrons.
    • 高一丈四尺 - 14 feet tall, describing the height of the other cauldrons.
    • 受一千二百石 - Could hold 1,200 shi, indicating the capacity of the other cauldrons.
    • 都用铜五十六万七百一十二斤 - A total of 567,112 catties of bronze were used, describing the material cost of the construction.
    • 禹治洪水 - Yu the Great controlled the floods, referring to a legendary figure who tamed the floods.
    • 观于河 - Observed the Yellow River, describing Yu's inspection of the river.
    • 见白面长人鱼身 - Saw a white-faced, long-bodied fish-like being, describing a mythical creature.
    • 曰吾河精也 - Said I am the spirit of the river, indicating the creature's identity.
    • 授禹河图 - Gave Yu the River Map, referring to a legendary map that helped Yu control the floods.
    • 而返于渊 - And returned to the depths, describing the creature's departure.
    • 又曰尧修河洛 - It also said that Yao improved the Yellow and Luo rivers, referring to another legendary figure.
    • 有五老相谓 - Five elders spoke to each other, describing a group of wise men.
    • 河图将来告帝以期 - The River Map will come to inform the emperor of the timing, indicating a prophecy.
    • 知我者重瞳也 - Those who know me have double pupils, referring to a physical characteristic of the emperor.
    • 五老乃翻为流星而升于昴 - The five elders then transformed into meteors and ascended to the constellation of the Pleiades, describing their transformation and departure.
    • 山东风俗 - Customs in Shandong, referring to the region.
    • 遇正月取五姓女 - During the first month, they would take five girls from different families, describing a traditional practice.
    • 年十余岁 - Around ten years old, indicating the age of the girls.
    • 共卧一榻 - They would sleep together on one bed, describing the practice.
    • 覆之以衾 - Cover them with a quilt, indicating the bedding.
    • 以箕扇之 - Use a winnowing basket to fan them, describing the method of fanning.
    • 良久如梦寐 - After a long time, they would fall into a dreamlike state, describing the effect of the practice.
    • 或欲刺文绣事笔砚理管 - Some would want to embroider, write, or manage brushes and cords, describing the activities during the dreamlike state.
    • 俄顷乃寤 - Soon they would wake up, indicating the end of the practice.
    • 谓之扇天卜以乞巧 - It was called 'fanning the sky' and was used to seek skill, describing the purpose of the practice.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 禹治洪水 - Refers to the legendary figure Yu the Great, who is credited with controlling the floods and founding the Xia Dynasty.
    • 观于河 - Refers to the Yellow River, one of the most important rivers in Chinese history.
    • 白面长人鱼身 - Refers to a mythical creature, possibly a water spirit or a river deity.
    • 河图 - Refers to the River Map, a legendary map that helped Yu control the floods and is associated with the early Chinese civilization.
    • 尧修河洛 - Refers to Emperor Yao, another legendary figure who is said to have improved the Yellow and Luo rivers.
    • 五老 - Refers to five wise elders, possibly representing the five elements or the five cardinal directions.
    • 重瞳 - Refers to a physical characteristic of the emperor, indicating his divine or extraordinary nature.
    • 昴 - Refers to the constellation of the Pleiades, a significant star cluster in Chinese astronomy.
    • 山东风俗 - Refers to the customs and traditions of the Shandong region, known for its rich cultural heritage.
    • 扇天卜以乞巧 - Refers to a traditional practice in Shandong, where young girls would sleep together and dream of various activities, believed to help them develop skills and talents.

    Final Translation

    Rinsing the mouth with clear water and drinking up to a dou without losing composure, or so it is said, the toxicity of alcohol enters through the teeth. People in the south have a preference for drinking tea. Sun Hao offered tea to Wei Zhao as a substitute for wine, and Xie An visited Lu Na and only set out tea and fruits. Northerners initially did not know about tea. During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, at the Lingyin Temple on Mount Tai, there was a monk who specialized in exorcism and taught Zen practitioners to stay awake, leading many to drink tea, and thus it became a custom.

    A man from Chu named Lu Hongjian wrote a treatise on tea, including methods for brewing and roasting tea, and created twenty-four tea utensils, storing them in a unified container. A person named Chang Boxiong expanded on Lu Hongjian's methods. Chang Boxiong drank too much tea and thus suffered from wind disease. Some say that northerners who did not drink tea often suffered from jaundice, and later, those who drank tea often suffered from back pain and died prematurely.

    Emperor Wu Zetian built the Hall of Brightness in the Eastern Capital, which was 300 feet tall. Nine cauldrons representing the nine provinces were placed under the Hall of Brightness, with the central cauldron representing the province of Yu, 18 feet tall and able to hold 1,800 shi. The others followed the directions, each 14 feet tall and able to hold 1,200 shi. A total of 567,112 catties of bronze were used.

    Yu the Great controlled the floods and observed the Yellow River, where he saw a white-faced, long-bodied fish-like being who said, "I am the spirit of the river," and gave Yu the River Map before returning to the depths. It also said that Yao improved the Yellow and Luo rivers, and five elders spoke to each other, saying, "The River Map will come to inform the emperor of the timing. Those who know me have double pupils." The five elders then transformed into meteors and ascended to the constellation of the Pleiades.

    In Shandong, during the first month, they would take five girls from different families, around ten years old, and have them sleep together on one bed, covering them with a quilt and using a winnowing basket to fan them. After a long time, they would fall into a dreamlike state, some wanting to embroider, write, or manage brushes and cords. Soon they would wake up, and it was called "fanning the sky" and was used to seek skill. [34]

    衡山五岳之南岳也黄帝以潜霍为山之副尔雅云霍山为南岳至汉武以衡山道逺徙祭于庐江潜山或曰衡山即霍山山海经衡山即岣嵝山常山者隂终阳始其道长乆故北山曰常山亦名恒出五谷四种五获
    　　一升粱米以纯苦酒一斗渍之三日出百蒸百曝逺行一餐十日不饥重餐四百九十日不饥又方米一斗赤石脂三斤水渍之二三日捣为圆如李大可辟谷
    　　灵寳五符经用白鲜米九蒸九曝
    　　庚桑名楚史记作尤桑
    　　夏棘字子棘为汤大夫列子作夏革
    　　月令曰椒是玉衡星精服之身轻能老柏是仙药又云进酒次第当从小起以年少者起先
    　　燃草爆竹起于庭燎
    　　正月望祭门先以杨枝揷门随杨枝所指以酒脯饮食及豆粥揷筯祭之
    　　酒杯藤出西域藤大如臂叶似葛花实如梧桐花坚可以酌酒有文章映彻可爱实大如指如荳香美消酒来至藤下摘花酌酒仍以其实醒酒国人寳之不中土张骞使大宛得之出张骞出关志 [35]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [35]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 衡山五岳之南岳也 - Formal statement identifying Mount Heng as the Southern Peak among the Five Great Mountains.
