    • 黄帝以潜霍为山之副 - Historical claim that Emperor Huangdi considered Mount Qianhao a secondary peak.
    • 尔雅云霍山为南岳 - Citation from *Er Ya* equating Mount Qianhao with the Southern Peak.
    • 至汉武以衡山道远徙祭于庐江潜山 - Historical fact that Emperor Wu of Han relocated the sacrificial site to Qianshan in Luyang due to the distance of Mount Heng.
    • 灵宝五符经用白鲜米九蒸九曝 - Reference to a specific ritual in *Lingbao Wufu Jing* involving nine steams and nine exposures of white fresh rice.
    • 庚桑名楚史记作尤桑 - Note that the name 'Gengsang' in *Shiji* is also known as 'Yousang'.
    • 夏棘字子棘为汤大夫列子作夏革 - Note that 'Xia Ji' (Summer Thorn) is also known as 'Xia Ge' in *Liezi*.
    • 月令曰椒是玉衡星精服之身轻能老柏是仙药又云进酒次第当从小起以年少者起先 - Reference to *Yueling* stating that consuming a certain herb makes one light and long-lived, and that the order of drinking should start with the youngest.
    • 燃草爆竹起于庭燎 - Description of the origin of firecrackers, which began with the burning of grass in court ceremonies.
    • 正月望祭门先以杨枝插门随杨枝所指以酒脯饮食及豆粥插箸祭之 - Ritual for the first full moon of the year, involving placing willow branches at the door and offering food and drink along with chopsticks.
    • 酒杯藤出西域藤大如臂叶似葛花实如梧桐花坚可以酌酒有文章映彻可爱实大如指如豆实香美消酒来至藤下摘花酌酒仍以其实醒酒国人宝之不传中土张骞使大宛得之出张骞出关志 - Description of a vine called 'jiubei' (wine cup vine) from the Western Regions, which produces fragrant fruits that can sober one up. The vine is highly valued and was brought to China by Zhang Qian.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 衡山 - One of the Five Great Mountains, located in Hunan Province.
    • 黄帝 - The Yellow Emperor, a legendary figure in Chinese history.
    • 潜霍 - Mount Qianhao, a secondary peak.
    • 尔雅 - An ancient Chinese dictionary.
    • 汉武 - Emperor Wu of Han, a powerful ruler of the Han Dynasty.
    • 灵宝五符经 - A Daoist text containing rituals and spells.
    • 庚桑 - A historical figure mentioned in *Shiji*.
    • 夏棘 - A historical figure mentioned in *Liezi*.
    • 月令 - *Yueling* (Monthly Orders), a text on seasonal activities.
    • 燃草爆竹 - Firecrackers, originally used in court ceremonies.
    • 正月望祭门 - A traditional ritual performed on the first full moon of the year.
    • 酒杯藤 - A vine from the Western Regions, known for its medicinal properties.
    • 张骞 - A famous Chinese explorer who traveled to the Western Regions.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • This long sentence contains multiple historical and geographical references and must be broken down into manageable segments.
    • The ritual described here involves specific steps and must be rendered clearly.
    • The sentence combines multiple pieces of information and must be segmented for clarity.
    • The origin of firecrackers is described succinctly but must be expanded for clarity.
    • The ritual involves multiple steps and must be broken down.
    • The description of the vine is detailed and must be segmented for clarity.

    Proper Nouns

    • 衡山 - Mount Heng.
    • 黄帝 - Emperor Huangdi.
    • 潜霍 - Mount Qianhao.
    • 尔雅 - *Er Ya*.
    • 汉武 - Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 灵宝五符经 - *Lingbao Wufu Jing*.
    • 庚桑 - Gengsang.
    • 夏棘 - Xia Ji.
    • 月令 - *Yueling*.
    • 燃草爆竹 - Firecrackers.
    • 正月望祭门 - First full moon ritual.
    • 酒杯藤 - Wine cup vine.
    • 张骞 - Zhang Qian.

    Mount Heng is the Southern Peak among the Five Great Mountains. Emperor Huangdi considered Mount Qianhao a secondary peak. Er Ya states that Mount Qianhao is the Southern Peak. Emperor Wu of Han, due to the distance of Mount Heng, relocated the sacrificial site to Qianshan in Luyang. Some say that Mount Heng is the same as Mount Qianhao. According to Shan Hai Jing, Mount Heng is also known as Mount Guimao. The term "Changshan" refers to the end of the yin and the beginning of the yang, and its path is long and enduring. Hence, the northern mountain is called Changshan, also known as Heng, which produces five grains, four types of crops, and five harvests.

    One measure of millet soaked in pure bitter wine for three days, then steamed and dried a hundred times, can sustain a traveler for ten days with a single meal. A heavy meal can sustain a person for 490 days without hunger. Another method involves soaking one measure of rice with three jin of red stone fat in water for two to three days, then pounding it into pills the size of a jujube. These pills can be used to abstain from food.

    The Lingbao Wufu Jing uses white fresh rice, which is steamed and dried nine times.

    Gengsang is also known as Yousang in Shiji.

    Xia Ji, also known as Xia Ge in Liezi, was a minister during the reign of King Tang.

    Yueling states that consuming a certain herb makes one light and long-lived, and that the order of drinking should start with the youngest.

    Firecrackers originated from the burning of grass in court ceremonies.

    On the first full moon of the year, a ritual is performed at the door. Willow branches are inserted into the door, and food and drink, along with porridge, are offered. Chopsticks are also used in the offering.

    The wine cup vine comes from the Western Regions. The vine is as thick as an arm, with leaves similar to those of kudzu and flowers like those of a paulownia tree. The flowers are hard and can be used to make wine. The fruit is fragrant and beautiful, and can be used to sober one up. When one comes to the vine, they pick the flowers and make wine, and can also use the fruit to sober up. The people of the Western Regions treasure this vine, and it was brought to China by Zhang Qian during his mission to Dayuan. This is recorded in Zhang Qian's Account of the Western Regions. [35]

    菊花舒时采茎叶杂黍米醸之至来年九月九日始熟谓之菊花酒
    　　孟诜云葡萄不问土地但之醸酒皆得美好
    　　昔有于王敦城下得一铜钲中间铸一物如羊头其身如篆文乃飞廉也
    　　仓颉本鸟迹为字取其孳乳相生故有六义秦用文烧书古文遂絶或曰大篆出周宣王史籀小篆出秦李斯及胡母敬又曰古之书起于秦省篆为以便文行剧务以明者篆之防也或曰程邈于云阳增损或云临淄人发古冡得铜棺前和外为字言齐太公世孙胡公之棺唯三字是古知自古非始于秦或曰秦人王次仲变仓颉旧文为今文始皇奇而召之三征不至秦槛车征之次仲化为大鸟飞去落二翮于山山有大翮小翮之名
    　　彭城县古彭祖之国也世本曰陆终之子其三曰籛是为彭祖下曰彭祖冡彭祖长年八百绵寿永世而亦有冡者盖亦元极之化
    　　蒲坂西舜所都有姓刘名堕探挹河流醖成芳酎饮伺枯枝之日醡于桑落之辰故酒得其名方土之贡最为佳酌每云索郎有顾思同旅语索郎反语为桑落也 [36]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [36]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 菊花舒時采莖葉雜黍米醸之 - Describes the process of making chrysanthemum wine, with '菊花舒時' indicating the time of harvesting and '雜黍米醸之' describing the brewing process.
    • 至來年九月九日始熟謂之菊花酒 - Explains the maturation process and the naming of the wine.
    • 孟诜云葡萄不問土地但醸酒皆得美好 - A quotation from Meng Shen, providing a general principle about grape wine.
    • 昔有於王敦城下得一銅鉦 - Introduces a historical anecdote about finding a bronze drum.
    • 中間鑄一物如羊頭其身如篆文乃飛廉也 - Describes the contents of the bronze drum and identifies the object as a mythical creature.
    • 古文遂絶或曰大篆出周宣王史籀 - Provides a historical context about the loss of ancient texts and the origin of large seal script.
    • 小篆出秦李斯及胡母敬 - Provides another historical context about the origin of small seal script.
    • 秦用文燒書古文遂絶 - Describes the burning of books and the loss of ancient texts.
    • 古之書起於秦省篆為 - Explains the origin and evolution of clerical script.
    • 以便文行劇務 - Explains the practical purpose of clerical script.
    • 程邈於雲陽增損 - Describes the development of clerical script.
    • 齊太公世孫胡公之棺唯三字是古 - Provides a historical context about the tomb of Duke Huan of Qi and the rarity of ancient clerical script.
    • 知自古非始于秦 - Clarifies the historical existence of clerical script.
    • 彭城古彭祖之國 - Provides the historical setting of Pengcheng.
    • 世本曰陸終之子其三曰籛是為彭祖下 - Provides a historical context about the legendary figure Peng Zu.
    • 彭祖長年八百綿壽永世 - Emphasizes the legendary longevity of Peng Zu.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 孟诜 - A famous physician and writer of the Tang Dynasty, known for his work on herbal medicine.
    • 王敦城 - Refers to the city of Wang Dun, an important city in ancient China.
    • 銅鉦 - A bronze drum, a significant artifact in ancient Chinese culture.
    • 飛廉 - A mythical creature in Chinese mythology, often depicted as a wind spirit.
    • 大篆 - Large seal script, a form of Chinese writing used in the Zhou Dynasty.
    • 小篆 - Small seal script, a form of Chinese writing used in the Qin Dynasty.
    • 秦李斯 - Li Si, a prominent statesman and writer of the Qin Dynasty.
    • 胡母敬 - Hu Mu Jing, another writer of the Qin Dynasty.
    • 程邈 - Cheng Miao, a calligrapher who developed clerical script.
    • 雲陽 - Yunyang, a place in ancient China.
    • 齊太公 - The Duke of Qi, a legendary figure in Chinese history.
    • 彭祖 - A legendary figure known for his longevity.
    • 彭城 - Pengcheng, an ancient city in present-day Xuzhou, Jiangsu Province.
    • 舜 - Emperor Shun, a legendary ruler in Chinese history.
    • 劉墮 - Liu Dui, a historical figure known for making wine.
    • 桑落 - A term used in the context of the wine, possibly referring to the timing of the wine-making process.

    When chrysanthemums bloom, their stems and leaves are harvested and mixed with millet to brew wine. By the ninth day of the ninth month of the following year, the wine becomes ripe and is called chrysanthemum wine.

    Meng Shen said that grapes, regardless of the land, when made into wine, all result in fine wine.

    In ancient times, someone found a bronze drum under the city of Wang Dun. Inside the drum was cast an object that looked like a ram's head, with its body inscribed with seal script. It was identified as the Fēi Lián, a mythical creature. Ancient texts were thus lost, or so it is said, large seal script originated from Shi Zhou, a historian of King Xuan of Zhou, and small seal script originated from Li Si and Hu Mu Jing during the Qin Dynasty. The Qin Dynasty used clerical script to burn books, and ancient texts were lost. Clerical script originated in the Qin Dynasty, simplifying seal script into clerical script to facilitate the writing of urgent documents. Cheng Miao in Yunyang made additions and deletions, or so it is said. A tomb was found in Linzi with a copper coffin, and on the front and outside of the coffin were inscribed characters in ancient clerical script, referring to the tomb of Duke Huan of Qi, a descendant of the Duke of Qi. Only three characters were in ancient clerical script, indicating that clerical script has existed since ancient times, not just starting in the Qin Dynasty.

    Pengcheng was the ancient country of Peng Zu. The Shibenz records that the third son of Lu Zhong was named Jian, known as Peng Zu. It is said that Peng Zu lived for eight hundred years, his life spanning many generations, and his tomb also indicates that he was a transformation of the ultimate.

    In the western part of Puzhen, where Emperor Shun once reigned, there was a man named Liu Dui who drew water from the river and fermented it into fragrant wine. He drank on the day when the branches were dry and pressed the wine on the day when the mulberry leaves fell. The wine thus got its name, and it was the best tribute of the region. It is often said that "Solan" is the name, and thinking of the same things as the companions, "Solan" spoken backward is "Sangluo." [36]

    海中有度朔山上有桃木蟠屈三千里枝东北鬼门万鬼所出入也荼与鬰垒居其门执苇索以食故十有二月岁竟腊之夜遂以荼垒并挂苇索于门
    　　王充云防稽本山名夏禹廵狩防计于此遂以名郡或曰此非五岳禹时吴裸断髪文身无防计事
    　　董生春秋繁露曰春旱以甲乙日为苍龙长一丈八尺居中央为小龙七各长四尺于东方皆东向其间相去八尺小童八人斋三日服青衣而舞之下三时准此皆燔雄鸡烧豭猪尾于里北门及市中以祈焉
    　　蜥蜴求雨法以土实巨瓮作木蜴小童探青竹衣青衣以舞歌曰蜴蜥蜴兴云吐雾雨若滂沱放汝归去
    　　交州安阳王有神人名臯通为安阳王治弩一张一发杀三百人
    　　今之所谓石九十二斤法为准汉秤三百四十一斤也今之蹶弩九石古之二十五石比魏武卒二人之力挽弓三石古之三十四钧比顔高之弓当五人有余 [37]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [37]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 海中有度朔山 - Refers to a mythical mountain in the sea, known for its extraordinary features.
    • 桃木蟠屈三千里 - Describes a peach tree that twists and turns over a distance of three thousand li, emphasizing its vast size.
    • 枝东北鬼门 - Indicates that the branches of the peach tree extend towards the northeast, where the 'Ghost Gate' is located.
    • 万鬼所出入也 - Means 'the place where ten thousand ghosts enter and exit,' indicating a gateway for spirits.
    • 荼与鬰垒居其门 - Refers to two figures, 'Tú' and 'Yú Lěi,' who reside at the gate.
    • 执苇索以食鬼 - Means 'holding reed ropes to feed the ghosts,' describing their role in managing the spirits.
    • 遂以荼垒并挂苇索于门 - Means 'thus, Tú and Yú Lěi are hung along with reed ropes on the door,' indicating a ritual practice.
    • 防稽本山名 - Refers to the original name of a mountain, which is now associated with the region of Fángjī (modern-day Shaoxing).
    • 夏禹廵狩防计于此 - Means 'Emperor Yu toured and held a meeting here,' referring to a historical event.
    • 遂以名郡 - Means 'thus, the name was given to the prefecture,' indicating the naming of a region.
    • 董生春秋繁露 - Refers to the work 'Luxuriant Dew of the Spring and Autumn Annals' by Dong Zhongshu.
    • 春旱以甲乙日为苍龙 - Means 'in spring drought, a blue dragon is made on the days of Jia and Yi,' referring to a ritual practice.
    • 苍龙长一丈八尺 - Describes a blue dragon that is eighteen chi long.
    • 居中央为小龙七 - Means 'seven small dragons are placed in the center,' indicating a specific arrangement.
    • 各长四尺 - Means 'each is four chi long,' describing the size of the small dragons.
    • 于东方皆东向 - Means 'all facing east in the east,' indicating the direction they face.
    • 其间相去八尺 - Means 'with a distance of eight chi between them,' describing the spacing.
    • 小童八人斋三日 - Means 'eight children fast for three days,' referring to a preparatory ritual.
    • 服青衣而舞之下 - Means 'dressed in green clothes and dancing below,' describing a ritual performance.
    • 下三时准此 - Means 'the lower three times follow this,' indicating a pattern or repetition.
    • 皆燔雄鸡烧豭猪尾 - Means 'all burn roosters and pig tails,' referring to sacrificial practices.
    • 于里北门及市中以祈焉 - Means 'at the north gate of the village and in the market to pray,' indicating the locations of the rituals.
    • 蜥蜴求雨法 - Refers to a method for summoning rain using lizards.
    • 以土实巨瓮 - Means 'fill a large jar with soil,' describing a ritual preparation.
    • 作木蜴 - Means 'make a wooden lizard,' referring to a ritual object.
    • 小童探青竹衣青衣以舞 - Means 'children dressed in green bamboo clothes dance,' describing a ritual performance.
    • 歌曰蜴蜥蜴兴云吐雾 - Means 'the song says, 'Lizard, lizard, rise clouds and spew mist,' indicating a chant.
    • 雨若滂沱放汝归去 - Means 'if the rain pours down, let you return,' indicating a conditional release.
    • 交州安阳王 - Refers to King An Yang of Jiaozhou, a historical figure.
    • 有神人名臯通 - Means 'there was a divine person named Gao Tong,' referring to a legendary figure.
    • 为安阳王治弩一张 - Means 'who made a bow for King An Yang,' indicating a specific task.
    • 一发杀三百人 - Means 'one shot killed three hundred people,' emphasizing the power of the bow.
    • 今之所谓石九十二斤法为准 - Refers to the current standard weight of 92 jin for a 'dan' (stone).
    • 汉秤三百四十一斤 - Means 'the Han balance weighs 341 jin,' indicating the weight system used during the Han dynasty.
    • 今之蹶弩九石 - Means 'the current crossbow is nine dan,' referring to the weight of the crossbow.
    • 古之二十五石 - Means 'ancient twenty-five dan,' indicating the weight system used in ancient times.
    • 比魏武卒二人之力 - Means 'compared to the strength of two Wei soldiers,' indicating a comparison.
    • 挽弓三石 - Means 'pulling a bow of three dan,' referring to the strength required.
    • 古之三十四钧 - Means 'ancient thirty-four jun,' indicating the weight system used in ancient times.
    • 比顔高之弓 - Means 'compared to Yan Gao's bow,' referring to a specific historical bow.
    • 当五人有余 - Means 'equivalent to more than five people,' indicating the strength of the bow.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 度朔山 - A mythical mountain in the sea, associated with the Peach Blossom Spring.
    • 桃木 - Peach wood is believed to have protective properties against evil spirits.
    • 荼与鬰垒 - Mythical figures who guard the Ghost Gate.
    • 苇索 - Reed ropes used in rituals to bind and manage spirits.
    • 防稽 - Modern-day Shaoxing, a region in Zhejiang Province.
    • 夏禹 - Emperor Yu, a legendary ruler who toured and held meetings.
    • 董生 - Dong Zhongshu, a prominent Confucian scholar and philosopher.
    • 春秋繁露 - A work by Dong Zhongshu that discusses Confucian philosophy and rituals.
    • 苍龙 - A blue dragon, often associated with the east and spring.
    • 小童 - Children, often involved in rituals and performances.
    • 青衣 - Green clothes, often worn in rituals.
    • 蜥蜴 - Lizards, used in rituals to summon rain.
    • 交州 - A historical region in southern China.
    • 安阳王 - King An Yang, a historical figure.
    • 臯通 - A legendary figure who made a powerful bow.
    • 魏武卒 - Elite soldiers of the State of Wei.
    • 顔高 - A historical figure known for his strong bow.

    Proper Nouns

    • 度朔山 - A mythical mountain.
    • 防稽 - A historical region.
    • 夏禹 - Emperor Yu.
    • 董生 - Dong Zhongshu.
    • 春秋繁露 - A work by Dong Zhongshu.
    • 交州 - A historical region.
    • 安阳王 - King An Yang.
    • 臯通 - A legendary figure.
    • 魏武卒 - Elite soldiers of the State of Wei.
    • 顔高 - A historical figure.

    In the sea, there is a mountain called Duoshuo Mountain, upon which grows a peach tree that twists and turns over a distance of three thousand li. Its branches extend northeast to the Ghost Gate, through which ten thousand ghosts enter and exit. Tú and Yú Lěi reside at the gate, holding reed ropes to feed the ghosts. Thus, on the night of the twelfth month of the year, when the New Year's Eve arrives, Tú and Yú Lěi are hung along with reed ropes on the door.

    Wang Chong said that Fángjī was originally the name of a mountain, where Emperor Yu toured and held a meeting. Thus, the name was given to the prefecture. Some say this is not one of the Five Great Mountains, and during the time of Yu, the people of Wu were unclothed, had their hair cut short, and had tattoos, so there were no meetings held there.

    Dong Zhongshu, in his work "Luxuriant Dew of the Spring and Autumn Annals," says that in the case of spring drought, a blue dragon is made on the days of Jia and Yi, eighteen chi long, placed in the center with seven small dragons, each four chi long, all facing east in the east, with a distance of eight chi between them. Eight children fast for three days, dressed in green clothes and dancing below. The lower three times follow this pattern. All burn roosters and pig tails at the north gate of the village and in the market to pray.

    The method for summoning rain using lizards involves filling a large jar with soil and making a wooden lizard. Children dressed in green bamboo clothes dance, singing, "Lizard, lizard, rise clouds and spew mist. If the rain pours down, let you return."

    King An Yang of Jiaozhou had a divine person named Gao Tong who made a bow for him. One shot killed three hundred people.

    The current standard weight of 92 jin for a "dan" (stone) is used as a reference. The Han balance weighs 341 jin. The current crossbow is nine dan, equivalent to ancient twenty-five dan, compared to the strength of two Wei soldiers. Pulling a bow of three dan is equivalent to ancient thirty-four jun, compared to Yan Gao's bow, which is equivalent to the strength of more than five people. [37]

    续博物志卷五
    <子部,小说家类,琐记之属,续博物志>
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　续博物志卷六
    　　宋　李石　撰
    　　少典产黄帝黄帝产元嚣元嚣产蟜极蟜极产高辛是为帝喾帝喾产放勲是为帝尧
    　　黄帝产昌意昌意产高阳是为颛帝【昌意元嚣弟黄帝次子也】颛帝产穷蝉穷蝉产敬康敬康产勾芒勾芒产蟜牛蟜牛产瞽瞍瞽瞍产重华是为帝舜及象
    　　颛帝产鲧鲧产文命是为禹
    　　唐六典三年一享帝喾氏于顿丘享唐尧于平阳稷契配享虞舜于河东咎繇配享夏禹于安邑伯益配享商汤于偃师伊尹配享周文王于酆太公配享武王于镐周公配享汉高祖于长陵萧何配享皆以仲春之月
    　　蜀州青城丈人山每岁春秋时享以蔬以县令行车频秦书曰苻坚高陆县民穿井得龟大二尺六寸背文负八卦古字坚以石为池养之十六年而死取其骨以问吉凶名为客龟曾于梦中自言我将归江南不遇死于秦解曰龟三万六千岁而终苻秦亡之证 [38]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [38]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 續博物志 - Title meaning 'Continuation of the *Wubu Zhi*', indicating a sequel or expansion.
    • 少典產黃帝 - Genealogical statement meaning 'the clan of Shao Dian gave birth to the Yellow Emperor'.
    • 蟜極產高辛 - Genealogical statement meaning 'Jiao Ji gave birth to Gaoxin'.
    • 帝喾产放勲 - Meaning 'Emperor Ku gave birth to Fangxun'.
    • 昌意元嚣弟 - Meaning 'Changyi was the younger brother of Yuanxiao'.
    • 颛帝产穷蝉 - Meaning 'Emperor Zuan gave birth to Qiongchan'.
    • 敬康产勾芒 - Meaning 'Jingkang gave birth to Goumang'.
    • 蟜牛产瞽瞍 - Meaning 'Jiaoniu gave birth to Gushu'.
    • 瞽瞍产重华 - Meaning 'Gushu gave birth to Zhonghua'.
    • 颛帝产鲧 - Meaning 'Emperor Zuan gave birth to Gun'.
    • 文命是为禹 - Meaning 'Wenming is known as Yu'.
    • 唐六典 - Refers to the *Tang Liudian*, an official code of the Tang dynasty.
    • 三年一享 - Means 'every three years, a sacrifice is held'.
    • 顿丘 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location for the sacrifice.
    • 平阳 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location for the sacrifice.
    • 稷契配享 - Means 'Ji and Xi are honored alongside'.
    • 虞舜 - Refers to Emperor Shun, a legendary ruler.
    • 河东 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location for the sacrifice.
    • 咎繇配享 - Means 'Gou Yao is honored alongside'.
    • 夏禹 - Refers to Emperor Yu, a legendary ruler.
    • 安邑 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location for the sacrifice.
    • 伯益配享 - Means 'Bo Yi is honored alongside'.
    • 商汤 - Refers to Emperor Tang, a legendary ruler.
    • 偃师 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location for the sacrifice.
    • 伊尹配享 - Means 'Yi Yin is honored alongside'.
    • 周文王 - Refers to King Wen of Zhou, a legendary ruler.
    • 酆 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location for the sacrifice.
    • 太公配享 - Means 'Taigong is honored alongside'.
    • 武王 - Refers to King Wu of Zhou, a legendary ruler.
    • 镐 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location for the sacrifice.
    • 周公配享 - Means 'Zhou Gong is honored alongside'.
    • 汉高祖 - Refers to Emperor Gaozu of Han, a legendary ruler.
    • 长陵 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location for the sacrifice.
    • 萧何配享 - Means 'Xiao He is honored alongside'.
    • 仲春之月 - Refers to the second month of spring.
    • 苻坚 - Refers to Fu Jian, a ruler of the Former Qin dynasty.
    • 高陆县 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location for the event.
    • 穿井得龟 - Means 'digging a well and finding a turtle'.
    • 背文负八卦古字 - Means 'with inscriptions on its back and ancient trigrams'.
    • 以石为池养之 - Means 'built a stone pool to keep it in'.
    • 十六年而死 - Means 'after sixteen years, it died'.
    • 取其骨以问吉凶 - Means 'took its bones to ask about auspiciousness and misfortune'.
    • 名为客龟 - Means 'named it the guest turtle'.
    • 曾于梦中自言我将归江南 - Means 'once in a dream, it said, 'I will return to Jiangnan''.
    • 不遇死于秦 - Means 'but did not meet my fate and died in Qin'.
    • 三万六千岁而终 - Means 'lives for thirty-six thousand years before ending'.
    • 苻秦亡之证 - Means 'a sign of the fall of Former Qin'.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 少典 - A legendary figure, often considered the ancestor of the Yellow Emperor.
    • 黄帝 - The Yellow Emperor, a legendary ruler and cultural hero.
    • 元嚣 - A legendary figure, the younger brother of Changyi.
    • 蟜极 - A legendary figure, the father of Gaoxin.
    • 高辛 - Gaoxin, a legendary figure and the father of Fangxun.
    • 帝喾 - Emperor Ku, a legendary ruler and the father of Fangxun.
    • 放勲 - Fangxun, a legendary figure and the father of Yu.
    • 昌意 - Changyi, a legendary figure and the younger brother of Yuanxiao.
    • 颛帝 - Emperor Zuan, a legendary ruler and the father of Qiongchan.
    • 穷蝉 - Qiongchan, a legendary figure and the father of Jingkang.
    • 敬康 - Jingkang, a legendary figure and the father of Goumang.
    • 勾芒 - Goumang, a legendary figure and the father of Jiaoniu.
    • 蟜牛 - Jiaoniu, a legendary figure and the father of Gushu.
    • 瞽瞍 - Gushu, a legendary figure and the father of Zhonghua.
    • 重华 - Zhonghua, a legendary figure and the father of Yu.
    • 鲧 - Gun, a legendary figure and the father of Yu.
    • 文命 - Wenming, another name for Yu.
    • 唐六典 - The *Tang Liudian*, an official code of the Tang dynasty.
    • 顿丘 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location for the sacrifice.
    • 平阳 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location for the sacrifice.
    • 稷契 - Ji and Xi, legendary figures honored alongside Emperor Shun.
    • 虞舜 - Emperor Shun, a legendary ruler.
    • 河东 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location for the sacrifice.
    • 咎繇 - Gou Yao, a legendary figure honored alongside Emperor Yu.
    • 夏禹 - Emperor Yu, a legendary ruler.
    • 安邑 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location for the sacrifice.
    • 伯益 - Bo Yi, a legendary figure honored alongside Emperor Tang.
    • 商汤 - Emperor Tang, a legendary ruler.
    • 偃师 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location for the sacrifice.
    • 伊尹 - Yi Yin, a legendary figure honored alongside King Wen of Zhou.
    • 周文王 - King Wen of Zhou, a legendary ruler.
    • 酆 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location for the sacrifice.
    • 太公 - Taigong, a legendary figure honored alongside King Wu of Zhou.
    • 武王 - King Wu of Zhou, a legendary ruler.
    • 镐 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location for the sacrifice.
    • 周公 - Zhou Gong, a legendary figure honored alongside Emperor Gaozu of Han.
    • 汉高祖 - Emperor Gaozu of Han, a legendary ruler.
    • 长陵 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location for the sacrifice.
    • 萧何 - Xiao He, a legendary figure honored alongside Fu Jian.
    • 苻坚 - Fu Jian, a ruler of the Former Qin dynasty.
    • 高陆县 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location for the event.
    • 穿井得龟 - Digging a well and finding a turtle, a specific event.
    • 背文负八卦古字 - With inscriptions on its back and ancient trigrams, describing the turtle.
    • 以石为池养之 - Built a stone pool to keep it in, describing the care for the turtle.
    • 十六年而死 - After sixteen years, it died, indicating the duration.
    • 取其骨以问吉凶 - Took its bones to ask about auspiciousness and misfortune, indicating a divination practice.
    • 名为客龟 - Named it the guest turtle, giving it a name.
    • 曾于梦中自言我将归江南 - Once in a dream, it said, 'I will return to Jiangnan', indicating a reported dream.
    • 不遇死于秦 - But did not meet my fate and died in Qin, indicating the outcome.
    • 三万六千岁而终 - Lives for thirty-six thousand years before ending, indicating the lifespan of the turtle.
    • 苻秦亡之证 - A sign of the fall of Former Qin, indicating the significance of the event.

    Continuation of the Wu Bu Zhi Volume Five
    Continuation of the Wu Bu Zhi (Miscellaneous Notes)
    　　Imperially Approved Collection of the Four Treasuries
    　　Continuation of the Wu Bu Zhi Volume Six
    　　By Li Shi of the Song Dynasty
    　　The clan of Shao Dian gave birth to the Yellow Emperor, the Yellow Emperor gave birth to Yuanxiao, Yuanxiao gave birth to Jiao Ji, Jiao Ji gave birth to Gaoxin, who is known as Emperor Ku, and Emperor Ku gave birth to Fangxun, who is known as Emperor Yao.
    　　The Yellow Emperor also gave birth to Changyi, who gave birth to Gao Yang, known as Emperor Zuan. Changyi was the younger brother of Yuanxiao and the second son of the Yellow Emperor. Emperor Zuan gave birth to Qiongchan, who gave birth to Jingkang, who gave birth to Goumang, who gave birth to Jiaoniu, who gave birth to Gushu, who gave birth to Zhonghua, known as Emperor Shun, and his brother Xiang.
    　　Emperor Zuan also gave birth to Gun, who gave birth to Wenming, known as Yu.
    　　According to the Tang Liudian, every three years, a sacrifice is held to Emperor Ku at Dunchou, to Emperor Yao at Pingyang, with Ji and Xi honored alongside. To Emperor Shun at Hedong, Gou Yao is honored alongside. To Emperor Yu at Anyi, Bo Yi is honored alongside. To Emperor Tang at Yanshi, Yi Yin is honored alongside. To King Wen of Zhou at Feng, Taigong is honored alongside. To King Wu of Zhou at Hao, Zhou Gong is honored alongside. To Emperor Gaozu of Han at Changling, Xiao He is honored alongside. All these sacrifices are held in the second month of spring.
    　　Every year during the spring and autumn festivals, the County Magistrate of Qingcheng Mountain in Shuzhou offers vegetarian sacrifices and leads the procession. According to the Qin Records, during the reign of Fu Jian, a villager in Gaolu County dug a well and found a turtle with inscriptions on its back and ancient trigrams. Fu Jian built a stone pool to keep it in, and after sixteen years, it died. He took its bones to ask about auspiciousness and misfortune, naming it the guest turtle. Once in a dream, it said, "I will return to Jiangnan," but did not meet my fate and died in Qin. The turtle lives for thirty-six thousand years before ending, and this is a sign of the fall of Former Qin. [38]

    秦龟即山中龟不入水水中神龟长一尺二者佳龟见蛇则呷而食之
    　　李徳曰扬雄蜀王本纪言张仪筑城依龟行迹筑之到此闻龟殻藏酒库宇文遇曰比尝为主库小吏见龟殻长六尺元和初高崇文截以为带胯
    　　野王县东南有孔子庙有碑魏太和元年孔灵度以旧宇毁址求修复野王令范众爱河内太守元真刺史高允表闻立碑于庙治中刘明别驾吕次文主簿向班虎茍灵龟以宣尼大圣非碑颂所称宜立记焉仲尼闻杀鸣犊旋车而反晋思之为立庙碑云孔氏官于洛阳下追思圣祖故立庙亦犹刘累迁鲁立尧祠于山矣
    　　鸷鸟雄小雌大庶鸟雄大雌小
    　　鸂鶒能敇水故宿水而物不害鸩能巫步禁虵啄木遇蠧以觜画字成符而蠧自出鹊有巢木鸷鸟不能见燕衔土避戊巳日则巢固而不倾鹳有长水石故能于巢中养鱼而水不涸燕恶艾雀欲夺之则衔置其中
    　　鹭有长翰毛江东人取以为攡名之曰白鹭纕虎鹰能飞捕虎豹身大如牛翼广二丈 [39]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [39]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 秦龟 - A type of turtle, possibly named after the Qin dynasty or region.
    • 即山中龟不入水 - Literally 'the mountain turtle does not enter the water,' suggesting a specific behavior or habitat.
    • 水中神龟长一尺 - 'The divine turtle in the water is one foot long,' describing a legendary turtle.
    • 二者佳龟 - 'Both are fine turtles,' indicating that both types are considered excellent.
    • 见蛇则呷而食之 - 'When it sees a snake, it sucks and eats it,' describing a specific behavior.
    • 李徳曰 - 'Li De said,' introducing a statement.
    • 扬雄蜀王本纪 - Refers to a historical text by Yang Xiong about the kings of Shu.
    • 张仪筑城依龟行迹筑之 - 'Zhang Yi built a city following the turtle's path,' suggesting a mythological or symbolic foundation.
    • 龟殻藏酒库 - 'The turtle shell was hidden in the wine cellar,' indicating a historical or legendary event.
    • 元和初 - The early Yuanhe period during the Tang dynasty.
    • 高崇文 - A historical figure who cut the turtle shell to make a belt and breechclout.
    • 野王县 - A specific county in ancient China.
    • 孔子庙 - A temple dedicated to Confucius.
    • 魏太和元年 - The first year of the Taihe era during the Wei dynasty.
    • 孔灵度 - A person involved in the restoration of the Confucius Temple.
    • 旧宇毁址 - 'The old structure was destroyed,' indicating a need for repair.
    • 野王令范众爱 - The magistrate of Yewang, Fan Zhong, who loved the temple.
    • 河内太守元真 - The governor of Hedong, Yuan Zhen.
    • 刺史高允 - The governor, Gao Yun.
    • 表闻立碑于庙 - 'Proposed to erect a stele in the temple,' describing a historical action.
    • 治中刘明别驾吕次文主簿向班虎茍灵龟 - Officials involved in the process.
    • 宣尼大圣 - A respectful title for Confucius.
    • 非碑颂所称宜立记焉 - 'Not suitable for a stele inscription, it should be recorded,' indicating a decision.
    • 仲尼闻杀鸣犊旋车而反 - Confucius heard about the killing of Mingdu and turned back, a historical event.
    • 晋思之为立庙碑云 - The Jin dynasty thought about it and erected a temple stele, describing a historical action.
    • 孔氏官于洛阳下追思圣祖故立庙 - The Kong family, officials in Luoyang, remembered their sage ancestor and built a temple.
    • 亦犹刘累迁鲁立尧祠于山矣 - Liu Lei moved to Lu and built a Yao temple on the mountain, drawing a parallel.
    • 鸷鸟 - A type of bird, possibly a raptor.
    • 庶鸟 - Common birds.
    • 鸂鶒 - A type of bird, possibly a kingfisher.
    • 鸩 - A type of bird, possibly a dianthus.
    • 啄木 - A woodpecker.
    • 鹊 - A magpie.
    • 燕 - A swallow.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 秦龟 - A type of turtle, possibly named after the Qin dynasty or region.
    • 扬雄蜀王本纪 - A historical text by Yang Xiong about the kings of Shu.
    • 张仪 - A famous statesman who built a city following a mythological path.
    • 龟殻藏酒库 - The turtle shell was hidden in a wine cellar, indicating a historical or legendary event.
    • 元和初 - The early Yuanhe period during the Tang dynasty.
    • 高崇文 - A historical figure who cut the turtle shell to make a belt and breechclout.
    • 野王县 - A specific county in ancient China.
    • 孔子庙 - A temple dedicated to Confucius.
    • 魏太和元年 - The first year of the Taihe era during the Wei dynasty.
    • 孔灵度 - A person involved in the restoration of the Confucius Temple.
    • 野王令范众爱 - The magistrate of Yewang, Fan Zhong, who loved the temple.
    • 河内太守元真 - The governor of Hedong, Yuan Zhen.
    • 刺史高允 - The governor, Gao Yun.
    • 宣尼大圣 - A respectful title for Confucius.
    • 仲尼闻杀鸣犊旋车而反 - Confucius heard about the killing of Mingdu and turned back, a historical event.
    • 晋思之为立庙碑云 - The Jin dynasty thought about it and erected a temple stele, describing a historical action.
    • 孔氏官于洛阳下追思圣祖故立庙 - The Kong family, officials in Luoyang, remembered their sage ancestor and built a temple.
    • 亦犹刘累迁鲁立尧祠于山矣 - Liu Lei moved to Lu and built a Yao temple on the mountain, drawing a parallel.

    A turtle native to the mountains, known as the Qin turtle, does not enter the water. In contrast, a divine turtle found in the water is one foot long, and both are considered fine turtles. When this turtle sees a snake, it sucks and eats it.

    Li De said: Yang Xiong's Records of the Kings of Shu mentions that Zhang Yi built a city following the path of a turtle. Upon arriving, he learned that the turtle shell was hidden in a wine cellar. Yuwen Yu said: I once served as a minor official in charge of the wine cellar and saw the turtle shell, which was six feet long. In the early Yuanhe period, Gao Chongwen cut it to make a belt and breechclout.

    In the southeastern part of Yewang County, there is a Confucius Temple with a stele. In the first year of the Taihe era during the Wei dynasty, Kong Lingdu, seeing that the old structure had been destroyed, sought to restore it. The magistrate of Yewang, Fan Zhong, loved the temple and reported this to the governor of Hedong, Yuan Zhen, and the governor, Gao Yun. They proposed to erect a stele in the temple. The secretary, Liu Ming, and the deputy governor, Lu Cizhen, along with the clerk, Xiang Ban, and the diviner, Gou Linggui, decided that since Confucius, the Great Sage, was too great to be honored by a stele inscription, it should be recorded. Confucius, upon hearing about the killing of Mingdu, turned back and returned. The Jin dynasty thought about it and erected a temple stele, saying that the Kong family, officials in Luoyang, remembered their sage ancestor and built a temple, just as Liu Lei moved to Lu and built a Yao temple on the mountain.

    Raptors are small males and large females, while common birds are large males and small females.

    The kingfisher can control water, so it stays near water and does not harm other creatures. The dianthus can perform witchcraft to ban snakes. The woodpecker encounters a worm and draws a symbol with its beak, causing the worm to emerge. The magpie has a nest in a tree, and raptors cannot see it. The swallow carries soil to avoid the days of Wu and Ji, making its nest sturdy and not prone to falling. The crane has a long water stone, allowing it to raise fish in its nest without the water drying up. The swallow dislikes the magpie and carries it to place it in its nest.

    The egret has long feathers, and people in Jiangdong use them to make fans, calling them "white heron fans." The tiger eagle can fly and capture tigers and leopards, with a body as large as a cow and wings spanning two zhang. [39]

    蘐草一名鹿葱花名宜男风土记云姙妇佩其花生男也
    　　李卫公云金钱花损眼
    　　牡丹初不载文字惟以药见本草唐则天以后洛花始盛沈宋元白亦不及也刘梦得有咏鱼朝恩宅牡丹但云一丛千朶谢灵运言永嘉竹间多牡丹今越花不及洛花逺甚或曰灵运之所谓牡丹今之芍药特盛于吴越
    　　杞菊山芋牛蒡道家以为嘉蔬江淮百合根大而味甘南地微苦其次决明合欢槐芽亦可食孙思邈以合欢为萱草嵇叔夜合欢蠲忿萱草忘忧两物也
    　　山萧一名山缲神异经作永嘉郡记作魅一名山骆一名蛟一名濯肉一名肉一名晖一名飞龙如鸠青色亦曰治鸟巢大如五斗器饰以土垩赤白相间状如射侯犯者役虎害人烧人庐舍昔值洪水食都树皮饿死化为鸟姚汪王三姓其孙也
    　　俗讳五月上屋言五月人蜕上屋见影魂当去
    　　二广有草名胡蔓以急水吞之即死慢水即缓死取毒蛇杀之以此草覆之洒水菌生其上末为毒药杀人 [40]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [40]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 蘐草一名鹿葱花名宜男 - 蘐草是鹿葱的别名，宜男暗示与男性后代有关，可能指一种被认为能促进男性生育的植物。
    • 風土記云姙婦佩其花生男也 - 風土記是一本关于地方风俗和地理的书籍，姙婦佩其花生男也指怀孕的妇女佩戴这种花会生男孩，这是一种民间信仰。
    • 李衛公云金錢花損眼 - 李衛公是历史人物，金錢花損眼指金钱花对眼睛有害，这是一种关于植物效果的信仰。
    • 牡丹初不載文字惟以藥見本草唐則天以後洛花始盛 - 牡丹最初只在药书中提到，唐则天之后洛阳的牡丹开始繁荣。
    • 沈宋元白亦不及也 - 沈宋元白指诗人沈佺期、宋之问、元稹和白居易，他们也没有提到牡丹。
    • 劉夢得有咏魚朝恩宅牡丹但云一丛千朶謝靈運言永嘉竹間多牡丹今越花不及洛花逺甚 - 劉夢得写了关于鱼朝恩宅邸牡丹的诗，但只说一丛千朵；谢灵运说永嘉竹林间有很多牡丹，现在越地的牡丹远不如洛阳的。
    • 或曰靈運之所謂牡丹今之芍藥特盛於吳越 - 有人认为谢灵运所说的牡丹实际上是芍药，在吴越地区特别繁盛。
    • 杞菊山芋牛蒡道家以為嘉蔬江淮百合根大而味甘南地微苦其次決明合歡槐芽亦可食孫思邈以合歡為萱草嵇叔夜合歡蠲忿萱草忘憂兩物也 - 杞菊山芋牛蒡是多种植物，道家认为它们是佳蔬；江淮地区的百合根大而味甜，南方的稍苦；决明、合欢、槐芽也可以食用；孙思邈认为合欢是萱草，嵇叔夜认为合欢和萱草可以缓解愤怒和忘记忧虑。
    • 山蕭一名山缲神異經作永嘉郡記作魅一名山骆一名蛟一名濯肉一名肉一名晖一名飛龍如鸠青色亦曰治鳥巢大如五斗器飾以土垩赤白相間狀如射侯犯者役虎害人燒人廬舍昔值洪水食都樹皮饿死化為鳥姚汪王三姓其孫也 - 山蕭是山缲的别名，神异经称其为某种东西，永嘉郡记称其为魅；它还有其他名字，如山骆、蛟、濯肉、肉、晖、飞龙等，也被称为治鸟，巢穴大如五斗器，装饰以红白土，形状像箭靶；冒犯它的人会被老虎奴役，受到伤害，房子被烧；过去在洪水期间，人们吃都城的树皮，饿死变成鸟，姚、汪、王三姓的后代。
    • 俗諱五月上屋言五月人蜕上屋見影魂當去 - 五月上屋顶是禁忌，据说五月人蜕皮，上屋顶看到自己的影子意味着灵魂会离开。
    • 二廣有草名胡蔓以急水吞之即死慢水即緩死取毒蛇殺之以此草覆之洒水菌生其上末為毒藥殺人 - 两广地区有一种草叫胡蔓，用急水吞服会立即死亡，用慢水吞服会慢慢死亡；取毒蛇杀死后，用这种草覆盖，洒水后长出的菌子可以制成毒药杀人。

    The Shè Cǎo, also known as Lù Cōng, has flowers called Yí Nán. According to the Fēng Tǔ Jì (Record of Local Customs), pregnant women who wear its flowers will give birth to boys. Li Wèi Gong said that the golden money flower harms the eyes. The peony was initially not recorded in texts but only mentioned in herbal medicine. After Empress Wu Zetian of the Tang Dynasty, the Luoyang peonies began to flourish. Poets Shen Quanqi, Song Zhiwen, Yuan Zhen, and Bai Juyi also did not mention them. Liu Yuxi wrote a poem about the peonies in Yu Chao'en's residence, but only said a cluster of thousands. Xie Lingyun said there were many peonies in the bamboo groves of Yongjia. Now the peonies in Yue are far inferior to those in Luoyang. Some say what Xie Lingyun called peonies are now known as peony, especially abundant in Wu and Yue. The plants called Qǐ Jú, Shān Yù, and Niú Bàng are considered fine vegetables by Daoists. The lilies in Jianghuai have large roots and sweet taste, while in the south, they are slightly bitter. Next, cassia seeds, happy trees, and locust buds can also be eaten. Sun Simiao considers the happy tree as the daylily. Ji Shuyue considers the happy tree and the daylily as two plants that can alleviate anger and forget worries. The Shān Xiāo, also known as Shān Sāo, is called Shén Yì Jīng (Book of Strange Things) as , and the Yǒng Jiā Jùn Jì (Yongjia County Records) call it a ghost. It is also called Mountain Camel, Dragon, Washed Meat,  Meat, Glow, Flying Dragon, and Blue Bird, also known as the Bird of Governance. Its nest is as large as a five-dou container, decorated with red and white earth, resembling a target. Those who offend it will be served by tigers, harmed, and their houses burned. In the past, during a flood, they ate the bark of the capital's trees, starved to death, and transformed into birds, the descendants of the surnames Yao, Wang, and Wang. It is taboo to go up onto the roof in May. It is said that in May, people shed their skin. Seeing one's shadow on the roof indicates that the soul will leave. In the two Guang regions, there is a plant called Hú Màn. If swallowed with fast water, it will cause immediate death; if swallowed with slow water, it will cause gradual death. To kill a venomous snake, cover it with this grass and sprinkle water on it. Fungi will grow on it, and the powder from these fungi will become a deadly poison. [40]

    北人淫南妇辞归以毒置食约以年月复还解以他药不尔毒发死矣谓之定年药南游者宜志之
    　　宋刘亮合仙丹须白蟾蜍白蝙蝠得而服之立死唐人陈子真得蝙蝠大如鸦食之一夕大防而死
    　　小蜘蛛长脚者一名蟏蛸一名长防俗名喜子陆玑防诗名长脚荆州河内人谓之喜母
    　　段成式云主夜神呪可辟恶梦呪曰婆珊婆演帝清泰小字二十三盖正月二十三日生也以是日为千秋节人臣奏对但云两旬三日或数物则云二十二更过二十四不敢斥尊也
    　　先贤传鲁有恭士名曰汜行年七十其恭益甚冬日行隂夏日行阳一食之间三起
    　　秦穆公时有人掘地得物若羊将献之道逢二童子谓曰此名为蝹常在地中食死人脑若欲杀之以柏东南枝捶其首由是墓皆植柏又曰柏为鬼廷
    　　汉孝和时南海献龙眼茘枝十里一置五里一候交州记曰龙眼高五六丈似荔枝而圆小谢承书曰唐羌字伯游上书谏以龙眼荔枝及生鲜献之驿马昼夜传送至有死者二物升殿未必延年益寿帝从之 [41]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [41]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 為之一空 - Set phrase meaning completely emptied; fields were left barren.
    • 淫 - Here, '淫' likely refers to a man taking advantage of a woman, but the context suggests a more specific act of poisoning.
    • 以毒置食 - Literally 'placing poison in food,' referring to a method of poisoning.
    • 約以年月 - 'Agreeing on a year and month,' implying a fixed time for the woman to return.
    • 不爾毒發死矣 - 'If not, the poison will be released and cause death.'
    • 謂之定年藥 - 'Called it the 'fixed-year drug,' a term for the poison.'
    • 南遊者宜志之 - 'Southern travelers should be aware of it,' a warning to travelers.
    • 仙丹 - 'Immortal elixir,' a term for a supposed life-extending potion.
    • 須白蟾蜍白蝙蝠 - 'Requires white toads and white bats,' ingredients for the elixir.
    • 得而服之立死 - 'If obtained and consumed, immediate death results.'
    • 大如鴉 - 'As large as a crow,' describing the size of the bat.
    • 大防 - Likely a typo or unclear term; possibly '大防' refers to a severe illness or death.
    • 長腳者 - 'With long legs,' describing the spider.
    • 蟏蛸 - 'A name for the spider,' a specific term.
    • 長防 - Possibly a typo or unclear term; may refer to a protective or harmful quality.
    • 喜子 - 'A common name for the spider,' possibly related to good luck.
    • 防詩 - Possibly a typo or unclear term; may refer to a specific poem or text.
    • 喜母 - 'Mother of good luck,' a term for the spider.
    • 主夜神呪 - 'A charm for the night god,' used to ward off bad dreams.
    • 婆珊婆演帝清泰 - A specific incantation or charm.
    • 小字二十三 - 'Small name twenty-three,' possibly referring to a nickname or a number.
    • 正月二十三日生也 - 'Born on the twenty-third day of the first month,' indicating a birth date.
    • 千秋節 - 'The Thousand Autumns Festival,' a celebration of longevity.
    • 兩旬三日 - 'Two ten-day periods and three days,' a way of referring to a specific date.
    • 不敢斥尊也 - 'Do not dare to directly address the emperor,' a sign of respect.
    • 恭士 - 'A respectful man,' a term for a virtuous person.
    • 行年七十 - 'At the age of seventy,' indicating the man's age.
    • 冬日行隂夏日行陽 - 'In winter, he walks in the shade; in summer, he walks in the sun,' describing his behavior.
    • 一食之間三起 - 'He gets up three times during one meal,' indicating his extreme respectfulness.
    • 蝹 - 'A creature that eats dead people's brains,' a mythical or dangerous animal.
    • 柏東南枝 - 'The southeast branch of a cypress tree,' used to kill the creature.
    • 鬼廷 - 'The court of ghosts,' a term for a place associated with the dead.
    • 龍眼 - 'Dragon eye,' a type of fruit.
    • 荔枝 - 'Lychee,' another type of fruit.
    • 十里一置五里一候 - 'Every ten miles, a station; every five miles, a relay,' describing the system for transporting the fruits.
    • 延年益壽 - 'To prolong life and increase longevity,' a term for the supposed benefits of the fruits.
    • 帝從之 - 'The emperor agreed,' indicating the outcome of the advice.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 北人 - 'Northern people,' likely referring to people from the north of China.
    • 南婦 - 'Southern woman,' likely referring to a woman from the south of China.
    • 宋劉亮 - 'Liu Liang of the Song dynasty,' a historical figure.
    • 唐陳子真 - 'Chen Zhen of the Tang dynasty,' another historical figure.
    • 陸機 - 'Lu Ji,' a historical figure or author.
    • 段成式 - 'Duan Chengshi,' a historical figure or author.
    • 先賢傳 - 'The Biography of the Virtuous,' a historical text.
    • 秦穆公 - 'Duke Mu of Qin,' a historical figure.
    • 漢孝和 - 'Emperor He of Han,' a historical figure.
    • 南海 - 'The South Sea,' a geographical region.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • This long sentence describes a complex situation involving poisoning and a fixed time for the woman to return. The sentence structure is elliptical and requires careful parsing.
    • This sentence describes two separate incidents involving the consumption of dangerous substances. The structure is compact and requires clear segmentation.
    • This sentence provides multiple names for a type of spider. The structure is elliptical and requires careful parsing.
    • This sentence describes a charm and its use. The structure is complex and requires careful parsing.
    • This sentence describes a virtuous man and his behavior. The structure is elliptical and requires careful parsing.
    • This sentence describes a mythological creature and its defeat. The structure is complex and requires careful parsing.
    • This sentence describes the transportation of fruits and the emperor's decision. The structure is complex and requires careful parsing.

    Proper Nouns

    • 北人 - Northern people.
    • 南婦 - Southern woman.
    • 宋劉亮 - Liu Liang of the Song dynasty.
    • 唐陳子真 - Chen Zhen of the Tang dynasty.
    • 陸機 - Lu Ji, a historical figure or author.
    • 段成式 - Duan Chengshi, a historical figure or author.
    • 先賢傳 - The Biography of the Virtuous, a historical text.
    • 秦穆公 - Duke Mu of Qin, a historical figure.
    • 漢孝和 - Emperor He of Han, a historical figure.
    • 南海 - The South Sea, a geographical region.

    A Northern man took advantage of a Southern woman and, upon her request to return, placed poison in her food with the agreement that she would return after a fixed year and month. If she did not return, the poison would be released, causing her death. This was called the "fixed-year drug," and Southern travelers should be aware of it.

    During the Song dynasty, Liu Liang attempted to create an immortal elixir that required white toads and white bats. Upon obtaining and consuming it, he died immediately. In the Tang dynasty, Chen Zhen obtained a bat as large as a crow, ate it, and died that very night from a severe illness.

    A small spider with long legs, known as "Xiao Shao" or "Chang Fang," is commonly called "Xizi." Lu Ji, in his poetry, referred to it as "Chang Jiao," and people in Jingzhou and Henan called it "Ximu."

    Duan Chengshi said that a charm for the night god could ward off bad dreams. The incantation is "Po Shan Po Yan Di Qing Tai," with a small name of twenty-three, indicating a birth date of the twenty-third day of the first month. This day is celebrated as the "Thousand Autumns Festival." Officials would mention "two ten-day periods and three days" or "twenty-two" when referring to this date, but would not directly address the emperor.

    According to the "Biography of the Virtuous," there was a respectful man from Lu named Fan, who was seventy years old. His respectfulness increased with age; in winter, he walked in the shade, and in summer, he walked in the sun. During one meal, he would get up three times.

    During the reign of Duke Mu of Qin, someone dug up an object resembling a sheep and intended to offer it. On the way, he encountered two children who said it was called "Yan," a creature that lived in the ground and ate dead people's brains. To kill it, one should use the southeast branch of a cypress tree to strike its head. This is why cypress trees are planted in tombs, and it is also said that cypress is the "court of ghosts."

    During the reign of Emperor He of Han, the South Sea presented lychees and longans. They were transported with a station every ten miles and a relay every five miles. The "Records of Jiaozhou" say that longans are five to six zhang tall, similar to lychees but smaller. Xie Cheng wrote that Tang Qiang, styled Boyou, submitted a memorial advising against the offering of lychees, longans, and fresh produce, as the rapid transportation by relay horses caused deaths. The two fruits were brought to the palace, but it was unlikely they would prolong life or increase longevity. The emperor agreed. [41]

    魏初传古文出邯郸淳石经古文转失淳法石长八尺广四尺碑石四十八枚广三十丈魏文帝刋典论六碑附于其次陆机太学賛别一碑在讲堂下载蔡邕韩说高堂谿等名太学弟子賛复一碑在外门中又一碑汉顺帝阳嘉八年立云建武二十七年造太学永建六年九月诏书修太学工徒十一万二千人阳嘉九年八月作毕有晋辟雍行礼碑是太始二年立
    　　子曰干之动直静专坤动辟静翕其根也天根毎日两度蹴入尾闾巨壑则海沸出潮
    　　世传费长房得符于壶公以是制服百鬼其后鬼窃其符因以杀长房
    　　道士尸解谓之身经太隂
    　　李泌在衡山事明瓉禅师瓉云欲学者先将笔砚碎却明瓒北宗大照之门人性懒羣僧令看盐雨至流于池羣僧敺之不怒冬月卧于灶前不起以粥洒其头因就头取吃号懒瓉作明心论
    　　后汉崔篆着易林六十四篇篆骃之祖父或曰卦林或曰象林
    　　白马犹缁布名实而已
    　　续博物志卷六 [42]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [42]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 魏初传古文 - Refers to the early transmission of ancient texts in the state of Wei.
    • 出邯郸淳石经古文转失淳法 - Indicates that the ancient texts from the stele of Shan Dan Chun were transmitted but lost the original method of Shan Dan Chun.
    • 魏文帝刋典论六碑附于其次 - States that Emperor Wen of Wei added six inscribed stones to the collection.
    • 陆机太学賛别一碑在讲堂下载蔡邕韩说高堂谿等名太学弟子賛复一碑在外门中 - Lists additional inscriptions and their locations, including those by Lu Ji, Cai Yong, Han Shuo, Gao Tang Xi, and others.
    • 又一碑汉顺帝阳嘉八年立云建武二十七年造太学永建六年九月诏书修太学工徒十一万二千人阳嘉九年八月作毕 - Provides details about the construction and completion of the Imperial Academy.
    • 有晋辟雍行礼碑是太始二年立 - Mentions a stone erected in the second year of Tai Shi, detailing the establishment of the Imperial Academy.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 魏初 - Refers to the early period of the state of Wei during the Three Kingdoms era.
    • 邯郸淳 - A famous calligrapher and scholar of the Han dynasty.
    • 魏文帝 - Emperor Wen of Wei, a ruler known for his cultural and literary patronage.
    • 陆机 - A prominent poet and essayist of the Jin dynasty.
    • 蔡邕 - A renowned calligrapher, musician, and scholar of the Han dynasty.
    • 韩说 - A historian and writer of the Han dynasty.
    • 高堂谿 - A scholar and official of the Han dynasty.
    • 汉顺帝 - Emperor Shun of Han, known for his efforts to restore Confucianism.
    • 阳嘉八年 - The eighth year of the Yang Jia era, a period during the reign of Emperor Shun of Han.
    • 建武二十七年 - The twenty-seventh year of the Jian Wu era, a period during the reign of Emperor Guangwu of Han.
    • 永建六年 - The sixth year of the Yong Jian era, another period during the reign of Emperor Shun of Han.
    • 太始二年 - The second year of the Tai Shi era, a period during the reign of Emperor Wen of Wei.

    Proper Nouns

    • 魏初 - Early period of the state of Wei.
    • 邯郸淳 - Shan Dan Chun, a famous calligrapher and scholar.
    • 魏文帝 - Emperor Wen of Wei.
    • 陆机 - Lu Ji, a prominent poet and essayist.
    • 蔡邕 - Cai Yong, a renowned calligrapher and scholar.
    • 韩说 - Han Shuo, a historian and writer.
    • 高堂谿 - Gao Tang Xi, a scholar and official.
    • 汉顺帝 - Emperor Shun of Han.
    • 阳嘉八年 - Eighth year of the Yang Jia era.
    • 建武二十七年 - Twenty-seventh year of the Jian Wu era.
    • 永建六年 - Sixth year of the Yong Jian era.
    • 太始二年 - Second year of the Tai Shi era.

    In the early period of Wei, ancient texts were transmitted from Shan Dan Chun's stele, but the original method of Shan Dan Chun was lost. The stele was eight feet long and four feet wide, and there were forty-eight stele stones, each thirty feet wide. Emperor Wen of Wei added six inscribed stones to the collection, and Lu Ji inscribed a separate stone in the lecture hall, which included the names of Cai Yong, Han Shuo, Gao Tang Xi, and other disciples of the Imperial Academy. Another stone was placed in the outer gate, and yet another stone was erected in the eighth year of Yang Jia, stating that the Imperial Academy was built in the twenty-seventh year of Jian Wu. In the sixth year of Yong Jian, the emperor issued an edict to repair the Imperial Academy, employing 112,000 workers, and the work was completed in the eighth month of the ninth year of Yang Jia. In the second year of Tai Shi, a stone was erected detailing the establishment of the Imperial Academy.

    Confucius said, "The movement of the Dry (Qian) is direct and still, the movement of the Earth (Kun) is open and still. The root of heaven is the daily entry and exit of the sea through the tailgate." It is said that Fei Changfang obtained a talisman from Hu Gong, which he used to subdue a hundred ghosts. Later, a ghost stole the talisman and used it to kill Fei Changfang.

    A Taoist priest who undergoes "corpse liberation" is said to have passed through the moon. Li Bi, while living on Mount Heng, served Master Ming Zan of the Northern School. Master Zan said that those who wish to learn should first break their writing utensils. Master Zan was known for his laziness, and the monks made him watch the salt rain flow into the pool. The monks drove him away, but he did not get angry. In winter, he would lie in front of the stove and not get up, and the monks would pour porridge on his head, which he would eat. He was called "Lazy Zan" and wrote the Mingxin Lun.

    During the Eastern Han, Cui Zhu wrote the Yi Lin (Book of Changes), consisting of sixty-four chapters. Cui Zhu was the ancestor of Cui Yin. It is also known as the Gu Lin or Xiang Lin.

    A white horse is still just a black cloth; it is all about name and reality.

    Continued Catalogue of Rare and Strange Things, Volume Six [42]

    钦定四库全书
    　　续博物志卷七
    　　宋　李石　撰
    　　老子谷神不死至用之不勤此章全是黄帝之言今在五千文内则老氏所著书恐非专已出
    　　正月不食生葱三月勿食小蒜四月勿食大蒜五月勿食薤六月七月勿食茱萸成血痢八九月勿食姜并肝心肺十月勿食椒十一月十二月勿食戴甲之物并脾肾
    　　眼者身之镜耳者体之牖视多则镜昏聴众则牖闭面者神之庭髪者脑之华心悲则面燋脑减则髪素精者体之神明者身之寳劳多则精散营竞则明消人死必视其形如生人足不青皮不皱目光不毁头髪尽脱而失形骨者皆尸解也武徳三年晋州人吉善行于羊角山见白衣父老呼善行曰为吾语唐天子吾为老君即汝祖也高祖因立庙高宗追尊元皇帝明皇亲注老子令学者习之两京及诸州各置元皇帝庙京师号元宫诸州号紫极宫寻改西京为太清宫东京为太微宫皆置学生
    　　莴菜出卨国有毒百虫不敢近蛇虺过其下误触之则目瞑不见物有中其毒者唯生姜汁解之
    　　食黄颡鱼后饮荆芥汤即死 [43]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [43]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 钦定四库全书 - Imperial collection of books, specifically the 'Four Treasuries,' compiled under the Qing dynasty.
    • 续博物志 - Continuation of the 'Book of Marvels,' an ancient Chinese encyclopedia.
    • 宋 李石 撰 - Authorship by Li Shi during the Song dynasty.
    • 老子谷神不死至用之不勤此章全是黄帝之言今在五千文内则老氏所著书恐非专已出 - Complex sentence structure. '谷神不死' refers to the concept of the 'valley spirit' or 'vital energy' in Taoist philosophy. '至用之不勤' suggests using this energy without effort. '此章全是黄帝之言' implies the chapter is entirely attributed to the Yellow Emperor. '今在五千文内则老氏所著书恐非专已出' suggests the same content is found in the 'Tao Te Ching' but may not be exclusively written by Laozi.
    • 正月不食生葱三月勿食小蒜四月勿食大蒜五月勿食薤六月七月勿食茱萸成血痢八九月勿食姜并肝心肺十月勿食椒十一月十二月勿食戴甲之物并脾肾 - List of dietary prohibitions for each month of the year, based on traditional Chinese medicine and dietary practices.
    • 眼者身之镜耳者体之牖视多则镜昏聴众则牖闭面者神之庭髪者脑之华心悲则面燋脑减则髪素精者体之神明者身之寳劳多则精散营竞则明消人死必视其形如生人足不青皮不皱目光不毁头髪尽脱而失形骨者皆尸解也 - Metaphors and idiomatic expressions related to the body and its functions. '眼者身之镜' means 'the eyes are the mirror of the body,' '耳者体之牖' means 'the ears are the windows of the body,' and so on. Discusses the effects of emotional states on physical appearance and the process of death and decomposition.
    • 莴菜出卨国有毒百虫不敢近蛇虺过其下误触之则目瞑不见物有中其毒者唯生姜汁解之 - Describes a type of lettuce (莴菜) that is said to be toxic to insects and snakes, and only ginger juice can counteract its poison.
    • 食黄颡鱼后饮荆芥汤即死 - Warning that eating yellow catfish and then drinking chrysanthemum herb soup can be fatal.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 钦定四库全书 - The 'Four Treasuries' was a massive compilation of Chinese literature commissioned by the Qing dynasty, reflecting the cultural and intellectual achievements of China.
    • 续博物志 - The 'Book of Marvels' is an ancient encyclopedia that covers a wide range of topics, including natural phenomena, mythology, and historical anecdotes.
    • 宋 李石 撰 - Li Shi was a scholar and official during the Song dynasty, known for his contributions to Chinese literature and history.
    • 老子谷神不死至用之不勤此章全是黄帝之言今在五千文内则老氏所著书恐非专已出 - References the 'Tao Te Ching' (五千文) and the Yellow Emperor (黄帝), both significant figures in Chinese philosophy and medicine.
    • 正月不食生葱三月勿食小蒜四月勿食大蒜五月勿食薤六月七月勿食茱萸成血痢八九月勿食姜并肝心肺十月勿食椒十一月十二月勿食戴甲之物并脾肾 - These dietary prohibitions are rooted in traditional Chinese medicine and reflect the belief in the importance of seasonal and dietary harmony.
    • 眼者身之镜耳者体之牖视多则镜昏聴众则牖闭面者神之庭髪者脑之华心悲则面燋脑减则髪素精者体之神明者身之寳劳多则精散营竞则明消人死必视其形如生人足不青皮不皱目光不毁头髪尽脱而失形骨者皆尸解也 - Reflects traditional Chinese views on the body, mind, and death, emphasizing the interconnectedness of physical and mental states.
    • 莴菜出卨国有毒百虫不敢近蛇虺过其下误触之则目瞑不见物有中其毒者唯生姜汁解之 - References specific plants and their properties, reflecting traditional Chinese herbal medicine.
    • 食黄颡鱼后饮荆芥汤即死 - Reflects traditional Chinese beliefs about the dangers of certain food combinations.

    Proper Nouns

    • 老子 - Laozi, the legendary founder of Taoism and author of the 'Tao Te Ching.'
    • 五千文 - The 'Tao Te Ching,' a foundational text of Taoism.
    • 黄帝 - The Yellow Emperor, a legendary figure in Chinese history and mythology.
    • 莴菜 - A type of lettuce.
    • 黄颡鱼 - A type of fish.
    • 荆芥 - A type of herb.

    The Four Treasuries
    Volume Seven of the Continued Book of Marvels
    By Li Shi of the Song dynasty

    The chapter on the "valley spirit" (谷神) being immortal and used without effort is entirely attributed to the Yellow Emperor. This chapter is found in the "Tao Te Ching" (五千文), but it may not be exclusively written by Laozi.

    In the first month, do not eat raw green onions; in the third month, do not eat small garlic; in the fourth month, do not eat large garlic; in the fifth month, do not eat leeks; in the sixth and seventh months, do not eat cinnamon, as it can cause bloody diarrhea; in the eighth and ninth months, do not eat ginger along with liver, heart, and lungs; in the tenth month, do not eat pepper; in the eleventh and twelfth months, do not eat armored plants, which include the spleen and kidneys.

    The eyes are the mirror of the body, and the ears are the windows of the body. If one looks too much, the mirror becomes dim; if one listens to too many things, the windows close. The face is the courtyard of the spirit, and the hair is the brilliance of the brain. When the heart is sad, the face becomes dry; when the brain diminishes, the hair turns white. Vital energy is the spirit of the body, and wisdom is the treasure of the person. When one labors too much, the vital energy disperses; when one is preoccupied with worldly affairs, wisdom fades. When a person dies, their form must be observed as if they were still alive: the feet do not turn blue, the skin does not wrinkle, the eyes do not lose their luster, and the hair falls out completely, leaving only the bones, which undergo a process called "尸解" (shijie).

    In the third year of Wu De, a man from Jin Prefecture named Ji Shanxing encountered a white-clad old man on Yangjiao Mountain. The old man called out to Ji Shanxing and said, "Tell the Tang Emperor on my behalf: I am Laozi, your ancestor." Emperor Gaozu then built a temple in honor of him. Emperor Gaozong later posthumously honored him as the Emperor Xuan Yuan. Emperor Ming Huang personally annotated the "Tao Te Ching" and ordered scholars to study it. Both the eastern and western capitals, as well as various prefectures, established temples dedicated to Emperor Xuan Yuan. The capital's temple was called the Xuan Yuan Palace, while the temples in the prefectures were called the Purple Extreme Palace. Later, the western capital's temple was renamed the Great Clarity Palace, and the eastern capital's temple was renamed the Great Micro Palace, both of which housed students.

    Lettuce (莴菜) grows in the land of Xie and is poisonous; no insects dare to approach it. If a snake passes over it and accidentally touches it, its eyes will become blind, and it will be unable to see. If someone is poisoned by it, only ginger juice can counteract the poison.

    If one eats yellow catfish and then drinks chrysanthemum herb (荆芥) soup, they will die. [43]

    草木之生动摇者生而不畅人之导引动摇四体者何故寿而不死
    　　尧若腊舜若腒桀纣之君垂尺余
    　　王充曰雷公有神雷声有器安得谓之神正月阳动故始雷五月阳盛故雷秋冬阳衰故雷潜太阳用事隂气乘之隂阳分争则相激射为毒中人则死中木则折人以震死试以斗水沃冶铸之火火气激烈则为雷声或曰雷火之及金石焦镕而漆噐不壊何也道家以雷烧石投井中石焦水寒激声大鸣
    　　顔渊与孔子俱上泰山东南望吴阊门外孔子见白马引顔渊指之若见吴阊门乎顔渊曰见之有系练之状孔子抚其目而止之顔渊髪白齿落遂以病死盖精力不及圣人而强役之也
    　　榝似茱茰而小赤色
    　　樝似梨而酢澁鹿食椒橘叶其肉自然作椒橘香江东呼蘽为藤似葛而麤大楩似豫章杼斗为栎或言皁斗棪实似柰柚似橙而酢
    　　淮南子曰狠者知而非知也愚者君子而非君子也戅者勇而非勇也使人相去也若玉之与石也葵之与苋也则论人易矣夫乱人者若芎防之与藁本蛇牀之与蘼芜 [44]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [44]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 草木之生动摇者生而不畅 - Metaphorical expression suggesting that movement in plants does not lead to full growth or vitality.
    • 人之导引动摇四体者何故寿而不死 - Rhetorical question about the benefits of physical exercise on longevity.
    • 尧若腊舜若腒桀纣之君垂尺余 - Complex simile comparing the appearance of ancient rulers to different states of preservation and their physical stature.
    • 雷公有神雷声有器安得谓之神 - Rhetorical question challenging the attribution of divine status to natural phenomena.
    • 正月阳动故始雷五月阳盛故雷秋冬阳衰故雷潜 - Describes the seasonal patterns of thunder according to the movement of yang energy.
    • 太阳用事隂气乘之隂阳分争则相激射为毒中人则死中木则折 - Explains how the interaction between yang and yin energies can lead to harmful effects on humans and plants.
    • 顔渊与孔子俱上泰山东南望吴阊门外孔子见白马引顔渊指之若见吴阊门乎顔渊曰见之有系练之状孔子抚其目而止之顔渊髪白齿落遂以病死盖精力不及圣人而强役之也 - Narrative about Confucius and Yan Hui observing something from Mount Tai, leading to Yan Hui's premature death due to overexertion.
    • 榝似茱茰而小赤色樝似梨而酢澁鹿食椒橘叶其肉自然作椒橘香江东呼蘽为藤似葛而麤大楩似豫章杼斗为栎或言皁斗棪实似柰柚似橙而酢 - Descriptions of various plants and their characteristics.
    • 淮南子曰狠者知而非知也愚者君子而非君子也戅者勇而非勇也使人相去也若玉之与石也葵之与苋也则论人易矣夫乱人者若芎防之与藁本蛇牀之与蘼芜 - Quote from the *Huainanzi* that discusses the difficulty of distinguishing between different types of people.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 尧若腊舜若腒桀纣之君垂尺余 - References to the legendary rulers Yao, Shun, Jie, and Zhou, and their physical appearances.
    • 雷公有神雷声有器安得谓之神 - References to the concept of gods and natural phenomena in ancient Chinese belief.
    • 正月阳动故始雷五月阳盛故雷秋冬阳衰故雷潜 - References to the Chinese lunar calendar and the concept of yang and yin energies.
    • 顔渊与孔子俱上泰山东南望吴阊门外孔子见白马引顔渊指之若见吴阊门乎顔渊曰见之有系练之状孔子抚其目而止之顔渊髪白齿落遂以病死盖精力不及圣人而强役之也 - References to Confucius and his disciple Yan Hui, and their philosophical and physical interactions.
    • 淮南子曰狠者知而非知也愚者君子而非君子也戅者勇而非勇也使人相去也若玉之与石也葵之与苋也则论人易矣夫乱人者若芎防之与藁本蛇牀之与蘼芜 - References to the *Huainanzi*, an important philosophical text from the Han dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 尧 - Yao, a legendary ruler.
    • 舜 - Shun, a legendary ruler.
    • 桀 - Jie, a tyrannical ruler.
    • 纣 - Zhou, a tyrannical ruler.
    • 顔渊 - Yan Hui, a disciple of Confucius.
    • 孔子 - Confucius, a famous philosopher.
    • 泰山 - Mount Tai, a sacred mountain.
    • 吴阊门 - Wu Gate, a gate in ancient China.
    • 淮南子 - *Huainanzi*, an important philosophical text.

    Plants that move grow but do not flourish; why do people who guide and move their bodies live long and not die?

    The rulers Yao, like preserved meat, and Shun, like dried meat, were tall, while the tyrants Jie and Zhou were short.

    Wang Chong said: If there is a god of thunder and an instrument for thunder, how can it be called a god? In January, yang energy begins to move, so thunder starts; in May, yang energy is at its peak, so thunder is loud; in autumn and winter, yang energy declines, so thunder is hidden. When yang energy is active, yin energy rises against it, and the two forces clash, creating a toxic force that can kill people or break trees. People can die from being struck by lightning; try pouring a bucket of water on a fire being smelted; the intense fire will produce a thunderous sound. Some say that when lightning strikes metal or stone, they are burned and melted, but why does the lacquer vessel not break? Taoists say that when lightning strikes a stone, they throw it into a well; the stone burns, and the cold water causes a loud sound.

    Yan Hui and Confucius both ascended Mount Tai and looked southeast at the Wu Gate. Confucius saw a white horse and pointed to it, asking Yan Hui, "Do you see the Wu Gate?" Yan Hui said, "I see it, but it has the appearance of a tethered rope." Confucius touched his eyes and stopped him. Yan Hui's hair turned white and his teeth fell out, and he died of illness. This was because his energy was not up to that of a sage, and he was overexerted.

    The plant jia resembles zhulian but is smaller and red. The plant sha resembles a pear but is sour and astringent. Deer eat the leaves of jiangzhu and yujue, and their flesh naturally takes on the aroma of pepper and orange. In Jiangdong, lai is called a vine, resembling kudzu but coarser. The tree pien resembles yuzhang, and zhu is a type of oak. Some say it is a type of zao. The fruit of yan resembles a crabapple, and you resembles an orange but is sour.

    The Huainanzi says: The fierce resemble knowledge but are not truly knowledgeable; the foolish resemble gentlemen but are not truly gentlemen; the rash resemble courage but are not truly courageous. People differ from each other like jade and stone, or like the kuai and xian plants. Thus, it is easy to judge people. Those who confuse people are like xiang and gao roots, or like shechuang and mihua. [44]

    蓂荚一名厯荚圣王以是占日月之数
    　　朱草状如小桑栽长三四尺枝叶皆丹汁如血朔望生落如蓂荚周而复始可以染绛成黼黻之章金银投其汁可使成圆泥投水为金浆以投酒为玊醴饮之令人长生出抱朴子
    　　骠国诸蛮并不养娑罗木子破其殻中如栁絮细织为幅服之谓之娑罗笼段
    　　蛮法煮盐成圑为颗质则以颗计之
    　　茶出银生诸山采无时杂椒姜烹而饮之
    　　生金出长傍诸山取法以春或冬先于山腹掘坑方夏水潦荡沙泥土注之坑秋始披而拣之有得片块大者重一斤或二斤小者不下三四两先纳官十分之八余许归私仍累劳効免征赋麸金出丽水河睒川有罪送淘金所为重役防同川银山出银矿私置冶官十之三诺睒川有锡山出锡
    　　苏躭郴县人尝与众儿共牧牛更直为师録每至躭直牛不逐自还既而辞母仙去又说年疫穿一井饮之可得无恙
    　　道士谭紫霄有观灯之术说人祸福必中 [45]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [45]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 蓂荚一名厯荚 - Mingjia, also known as Lijia (a type of plant used in ancient Chinese calendars).
    • 圣王以是占日月之数 - Sage kings used this to determine the number of days and months (referring to the use of the plant for calendrical purposes).
    • 朱草状如小桑栽长三四尺枝叶皆丹汁如血 - Red grass, shaped like a small mulberry tree, grows three or four feet tall with branches and leaves all red, the juice like blood (describing the appearance and properties of the red grass).
    • 朔望生落如蓂荚周而复始 - It grows and falls monthly like the Mingjia, repeating in cycles (describing the cyclical nature of the plant's growth).
    • 可以染绛成黼黻之章 - It can be used to dye red and create patterns of black and white (referring to the use of the plant for dyeing and creating patterns).
    • 金银投其汁可使成圆泥投水为金浆以投酒为玊醴饮之令人长生 - Gold and silver, when dipped in its juice, can be made into round lumps; when thrown into water, they become golden liquid; when added to wine, they turn into jade-like liquor, which, when drunk, grants immortality (describing the magical properties of the plant).
    • 骠国诸蛮并不养娑罗木子破其殻中如栁絮细织为幅服之谓之娑罗笼段 - The people of Biao Kingdom do not cultivate the seeds of the Sera tree; when broken open, they contain fine fibers like willow fluff, which are woven into fabric called Sera Longduan (describing the cultivation and use of the Sera tree).
    • 蛮法煮盐成圑为颗质则以颗计之 - The barbarian method of cooking salt into lumps, each lump counted as a unit (describing the method of salting and counting).
    • 茶出银生诸山采无时杂椒姜烹而饮之 - Tea comes from the silver-producing mountains, harvested at any time, mixed with pepper and ginger, and brewed for drinking (describing the cultivation and preparation of tea).
    • 生金出长傍诸山取法以春或冬先于山腹掘坑方夏水潦荡沙泥土注之坑秋始披而拣之有得片块大者重一斤或二斤小者不下三四两先纳官十分之八余许归私仍累劳効免征赋麸金出丽水河睒川有罪送淘金所为重役防同川银山出银矿私置冶官十之三诺睒川有锡山出锡 - Native gold is found in the mountains near Chang, harvested in spring or winter by digging pits in the mountain, filled with water and silt in summer, and then sifted in autumn. Large pieces weigh one or two catties, smaller ones at least three or four taels. Eight-tenths go to the government, the rest to the private sector, with accumulated labor exempt from taxes. Flourish gold is found in Lishui River and Heishan Creek; those with crimes are sent to the gold washing place for heavy labor. Silver is mined in the mountains of Fangtong River, with private smelting offices taking three-tenths. Tin is found in the mountains of Nuo River (describing the mining and distribution of gold, silver, and tin).
    • 苏躭郴县人尝与众儿共牧牛更直为师録每至躭直牛不逐自还既而辞母仙去又说年疫穿一井饮之可得无恙 - Su Dan, a native of Chencounty, once herded cows with other children, taking turns to be the leader. Whenever it was his turn, the cows would return on their own. Later, he left his mother to become a immortal. He also said that during an epidemic, digging a well and drinking from it could prevent illness (describing the life and magical abilities of Su Dan).
    • 道士谭紫霄有观灯之术说人祸福必中 - Taoist Tan Zixiao has the skill of observing lights, predicting people's fortune and misfortune, always accurately (describing the magical abilities of Taoist Tan Zixiao).

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 蓂荚 - A type of plant used in ancient Chinese calendars.
    • 圣王 - Sage kings, referring to wise and virtuous rulers in ancient China.
    • 朱草 - Red grass, a mythical plant with magical properties.
    • 骠国 - Biao Kingdom, a region in ancient China.
    • 娑罗木 - Sera tree, a type of tree with fine fibers used for weaving.
    • 蛮法 - Barbarian methods, referring to the practices of non-Han Chinese peoples.
    • 银生诸山 - Silver-producing mountains, referring to the locations where silver was mined.
    • 生金 - Native gold, referring to naturally occurring gold.
    • 长傍诸山 - Mountains near Chang, referring to the specific location of gold mining.
    • 苏躭 - Su Dan, a legendary figure in Chinese folklore.
    • 道士谭紫霄 - Taoist Tan Zixiao, a legendary Taoist with magical abilities.

    The Mingjia, also known as the Lijia, was used by sage kings to determine the number of days and months. The red grass, shaped like a small mulberry tree, grows three or four feet tall with branches and leaves all red, the juice like blood. It grows and falls monthly like the Mingjia, repeating in cycles. It can be used to dye red and create patterns of black and white. Gold and silver, when dipped in its juice, can be made into round lumps; when thrown into water, they become golden liquid; when added to wine, they turn into jade-like liquor, which, when drunk, grants immortality, as described in the Baopu Zi. The people of Biao Kingdom do not cultivate the seeds of the Sera tree; when broken open, they contain fine fibers like willow fluff, which are woven into fabric called Sera Longduan. The barbarian method of cooking salt into lumps, each lump counted as a unit. Tea comes from the silver-producing mountains, harvested at any time, mixed with pepper and ginger, and brewed for drinking. Native gold is found in the mountains near Chang, harvested in spring or winter by digging pits in the mountain, filled with water and silt in summer, and then sifted in autumn. Large pieces weigh one or two catties, smaller ones at least three or four taels. Eight-tenths go to the government, the rest to the private sector, with accumulated labor exempt from taxes. Flourish gold is found in Lishui River and Heishan Creek; those with crimes are sent to the gold washing place for heavy labor. Silver is mined in the mountains of Fangtong River, with private smelting offices taking three-tenths. Tin is found in the mountains of Nuo River. Su Dan, a native of Chencounty, once herded cows with other children, taking turns to be the leader. Whenever it was his turn, the cows would return on their own. Later, he left his mother to become an immortal. He also said that during an epidemic, digging a well and drinking from it could prevent illness. The Taoist Tan Zixiao has the skill of observing lights, predicting people's fortune and misfortune, always accurately. [45]

    郭颁世语云魏黄初盗发吴芮见芮尸容貌衣服并如生吴平发冡人于寿春见南蛮校尉吴纲曰君形貎何长沙王吴芮乎但君微短耳纲瞿然曰是先祖也自芮卒至发四百年至纲又四十年矣
    　　丹水有石穴蝙蝠大者多倒悬百岁者倒悬得而服之使人神仙
    　　陶居云学道之士居山宜养白犬白鸡可以辟邪苏枋木自然虫粪为紫粉
    　　秦二十一年铸金狄十二释氏之源本霍去病讨休屠王获其祭天金人武帝以为神列于甘泉宫魏黄初元年徙长安铜狄重不可致或言金狄泣因留霸城南人有见蓟子训与父老共摩铜狄曰正见铸此时计尔日已近五百年矣董卓毁其九为钱苻坚毁其二为钱其一百姓推置陜北河中或云翁仲头髻常出晋军至髻不复出
    　　陈正敏所取者陈抟李渎林逋魏野皆遯世之士希夷先生陈抟服气辟谷二十余年或寝百余日不起太平兴国中召入复放还山一旦自作表云臣抟大数有终圣朝难恋已于今月二十二日化形于莲华峰如期而卒七日支体犹温五色云蔽洞口弥月不散已百余岁矣 [46]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [46]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 盗发吴芮 - Robbers dug up Wu Rui's grave.
    • 见芮尸容貌衣服并如生 - Seeing Wu Rui's body, appearance, clothing, and all were like a living person.
    • 吴平发冢人 - Wu Ping, the grave robbers.
    • 于寿春见南蛮校尉吴纲 - In Shouchun, seeing Wu Gang, the Southern Barbarian Commandant.
    • 君形貌何似长沙王吴芮乎 - Your appearance is like King Wu Rui of Changsha.
    • 但君微短耳 - But you are slightly shorter.
    • 瞿然 - Startled or shocked.
    • 先祖也 - Ancestor.
    • 自芮卒至冢发四百年 - From Wu Rui's death until the grave was dug up, four hundred years passed.
    • 至纲又四十年矣 - Until Wu Gang, another forty years have passed.
    • 丹水有石穴 - The Dan River has a stone cave.
    • 蝙蝠大者多倒悬 - Large bats often hang upside down.
    • 百岁者倒悬得而服之使人神仙 - Those that are a hundred years old, if hung upside down and eaten, can make a person become a god.
    • 陶居云 - Tao Mengju says.
    • 学道之士 - Those who study the Dao.
    • 居山宜养白犬白鸡 - Living in the mountains, one should raise white dogs and white chickens.
    • 可以辟邪 - Can ward off evil.
    • 苏枋木自然虫粪为紫粉 - Sufang wood naturally produces purple powder from insect droppings.
    • 秦二十一年铸金狄十二 - In the twenty-first year of Qin, twelve golden statues were cast.
    • 释氏之源本 - The origin of Buddhism.
    • 霍去病讨休屠王获其祭天金人 - Huo Qubing campaigned against Xutu Wang and captured the golden statue used for worshipping heaven.
    • 武帝以为神列于甘泉宫 - Emperor Wu of Han considered it a god and placed it in Ganquan Palace.
    • 魏黄初元年徙长安铜狄重不可致 - In the first year of Huangchu in Wei, the bronze statues in Chang'an were too heavy to move.
    • 或言金狄泣因留霸城南 - Some say the bronze statues wept and were left in the south of Bachi.
    • 人有见蓟子训与父老共摩铜狄 - Someone saw Jizixun and the elders rubbing the bronze statues together.
    • 正见铸此时计尔日已近五百年矣 - Just saw the casting at that time, calculated from that day, it has been nearly five hundred years.
    • 董卓毁其九为钱 - Dong Zhuo destroyed nine of them into coins.
    • 苻坚毁其二为钱 - Fu Jian destroyed two of them into coins.
    • 其一百姓推置陜北河中 - One of them, the people pushed and placed it in the river north of Shanxi.
    • 或云翁仲头髻常出 - Some say the topknots of the statues often appear.
    • 晋军至髻不复出 - The Jin army arrived, and the topknots no longer appeared.
    • 陈正敏所取者 - The ones Chen Zhengmin chose.
    • 陈抟李渎林逋魏野 - Chen Tuan, Li Du, Lin Bu, and Wei Ye, all historical figures.
    • 遁世之士 - Reclusive scholars.
    • 希夷先生陈抟 - Mystic and ethereal master Chen Tuan.
    • 服气辟谷 - Practicing breath control and abstaining from food.
    • 太平兴国中召入复放还山 - During the Taiping Xingguo period, summoned into the court and then sent back to the mountains.
    • 一旦自作表云 - One day, wrote a memorial saying.
    • 臣抟大数有终圣朝难恋 - I, Chen Tuan, the great cycle is coming to an end, difficult to stay in the holy court.
    • 已于今月二十二日化形于莲华峰 - On the twenty-second day of this month, transformed into a form at Lianhua Peak.
    • 如期而卒 - Died as scheduled.
    • 七日支体犹温 - Seven days later, limbs were still warm.
    • 五色云蔽洞口弥月不散 - Five-colored clouds covered the cave entrance for a month and did not disperse.
    • 已百余岁矣 - Already over a hundred years old.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 郭颁世语 - A historical record or collection of anecdotes, likely written by Guo Ban.
    • 吴芮 - Wu Rui, a historical figure from the early Han dynasty.
    • 吴平 - Wu Ping, another historical figure.
    • 寿春 - Shouchun, a city in Anhui province.
    • 南蛮校尉 - A military title, indicating a high-ranking official.
    • 吴纲 - Wu Gang, a historical figure.
    • 长沙王 - King of Changsha, a title held by Wu Rui.
    • 丹水 - The Dan River.

    Guo Ban's Shi Yu records that during the Huangchu era of Wei, robbers dug up the tomb of Wu Rui and saw his body, whose appearance and clothing were all like those of a living person. Wu Ping, a grave robber from Shouchun, saw Nanman Xiaowei Wu Gang and said, "Your appearance is like that of King Wu Rui of Changsha, but you are slightly shorter." Wu Gang was startled and said, "He is my ancestor." From the time of Wu Rui's death to the time the tomb was dug up was four hundred years, and then another forty years had passed until Wu Gang.

    The Dan River has a stone cave where large bats often hang upside down. Those that are a hundred years old, if hung upside down and eaten, can make a person become a god.

    Tao Mengju said that those who study the Dao should live in the mountains and raise white dogs and white chickens, which can ward off evil. The Su Fang wood naturally produces purple powder from insect excrement.

    In the twenty-first year of Qin, twelve golden statues were cast. The origin of Buddhism is based on Huo Qubing's campaign against Xutu Wang, during which he captured a golden statue used for worshipping heaven. Emperor Wu of Han considered it a god and placed it in Ganquan Palace. In the first year of Huangchu in Wei, the bronze statues in Chang'an were too heavy to move. Some said that the bronze statues wept, so they were left in the south of Bachi. Someone saw Jizixun and the elders together rubbing the bronze statues and said, "Just saw the casting at that time, calculated from that day, it has been nearly five hundred years." Dong Zhuo destroyed nine of them into coins, and Fu Jian destroyed two of them into coins. One of them, the people pushed and placed it in the river north of Shanxi. Some say that the topknots of the statues often appeared, but when the Jin army arrived, the topknots no longer appeared.

    Chen Zhengmin chose Chen Tuan, Li Du, Lin Bu, and Wei Ye, all reclusive scholars. The mystic and ethereal Master Chen Tuan practiced breath control and abstained from food for over twenty years. Sometimes he slept for over a hundred days without waking. During the Taiping Xingguo period, he was summoned into the court and then sent back to the mountains. One day, he wrote a memorial saying, "I, Chen Tuan, the great cycle is coming to an end, the holy court is difficult to stay in. On the twenty-second day of this month, I will transform into a god on Lianhua Peak." He died as scheduled. Seven days after his death, his limbs were still warm. Five-colored clouds covered the cave entrance for a month without dispersing. He was already over a hundred years old. [46]

    刘向列仙葛洪神仙沈玢续仙曾慥集仙传以异代事着于本朝者自尔朱洞吕嵓陈抟贺元曾被祖宗眷异者名臣如欧阳修刘凡石延年皆在贺元仕石晋为水部员外郎章圣东封谒道左天圣初使弟子喻澄诣阙献浮屠老子像直数十万
    　　毛女在华山山客猎师世世见之体生毛自言秦始皇宫人陈抟在华山或谤以与毛女往来
    　　孔安国撰孔子弟子七十二人刘向传列仙亦七十二人皇甫士安撰高士亦七十二人陈长文撰耆旧亦七十二人
    　　李泌絶粒五六载身轻能行于屛风上引指使气可以吹烛至灭导引骨珊然有声号鏁子骨
    　　鬻子名熊楚人为文王师年九十见文王曰使臣捕兽逐麋已老矣使臣坐策国事尚少也
    　　按葛仙公云素书乃河上公以授汉文帝也黄石公所授张子房多云隂符非也一云三略乃子房所着非所授之书或曰河上公授汉文帝不应于子房墓中得之黄石公丹书身之八杀贪残奸酗狡佞讦愎命之四业背惠恃已狎不肖妬贤能 [47]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [47]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 劉向列仙傳葛洪神仙傳沈玢續仙傳曾慥集仙傳 - Titles of books that compile biographies of immortals and legendary figures.
    • 異代事著於本朝者 - Events from different dynasties being recorded in the current dynasty's history.
    • 自爾朱洞呂嵓陳摶賀元曾被祖宗眷異者 - Individuals who were favored by the ancestors of the current dynasty.
    • 名臣如歐陽修劉凡石延年皆在 - Famous officials such as Ouyang Xiu, Liu Fan, and Shi Yanian.
    • 賀元仕石晉為水部員外郎 - He Yuan served as an external officer of the Water Department during the Later Jin dynasty.
    • 天聖初使弟子喻澄詣闕獻浮屠老子像直數十萬 - During the early Tian Sheng period, he sent his disciple Yu Cheng to the imperial court to present statues of Buddha and Laozi, valued at tens of thousands of coins.
    • 毛女在華山山客獵師世世見之 - Describes a woman with fur on her body, seen by hunters and their teachers on Mount Hua generation after generation.
    • 體生毛自言秦始皇宮人 - She says she was a palace woman during the Qin Dynasty.
    • 陳摶在華山或謗以與毛女往來 - Chen Tuan, also on Mount Hua, was sometimes criticized for associating with the fur-covered woman.
    • 孔安國撰孔子弟子七十二人 - Kong Anguo compiled a list of seventy-two disciples of Confucius.
    • 劉向傳列仙亦七十二人 - Liu Xiang compiled a list of seventy-two immortals.
    • 皇甫士安撰高士亦七十二人 - Huangfu Shian compiled a list of seventy-two recluses.
    • 陳長文撰耆舊亦七十二人 - Chen Changwen compiled a list of seventy-two elderly scholars.
    • 李泌絕粒五六載身輕能行於屏風上 - Li Bi abstained from food for five or six years and could walk on a folding screen.
    • 引指使氣可以吹燭至滅 - He could direct his breath to extinguish a candle.
    • 導引骨珊然有聲號鏁子骨 - His bones made a tinkling sound when he moved, known as 'lock bone'.
