    • 鬻子名熊楚人為文王師年九十見文王 - Yu Zi, also known as Xiong, was a teacher to King Wen of Zhou and met him at the age of ninety.
    • 使臣捕獸逐麋已老矣使臣坐策國事尚少也 - If asked to hunt animals, he would say he is too old, but if asked to advise on state affairs, he would say he is still young.
    • 按葛仙公云素書乃河上公以授漢文帝也 - According to Ge Xian Gong, the *Sushu* (White Book) was passed down from He Shang Gong to Emperor Wen of Han.
    • 黃石公所授張子房多云隂符非也 - The *Yinfu* (Dark Symbol) was not what Zhang Zifang received from Yellow Stone Gong.
    • 一云三略乃子房所著非所授之書 - Another version says *Sanlue* (Three Strategies) was written by Zhang Zifang, not given to him.
    • 或曰河上公授漢文帝不應於子房墓中得之 - Some say that since He Shang Gong taught Emperor Wen of Han, it wouldn't be found in Zhang Zifang's tomb.
    • 黄石公丹书身之八杀贪残奸酗狡佞讦愎命之四业背惠恃已狎不肖妒贤能 - Yellow Stone Gong's red book describes eight kinds of people who should be punished and four types of behavior that should be avoided.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 劉向 - A historian and literary critic of the Western Han Dynasty.
    • 葛洪 - A Daoist alchemist and writer of the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 沈玢 - A scholar of the Southern Tang Dynasty.
    • 曾慥 - A Song Dynasty scholar and compiler of Daoist texts.
    • 異代事著於本朝者 - Refers to the practice of recording historical events from previous dynasties in the current dynasty's historical records.
    • 自爾朱洞呂嵓陳摶賀元曾被祖宗眷異者 - Refers to individuals who were favored by the ancestors of the current dynasty.
    • 名臣如歐陽修劉凡石延年皆在 - Mentions famous officials such as Ouyang Xiu, Liu Fan, and Shi Yanian.
    • 賀元仕石晉為水部員外郎 - Indicates that He Yuan served as an external officer of the Water Department during the Later Jin dynasty.
    • 天聖初使弟子喻澄詣闕獻浮屠老子像直數十萬 - During the early Tian Sheng period, he sent his disciple Yu Cheng to the imperial court to present statues of Buddha and Laozi, valued at tens of thousands of coins.
    • 毛女在華山山客獵師世世見之 - Describes a woman with fur on her body, seen by hunters and their teachers on Mount Hua generation after generation.
    • 體生毛自言秦始皇宮人 - She says she was a palace woman during the Qin Dynasty.
    • 陳摶在華山或謗以與毛女往來 - Chen Tuan, also on Mount Hua, was sometimes criticized for associating with the fur-covered woman.
    • 孔安國撰孔子弟子七十二人 - Kong Anguo compiled a list of seventy-two disciples of Confucius.
    • 劉向傳列仙亦七十二人 - Liu Xiang compiled a list of seventy-two immortals.
    • 皇甫士安撰高士亦七十二人 - Huangfu Shian compiled a list of seventy-two recluses.
    • 陳長文撰耆舊亦七十二人 - Chen Changwen compiled a list of seventy-two elderly scholars.
    • 李泌絕粒五六載身輕能行於屏風上 - Li Bi abstained from food for five or six years and could walk on a folding screen.
    • 引指使氣可以吹燭至滅 - He could direct his breath to extinguish a candle.
    • 導引骨珊然有聲號鏁子骨 - His bones made a tinkling sound when he moved, known as 'lock bone'.
    • 鬻子名熊楚人為文王師年九十見文王 - Yu Zi, also known as Xiong, was a teacher to King Wen of Zhou and met him at the age of ninety.
    • 使臣捕獸逐麋已老矣使臣坐策國事尚少也 - If asked to hunt animals, he would say he is too old, but if asked to advise on state affairs, he would say he is still young.
    • 按葛仙公云素書乃河上公以授漢文帝也 - According to Ge Xian Gong, the *Sushu* (White Book) was passed down from He Shang Gong to Emperor Wen of Han.
    • 黃石公所授張子房多云隂符非也 - The *Yinfu* (Dark Symbol) was not what Zhang Zifang received from Yellow Stone Gong.
    • 一云三略乃子房所著非所授之書 - Another version says *Sanlue* (Three Strategies) was written by Zhang Zifang, not given to him.
    • 或曰河上公授漢文帝不應於子房墓中得之 - Some say that since He Shang Gong taught Emperor Wen of Han, it wouldn't be found in Zhang Zifang's tomb.
    • 黄石公丹书身之八杀贪残奸酗狡佞讦愎命之四业背惠恃已狎不肖妒贤能 - Yellow Stone Gong's red book describes eight kinds of people who should be punished and four types of behavior that should be avoided.

    The Transmission of Immortals by Liu Xiang, the Record of Immortals by Ge Hong, the Continuation of Immortals by Shen Bin, and the Collection of Immortals by Zeng Cao compile stories of immortals and legendary figures from different dynasties that are recorded in the current dynasty. Among those favored by the ancestors of the current dynasty are Zhu Dun, Lü Yan, Chen Tuan, He Yuan, and others. Famous officials such as Ouyang Xiu, Liu Fan, and Shi Yanian are also included. He Yuan served as an external officer of the Water Department during the Later Jin dynasty. During the early Tian Sheng period, he sent his disciple Yu Cheng to the imperial court to present statues of Buddha and Laozi, valued at tens of thousands of coins.

    There is a woman with fur on her body, known as the "Fur Woman," who has been seen by hunters and their teachers on Mount Hua generation after generation. She claims to have been a palace woman during the Qin Dynasty. Chen Tuan, also on Mount Hua, was sometimes criticized for associating with the Fur Woman.

    Kong Anguo compiled a list of seventy-two disciples of Confucius, Liu Xiang compiled a list of seventy-two immortals, Huangfu Shian compiled a list of seventy-two recluses, and Chen Changwen compiled a list of seventy-two elderly scholars.

    Li Bi abstained from food for five or six years and could walk on a folding screen. He could direct his breath to extinguish a candle. When he moved, his bones made a tinkling sound, known as "lock bone."

    Yu Zi, also known as Xiong, was a teacher to King Wen of Zhou and met him at the age of ninety. He said, "If you ask me to hunt animals, I am too old; but if you ask me to advise on state affairs, I am still young."

    According to Ge Xian Gong, the Sushu (White Book) was passed down from He Shang Gong to Emperor Wen of Han. The Yinfu (Dark Symbol) was not what Zhang Zifang received from Yellow Stone Gong. Another version says Sanlue (Three Strategies) was written by Zhang Zifang, not given to him. Some say that since He Shang Gong taught Emperor Wen of Han, it wouldn't be found in Zhang Zifang's tomb. Yellow Stone Gong's red book describes eight kinds of people who should be punished and four types of behavior that should be avoided: those who are greedy, cruel, treacherous, and violent; those who are deceitful, cunning, and arrogant; those who betray favors and rely on themselves; those who associate with the unworthy and envy the virtuous. [47]

    昔有一人好道而不知求道之方惟朝夕拜跪向一枯树輙云乞长生如此二十八年不倦枯木一旦忽然生华华又有汁甜如有人教令食之遂取此华及汁并食之食讫即仙
    　　刘少翁曽数入太华山礼拜向山如此二十年遂忽一旦得见西岳丈人授其仙道
    　　一人数旦旦诣河边拜河水如此十年河侯河伯遂与相见与之白璧十双教授水行不溺法
    　　薏苡一名簳珠子蒸令气馏曝干挼取之作饭麪主不饥
    　　作干姜法水淹三日毕置流水中六日更去皮然后曝干入瓮瓶谓之醸也
    　　淫羊藿一名仙灵脾淫羊一日百遍食藿所致
    　　新生艾作干菜妙作馄饨吞三五枚以饭压之良疗一切鬼气
    　　苏合香本草云生中台川陶云是师子屎又云是诸香汁煎之非一物从西域来者如紫檀重如石蘡薁是山葡萄 [48]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [48]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 好道而不知求道之方 - Means 'loves the Dao but does not know the way to seek it,' highlighting a common theme in Daoist literature of loving the Dao without understanding its path.
    • 惟朝夕拜跪向一枯树 - Means 'only worships and kneels to a dry tree all day and night,' indicating a specific ritualistic practice.
    • 輙云乞长生 - Means 'always says to beg for immortality,' emphasizing the repetitive nature of the prayer.
    • 如此二十八年不倦 - Means 'like this for twenty-eight years without tiring,' highlighting the persistence of the practice.
    • 枯木一旦忽然生华 - Means 'the dry tree suddenly blossoms,' indicating an unexpected and miraculous event.
    • 华又有汁甜如 - Means 'the flowers have sap as sweet as honey,' describing the miraculous quality of the sap.
    • 遂取此华及汁并食之 - Means 'then he took the flowers and sap and ate them together,' indicating the consumption of the miraculous substance.
    • 食讫即仙 - Means 'after eating, he became an immortal,' indicating the immediate result of the miraculous consumption.
    • 刘少翁曽数入太华山礼拜向山如此二十年 - Means 'Liu Shao-weng often entered Taihuashan to worship the mountain like this for twenty years,' highlighting his persistent practice.
    • 遂忽一旦得见西岳丈人授其仙道 - Means 'he suddenly met the Western Peak Elder and was taught the path to immortality,' indicating the immediate result of his persistent practice.
    • 一人数旦旦诣河边拜河水如此十年 - Means 'one person went to the river every day to worship the river water like this for ten years,' highlighting the persistent practice.
    • 河侯河伯遂与相见与之白璧十双教授水行不溺法 - Means 'the River Lord and River God then met him, gave him ten pairs of white jade, and taught him the method of walking on water without drowning,' indicating the immediate result of his persistent practice.
    • 薏苡一名簳珠子蒸令气馏曝干挼取之作饭麪主不饥 - Means 'Coix seed, also known as bamboo pearl, is steamed until steam distills, dried in the sun, and then made into rice or noodles to prevent hunger,' indicating a medicinal use.
    • 作干姜法水淹三日毕置流水中六日更去皮然后曝干入瓮瓶谓之醸也 - Means 'The method of making dried ginger involves soaking it in water for three days, then placing it in running water for six days, removing the skin, and then drying it in the sun before placing it in jars or bottles, which is called 'fermentation,' indicating a specific preparation method.
    • 淫羊藿一名仙灵脾淫羊一日百遍食藿所致 - Means 'Epimedium, also known as fairy spleen, is caused by a goat eating it a hundred times a day,' indicating a medicinal plant with a specific origin story.
    • 新生艾作干菜妙作馄饨吞三五枚以饭压之良疗一切鬼气 - Means 'Newly harvested mugwort is made into dried vegetables, which are excellent for making dumplings. Eating three or five dumplings with rice can effectively treat all kinds of ghostly illnesses,' indicating a medicinal use.
    • 苏合香本草云生中台川陶云是师子屎又云是诸香汁煎之非一物从西域来者如紫檀重如石蘡薁是山葡萄 - Means 'Storax, according to the *Bencao*, is said to grow in the Central Plains. Tao Hongjing said it is lion dung, and others say it is a mixture of various fragrant juices. It is not a single substance but comes from the Western Regions, like sandalwood, heavy as stone, and is also known as mountain grapes,' indicating a complex description of the source and properties of storax.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 好道 - Refers to the pursuit of the Dao, a central theme in Daoist philosophy.
    • 太华山 - Refers to Taihuashan, a famous mountain in Shaanxi Province known for its spiritual significance.
    • 西岳丈人 - Refers to the Western Peak Elder, a deity associated with immortality and Daoist practices.
    • 河侯河伯 - Refers to the River Lord and River God, deities associated with rivers and water.
    • 薏苡 - A medicinal plant used in traditional Chinese medicine.
    • 干姜 - A processed form of ginger used in traditional Chinese medicine.
    • 淫羊藿 - A medicinal plant used in traditional Chinese medicine.
    • 艾 - Mugwort, a plant used in traditional Chinese medicine and rituals.
    • 苏合香 - Storax, a resin used in traditional Chinese medicine and perfumery.
    • 中台川 - Refers to the Central Plains, a region in China known for its historical and cultural significance.
    • 师子屎 - Refers to the excrement of lions, which was believed to have medicinal properties.
    • 诸香汁 - Refers to the mixture of various fragrant juices, indicating a complex preparation method.
    • 紫檀 - Sandalwood, a valuable wood used in traditional Chinese medicine and perfumery.
    • 石蘡薁 - Mountain grapes, a type of wild grape used in traditional Chinese medicine.

    Proper Nouns

    • 刘少翁 - The name of the person who practices Daoism.
    • 太华山 - The name of the mountain where Liu Shao-weng practices.
    • 西岳丈人 - The name of the deity who teaches immortality.
    • 河侯 - The name of the River Lord.
    • 河伯 - The name of the River God.
    • 薏苡 - The name of the medicinal plant.
    • 干姜 - The name of the processed form of ginger.
    • 淫羊藿 - The name of the medicinal plant.
    • 艾 - The name of the plant used in traditional Chinese medicine and rituals.
    • 苏合香 - The name of the resin.
    • 中台川 - The name of the region.
    • 师子屎 - The name of the excrement of lions.
    • 诸香汁 - The name of the mixture of various fragrant juices.
    • 紫檀 - The name of the wood.
    • 石蘡薁 - The name of the wild grape.

    Final Translation

    Once upon a time, there was a man who loved the Dao but did not know how to seek it. He only worshipped and knelt to a dry tree every day and night, always saying to beg for immortality. He did this for twenty-eight years without tiring. Suddenly, the dry tree blossomed, and the flowers had sap as sweet as honey. Someone taught him to eat the flowers and sap, so he took them and ate them together. After eating, he became an immortal.

    Liu Shao-weng often entered Taihuashan to worship the mountain like this for twenty years. He suddenly met the Western Peak Elder and was taught the path to immortality.

    One person went to the river every day to worship the river water like this for ten years. The River Lord and River God then met him, gave him ten pairs of white jade, and taught him the method of walking on water without drowning.

    Coix seed, also known as bamboo pearl, is steamed until steam distills, dried in the sun, and then made into rice or noodles to prevent hunger.

    The method of making dried ginger involves soaking it in water for three days, then placing it in running water for six days, removing the skin, and then drying it in the sun before placing it in jars or bottles, which is called 'fermentation.'

    Epimedium, also known as fairy spleen, is caused by a goat eating it a hundred times a day.

    Newly harvested mugwort is made into dried vegetables, which are excellent for making dumplings. Eating three or five dumplings with rice can effectively treat all kinds of ghostly illnesses.

    Storax, according to the Bencao, is said to grow in the Central Plains. Tao Hongjing said it is lion dung, and others say it is a mixture of various fragrant juices. It is not a single substance but comes from the Western Regions, like sandalwood, heavy as stone, and is also known as mountain grapes. [48]

    江东人通名杨栁杨叶短栁叶长
    　　高座道人不肯作汉语或问此意简文曰此简应对之烦和尚一名尸梨宻宋云高座西域人三国王子以譲弟遂为沙门永嘉中到中国
    　　顺帝时琅琊宫崇上其师于吉于曲阳泉水上得神书百七十卷号太平清领书其言以隂阳五行为家而多巫觋杂语后张角颇有其书按江表传道士于吉东来呉防立精舍烧香读书孙策斩之日昔南阳张津为交州刺史常着绛帕头鼔琴焚香读邪俗道书为蛮夷所杀此甚无益
    　　真诰云吞琅玕之华而更营丘墓者羡门子高丘子洪崖先生羡门子墓在幽州渔阳高丘子墓在中山洪崖先生墓在武威此三郡县人并云上古死人空
    　　王子乔墓在京陵战国时有发墓者惟见一劒
    　　汉地理志大江九江中一源而三目
    　　扬雄益州箴岩岩岷山古曰梁州鲁城北有孔子学堂蜀有文翁讲堂北海有郑司农儒林讲堂宅亦曰第言有甲乙之次第也汉书成帝有甲观中画堂魏有茅堂

    　　续博物志卷七
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　续博物志卷八 [49]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [49]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 江东人通名杨栁杨叶短栁叶长 - This phrase is a riddle or puzzle, where '杨栁' and '杨叶短栁叶长' are clues to a person's name. The answer is likely a play on words or a pun.
    • 高座道人不肯作汉语 - Refers to a Buddhist monk who refuses to speak in Chinese, indicating a preference for the original language.
    • 此简应对之烦和尚一名尸梨宻 - Refers to a monk named Shi Limi, who is likely a specific figure in Buddhist tradition.
    • 宋云高座西域人 - '宋云' and '高座' are proper names, with '西域人' indicating that the person is from the Western Regions (Central Asia).
    • 永嘉中到中国 - '永嘉' refers to the Yongjia period (307-313 CE) during the Western Jin Dynasty.
    • 顺帝时琅琊宫崇上其师于吉于曲阳泉水上得神书百七十卷号太平清领书 - '顺帝' refers to Emperor Shun of Han (125-144 CE). '太平清领书' is a Daoist text.
    • 其言以阴阳五行为家而多巫觋杂语 - This phrase describes the content of the '太平清领书,' which combines Daoist philosophy with shamanistic practices.
    • 后张角颇有其书 - '张角' refers to Zhang Jiao, a leader of the Yellow Turban Rebellion, who used Daoist texts.
    • 按江表传道士于吉东来呉防立精舍烧香读书孙策斩之 - '江表传' is a historical text, and '孙策' is a warlord during the late Han Dynasty.
    • 昔南阳张津为交州刺史常着绛帕头鼓琴焚香读邪俗道书为蛮夷所杀此甚无益 - '张津' is a historical figure, and '绛帕头' refers to a red headscarf.
    • 真诰云吞琅玕之华而更营丘墓者羡门子高丘子洪崖先生 - '真诰' is a Daoist text, and '羡门子' and '高丘子' are legendary figures.
    • 王子乔墓在京陵战国时有发墓者惟见一劒 - '王子乔' is a legendary figure, and '京陵' is a place name.
    • 汉地理志大江九江中一源而三目 - '汉地理志' is a historical text, and '大江九江' refers to the Yangtze River and its tributaries.
    • 扬雄益州箴岩岩岷山古曰梁州鲁城北有孔子学堂蜀有文翁讲堂北海有郑司农儒林讲堂宅亦曰第言有甲乙之次第也 - '扬雄' is a famous scholar, and '益州箴' is a text he wrote. '文翁讲堂' and '郑司农儒林讲堂' are historical educational institutions.
    • 汉书成帝有甲观中画堂魏有茅堂 - '汉书' is a historical text, and '甲观中画堂' and '茅堂' are architectural terms.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 江东人通名杨栁杨叶短栁叶长 - This is a riddle that likely refers to a person's name or a play on words.
    • 高座道人不肯作汉语 - Refers to a Buddhist monk who refuses to speak in Chinese, emphasizing the importance of the original language.
    • 尸梨宻 - A proper name, likely referring to a specific monk or figure in Buddhist tradition.
    • 宋云高座西域人 - '宋云' and '高座' are proper names, with '西域人' indicating that the person is from the Western Regions (Central Asia).
    • 永嘉中到中国 - '永嘉' refers to the Yongjia period (307-313 CE) during the Western Jin Dynasty.
    • 顺帝时琅琊宫崇上其师于吉于曲阳泉水上得神书百七十卷号太平清领书 - '顺帝' refers to Emperor Shun of Han (125-144 CE). '太平清领书' is a Daoist text.
    • 张角 - A leader of the Yellow Turban Rebellion who used Daoist texts.
    • 孙策 - A warlord during the late Han Dynasty.
    • 张津 - A historical figure who was killed by local tribes.
    • 真诰 - A Daoist text.
    • 王子乔 - A legendary figure.
    • 京陵 - A place name.
    • 汉地理志 - A historical text.
    • 大江九江 - Refers to the Yangtze River and its tributaries.
    • 扬雄 - A famous scholar.
    • 益州箴 - A text written by Yang Xiong.
    • 文翁讲堂 - A historical educational institution.
    • 郑司农儒林讲堂 - Another historical educational institution.
    • 汉书 - A historical text.

    Proper Nouns

    • 江东人 - People from the Jiangdong region.
    • 杨栁 - A person's name or a play on words.
    • 杨叶短栁叶长 - A person's name or a play on words.
    • 高座道人 - A Buddhist monk.
    • 尸梨宻 - A proper name, likely referring to a specific monk or figure in Buddhist tradition.
    • 宋云 - A proper name.
    • 高座 - A proper name.
    • 西域人 - A person from the Western Regions (Central Asia).
    • 永嘉 - The Yongjia period (307-313 CE) during the Western Jin Dynasty.
    • 顺帝 - Emperor Shun of Han (125-144 CE).
    • 琅琊宫崇 - A historical figure.
    • 于吉 - A historical figure.
    • 曲阳 - A place name.
    • 泉水 - A place name.
    • 太平清领书 - A Daoist text.
    • 张角 - A leader of the Yellow Turban Rebellion.
    • 孙策 - A warlord during the late Han Dynasty.
    • 张津 - A historical figure.
    • 绛帕头 - A red headscarf.
    • 真诰 - A Daoist text.
    • 羡门子 - A legendary figure.
    • 高丘子 - A legendary figure.
    • 洪崖先生 - A legendary figure.
    • 王子乔 - A legendary figure.
    • 京陵 - A place name.
    • 汉地理志 - A historical text.
    • 大江九江 - The Yangtze River and its tributaries.
    • 扬雄 - A famous scholar.
    • 益州箴 - A text written by Yang Xiong.
    • 文翁讲堂 - A historical educational institution.
    • 郑司农儒林讲堂 - Another historical educational institution.
    • 汉书 - A historical text.

    People from Jiangdong are often called "杨栁" or "杨叶短栁叶长," which is a play on words or a riddle.
    The High Seat monk refuses to speak in Chinese. Someone asked him about the meaning, and Jianwen replied, "This is a simple way to respond, but it's too complicated for monks. One of them is named Shilim." Song Yun, the High Seat monk, is a Western Regions person. According to the Three Kingdoms tradition, a prince gave his position to his younger brother and became a monk. He arrived in China during the Yongjia period.
    During the reign of Emperor Shun, a man from Langya named Gong Chong presented his teacher Yu Ji, who had obtained a divine book of 170 volumes called Tai Ping Qing Ling Shu (Peaceful Governance Scripture) by the waters of Quyang. The book combines the principles of Yin-Yang and the Five Elements with shamanistic practices. Later, Zhang Jiao had a copy of this book. According to the Jiangbiao (Records of Jiangdong), the Daoist Yu Ji came from the East and established a study hall in Wu, where he burned incense and read books. Sun Ce ordered his execution. It was said that in the past, Zhang Jin, the Governor of Jiaozhou, often wore a red headscarf, played the qin, burned incense, and read Daoist books that were considered heretical. He was killed by local tribes, which was very detrimental.
    The True Rites (Zhen Gao) mentions that those who swallowed the flowers of the Linggan tree and built tombs in the hills were Xianmenzi, Gaohuzi, and Hongyajianshi. Xianmenzi's tomb is in Youzhou's Yuyang, Gaohuzi's tomb is in Zhongshan, and Hongyajianshi's tomb is in Wuwei. The people of these three counties all claim that these are the tombs of ancient deceased people.
    The tomb of Prince Ziqiao is in Jingling. During the Warring States period, when someone opened the tomb, they only found a sword.
    According to the History of the Han Dynasty, the Yangtze River and its nine tributaries have one source but three outlets.
    Yang Xiong wrote the Yizhou Zhen (Admonition to the State of Yi), which describes the rugged mountains of Minshan, an ancient region called Liangzhou. In the north of Lucheng, there was a Confucian academy, and in Shu, there was the Wen翁讲堂 (Wen翁 Lecture Hall). In the north of the Bohai Sea, there was the Zheng Sinao儒林讲堂 (Zheng Sinao Confucian Lecture Hall). The term "宅" (residence) is also used to mean "第" (rank), indicating a hierarchy.
    According to the Book of Han, Emperor Cheng had a viewing pavilion with painted halls, and the Wei dynasty had a thatched pavilion. [49]

    宋　李石　撰
    　　后汉襄楷延熹中上疏云宫中立黄老浮屠祠陛下嗜欲不去岂获其祚或云老子入西北为浮屠浮屠不三宿桑下不欲乆生恩爱精之至也天神遗以好女浮屠曰此但革囊盛血遂不盼之
    　　汉帝问陈蕃徐稚宏孰为后先蕃答生公族不镂自雕至于徐稚杰出薄城故宜为先
    　　伏生为秦博士文帝撰五经尚书大传使掌故欧阳生等受尚书于伏生漯水有征君伏生墓
    　　晋书地道志曰辽西人见辽水上有浮棺欲破之语曰我孤竹君也汝破我何为因为立祠或曰汉灵帝时辽西太守廉翻因梦得之【此与前志小异】
    　　沈彬保大中以尚书致仕居江西高安手植一木于平野戒诸子曰必葬我此地子孙伐木掘土深丈余得一石椁上刋八字云开成二年寿椁一所即葬之
    　　墨子恶乐故朝歌回车论譔考谶曰邑名朝歌顔子不舍七十弟子掩目宰予独顾由蹷堕车宋均曰子路患宰予顾视凶地故以足蹷之
    　　齐时有发古得铭云青州二十子东海女郎河东贾昊以为司马越女嫁为茍晞子妇验之果然 [50]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [50]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 上疏 - Presenting a memorial or written petition to the emperor.
    • 延熹 - The reign title of Emperor Huan of Han.
    • 黄老 - Huang-Lao philosophy, a blend of Daoist and Legalist thought.
    • 浮屠 - Another term for Buddhism, derived from the Sanskrit 'Buddha'.
    • 嗜欲 - Strong desires or lusts.
    • 祚 - Mandate or destiny.
    • 三宿桑下 - A metaphor for avoiding attachment.
    • 革囊盛血 - Leather bag filled with blood, a metaphor for the impermanence and impurity of the body.
    • 镂自雕 - Carved out, metaphor for self-cultivation.
    • 薄城 - Thin city, a metaphor for simplicity and purity.
    • 博士 - Scholar-official.
    • 五经 - Five Classics.
    • 尚书大传 - Great Commentary on the *Shangshu*.
    • 掌故 - Scholar-officials responsible for historical records.
    • 漯水 - Lü Water.
    • 浮棺 - Floating coffin.
    • 孤竹君 - Lord of Guxu, a legendary figure.
    • 梦得之 - Had a dream about it.
    • 致仕 - Retired from office.
    • 寿椁 - Coffin for a long life.
    • 开成二年 - Year Two of the Kaiceng era.
    • 恶乐 - Disliked music.
    • 朝歌 - Town of Chaoguo.
    • 七十弟子掩目 - Seventy disciples covered their eyes.
    • 独顾 - Looked back.
    • 蹷堕车 - Fell from the chariot.
    • 古 - Ancient tomb.
    • 铭 - Inscription.
    • 青州二十子 - Twenty sons of Qingzhou.
    • 东海女郎 - Daughter of the East Sea.
    • 河东贾昊 - Jia Hao of Hedong.
    • 司马越 - Sima Yue, a historical figure.
    • 茍晞 - Jixi, a historical figure.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 后汉 - Later Han dynasty.
    • 黄老 - Huang-Lao philosophy.
    • 浮屠 - Buddhism.
    • 老子 - Laozi, the legendary founder of Daoism.
    • 陈蕃 - Chen Fan, a prominent official.
    • 徐稚 - Xu Zhi, a reclusive scholar.
    • 伏生 - Fu Sheng, a scholar of the Qin dynasty.
    • 文帝 - Emperor Wen of Han.
    • 五经 - Five Classics.
    • 尚书大传 - Great Commentary on the *Shangshu*.
    • 欧阳生 - Ouyang Sheng, a scholar-official.
    • 漯水 - Lü Water.
    • 晋书 - *Jin Shu* (Book of Jin).
    • 辽西 - Liaoxi, a region in present-day Liaoning.
    • 辽水 - Liao River.
    • 浮棺 - Floating coffin.
    • 孤竹君 - Lord of Guxu, a legendary figure.
    • 汉灵帝 - Emperor Ling of Han.
    • 廉翻 - Lián Fān, governor of Liaoxi.
    • 沈彬 - Shen Bin, a historical figure.
    • 高安 - Gao'an, a place in Jiangxi.
    • 开成二年 - Year Two of the Kaiceng era.
    • 墨子 - Mencius, a philosopher.
    • 朝歌 - Town of Chaoguo.
    • 颜子 - Confucius.
    • 七十弟子 - Seventy disciples of Confucius.
    • 宰予 - Zai Yu, a disciple of Confucius.
    • 子路 - Zilu (Subu), a disciple of Confucius.
    • 宋均 - Song Jun, a commentator.
    • 齐时 - During the Qi dynasty.
    • 古 - Ancient tomb.
    • 青州 - A region in present-day Shandong.
    • 东海 - East Sea.
    • 河东贾昊 - Jia Hao of Hedong.
    • 司马越 - Sima Yue, a historical figure.
    • 茍晞 - Jixi, a historical figure.

    Translation

    Song Dynasty, Li Shi, compiled

    In the Later Han dynasty, Xiang Kai presented a memorial during the Yanyi period, stating that there is a temple for Huang-Lao and Buddha in the palace. If the emperor does not abandon his desires, he will not receive his mandate. Some say Laozi entered the northwest and became a Buddha. The Buddha does not stay three nights under a mulberry tree; he does not wish to long dwell in affection. A heavenly deity sent a beautiful woman to the Buddha, who said, "This is just a leather bag filled with blood," and did not look at her.

    The Han emperor asked Chen Fan and Xu Zhi, who should come first. Chen Fan replied that Xu Zhi was born into a noble family and did not need to be carved out; Xu Zhi was outstanding and should come first.

    Fu Sheng was a scholar of the Qin dynasty. Emperor Wen of Han compiled the Five Classics and the Great Commentary on the Shangshu. He appointed Ouyang Sheng and others to study the Shangshu with Fu Sheng. There is a tomb of Fu Sheng in Lü Water.

    The Jin Shu (Book of Jin) records in the section on geography that people in Liaoxi saw a floating coffin on the Liao River and wanted to break it open. The coffin said, "I am the Lord of Guxu." They asked why they should break it open, and they built a shrine for it. Some say that during the reign of Emperor Ling of Han, the governor of Liaoxi, Lián Fān, had a dream about it.

    Shen Bin retired from his post as a shangshu and lived in Gao'an, Jiangxi. He planted a tree in a flat field and warned his sons, "You must bury me here." His descendants felled the tree and dug deep, finding a stone coffin with eight characters carved on it: "Kaiceng Year Two, a coffin for a long life. One place. Buried here."

    Mencius disliked music, so he turned his chariot away from the town of Chaoguo. The Lunzhuan (Commentary on the Analects) says that the town is named Chaoguo. Confucius did not stop there, and seventy of his disciples covered their eyes. Zai Yu alone looked back, and Zilu (Subu) fell from his chariot. Song Jun said that Zilu was troubled by Zai Yu looking back at the ominous place, so he kicked him.

    During the Qi dynasty, someone opened an ancient tomb and found an inscription: "Twenty sons of Qingzhou, a daughter of the East Sea, and a wife of Jixi." Jia Hao of Hedong believed it was the daughter of Sima Yue, who married Jixi. This was verified as true. [50]

    东都殖业坊十字街有王戎墓隋代醸家穿其旁作窖得铭曰晋司徒尚书令安丰侯王君铭
    　　广川王去疾发魏王子且渠甚浅狭无棺柩有石牀牀下悉是云母牀上两尸一男一女皆年二十许东首裸卧顔色如生人髪齿牙亦如生人王畏惧不敢近
    　　周灵王冡尸亦不壊惟玊蟾蜍一枚
    　　幽王冡甚高壮云母深尺余百余尸相枕藉皆不朽惟一男子余皆女子
    　　栾书冡有一白狐王击之伤其足王寻病足死
    　　萧颖士与行侣共济瓜洲舟中二少年相顾曰甚有肖鄱阳忠烈王颖士是鄱阳曾孙即自款陈二子曰吾识而祖乆矣遽负担而去颖士北归盱眙吏获发冡盗五六人颖士认江中二少年亦在内颍士以前说询之曰我尝开鄱阳王冡当门贵人顔色如生与頴士相此与吴纲似吴芮事颇同芮事出水经颖士薛用弱集异记
    　　九真山多大蛇名曰髯蛇长十丈围七八尺吞鹿角骨鑚皮出夷以大竹籖杀而食之杨氏南裔异物志曰髯惟大蛇既洪且长采色駮荦其文锦章食犬吞鹿成养创宾享嘉燕是豆是觞 [51]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [51]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 东都殖业坊十字街 - A bustling commercial area in the Eastern Capital.
    • 甚浅狭无棺柩 - Describes the tomb as very shallow and narrow, with no coffin or casket.
    • 东首裸卧 - Describes the bodies lying facing east, naked.
    • 顔色如生人 - Describes the bodies as looking like living people, suggesting they were well-preserved.
    • 畏惧不敢近 - Describes the fear and hesitation of the person who discovered the tomb.
    • 惟玉蟾蜍一枚 - Describes a single jade toad found in the tomb.
    • 惟一男子余皆女子 - Describes the bodies found in the tomb, with one male and many females.
    • 遽负担而去 - Describes the quick departure of the person who discovered the tomb.
    • 甚有肖鄱阳忠烈王 - Describes the resemblance to Prince Zhonglie of Poyang, a historical figure.
    • 北归盱眙 - Describes the journey back north to Xuyi.
    • 吏获发冡盗 - Describes the capture of tomb robbers by officials.
    • 颍士以前说询之 - Describes the questioning of the tomb robbers by Xuyi.
    • 我尝开鄱阳王冡 - Describes the opening of Prince Zhonglie of Poyang's tomb.
    • 当门贵人 - Describes a noble person found at the entrance of the tomb.
    • 頴士相 - Describes the resemblance to Xuyi.
    • 吴纲似吴芮事 - Describes a similarity to the story of Wu Rui.
    • 芮事出水经 - Describes the story of Wu Rui being recorded in the *Shui Jing* (Waterways Classic).
    • 薛用弱集异记 - A historical text that records strange and unusual events.
    • 九真山 - A mountain name, likely a place in ancient China.
    • 髯蛇 - A type of snake with a beard-like appearance, often associated with supernatural or mystical qualities in Chinese folklore.
    • 采色駮荦 - Describes the snake's colorful and varied appearance.
    • 其文锦章 - Describes the snake's skin as having a pattern like brocade.
    • 食犬吞鹿 - Describes the snake eating dogs and deer.
    • 鑚皮出 - Describes the snake emerging from the skin.
    • 夷以大竹籤杀而食之 - Describes the method of killing and eating the snake.
    • 杨氏南裔异物志 - A historical text that records unusual creatures in the southern regions.
    • 是豆是觞 - Describes the snake being served as food or drink.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 东都 - The Eastern Capital, likely referring to Luoyang during the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 殖业坊十字街 - A bustling commercial area in the Eastern Capital.
    • 广川王去疾 - A historical figure, likely a king or noble from the region of Guangchuan.
    • 周灵王 - King Ling of Zhou, a historical figure from the Zhou dynasty.
    • 幽王 - King You of Zhou, another historical figure from the Zhou dynasty.
    • 栾书 - A historical figure from the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 萧颖士 - A historical figure, likely a scholar or official.
    • 鄱阳忠烈王 - Prince Zhonglie of Poyang, a historical figure.
    • 吴芮 - A historical figure, likely a noble or official.
    • 九真山 - A mountain name, likely a place in ancient China.
    • 髯蛇 - A type of snake with a beard-like appearance, often associated with supernatural or mystical qualities in Chinese folklore.
    • 杨氏南裔异物志 - A historical text that records unusual creatures in the southern regions.

    Proper Nouns

    • 东都 - The Eastern Capital.
    • 殖业坊十字街 - A specific location in the Eastern Capital.
    • 王戎 - A historical figure, likely a noble or official.
    • 广川王去疾 - A historical figure, likely a king or noble from the region of Guangchuan.
    • 魏王子且渠 - A historical figure, likely a prince or noble.
    • 周灵王 - King Ling of Zhou.
    • 幽王 - King You of Zhou.
    • 栾书 - A historical figure from the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 萧颖士 - A historical figure, likely a scholar or official.
    • 鄱阳忠烈王 - Prince Zhonglie of Poyang.
    • 吴芮 - A historical figure, likely a noble or official.
    • 九真山 - A mountain name, likely a place in ancient China.
    • 髯蛇 - A type of snake with a beard-like appearance.
    • 杨氏南裔异物志 - A historical text that records unusual creatures in the southern regions.

    In the Eastern Capital, at the bustling intersection of the Zhiye Lane, there was a tomb of Wang Rong, a nobleman from the Jin dynasty. During the Sui dynasty, a wine merchant dug a pit near the tomb and found an inscription that read, "The tomb of Wang Jun, the Duke of Anfeng, who was a Grand Tutor and Minister of Works."

    The tomb of King Quji of Guangchuan was opened by King Wuzi of Wei. It was very shallow and narrow, with no coffin or casket. There was a stone bed, and beneath it was entirely covered in mica. On the bed lay two bodies, one male and one female, both around twenty years old. They were lying facing east, naked, and their appearance was like that of living people. Their hair and teeth were also like those of living people. The king was so frightened that he dared not approach.

    In the tomb of King Ling of Zhou, the bodies were also not decayed. There was only one jade toad.

    The tomb of King You of Zhou was very high and majestic. The mica was deep by more than a foot, and over a hundred bodies were lying on top of each other, all uncorrupted. There was only one man among them, and the rest were women.

    In the tomb of Luan Shu, there was a white fox. The king struck it and injured its foot. The king later fell ill and died from his foot injury.

    Xiao Yingshi was traveling with his companions when they reached Guazhou. In a boat, two young men looked at each other and said, "You look very much like Prince Zhonglie of Poyang." Xiao Yingshi was the grandson of Prince Zhonglie. He immediately introduced himself to the two young men, who said, "We have known your ancestor for a long time." They quickly left, carrying their belongings. Xiao Yingshi returned north to Xuyi, where officials captured five or six tomb robbers. Xiao Yingshi recognized the two young men from the river and questioned them. They said, "We once opened the tomb of Prince Zhonglie of Poyang. At the entrance, we found a noble person whose appearance was like that of Xiao Yingshi. This is similar to the story of Wu Gang, who is like Wu Rui. The story of Wu Rui is recorded in the Shui Jing. Xiao Yingshi also mentioned this in his Ji Yi Ji."

    Mount Jiu Zhen is home to many large snakes, known as beard-like snakes. They are ten zhang long and have a circumference of seven or eight chi. They eat deer and antelope, and their horns and bones are used to make brocade. The people of the region kill them with large bamboo spears and eat them. According to Yang Shi Nan Yi Wu Zhi, the beard-like snake is both large and long, with colorful and varied patterns like brocade. It eats dogs and deer, and its skin is used to make brocade. It is served as food or drink at banquets. [51]

    武强县有行于涂得一小蛇养之名担生长而噬人里人遂捕系狱担生负而奔邑沦为湖县官吏为鱼矣
    　　汉光武二年鲜于冀为清河太守作公未就而亡后守赵高计工用二百万黄秉刘适言四百万冀乃鬼见白日导从入府与高及秉等对共计校定为适秉所割匿冀乃书表自理言高贵不尚节垅亩之夫而箕踞遗世求显偷窃银艾鄙辱天官臣不胜鬼言去三十里车马皆灭不见秉等伏地物故高以状闻诏差代以旌幽中之讼
    　　老人晨渡朝歌水而怯纣曰老者髓不实故晨寒因斮胫以视髓
    　　纣有新声靡乐作邑朝歌史记曰朝歌之音歌不时也虞虢之间傅说室傅岩俗名之为圣人窟
    　　刘向叙晏子春秋称古冶子曰吾尝济于河鼋衔左以入砥柱之流吾从而杀之
    　　孔子临狄水而歌曰狄水衍兮风扬波船楫颠倒更相加汲县旧汲郡有石夹水为磻溪太公钓处有太公泉太公庙牧野竹书作坶野有比干墓前有石铭题云商大夫比干之墓所记唯此汉高祖孝文各为立碑 [52]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [52]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 行于涂 - Literally 'walking on the road,' used here to describe someone moving along a path or journey.
    • 得一小蛇 - A literal phrase meaning 'found a small snake,' which may carry symbolic significance depending on context.
    • 养之 - Means 'to raise or nurture it,' indicating care for the snake.
    • 名担 - A proper name given to the snake, '担' (dàn), which could be transliterated or translated based on its meaning.
    • 生长而噬人 - Literally 'grew and bit people,' describing the snake's development and eventual aggression.
    • 捕系狱 - Means 'captured and imprisoned in jail,' referring to the snake's capture and confinement.
    • 负而奔 - Literally 'carried and fled,' describing the person's action of carrying the snake and escaping.
    • 沦为湖县官吏为鱼矣 - Literally 'became a fish of the lake county official,' a metaphorical expression suggesting transformation or loss of status.
    • 汉光武二年 - Refers to the second year of Emperor Guangwu of Han, marking the historical context.
    • 鲜于冀为清河太守 - Describes a historical figure, 'Xian Yu Ji,' who served as the governor of Qinghe.
    • 公未就而亡 - Literally 'the public office was not completed and he died,' indicating that the official did not finish his duties.
    • 计工用二百万 - Means 'calculated the cost of labor at two million,' referring to the budget for a project.
    • 黄秉刘适言四百万 - Refers to another person, 'Huang Bing Liu Shì,' who stated the cost as four million.
    • 割匿 - Means 'to cut off and hide,' describing the act of concealing something.
    • 自理 - Means 'to justify oneself,' referring to the act of self-defense or explanation.
    • 箕踞 - A posture where one sits with legs spread out, often associated with disrespect or arrogance.
    • 遗 - Refers to the legacy or inheritance of a position or title.
    • 银艾 - Refers to the insignia of office, often used metaphorically to describe an official's status.
    • 鄙辱天官 - Literally 'to belittle and disgrace the heavenly official,' a metaphorical expression suggesting disrespect to a high-ranking official.
    • 鬼言 - Refers to communication with spirits or ghosts.
    • 导从入府 - Means 'led and entered the government office,' describing the ghost's actions.
    • 伏地物故 - Literally 'fell to the ground and died,' describing the person's fate.
    • 晨渡朝歌水 - Means 'crossed the Chao Ge River in the morning,' describing the elderly person's action.
    • 髓不实 - Literally 'the bone marrow is not full,' a metaphorical expression suggesting weakness or vulnerability.
    • 斮胫 - Means 'cut off the leg,' describing a violent act.
    • 砥柱之流 - Refers to the turbulent waters of the 'Dishu' (砥柱), a metaphor for a challenging or dangerous situation.
    • 箕踞遗 - Refers to the legacy of a disrespectful or arrogant posture.
    • 圣人窟 - Literally 'cave of the sage,' a metaphorical expression suggesting a place of wisdom or enlightenment.
    • 刘向叙晏子春秋 - Refers to a historical text, 'Yanzi Chunqiu,' written by Liu Xiang, describing the story of Yanzi.
    • 古冶子 - Refers to an ancient figure, 'Gu Yezhi,' who is mentioned in the text.
    • 鼋衔左 - Literally 'the turtle carried the left side,' describing a mythological event.
    • 孔子临狄水而歌 - Refers to Confucius singing by the Di River, a metaphorical expression suggesting reflection or contemplation.
    • 狄水衍兮风扬波 - Literally 'the Di River spreads, the wind raises waves,' describing the scene.
    • 船楫颠倒 - Means 'boats capsize,' describing a chaotic or turbulent situation.
    • 更相加 - Means 'pile on top of each other,' describing a chaotic or overwhelming situation.
    • 汲县旧汲郡 - Refers to the ancient county of Jixian, which was part of the Jiquan region.
    • 磻溪 - Refers to the 'Panchi' (磻溪), a place associated with Laozi's encounter with Confucius.
    • 太公钓处 - Refers to the place where Jiang Ziya (姜子牙) fished, a metaphorical expression suggesting wisdom or preparation.
    • 太公泉 - Refers to the 'Taigong Spring,' a place associated with Jiang Ziya.
    • 太公庙 - Refers to the temple dedicated to Jiang Ziya.
    • 牧野 - Refers to the 'Muyu' (牧野), a place associated with the Battle of Muye.
    • 竹书 - Refers to the 'Zhu Shu' (竹书), an ancient text.
    • 比干墓 - Refers to the tomb of Bi Gan, a loyal minister of the Shang dynasty.
    • 石铭题 - Refers to the stone inscription on the tomb, a metaphorical expression suggesting a record or memorial.
    • 博学求之 - Means 'to seek knowledge broadly,' describing the act of learning.
    • 无不利于事也 - Means 'nothing is unhelpful in matters,' describing the value of learning.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 武强县 - Refers to Wuchang County, a historical place in China.
    • 汉光武二年 - Refers to the second year of Emperor Guangwu of Han, marking the historical context.
    • 鲜于冀为清河太守 - Describes a historical figure, 'Xian Yu Ji,' who served as the governor of Qinghe.
    • 朝歌 - Refers to the city of Chao Ge, a historical place in China.
    • 孔子 - Refers to Confucius, a famous philosopher and teacher in Chinese history.
    • 狄水 - Refers to the Di River, a historical river in China.
    • 汲县 - Refers to the ancient county of Jixian, which was part of the Jiquan region.
    • 磻溪 - Refers to the 'Panchi' (磻溪), a place associated with Laozi's encounter with Confucius.
    • 太公 - Refers to Jiang Ziya (姜子牙), a famous military strategist and advisor in Chinese history.
    • 牧野 - Refers to the 'Muyu' (牧野), a place associated with the Battle of Muye.
    • 比干 - Refers to Bi Gan, a loyal minister of the Shang dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 武强县 - The name of a historical county in China.

    In Wuchang County, there was a man who, while traveling on the road, found a small snake and named it "Dàn." As it grew, it began to bite people, and the locals captured and imprisoned it in jail. The man, however, carried the snake and fled to the county, where it transformed into a fish of the lake county official.

    In the second year of the reign of Emperor Guangwu of Han, Xian Yu Ji served as the governor of Qinghe. He began to construct a public office but died before completing it. After his death, the next governor, Zhao Gao, calculated the cost of labor at two million. Huang Bing Liu Shì, however, stated the cost as four million. Xian Yu Ji then appeared as a ghost, entering the government office during the day, accompanied by others. He confronted Zhao Gao and Huang Bing Liu Shì, and they recalculated the cost, which was ultimately hidden by Huang Bing Liu Shì. Xian Yu Ji then wrote a petition to justify himself, stating that he, a high-ranking official, did not uphold the principles of the common people and sat arrogantly, leaving his legacy to the world. He sought prominence through deceit, stealing the insignia of office and disgracing the heavenly official. The ghost spoke to him, and thirty miles away, his chariots and horses disappeared, and Zhao Gao and Huang Bing Liu Shì fell to the ground and died. Zhao Gao reported the incident, and the emperor ordered a replacement to investigate the hidden case.

    An elderly man crossed the Chao Ge River in the morning and felt cold due to the weakness of his bone marrow. He cut off his leg to examine the marrow.

    King Zhou had new music and decadent songs composed in the city of Chao Ge. The Records of the Grand Historian states that the music of Chao Ge was not played at the right time. Between the states of Yu and Guo, Fu Yue lived in the cave of Fu Yan, which was commonly known as the "Cave of the Sage."

    Liu Xiang narrated in the Yanzi Chunqiu that Gu Yezhi said, "I once crossed the river, and a turtle carried the left side into the turbulent waters of the Dishu, and I followed and killed it."

    Confucius sang by the Di River, saying, "The Di River spreads, the wind raises waves, and boats capsize, piling on top of each other." In the old county of Jixian, which was part of the Jiquan region, there was a stone inscription on the bank of the river, known as the "Panchi," where Jiang Ziya fished. There was also the "Taigong Spring" and the "Taigong Temple." In Muyu, the Zhu Shu records that there was a tomb of Bi Gan, a loyal minister of the Shang dynasty. On the tomb, there was a stone inscription in clerical script, which said, "The tomb of the minister of the Shang dynasty, Bi Gan." The inscription only recorded this, and Emperor Gaozu and Emperor Wen of Han each erected a stele in honor of him. [52]

    顔舍字洪都兄畿得疾死于医家人迎丧引丧者颠仆曰我寿命未死但服药太多伤五臓耳及还妇梦之曰吾当复生急开椁以手刮棺指爪尽伤气息甚微饮食所须托之以梦舍侍养足不出户者十三年畿竟不起
    　　二广深谿石壁上有影如澹墨画船人行以为其祖考祭之不敢慢俗好于门画虎头书聻字谓隂司刀鬼名读汉旧史傩逐疫又立桃人苇索沧耳虎等聻字盖沧耳也
    　　俗钟馗起于唐明皇之梦非也北史尧暄本名钟葵字辟邪于劲字钟葵宋宗慤妹名钟葵非特明皇时但葵馗二字异耳又曰终葵菜名闽中多木绵植之数千株采其花纺为布名吉贝南史言林邑等国出古贝木以古为吉讹也
    　　枞松叶柏身桧柏叶松身
    　　黄公好谦女有国色而谦其美以为丑也女至难嫁有鳏夫冒娶之乃国色果信黄公之谦
    　　魏田父耕于野而得玉弗识也邻人隂欲得之绐曰此怪石也田父置玉于室其光烛夜果以为怪弃之于野邻人从而盗之以献魏王玉工望而拜曰此无价之寳 [53]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [53]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 顔舍字洪都 - Standard biographical formula meaning 'Yan She, courtesy name Hongdu.'
    • 得疾 - Meaning 'to fall ill.'
    • 死于医家 - Meaning 'died at the doctor's house.'
    • 迎丧引丧者颠仆 - Meaning 'those who came to mourn and lead the funeral procession stumbled.'
    • 颠仆曰 - Meaning 'stumbled and said.'
    • 我寿命未死但服药太多伤五臓耳 - Meaning 'My lifespan is not yet over, but I have taken too many medicines, which have injured my five organs.'
    • 及还 - Meaning 'when he returned.'
    • 妇梦之 - Meaning 'his wife dreamed of him.'
    • 吾当复生 - Meaning 'I will be reborn.'
    • 急开椁以手刮棺指爪尽伤 - Meaning 'quickly open the coffin and scrape the coffin with my hands, scratching my nails until they are all injured.'
    • 气息甚微 - Meaning 'the breath is very weak.'
    • 饮食所须托之以梦 - Meaning 'the food and drink needed were entrusted to the dream.'
    • 舍侍养足不出户者十三年畿竟不起 - Meaning 'Yan She cared for him for thirteen years without leaving the house, and Jie (the brother) ultimately did not revive.'
    • 二广深谿石壁上有影 - Meaning 'on the deep ravines and stone walls of the two Guang regions, there are ghost shadows.'
    • 如澹墨画 - Meaning 'like light ink paintings.'
    • 船人行以为其祖考 - Meaning 'the boatmen think these are their ancestors.'
    • 祭之不敢慢 - Meaning 'they worship them without being lax.'
    • 俗好于门画虎头书聻字 - Meaning 'the custom is to paint tiger heads and write the character '聻' on the door.'
    • 谓隂司刀鬼名 - Meaning 'it is said to be the name of a ghost with a knife in the underworld.'
    • 读汉旧史傩逐疫 - Meaning 'reading the old history of the Han dynasty, the ritual of driving away epidemic ghosts.'
    • 又立桃人苇索沧耳虎等聻字 - Meaning 'also setting up peachwood figures, reed ropes, and tiger-shaped objects with the character '聻'.
    • 盖沧耳也 - Meaning 'it is said to be the name of the ghost '沧耳'.
    • 枞松叶柏身 - Meaning 'the fir has pine-like leaves and a cypress-like trunk.'
    • 桧柏叶松身 - Meaning 'the cypress has juniper-like leaves and a pine-like trunk.'
    • 黄公好谦女有国色而谦其美以为丑也 - Meaning 'Yellow Gong likes humility, the woman has a national beauty, but he considers her beauty as ugliness.'
    • 女至难嫁 - Meaning 'the woman is very hard to marry.'
    • 有鳏夫冒娶之 - Meaning 'a widower secretly married her.'
    • 乃国色 - Meaning 'she is indeed a national beauty.'
    • 果信黄公之谦 - Meaning 'indeed, Yellow Gong's humility is true.'
    • 魏田父耕于野而得玉 - Meaning 'a farmer in Wei found a piece of jade while plowing in the fields.'
    • 弗识也 - Meaning 'he did not recognize it.'
    • 邻人隂欲得之 - Meaning 'his neighbor secretly wanted to get it.'
    • 绐曰此怪石也 - Meaning 'he deceived him by saying it was a strange stone.'
    • 田父置玉于室其光烛夜 - Meaning 'the farmer placed the jade in his room, and its light illuminated the night.'
    • 果以为怪弃之于野 - Meaning 'he indeed considered it strange and discarded it in the wild.'
    • 邻人从而盗之 - Meaning 'the neighbor then stole it.'
    • 以献魏王 - Meaning 'and presented it to the King of Wei.'
    • 玉工望而拜曰此无价之寳 - Meaning 'the jade worker looked at it and bowed, saying it is an invaluable treasure.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 顔舍 - Historical figure Yan She.
    • 兄畿 - Historical figure Yan Jie, Yan She's brother.
    • 二广 - Refers to the two Guang regions (Guangdong and Guangxi).
    • 钟馗 - Legendary figure in Chinese folklore, often depicted as a demon-catcher.
    • 终葵 - Type of plant.
    • 吉贝 - Type of cloth.
    • 魏 - The state of Wei during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 魏王 - The king of Wei.
    • 玉工 - Jade worker.

    Yan She, courtesy name Hongdu, had an elder brother named Yan Jie who fell ill and died at the doctor's house. Those who came to mourn and lead the funeral procession stumbled and said, "My lifespan is not yet over, but I have taken too many medicines, which have injured my five organs." When Yan She returned, his wife dreamed of him and said, "I will be reborn. Quickly open the coffin and scrape the coffin with my hands, scratching my nails until they are all injured. The breath is very weak. The food and drink needed were entrusted to the dream." Yan She cared for him for thirteen years without leaving the house, and Yan Jie ultimately did not revive.

    In the two Guang regions, on the deep ravines and stone walls, there are ghost shadows like light ink paintings. The boatmen think these are their ancestors and worship them without being lax. The custom is to paint tiger heads and write the character '聻' on the door, which is said to be the name of a ghost with a knife in the underworld. Reading the old history of the Han dynasty, the ritual of driving away epidemic ghosts, also setting up peachwood figures, reed ropes, and tiger-shaped objects with the character '聻,' it is said to be the name of the ghost '沧耳.'

    The common belief is that Zhong Kui arose from Emperor Minghuang's dream, but it is not true. The Northern History records Yao Xuan's original name as Zhong Kui, with the courtesy name Pi Xie. Yu Jin's courtesy name is Zhong Kui, and Song Zongque's sister's name is Zhong Kui. Not only during Emperor Minghuang's time, but the characters '葵' and '馗' are different. Also said to be the name of a plant, in Fujian, many trees of cotton are planted, thousands of them. The flowers are collected and spun into cloth called Jibi. The Southern History says that the countries of Linyi and others produce the ancient wood called Gubei, which is a misinterpretation of '古' as '吉.'

    The fir has pine-like leaves and a cypress-like trunk, while the cypress has juniper-like leaves and a pine-like trunk.

    Yellow Gong likes humility. The woman has a national beauty, but he considers her beauty as ugliness. The woman is very hard to marry. A widower secretly married her, and she is indeed a national beauty. Indeed, Yellow Gong's humility is true.

    A farmer in Wei found a piece of jade while plowing in the fields and did not recognize it. His neighbor secretly wanted to get it and deceived him by saying it was a strange stone. The farmer placed the jade in his room, and its light illuminated the night. He indeed considered it strange and discarded it in the wild. The neighbor then stole it and presented it to the King of Wei. The jade worker looked at it and bowed, saying it is an invaluable treasure. [53]

    陶居书自奇世传画板帖及焦山下瘗铭皆其遗迹
    　　王防稽七子凝操徽涣献五人书迹传世惟肃二人未见而五子书各不同盖善学父书者
    　　郝氏夜祠佛鬼车乘烛光而下翼广丈余九首互相低昻其家呼犬持杖逐之坠一羽长三尺许广八九寸色鵞雁
    　　江浙间有鸟名飞生狐首肉翅四足如兽飞而生子即随母后人有难产以其爪安胷腹间立验亦有得其皮者
    　　有异鸟鸣如婴儿巢于木探得其雏大如鵞青首白面觜爪鹰鹞或曰此鹏也
    　　高丽人参赞三桠五叶背阳向隂欲来求我椵树相寻椵木叶似桐甚大隂广参多生其隂
    　　王孙一名黄孙一名黄昏孙思邈有黄昏散注云黄昏木或曰合欢合昏夜合花陈无已云探囊一试黄昏汤草部木部黄昏为二物郭璞曰守宫槐昼日聂合而夜舒布也江东有木与此相反俗因名合昏古今注云合欢似梧桐枝叶互相结风来解使人不忿嵇康种之于舍前 [54]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [54]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 自奇世传 - Rare and valuable; used to describe the works of Tao Yuanming.
    • 以其爪安胷腹间立验 - Using its claws to place on the chest and abdomen, instantly effective.
    • 背阳向隂 - Facing away from the sun and toward the shade; describes the growing conditions of ginseng.
    • 守宫槐昼日聂合而夜舒布也 - The day-blooming night-blooming tree, which closes during the day and opens at night.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 陶淵明 - A famous poet and philosopher from the Eastern Jin dynasty, known for his reclusive lifestyle and poetic works.
    • 王防稽 - A historical figure, possibly a scholar or official, whose seven sons are mentioned.
    • 高丽 - The ancient Korean kingdom of Goryeo.
    • 孙思邈 - A renowned physician and alchemist from the Tang dynasty, known for his medical texts.
    • 郭璞 - A scholar and geographer from the Eastern Jin dynasty, known for his commentary on the *Shuowen Jiezi*.

    Proper Nouns

    • 陶淵明 - Tao Yuanming, a famous poet and philosopher from the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 王防稽 - Wang Fangqi, a historical figure, possibly a scholar or official.
    • 高丽 - The ancient Korean kingdom of Goryeo.
    • 孙思邈 - Sun Simiao, a renowned physician and alchemist from the Tang dynasty.
    • 郭璞 - Guo Pu, a scholar and geographer from the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 黄昏 - A plant known as 'evening' or 'night-blooming' plant.

    Tao Yuanming's writings, calligraphy, paintings, and inscriptions at the foot of Jiaoshan are all his legacies.
    The seven sons of Wang Fangqi, known as Neng, Cao, Hui, Huan, and Xian, have their calligraphy passed down through the ages, except for Xuan and Su who are not seen. Their styles differ, indicating they are skilled at learning from their father's calligraphy.
    At night, the Hao family was worshipping Buddha when a ghostly vehicle descended on candlelight, with wings spanning over a zhang and nine heads moving up and down. The family called a dog and chased it with a stick, causing one feather to fall, three feet long and eight to nine inches wide, colored like a goose or a swan.
    In the Jiangsu and Zhejiang regions, there is a bird named Feisheng, with a fox-like head, meaty wings, and four legs like a beast. It flies and lays eggs, and after birth, the offspring follow the mother. For those with difficult childbirth, using its claws to place on the chest and abdomen is instantly effective, and some have obtained its skin.
    There is an unusual bird that makes sounds like a baby and nests in trees. When its nest is found, the chick is as large as a goose, with a blue head and white face, and beak and claws resembling those of an eagle or a hawk. Some say it is a phoenix.
    Ginseng from Goryeo is praised for its three branches and five leaves, facing away from the sun and toward the shade. If one seeks me, they should find the mulberry tree, whose leaves resemble those of the paulownia tree, very large and providing ample shade. Ginseng often grows in the shade of such trees.
    The prince, also known as Huangsun or Huanghun, has a medicine called Huanghun San, which is said to be derived from the Huanghun plant. Some call it the happy tree or the night-blooming flower. Chen Wuyi mentioned that one can try the Huanghun decoction, which is found in both the herb and wood sections of the materia medica. Guo Pu said it is the day-blooming night-blooming tree, which closes during the day and opens at night. In Jiangdong, there is a tree that is the opposite, and it is commonly called the night-blooming tree. The Zizhi Tongjian notes that the happy tree resembles the paulownia tree, with intertwined branches and leaves that spread out in the wind, making one feel less resentful. Ji Kang planted it in front of his house. [54]

    续博物志卷八
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　续博物志卷九
    　　宋　李石　撰
    　　南昌县出然石异物志云色黄而理疎以水灌之便以鼎炊物可熟置之则冷如此无穷元康中雷孔章入洛赍以示张公张公曰此谓然石
    　　晋书曰汉末博士炖煌侯瑾善内学弱水出流沙流沙与水流行也在西海郡北山有赤石白色以两石相打则水润打之不已则润尽火出山石皆然炎起数丈不灭有大黒风自流沙出掩之乃灭其石如初不敢轻近耳
    　　火井以草爨之则烟腾火发汤井以草纳之则露凝铎销状如刀戈残刄有孔旁达出丽水饰以金所指无不洞夷人尤寳月以血祭之俗谓天降非人铸郁刃铸以毒药冶取跃如星者淬以马血成之伤人即死刀劒以柔铁为茎干不可纯用钢纯钢不折则缺 [55]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [55]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 續博物志 - Title of the text, meaning 'Continuation of the *Records of Marvels*', indicating it is a sequel to an earlier work.
    • 元康中 - Refers to the Yuan Kang era during the Western Jin dynasty (290-299 CE).
    • 然石 - A type of stone that can burn, likely a reference to a natural phenomenon or a specific type of stone.
    • 漢末 - Refers to the end of the Han dynasty (220 CE).
    • 博士 - A scholar or expert, often associated with Confucian studies.
    • 燉煌 - A place name, referring to Dunhuang, a city in modern-day Gansu Province.
    • 內學 - Refers to internal or esoteric learning, often associated with Confucian or Daoist studies.
    • 弱水 - A mythical river, often associated with distant or inaccessible places.
    • 流沙 - Refers to shifting sand, often associated with deserts or barren lands.
    • 赤石 - Red stone, likely a specific type of stone.
    • 白色 - White color.
    • 兩石相打 - Striking two stones together.
    • 水潤 - Moisture or dampness.
    • 潤盡 - When moisture is exhausted.
    • 火出 - Fire emerges.
    • 山石皆然 - The stones in the mountain all burn.
    • 炎起數丈 - Flames rise several zhang (a unit of length).
    • 大黒風 - A strong, dark wind.
    • 掩之 - Covering or blocking.
    • 其石如初 - The stones return to their original state.
    • 不敢輕近耳 - One dares not approach lightly.
    • 火井 - A well that produces fire, likely a reference to natural gas or oil.
    • 草爨之 - Using grass to ignite.
    • 煙騰火發 - Smoke rises and fire ignites.
    • 湯井 - A well that produces hot water, likely a reference to a natural hot spring.
    • 草納之 - Using grass to introduce or activate.
    • 露凝 - Dew condenses.
    • 鐸銷 - A bell melts.
    • 狀如刀戈殘刄 - Shaped like the remnants of swords and spears.
    • 有孔旁達 - With holes extending.
    • 出麗水 - Emerging from a beautiful river.
    • 飾以金 - Decorated with gold.
    • 所指無不洞夷 - Pointing at anything makes it penetrate or smooth.
    • 人尤寳 - People especially treasure it.
    • 月以血祭之 - Sacrificing blood to it under the moon.
    • 俗謂天降 - Commonly said to be a heavenly descent.
    • 非人铸 - Not made by humans.
    • 郁刃 - A type of blade or sword.
    • 铸以毒药 - Casting with poison.
    • 冶取跃如星者 - Smelting and selecting those that leap like stars.
    • 淬以马血 - Quenching with horse blood.
    • 伤人即死 - Harming someone results in death.
    • 刀劒 - Swords and knives.
    • 柔铁 - Soft iron.
    • 茎干 - Stem or shaft.
    • 不可纯用钢 - Cannot be purely made of steel.
    • 纯钢不折则缺 - Pure steel does not break but may have flaws.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 續博物志 - A continuation of the *Records of Marvels*, a text that likely contains various accounts of natural phenomena, legends, and historical events.
    • 文言文 - Classical Chinese, a literary style used in ancient Chinese texts, characterized by its concise and formal language.
    • 元康中 - The Yuan Kang era (290-299 CE) of the Western Jin dynasty, a period of political instability and cultural development.
    • 雷孔章 - A historical figure, likely a scholar or official, whose name suggests a connection to thunder or lightning.
    • 張公 - A title or nickname for a prominent figure, possibly a scholar or official, indicating a respected position.
    • 然石 - A type of stone that can burn, likely a reference to a natural phenomenon or a specific type of stone, such as pyrite or flint.
    • 漢末 - The end of the Han dynasty (220 CE), a period of significant political and social change.
    • 博士 - A scholar or expert, often associated with Confucian studies, indicating a high level of learning and authority.
    • 燉煌 - Dunhuang, a city in modern-day Gansu Province, known for its historical and cultural significance.
    • 侯瑾 - A historical figure, likely a scholar or official, whose name suggests a connection to the city of Dunhuang.
    • 內學 - Internal or esoteric learning, often associated with Confucian or Daoist studies, indicating a focus on deep and specialized knowledge.
    • 弱水 - A mythical river, often associated with distant or inaccessible places, symbolizing the unknown or the exotic.
    • 流沙 - Shifting sand, often associated with deserts or barren lands, symbolizing instability or danger.
    • 赤石 - Red stone, likely a specific type of stone, possibly a reference to a natural phenomenon or a specific location.
    • 白色 - White color, often associated with purity or simplicity.
    • 兩石相打 - Striking two stones together, a common method for creating sparks or igniting materials.
    • 水潤 - Moisture or dampness, indicating the presence of water.
    • 潤盡 - When moisture is exhausted, suggesting a dry or arid state.
    • 火出 - Fire emerges, indicating a natural or supernatural phenomenon.
    • 山石皆然 - The stones in the mountain all burn, suggesting a widespread and intense phenomenon.
    • 炎起數丈 - Flames rise several zhang (a unit of length), indicating a significant height and intensity.
    • 大黒風 - A strong, dark wind, often associated with storms or natural disasters.
    • 掩之 - Covering or blocking, suggesting an attempt to extinguish or control the fire.
    • 其石如初 - The stones return to their original state, indicating a cyclical or self-renewing process.
    • 不敢輕近耳 - One dares not approach lightly, suggesting a sense of awe or fear.
    • 火井 - A well that produces fire, likely a reference to natural gas or oil, indicating a source of energy.
    • 草爨之 - Using grass to ignite, a common method for starting fires.
    • 煙騰火發 - Smoke rises and fire ignites, indicating a sudden and dramatic event.
    • 湯井 - A well that produces hot water, likely a reference to a natural hot spring, indicating a source of heat.
    • 草納之 - Using grass to introduce or activate, a common method for starting or maintaining fires.
    • 露凝 - Dew condenses, indicating a cool or humid environment.
    • 鐸銷 - A bell melts, suggesting a high temperature or intense heat.
    • 狀如刀戈殘刄 - Shaped like the remnants of swords and spears, indicating a sharp or jagged appearance.
    • 有孔旁達 - With holes extending, suggesting a network or interconnected system.
    • 出麗水 - Emerging from a beautiful river, indicating a source of beauty or purity.
    • 飾以金 - Decorated with gold, suggesting a precious or valuable object.
    • 所指無不洞夷 - Pointing at anything makes it penetrate or smooth, suggesting a powerful or transformative effect.
    • 人尤寳 - People especially treasure it, indicating a high value or importance.
    • 月以血祭之 - Sacrificing blood to it under the moon, suggesting a ritual or ceremonial practice.
    • 俗謂天降 - Commonly said to be a heavenly descent, indicating a divine or supernatural origin.
    • 非人铸 - Not made by humans, suggesting a natural or supernatural origin.
    • 郁刃 - A type of blade or sword, possibly a reference to a specific type of weapon.
    • 铸以毒药 - Casting with poison, suggesting a dangerous or harmful process.
    • 冶取跃如星者 - Smelting and selecting those that leap like stars, indicating a selective and precise process.
    • 淬以马血 - Quenching with horse blood, suggesting a traditional or ritualistic process.
    • 伤人即死 - Harming someone results in death, indicating a deadly or lethal effect.
    • 刀劒 - Swords and knives, indicating weapons or tools.
    • 柔铁 - Soft iron, indicating a malleable or easily shaped material.
    • 茎干 - Stem or shaft.
    • 不可纯用钢 - Cannot be purely made of steel.
    • 纯钢不折则缺 - Pure steel does not break but may have flaws.

    Continuation of the Records of Marvels, Volume Eight
    　　Imperially Approved Complete Library of the Four Treasuries
    　　Continuation of the Records of Marvels, Volume Nine
    　　By Li Shi of the Song Dynasty
    　　The county of Nanchang produces a peculiar stone called "Ran Shi." According to the Record of Strange Things, it is yellow in color and has a loose texture. When water is poured on it, it ignites. Using a tripod to cook food over it, the food can be cooked, but when placed on it, it cools down. This process continues infinitely. During the Yuan Kang period, Lei Kongzhang brought one to Luoyang and showed it to Zhang Gong. Zhang Gong said, "This is called Ran Shi."

    　　The Book of Jin states that at the end of the Han dynasty, Doctor Hou Jin of Dunhuang was skilled in internal learning. The Weak Water emerges from the shifting sands, where water flows amidst the sands. In the northern mountains of the Western Sea Prefecture, there are red stones and white stones. When two stones are struck together, they become moist. If struck repeatedly, the moisture dries up and fire emerges. The stones in the mountain all burn, with flames rising several zhang high and not extinguishing. A strong, dark wind emerges from the shifting sands and covers them, causing the fire to go out. The stones then return to their original state, and one dares not approach them lightly.

    　　A fire well, when grass is used to ignite it, produces smoke and fire. A hot spring well, when grass is introduced, causes dew to condense and a bell to melt. It is shaped like the remnants of swords and spears, with holes extending and emerging from a beautiful river. It is decorated with gold, and pointing at anything makes it penetrate or smooth. People especially treasure it, and it is commonly said to be a heavenly descent, not made by humans. The Yurui sword is cast with poison, and those that leap like stars are selected and quenched with horse blood. Wounding someone with it results in death. Swords and knives are made with soft iron as the stem and shaft, and cannot be purely made of steel. Pure steel does not break but may have flaws. [55]

    天寳中交趾贡龙脑如蝉老龙脑树节方有禁中呼为瑞龙脑上惟赐贵妃十枚香气彻十余歩
    　　蓬莱山使高丽者望之甚逺前后下峭拔可爱其岛属昌国县其上平广可以种莳岛人云蓬莱三仙山越弱水三万里不应指顾间便见此外不复见山
    　　马绪谪潮得巨蚁长尺余盐渍之归夸北人
    　　两头蛇马鼈食牛血所化
    　　鸤鸠毛氏曰秸鞠郭璞谓布谷雎鸠毛氏云王睢郭璞谓鹗鸒斯毛氏云卑居乂云鸦乌郭璞云小雀好羣飞毛云桑扈为窃脂郭璞云为青雀好盗脂
    　　海州有人持一束黑物形如竹篾其人云海鱼腮中毛可作屛风贴色似水牛角头似猪鬃长三四寸广可一寸
    　　人家有小虫至微而响甚寻之卒不可见号窃虫大如半胡麻形似鼠妇有两角白色振其头则有声孟康朝作赋比之鬼魅
    　　海州有沟水通淮过漕船寳应中堰破水涸东海主修堰堰成輙壊或说梁代筑浮山堰以铁数万斤填积其下乃成堰之壊如雷声至是声闻上流盖铁味辛辛能害目鱼龙防目故畏铁也有人淬劒于池池鱼皆浮去 [56]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [56]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 天寳中 - Refers to the Tianbao era (742-756) of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, a period of prosperity and cultural flourishing.
    • 交趾 - Refers to Jiaozhi, an ancient Chinese term for the region that is now northern Vietnam.
    • 贡龙脑 - ‘贡’ means to present as a tribute, and ‘龙脑’ refers to camphor or borneol, a fragrant resin used in medicine and incense.
    • 如蝉 - Describes the appearance of the camphor, comparing it to cicada wings.
    • 老龙脑树节方有 - Refers to the rare occurrence of camphor in the bark of old camphor trees.
    • 禁中 - Refers to the inner palace or the emperor's private quarters.
    • 瑞龙脑 - ‘瑞’ means auspicious, so ‘瑞龙脑’ is a special, auspicious form of camphor.
    • 上惟赐贵妃十枚 - ‘上’ refers to the emperor, and ‘贵妃’ refers to Yang Guifei, Emperor Xuanzong's favorite concubine.
    • 香气彻十余歩 - Describes the strong fragrance of the camphor that could be smelled from a considerable distance.
    • 蓬莱山 - Refers to Mount Penglai, a legendary mountain in Chinese mythology, often associated with immortals and the divine.
    • 使高丽者 - Refers to envoys sent to Goryeo (Korea).
    • 望之甚逺 - Means that the mountain could be seen from a great distance.
    • 前后下峭拔可爱 - Describes the mountain's steep and majestic appearance.
    • 岛属昌国县 - Indicates that the island belongs to Changguo County.
    • 其上平广可以种莳 - Describes the island's flat and expansive surface suitable for cultivation.
    • 马绪谪潮得巨蚁长尺余 - ‘谪’ means exile, and ‘潮’ refers to the coastal region. The sentence describes an ant found by Ma Xu, who was exiled to the coast, that was unusually large.
    • 盐渍之 - Means the ant was salted for preservation.
    • 归夸北人 - Refers to Ma Xu returning to the north and boasting about the ant to others.
    • 两头蛇 - Refers to a snake with two heads, a rare and unusual creature.
    • 马鼈食牛血所化 - Suggests that the two-headed snake is a result of a horse turtle (a type of turtle) eating cow blood.
    • 鸤鸠 - Refers to the cuckoo bird.
    • 秸鞠 - Refers to a type of bird, possibly a sparrow or another small bird.
    • 布谷 - Another name for the cuckoo bird.
    • 雎鸠 - Refers to a type of bird, often associated with the mandarin duck in Chinese literature.
    • 王睢 - Another name for the mandarin duck.
    • 卑居 - Refers to a type of bird, possibly a sparrow or another small bird.
    • 鸦乌 - Refers to a type of bird, often associated with crows or ravens.
    • 小雀好羣飞 - Describes a small bird that likes to fly in flocks.
    • 桑扈 - Refers to a type of bird, often associated with the cuckoo.
    • 窃脂 - Refers to a bird that steals fat, possibly a reference to a specific bird known for this behavior.
    • 青雀 - Refers to a type of bird, often associated with the blue magpie.
    • 海州有人持一束黑物形如竹篾 - Describes a person from Hai Prefecture holding a bundle of black material shaped like bamboo strips.
    • 海鱼腮中毛 - Refers to the hair-like substance found in the gills of a sea fish.
    • 可作屛风贴 - Means the substance can be used as a material for screen decorations.
    • 色似水牛角头似猪鬃 - Describes the color and texture of the substance, comparing it to water buffalo horn and pig bristles.
    • 长三四寸广可一寸 - Specifies the dimensions of the substance.
    • 人家有小虫至微而响甚 - Describes a tiny insect that makes a loud sound.
    • 寻之卒不可见 - Means the insect is difficult to find despite its loud noise.
    • 号窃虫 - Refers to the insect by the name 'qie chong'.
    • 大如半胡麻 - Describes the size of the insect, comparing it to half a sesame seed.
    • 形似鼠妇有两角白色 - Describes the shape and appearance of the insect, comparing it to a woodlouse and noting its white color with two horns.
    • 振其头则有声 - Means shaking the insect's head produces a sound.
    • 孟康朝作赋比之鬼魅 - Refers to Meng Kang, a scholar who wrote a poem comparing the insect to a ghost or demon.
    • 海州有沟水通淮过漕船 - Describes a canal in Hai Prefecture that connects to the Huai River and is used for transporting grain.
    • 寳应中堰破水涸 - Refers to the Baoying period (762-763) when a dam broke, causing the water to dry up.
    • 东海主修堰 - Indicates that the Eastern Sea (likely referring to the East China Sea) was responsible for repairing the dam.
    • 堰成輙壊 - Means the dam would repeatedly break after being repaired.
    • 或说梁代筑浮山堰以铁数万斤填积其下乃成 - Suggests that during the Liang Dynasty, a dam was built at Fushan using thousands of pounds of iron to stabilize it.
    • 堰之壊如雷声至是声闻上流 - Describes the dam breaking with a sound like thunder, which could be heard upstream.
    • 盖铁味辛辛能害目鱼龙防目故畏铁也 - Explains that the dam broke because the iron had a pungent taste that harmed the eyes of fish and dragons, causing them to avoid the area.
    • 有人淬劒于池池鱼皆浮去 - Describes someone tempering a sword in a pond, causing the fish to float away due to the disturbance.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 天寳中 - The Tianbao era was a period of peace and prosperity under Emperor Xuanzong, but it also saw the rise of corruption and the eventual An Lushan Rebellion.
    • 交趾 - Jiaozhi was a significant region in southern China during the Tang Dynasty, known for its exotic products and tribute.
    • 贡龙脑 - Camphor was a valuable commodity in ancient China, used for medicinal and aromatic purposes.
    • 禁中 - The inner palace was a symbol of imperial power and exclusivity.
    • 瑞龙脑 - The term '瑞' (auspicious) suggests that the camphor was considered a rare and valuable gift.
    • 贵妃 - Yang Guifei was one of the most famous concubines in Chinese history, known for her beauty and influence over Emperor Xuanzong.
    • 蓬莱山 - Mount Penglai is a mythical mountain in Chinese folklore, often associated with immortals and the divine.
    • 使高丽者 - Envoys to Goryeo (Korea) were important for diplomatic and trade relations.
    • 马绪 - Ma Xu was a historical figure, though the specific details of his exile are not well-documented.
    • 两头蛇 - Two-headed snakes are rare and often considered omens in Chinese culture.
    • 马鼈 - A type of turtle that is not commonly known in modern Chinese.
    • 鸤鸠 - The cuckoo bird is often associated with spring and renewal in Chinese poetry.
    • 秸鞠 - A type of bird that is not well-documented in historical texts.
    • 布谷 - The cuckoo bird is known for its distinctive call, which is often associated with the arrival of spring.
    • 雎鸠 - The mandarin duck is a symbol of love and fidelity in Chinese culture.
    • 王睢 - Another name for the mandarin duck, often used in poetry.
    • 卑居 - A type of bird that is not well-documented in historical texts.
    • 鸦乌 - Crows and ravens are often associated with death and the supernatural in Chinese folklore.
    • 小雀好羣飞 - Small birds flying in flocks are a common sight in nature and are often used in poetry to describe peaceful scenes.
    • 桑扈 - The cuckoo bird is often associated with the cuckoo in Chinese literature.
    • 窃脂 - A bird that steals fat is a rare and unusual creature, often used in folklore.
    • 青雀 - The blue magpie is a colorful bird that is often associated with good luck.
    • 海州 - Hai Prefecture was an important coastal region in ancient China, known for its trade and natural resources.

    During the Tianbao era, Jiaozhi presented camphor to the imperial court, which resembled cicada wings. This rare camphor was found only in the bark of old camphor trees and was called "auspicious camphor" within the inner palace. The emperor bestowed ten pieces of this camphor upon Yang Guifei, and its fragrance could be smelled from more than ten steps away.

    Mount Penglai, visible from a great distance by envoys to Goryeo, was renowned for its steep and majestic appearance. The island belonged to Changguo County and had a flat, expansive surface suitable for cultivation. Islanders claimed that Mount Penglai was one of the Three Immortal Mountains, separated by the weak waters of the Three Ten Thousand Li, and could be seen with a mere glance, unlike any other mountain.

    Ma Xu, exiled to the coastal region, discovered a giant ant measuring more than a foot in length. He salted it for preservation and returned to the north, boasting about it to others.

    A two-headed snake was said to be the result of a horse turtle consuming cow blood.

    The cuckoo bird, according to Mao's commentary, is also known as the "straw bird." Guo Pu identified it as the "cuckoo" and "mandarin duck." Mao further noted it as the "eagle," "sparrow," and "crow." Guo Pu described it as a small bird that likes to fly in flocks. Mao mentioned the "cuckoo" as the "stealer of fat," while Guo Pu identified it as the "blue magpie" that steals fat.

    In Hai Prefecture, a person held a bundle of black material shaped like bamboo strips, which he claimed was the hair-like substance found in the gills of a sea fish. This material could be used for screen decorations, with a color resembling water buffalo horn and a texture like pig bristles, measuring about three to four inches in length and one inch in width.

    In one household, there was a tiny insect that, though minuscule, made a loud sound. Despite its loud noise, it was difficult to find. Known as the "stealing insect," it was about half the size of a sesame seed, shaped like a woodlouse, and had two white horns. Shaking its head produced a sound. Meng Kang composed a poem comparing this insect to a ghost or demon.

    In Hai Prefecture, there was a canal connecting to the Huai River, used for transporting grain. During the Baoying period, a dam broke, causing the water to dry up. The Eastern Sea was responsible for repairing the dam, but it repeatedly broke after being repaired. Some suggested that during the Liang Dynasty, a dam was built at Fushan using thousands of pounds of iron to stabilize it. The dam broke with a sound like thunder, which could be heard upstream. It was believed that the iron had a pungent taste that harmed the eyes of fish and dragons, causing them to avoid the area. Someone who tempered a sword in a pond caused the fish to float away due to the disturbance. [56]

    张勃吴録曰彭泽有鸟接食圑饭投之高下无失巫山迎船鸟亦然
    　　凡鸟三指向前一向后鹦鹉两指向后
    　　汉明帝永平五年长安迎取飞廉并铜马置上西门外晋灼曰飞廉鹿身头如雀有角而蛇尾豹文应劭曰飞廉神禽能致风气
    　　宣王好射三石之弓而自以为九石臣下从而悦之以非大王不能用其实三石也九石名而已
    　　周官全羽为旞析羽为旌道路用旌节即此旌也顾恺之画苏武所执之旌上圆如幢下复数层红羽骖骖然如夜合花此析羽也今卤簿中节颇此其首亦圆相去尺余有数重圆板以牦牛尾缀之亦析羽遗法然苏武汉节则旌节之制至于龙节虎节玉节之则今虎符之但可合符不必尽为旌耳顾画节首一物若书卷岂亦符乎汉铜符以铜为之竹符以竹五寸镌刻篆书其上
    　　砚谱载天下之砚四十余品以青州红丝石砚为第一端州斧柯山石为第二歙州龙尾石为第三
    　　夜蔵饮食于噐中覆之不宻鼠欲盗食不可至环噐而走泪滴噐中食之得黄疾通身如蜡 [57]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [57]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 接食圑饭 - Literal expression meaning 'catching and eating round rice,' likely referring to a bird's feeding behavior.
    • 投之高下无失 - Means 'throwing it high or low without missing,' describing the bird's accuracy in catching food.
    • 巫山迎船鸟 - Refers to a bird associated with the Wushan area, known for greeting ships.
    • 凡鸟三指向前一向后 - Describes the typical bird's wing structure with three forward-facing fingers and one backward-facing finger.
    • 鹦鹉两指向后 - Specifies that parrots have two fingers pointing backward.
    • 汉明帝永平五年 - Indicates the year 62 AD during the reign of Emperor Ming of Han.
    • 长安迎取飞廉并铜马 - Refers to the act of bringing the wind god Feilian and a bronze horse from Chang'an.
    • 置上西门外 - Means 'placed at the west gate of the upper city.'
    • 晋灼曰 - Refers to Jin Zhuo, a commentator, who provides an explanation.
    • 飞廉鹿身头如雀有角而蛇尾豹文 - Describes Feilian as having a deer body, a bird's head, antlers, a snake's tail, and leopard-like patterns.
    • 应劭曰 - Refers to Ying Shao, another commentator, who provides an alternative explanation.
    • 飞廉神禽能致风气 - Describes Feilian as a divine bird capable of summoning wind.
    • 宣王好射三石之弓 - Refers to King Xuan of Zhou, who enjoyed shooting a bow that could draw three stones.
    • 自以为九石 - Means 'considered it to be nine stones,' indicating a misperception of its strength.
    • 臣下从而悦之 - Means 'ministers followed suit and flattered him,' suggesting a culture of flattery.
    • 以非大王不能用其实三石也九石名而已 - Explains that the actual strength was three stones, but it was called nine stones, indicating a discrepancy between reality and perception.
    • 周官全羽为旞析羽为旌 - Refers to the Zhou ritual text, where full feathers are used for *suì* and split feathers for *jīng*.
    • 道路用旌节即此旌也 - Indicates that the *jīng* used on roads is the same as the *jīng* described.
    • 顾恺之画苏武所执之旌 - Refers to Gu Kaizhi's painting of Su Wu holding a *jīng*.
    • 上圆如幢下复数层红羽骖骖然如夜合花 - Describes the *jīng* as having a round top like a banner, multiple layers of red feathers, and a shape resembling a night-blooming flower.
    • 此析羽也 - Indicates that this is an example of split feathers.
    • 今卤簿中节颇此 - Refers to the current imperial processions, where the *jié* (staff) is similar to the *jīng*.
    • 其首亦圆相去尺余有数重圆板以牦牛尾缀之 - Describes the *jié* as having a round top, spaced about a foot apart, with several layers of circular plates attached by yak tails.
    • 亦析羽遗法 - Indicates that this is a legacy of the split feather tradition.
    • 苏武汉节则旌节之制 - Refers to Su Wu's *jié*, which follows the *jīng* and *jié* system.
    • 至于龙节虎节玉节之 - Refers to different types of *jié* used by various ranks, including dragon, tiger, and jade *jié*.
    • 则今虎符之 - Indicates that these are similar to the current tiger-shaped seals.
    • 但可合符不必尽为旌耳 - Suggests that these *jié* are used for verification but not necessarily as *jīng*.
    • 顾画节首一物若书卷 - Describes the *jié* as having a top that looks like a scroll.
    • 岂亦符乎 - Questions whether this is also a seal.
    • 汉铜符以铜为之 - Refers to the Han dynasty's copper seals.
    • 竹符以竹五寸镌刻篆书其上 - Describes the Han dynasty's bamboo seals, which were five inches long and inscribed with seal script.
    • 砚谱载天下之砚四十余品 - Refers to a catalog of over forty types of inkstones.
    • 以青州红丝石砚为第一 - Indicates that the red silk stone inkstone from Qingzhou is ranked first.
    • 端州斧柯山石为第二 - Indicates that the stone from Fuxi Mountain in Duanzhou is ranked second.
    • 歙州龙尾石为第三 - Indicates that the dragon-tail stone from Shezhou is ranked third.
    • 夜蔵饮食于噐中覆之不宻 - Refers to storing food in a container that is not tightly covered at night.
    • 鼠欲盗食不可至环噐而走 - Describes mice trying to steal food but being unable to reach it, running around the container.
    • 泪滴噐中食之得黄疾通身如蜡 - Indicates that the mice's tears fall into the food, causing a disease that makes their bodies turn yellow and waxy.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 彭泽 - A place name, likely referring to Pengze County in Jiangxi Province.
    • 巫山 - A famous mountain range in China, known for its scenic beauty and cultural significance.
    • 汉明帝永平五年 - The year 62 AD during the reign of Emperor Ming of Han.
    • 长安 - The ancient capital of China, now known as Xi'an.
    • 飞廉 - The wind god in Chinese mythology.
    • 铜马 - A bronze horse, likely a symbol of power and prestige.
    • 晋灼 - A commentator from the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 应劭 - A historian and commentator from the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 宣王 - King Xuan of Zhou, a ruler from the Western Zhou dynasty.
    • 周官 - The Zhou ritual text, which outlines the duties and rituals of the Zhou dynasty.
    • 顾恺之 - A famous painter from the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 苏武 - A historical figure known for his loyalty and endurance during the Han dynasty.
    • 龙节虎节玉节 - Different types of *jié* used by various ranks, including dragon, tiger, and jade *jié*.
    • 虎符 - Tiger-shaped seals used for military verification.
    • 青州红丝石砚 - A type of inkstone from Qingzhou, known for its quality.
    • 端州斧柯山石 - A type of stone from Fuxi Mountain in Duanzhou, known for its quality.
    • 歙州龙尾石 - A type of stone from Shezhou, known for its quality.

    Proper Nouns

    • 彭泽 - A place name, likely referring to Pengze County in Jiangxi Province.
    • 巫山 - A famous mountain range in China, known for its scenic beauty and cultural significance.
