    • 汉明帝永平五年 - The year 62 AD during the reign of Emperor Ming of Han.
    • 长安 - The ancient capital of China, now known as Xi'an.
    • 飞廉 - The wind god in Chinese mythology.
    • 铜马 - A bronze horse, likely a symbol of power and prestige.
    • 晋灼 - A commentator from the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 应劭 - A historian and commentator from the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 宣王 - King Xuan of Zhou, a ruler from the Western Zhou dynasty.
    • 周官 - The Zhou ritual text, which outlines the duties and rituals of the Zhou dynasty.
    • 顾恺之 - A famous painter from the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 苏武 - A historical figure known for his loyalty and endurance during the Han dynasty.
    • 龙节虎节玉节 - Different types of *jié* used by various ranks, including dragon, tiger, and jade *jié*.
    • 虎符 - Tiger-shaped seals used for military verification.
    • 青州红丝石砚 - A type of inkstone from Qingzhou, known for its quality.
    • 端州斧柯山石 - A type of stone from Fuxi Mountain in Duanzhou, known for its quality.
    • 歙州龙尾石 - A type of stone from Shezhou, known for its quality.

    Zhang Bo's Wu Lu records that in Pengze there is a bird that catches and eats round rice, throwing it accurately, high or low without missing. The bird in Wushan that greets ships is also like this.

    All birds have three fingers pointing forward and one pointing backward, except for parrots, which have two fingers pointing backward.

    In the fifth year of Yongping during the reign of Emperor Ming of Han, the wind god Feilian and a bronze horse were brought from Chang'an and placed at the west gate of the upper city. Jin Zhuo said that Feilian has a deer body, a bird's head like a sparrow, antlers, a snake's tail, and leopard-like patterns. Ying Shao said that Feilian is a divine bird capable of summoning wind.

    King Xuan of Zhou enjoyed shooting a bow that could draw three stones, but he considered it to be nine stones. Ministers followed suit and flattered him, saying that only the great king could use it. In reality, its strength was three stones, but it was called nine stones, indicating a discrepancy between reality and perception.

    According to the Zhouguan, full feathers are used for suì and split feathers for jīng. The jīng used on roads is the same as the jīng described. Gu Kaizhi painted the jīng that Su Wu held, which had a round top like a banner, multiple layers of red feathers, and a shape resembling a night-blooming flower. This is an example of split feathers. In current imperial processions, the jié (staff) is similar to the jīng. Its top is also round, spaced about a foot apart, with several layers of circular plates attached by yak tails. This is a legacy of the split feather tradition. However, Su Wu's jié follows the jīng and jié system. As for dragon, tiger, and jade jié, these are similar to the current tiger-shaped seals. They are used for verification but not necessarily as jīng. Gu Kaizhi's painting of the jié shows a top that looks like a scroll. Is this also a seal? The Han dynasty's copper seals were made of copper, and the bamboo seals were five inches long, inscribed with seal script.

    A catalog of over forty types of inkstones records that the red silk stone inkstone from Qingzhou is ranked first, the stone from Fuxi Mountain in Duanzhou is ranked second, and the dragon-tail stone from Shezhou is ranked third.

    If food is stored in a container that is not tightly covered at night, mice will try to steal it but be unable to reach it, running around the container. Their tears fall into the food, causing a disease that makes their bodies turn yellow and waxy. [57]

    鍮石金碔砆玉木兰类桂奚醢类檀蛇牀类蘼芜人参类荠苨桧类柏狐狸狗鸑鷟鳯野马麟
    　　肃宗时有告保定太守反者令李泌乘千里马往案之千里马宁逺所献髪拳紫色状如八骏乘者必衣裘冒絮似北人抱持早发扶风初驰三十里则皆旁侧跳三十里外乃隤然而去惟闻耳傍风声飞前蹄之土过耳辰时达保定申时归奏事
    　　颛帝有三子而亡去为疫鬼其一居江水为疫鬼其一居弱水为罔两其一居人宫室塸堣善惊人小儿
    　　脚弱病用杉木为桶濯足排樟脑两股间以脚掤系定月余即效
    　　杂令曰蹄人者绊其足齧人者截其耳说文牛触者角着横木
    　　有一狠子生平多逆父防父临死嘱曰必葬我水中意其逆命得葬土中至是狠子曰生平逆父命死不敢达防也破家筑沙潭水心以葬
    　　本草经曰虎啸风生龙吟云起磁石引鍼琥珀拾芥漆得蟹而散麻得漆而涌桂得葱而软树得桂而枯戎盐累獭胆分杯其气爽之相关感也 [58]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [58]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 鍮石金 - A comparison where *鍮石* (bronze-like stone) is likened to *金* (gold), suggesting a false or inferior counterpart.
    • 碔砆玉 - A comparison where *碔砆* (a kind of stone) is likened to *玉* (jade), again indicating a false or inferior counterpart.
    • 木兰类桂 - Magnolia is compared to cinnamon for its fragrance and value.
    • 奚醢类檀 - A type of sauce is compared to sandalwood for its aroma.
    • 蛇牀类蘼芜 - Herbs are compared for their medicinal properties.
    • 人参类荠苨 - Ginseng is compared to other plants for its medicinal value.
    • 桧类柏 - Cypress is compared to pine for its appearance and use.
    • 狐狸狗 - Foxes and dogs are compared for their behavior and appearance.
    • 鸑鷟鳯 - Mythical birds are compared for their beauty and significance.
    • 野马麟 - Wild horses are compared to unicorns for their rarity and majesty.
    • 乘千里马往案之 - Refers to riding a thousand-mile horse to investigate, emphasizing speed and urgency.
    • 千里马宁远所献 - Indicates that the thousand-mile horse was a gift from Ning Yuan, highlighting the source of the horse.
    • 髪拳紫色状如八骏 - Describes the horse's appearance as curly-haired, purple, and resembling the eight legendary horses.
    • 乘者必衣裘冒絮似北人 - Suggests that only those who ride this horse wear furs and head coverings, implying a northern origin.
    • 抱持早发扶风 - Indicates that the rider held the horse tightly and set out early from Fufeng, emphasizing preparation and urgency.
    • 初驰三十里则皆旁侧跳 - Describes the horse's initial speed and jumping behavior, suggesting its exceptional qualities.
    • 三十里外乃隤然而去 - Indicates that after thirty miles, the horse slows down, showing its endurance and control.
    • 惟闻耳傍风声飞前蹄之土过耳 - Describes the sound of the horse's hooves and the sensation of being carried by the wind, emphasizing its speed and power.
    • 辰时达保定申时归奏事 - Indicates the precise timing of the journey, from 7 AM to 5 PM, highlighting the efficiency of the ride.
    • 颛帝有三子而亡去为疫鬼 - States that Emperor Zhuangdi had three sons who became plague demons, indicating a mythological background.
    • 其一居江水为疫鬼 - Suggests that one son resides in the Yangtze River and causes plagues, emphasizing the geographical distribution of the demons.
    • 其一居弱水为罔两 - Indicates that another son resides in the weak water and becomes a shadow demon, suggesting a different role.
    • 其一居人宫室塸堣善惊人小儿 - States that the third son resides in human dwellings and frightens children, emphasizing the specific behavior of the demon.
    • 脚弱病用杉木为桶濯足排樟脑两股间以脚掤系定月余即效 - Describes a treatment for foot weakness using a bucket made of cypress wood, camphor, and other materials, emphasizing the method and its effectiveness.
    • 杂令曰蹄人者绊其足齧人者截其耳 - Refers to a law that punishes those who kick or bite others by binding their feet or cutting off their ears, indicating strict legal measures.
    • 说文牛触者角着横木 - Refers to a dictionary entry that describes how to handle a bull that charges by placing a horizontal beam on its horns, emphasizing a practical solution.
    • 有一狠子生平多逆父防父临死嘱曰必葬我水中意其逆命得葬土中至是狠子曰生平逆父命死不敢达防也破家筑沙潭水心以葬 - Describes a son who often defied his father, who, on his deathbed, requested to be buried in water. The son, fearing to disobey, built a sand pond in the middle of a river to fulfill the request, emphasizing the son's obedience and the father's wishes.
    • 本草经曰虎啸风生龙吟云起磁石引鍼琥珀拾芥漆得蟹而散麻得漆而涌桂得葱而软树得桂而枯戎盐累獭胆分杯其气爽之相关感也 - Refers to the *Bencao Jing* (Herbal Classic), describing various natural phenomena and their interactions, emphasizing the interconnectedness of nature.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 鍮石金 - A common comparison in classical Chinese literature to highlight the distinction between true and false.
    • 碔砆玉 - Another common comparison to highlight the distinction between true and false.
    • 木兰类桂 - Magnolia is often compared to cinnamon for its fragrance and value.
    • 奚醢类檀 - A type of sauce is compared to sandalwood for its aroma.
    • 蛇牀类蘼芜 - Herbs are often compared for their medicinal properties.
    • 人参类荠苨 - Ginseng is compared to other plants for its medicinal value.
    • 桧类柏 - Cypress is compared to pine for its appearance and use.
    • 狐狸狗 - Foxes and dogs are often compared for their behavior and appearance.
    • 鸑鷟鳯 - Mythical birds are often compared for their beauty and significance.
    • 野马麟 - Wild horses are compared to unicorns for their rarity and majesty.
    • 千里马 - A legendary horse known for its speed and endurance.
    • 宁远 - A place name, likely referring to a region in ancient China.
    • 八骏 - The eight legendary horses of Emperor Yao.
    • 扶风 - A place name, likely referring to a region in ancient China.
    • 颛帝 - Emperor Zhuangdi, a legendary ruler in Chinese mythology.
    • 疫鬼 - Plague demons, often associated with disease and death.
    • 弱水 - A mythical river, often associated with the weak water in Chinese mythology.
    • 罔两 - Shadow demons, often associated with illusions and deception.
    • 人宫室 - Human dwellings, often associated with the home and family.
    • 塸堣 - A type of dwelling, often associated with rural or rustic settings.
    • 脚弱病 - A condition affecting the legs, often treated with herbal remedies.
    • 杉木 - Cypress wood, often used in traditional Chinese medicine.
    • 樟脑 - Camphor, often used in traditional Chinese medicine.
    • 杂令 - A type of law or decree, often used to maintain order.
    • 说文 - The *Shuowen Jiezi*, a dictionary of Chinese characters.
    • 本草经 - The *Bencao Jing*, an ancient herbal text.

    False Gold and Jade, and Other Comparisons
    Bronze-like stone is likened to gold, and wufu (a kind of stone) is likened to jade. Magnolia is compared to cinnamon, and xihai (a type of sauce) is compared to sandalwood. Shechuang (a type of herb) is compared to miling (another herb), and ginseng is compared to jisi (a type of plant). Cypress is compared to pine, and fox is likened to dog. The mythical bird yuezhao is compared to the phoenix, and the wild horse is likened to the unicorn.

    During the reign of Emperor Suzong, there was a report of a rebellion by the governor of Baoding. The emperor ordered Li Bi to ride a thousand-mile horse to investigate. The thousand-mile horse was a gift from Ning Yuan, with curly purple hair resembling the eight legendary horses. Only those who ride this horse wear furs and head coverings, suggesting a northern origin. The rider held the horse tightly and set out early from Fufeng. Initially, the horse ran thirty miles, jumping and leaping on the side, but after thirty miles, it slowed down. One could only hear the wind sound by the ear and the flying soil from the front hooves. The journey took from 7 AM to 5 PM, from Fufeng to Baoding.

    Emperor Zhuangdi had three sons who became plague demons. One son resided in the Yangtze River and caused plagues, another in the weak water and became a shadow demon, and the third in human dwellings and frightened children.

    For foot weakness, a bucket made of cypress wood is used to wash the feet, with camphor placed between the thighs. The feet are bound with a strap, and the treatment is effective after a month.

    The law states that those who kick others are bound, and those who bite others have their ears cut off. According to the Shuowen Jiezi, when a bull charges, a horizontal beam is placed on its horns.

    There was a son who often defied his father. On his deathbed, the father requested to be buried in water, fearing that his son would disobey. The son, fearing to disobey, built a sand pond in the middle of a river to fulfill the request.

    The Bencao Jing states: "When a tiger roars, the wind arises; when a dragon sings, clouds rise. Magnetite attracts needles, amber picks up small objects, lacquer disperses when mixed with crab, hemp rises when mixed with lacquer, cinnamon softens when mixed with green onion, and trees wither when mixed with cinnamon. Military salt and otter bile, when mixed, have a refreshing effect, showing their interrelated influences." [58]

    一曰偏记二曰小録三曰逸事四曰鏁言五曰郡事六曰家史七曰别传八曰杂记九曰地理十曰都邑
    　　唐大顺元年六月资州兵王全义妻如孕觉物渐下入股至足大拇痛甚拆而生珠如弹圆渐长大如杯
    　　西京相璠曰京有小索亭世语以为本索氏兄弟居又为索水晋志京有大索小索亭汉书京索之间也
    　　纪信在荥阳城西北三里
    　　汉长安巧工丁缓作卧褥香炉炉体常平又作七轮扇连七轮大皆径尺一人运之满堂寒战今二法并存
    　　计然云人受命于天地变化而生一月而膏二月而脉三月而胚谓如水泡之状四月而胎谓如水中虾防胎也五月而筋六月而骨谓血气变为肉肉为脂脂为骨也七月而成形八月而动九月而躁十月而生
    　　九章筭法方田一粟米二差分三少广四均输五方程六傍要七盈足八钩股九
    　　语林云王中郎以围棊为坐或亦谓之为手谈又谓之为棊圣
    　　窭薮王叡云东方朔以为寄生卖饼人结茅为绖以戴头上状如环
    　　蟹鬭精上有孔其中有子有泥食之杀人 [59]

    1. The first category is bianji (偏记), the second is xiaolu (小录), the third is yishi (逸事), the fourth is suoyan (琐言), the fifth is junshi (郡事), the sixth is jishi (家史), the seventh is bietsuan (别传), the eighth is zaji (杂记), the ninth is dili (地理), and the tenth is duyi (都邑).

    In the first month of Dashi (大顺) in the year 890 of the Tang Dynasty, the wife of Wang Quanyi, a soldier from Zizhou (资州), felt a sensation of something gradually descending into her thigh, reaching her foot, and causing severe pain in her big toe. She then gave birth to a pearl-like object, which grew larger and eventually became as big as a cup.

    Xijing Xiang Fan said that there was a small Suxing Pavilion (小索亭) in the capital, which was believed to be the residence of the Suxing brothers and was also called Suxing Water (索水). According to the Jin Zhi (晋志), there were large and small Suxing Pavilions in the capital, and the Han Shu (汉书) mentions the area between Jing and Suxing.

    Ji Xun's tomb (纪信) is located three li northwest of Xingyang City (荥阳城).

    In the capital of Han, the ingenious craftsman Ding Huan created a bed warmer perfumed incense burner, whose body remained level. He also made a seven-wheeled fan, each wheel being one foot in diameter, which could be operated by one person, cooling the entire hall. Both of these inventions still exist today.

    Ji Ran said that humans receive their mandate from the changes of heaven and earth, and in the first month, they form a fatty substance; in the second month, they develop veins; in the third month, they form an embryo, like a water bubble; in the fourth month, they develop into a fetus, like a shrimp in water; in the fifth month, they develop muscles; in the sixth month, they develop bones; in the seventh month, they take shape; in the eighth month, they move; in the ninth month, they become active; and in the tenth month, they are born.

    The Jiu Zhang Suanshu (九章筭法) includes: fangtian (方田) for land measurement, sumi (粟米) for grain measurement, chafen (差分) for proportional distribution, shaoguang (少广) for area calculation, junshu (均输) for fair distribution, fangcheng (方程) for solving equations, bangyao (傍要) for auxiliary methods, yingzu (盈足) for surplus and deficit, and gougou (钩股) for right triangles.

    The Yu Lin (语林) says that Wang Zhonglang played weiqi (围棊) as a form of sitting meditation, which was also called "hand talk" or "the sage of chess."

    Wang Rui, in Ju Sou (窭薮), said that Dongfang Shuo (东方朔) believed that beggars who sold pies would tie straw ropes around their heads, forming a ring-like shape.

    The Xie Dou Jing (蟹鬭精) has a hole on top, containing eggs and mud. Eating it can be fatal. [59]

    露布防书之别名以帛书揭之竿魏武奏事谓之露板公孙龙以书有四目四聪遂以聴天地人为臧之三耳臧善也以白马非白马教也其意若改其说无以教人
    　　易之生物竒偶三才之数三三而九九九八十一主日日数十故人十月而生八九七十二偶以承奇奇主辰辰主月月主马马十二月而生七九六十三三主斗斗主狗狗三月而生六九五十四四主时时主豕豕四月而生五九四十五五主音音主猿猿五月而生四九三十六六主律律主禽兽故禽鹿六月而生三九二十七七主星星主虎虎七月而生二九一十八八主风故虫八月而化
    　　陶答子妻曰南山有豹雾雨七日不下食者欲以泽其衣毛而成其文章故藏以逺害
    　　齐民要术曰梧桐山石间生者为乐器则鸣
    　　周书曰清明之日桐始华桐若不华岁有大寒
    　　太行即五行之异名淮南子曰武王欲筑宫于五行之山
    　　猗顿鲁之穷士耕则常寒闻朱公富往问术焉朱公曰子欲速富常畜五牸
    　　童子操戈与孟贲战孟贲无不胜童子修礼尽敬虽孟贲不忍犯 [60]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [60]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 露布防书之别名 - Another name for a public proclamation.
    • 以帛书揭之竿 - Written on silk and displayed on a pole; describes the method of displaying official documents publicly.
    • 魏武奏事谓之露板 - Cao Cao referred to official reports as '露板'.
    • 公孙龙以书有四目四聪 - Gongsun Long said that books have four eyes and four ears; a metaphorical expression referring to the comprehensive nature of books.
    • 遂以聴天地人为臧之三耳 - Thus, they listen to heaven, earth, and people as the three ears of wisdom; a metaphorical expression suggesting that books serve as a means to understand the world.
    • 臧善也 - Good is to be praised.
    • 以白马非白马教也 - To teach that a white horse is not a horse; refers to a famous logical paradox by Gongsun Long.
    • 其意若改其说无以教人 - If the meaning were changed, there would be no way to teach; explains the importance of maintaining the original meaning in teaching.
    • 易之生物竒偶三才之数 - The *Yi Jing* (Book of Changes) describes the numbers of odd and even, the three talents; refers to the philosophical concept of the three talents (heaven, earth, and humanity) in the *Yi Jing*.
    • 三三而九九九八十一主日 - Three threes make nine, nine nines make eighty-one, which governs the sun; a numerical and symbolic reference in the *Yi Jing*.
    • 日数十故人十月而生 - The sun has ten days, so humans are born after ten months; a symbolic explanation of human gestation.
    • 八九七十二偶以承奇 - Eight nines make seventy-two, which is even to complement the odd; a numerical and symbolic reference.
    • 奇主辰辰主月月主马 - Odd governs the stars, stars govern the moon, and the moon governs the horse; a symbolic explanation of celestial and terrestrial relationships.
    • 马十二月而生 - Horses are born after twelve months; a symbolic explanation of animal gestation.
    • 七九六十三三主斗斗主狗 - Seven nines make sixty-three, which governs the constellations, and the constellations govern the dog; a symbolic explanation of celestial and terrestrial relationships.
    • 狗三月而生 - Dogs are born after three months; a symbolic explanation of animal gestation.
    • 六九五十四四主时 - Six nines make fifty-four, which governs time; a symbolic explanation of time.
    • 时主豕 - Time governs the pig; a symbolic explanation of celestial and terrestrial relationships.
    • 豕四月而生 - Pigs are born after four months; a symbolic explanation of animal gestation.
    • 五九四十五五主音 - Five nines make forty-five, which governs sound; a symbolic explanation of sound.
    • 音主猿 - Sound governs the monkey; a symbolic explanation of celestial and terrestrial relationships.
    • 猿五月而生 - Monkeys are born after five months; a symbolic explanation of animal gestation.
    • 四九三十六六主律 - Four nines make thirty-six, which governs law; a symbolic explanation of law.
    • 律主禽兽 - Law governs animals; a symbolic explanation of celestial and terrestrial relationships.
    • 故禽鹿六月而生 - Therefore, deer are born after six months; a symbolic explanation of animal gestation.
    • 三九二十七七主星 - Three nines make twenty-seven, which governs stars; a symbolic explanation of stars.
    • 星主虎 - Stars govern the tiger; a symbolic explanation of celestial and terrestrial relationships.
    • 虎七月而生 - Tigers are born after seven months; a symbolic explanation of animal gestation.
    • 二九一十八八主风 - Two nines make eighteen, which governs wind; a symbolic explanation of wind.
    • 故虫八月而化 - Therefore, insects transform after eight months; a symbolic explanation of insect development.
    • 陶答子妻曰南山有豹雾雨七日不下 - Taodazi's wife said that there is a leopard in the south mountain that does not descend for seven days of fog and rain; a metaphorical statement about perseverance.
    • 食者欲以泽其衣毛而成其文章 - Those who eat want to moisten their fur and complete their patterns; a metaphorical statement about nourishment and growth.
    • 故藏以远害 - Therefore, they hide to avoid harm; a metaphorical statement about self-preservation.
    • 齐民要术曰梧桐山石间生者为乐器则鸣 - The *Qi Min Yao Shu* (Essential Techniques for the Common People) says that梧桐 trees growing in rocks and mountains, when used as musical instruments, will sound; a reference to the *Qi Min Yao Shu*.
    • 周书曰清明之日桐始华桐若不华岁有大寒 - The *Zhou Shu* (Book of Zhou) says that on the day of Qingming, the paulownia tree begins to flower. If it does not flower, there will be a severe cold year; a reference to the *Zhou Shu*.
    • 太行即五行之异名 - Taihang is another name for the Five Elements; a reference to the *Huainanzi*.
    • 淮南子曰武王欲筑宫于五行之山 - The *Huainanzi* says that King Wu wanted to build a palace on the mountain of the Five Elements; a reference to the *Huainanzi*.
    • 猗顿鲁之穷士耕则常寒 - Yidi was a poor man from Lu who always felt cold when he plowed; a reference to Yidi's story.
    • 闻朱公富往问术焉 - He heard that Zhu Gong was rich and went to ask him for the secret; a reference to Yidi's story.
    • 朱公曰子欲速富常畜五牸 - Zhu Gong said, 'If you want to become rich quickly, always keep five female livestock'; a reference to Yidi's story.
    • 童子操戈与孟贲战 - A child wielding a spear fought against Meng Ben; a reference to a historical or legendary event.
    • 孟贲无不胜 - Meng Ben never lost; a reference to Meng Ben's prowess.
    • 童子修礼尽敬虽孟贲不忍犯 - The child practiced rituals and showed utmost respect, so even Meng Ben did not dare to offend him; a reference to the power of respect and propriety.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 露布 - Refers to a public announcement or edict.
    • 魏武 - Cao Cao, a prominent warlord and statesman during the late Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 公孙龙 - A philosopher from the Warring States period known for his logical paradoxes.
    • 易之生物竒偶三才之数 - Refers to the *Yi Jing* (Book of Changes) and its philosophical concepts.
    • 三三而九九九八十一主日 - Numerical and symbolic references in the *Yi Jing*.
    • 陶答子妻 - Refers to the wife of Taodazi, a historical figure.
    • 齐民要术 - A book on agriculture and daily life during the Northern Wei dynasty.
    • 周书 - A historical text from the Zhou dynasty.
    • 太行 - Refers to the Taihang Mountains.
    • 五行 - The Five Elements (wood, fire, earth, metal, water).
    • 淮南子 - A philosophical text from the Han dynasty.
    • 猗顿 - A historical figure known for his wealth.
    • 鲁 - The state of Lu during the Warring States period.
    • 朱公 - A wealthy merchant.
    • 童子 - A young boy.
    • 孟贲 - A legendary warrior.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The sentence '露布防书之别名以帛书揭之竿' is elliptical and must be read as a single unit describing the nature of public proclamations.
    • The sentence '魏武奏事谓之露板' is elliptical and must be read as a single unit describing Cao Cao's term for official reports.
    • The sentence '公孙龙以书有四目四聪' is elliptical and must be read as a single unit describing Gongsun Long's metaphorical expression about books.

    Translation:

    Another name for a public proclamation is "露布防书," which refers to a document written on silk and displayed on a pole. Cao Cao referred to official reports as "露板." Gongsun Long said that books have four eyes and four ears, thus they listen to heaven, earth, and people as the three ears of wisdom. Good is to be praised. To teach that a white horse is not a horse. If the meaning were changed, there would be no way to teach.

    The Yi Jing (Book of Changes) describes the numbers of odd and even, the three talents. Three threes make nine, nine nines make eighty-one, which governs the sun. The sun has ten days, so humans are born after ten months. Eight nines make seventy-two, which is even to complement the odd. Odd governs the stars, stars govern the moon, and the moon governs the horse. Horses are born after twelve months. Seven nines make sixty-three, which governs the constellations, and the constellations govern the dog. Dogs are born after three months. Six nines make fifty-four, which governs time. Time governs the pig. Pigs are born after four months. Five nines make forty-five, which governs sound. Sound governs the monkey. Monkeys are born after five months. Four nines make thirty-six, which governs law. Law governs animals. Therefore, deer are born after six months. Three nines make twenty-seven, which governs stars. Stars govern the tiger. Tigers are born after seven months. Two nines make eighteen, which governs wind. Therefore, insects transform after eight months.

    Taodazi's wife said that there is a leopard in the south mountain that does not descend for seven days of fog and rain. Those who eat want to moisten their fur and complete their patterns. Therefore, they hide to avoid harm.

    The Qi Min Yao Shu (Essential Techniques for the Common People) says that梧桐 trees growing in rocks and mountains, when used as musical instruments, will sound. The Zhou Shu (Book of Zhou) says that on the day of Qingming, the paulownia tree begins to flower. If it does not flower, there will be a severe cold year.

    Taihang is another name for the Five Elements. The Huainanzi says that King Wu wanted to build a palace on the mountain of the Five Elements.

    Yidi was a poor man from Lu who always felt cold when he plowed. He heard that Zhu Gong was rich and went to ask him for the secret. Zhu Gong said, "If you want to become rich quickly, always keep five female livestock."

    A child wielding a spear fought against Meng Ben. Meng Ben never lost. The child practiced rituals and showed utmost respect, so even Meng Ben did not dare to offend him. [60]

    鸠食桑椹则醉猫食薄荷则醉虎食狗则醉
    　　管子曰聴宫如牛鸣窌中
    　　牛聋瞆面牛鼓簧是也
    　　积皁荚置油瓶其中永不蛀踏鸡子殻令人患白癜风践壊灶土令人害疮
    　　桑柴灰取汁煎白矾治壁镜蛇毒石榴名天浆已乳石毒
    　　桃根为印可召鬼
    　　卧欲缩足不欲左脇
    　　牖里在汤隂县广雅称狱犴也夏曰夏台商曰羑里周曰囹圄皆圜土也

    　　续博物志卷九
    　　钦定四库全书
    　　续博物志卷十
    　　宋　李石　撰
    　　旧说千岁之龟巢于莲叶游于卷耳之上
    　　按干凿度日垂皇策者盖伏羲用蓍卦已重矣然而世质民淳法惟用七八六十四卦皆不动若干止于乾坤止于坤不能变也夏商因之皆以七八为占连山归蔵是已后世浇薄始用九六为占不如是不足以应天下之变
    　　诸侯叛桀关龙逢引皇图以谏桀桀杀之伊尹谏桀桀曰天之有日如吾之有民日亡吾乃亡矣【与前志小异】 [61]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [61]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 鸠食桑椹则醉猫食薄荷则醉虎食狗则醉 - Three idiomatic sayings describing exaggerated reactions of animals to specific foods: crows get drunk on mulberries, cats get drunk on mint, and tigers get drunk on dogs.
    • 聴宫如牛鸣窌中 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'listening to the palace is like hearing a cow bellow in a well,' suggesting a deep, resonant, or hollow sound.
    • 牛聋瞆面牛鼓簧是也 - Humorous or satirical expression meaning 'it's like a cow with deaf ears and a mute face, playing a flute,' suggesting a lack of understanding or a ridiculous situation.
    • 积皁荚置油瓶其中永不蛀踏鸡子殻令人患白癜风践壊灶土令人害疮 - Series of folk remedies or superstitions: 'accumulating soapy beans and placing them in an oil bottle prevents insects; stepping on eggshells causes vitiligo; trampling on ruined hearth soil causes sores.'
    • 桑柴灰取汁煎白矾治壁镜蛇毒石榴名天浆已乳石毒 - Description of medicinal uses: 'mulberry charcoal ash is used to treat snake venom; pomegranates are called 'heavenly juice' and are used to treat stone toxins.'
    • 桃根为印可召鬼 - Folk belief: 'a peach root stamp can summon ghosts.'
    • 卧欲缩足不欲左脇 - Health or sleep advice: 'when sleeping, tuck your feet and avoid lying on your left side.'
    • 干凿度日垂皇策者盖伏羲用蓍卦已重矣然而世质民淳法惟用七八六十四卦皆不动若干止于乾坤止于坤不能变也夏商因之皆以七八为占连山归蔵是已后世浇薄始用九六为占不如是不足以应天下之变 - Complex statement about the evolution of divination methods: 'The *Gan Zao Du Ri* (Book of Ancient Divination) states that Fuxi used the *Qian* and *Kun* hexagrams, but in a world of simple people, only the sixty-four hexagrams were used, and they remained unchanged. In the Xia and Shang dynasties, only the numbers seven and eight were used for divination, which was called *Lianshan* and *Guicang*. Later, in a world of decadence, the numbers nine and six were used for divination, which was necessary to respond to the changes in the world.'
    • 诸侯叛桀关龙逢引皇图以谏桀桀杀之伊尹谏桀桀曰天之有日如吾之有民日亡吾乃亡矣 - Historical account: 'When the feudal lords rebelled against Jie, Guan Longfeng used the *Huangtu* (Yellow River Map) to advise Jie, but Jie killed him. Yi Yin also advised Jie, but Jie said, 'Just as the sun is to the sky, the people are to me. If the sun sets, I will die.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 鸠食桑椹则醉猫食薄荷则醉虎食狗则醉 - References to traditional beliefs about animals' reactions to certain foods.
    • 聴宫如牛鸣窌中 - Reference to a metaphorical expression related to the sound of the palace.
    • 牛聋瞆面牛鼓簧是也 - Reference to a satirical expression related to a ridiculous situation.
    • 积皁荚置油瓶其中永不蛀踏鸡子殻令人患白癜风践壊灶土令人害疮 - References to traditional folk remedies and superstitions.
    • 桑柴灰取汁煎白矾治壁镜蛇毒石榴名天浆已乳石毒 - References to traditional medicine.
    • 桃根为印可召鬼 - Reference to a superstitious practice.
    • 卧欲缩足不欲左脇 - Reference to traditional health wisdom.
    • 牖里在汤隂县广雅称狱犴也夏曰夏台商曰羑里周曰囹圄皆圜土也 - References to historical and geographical locations and their significance.
    • 千岁之龟巢于莲叶游于卷耳之上 - Reference to mythological or poetic imagery.
    • 干凿度日垂皇策者盖伏羲用蓍卦已重矣然而世质民淳法惟用七八六十四卦皆不动若干止于乾坤止于坤不能变也夏商因之皆以七八为占连山归蔵是已后世浇薄始用九六为占不如是不足以应天下之变 - References to the evolution of divination methods and historical philosophical statements.
    • 诸侯叛桀关龙逢引皇图以谏桀桀杀之伊尹谏桀桀曰天之有日如吾之有民日亡吾乃亡矣 - References to historical events and figures.

    Proper Nouns

    • 鸠 - Crow
    • 桑椹 - Mulberry
    • 猫 - Cat
    • 薄荷 - Mint
    • 虎 - Tiger
    • 狗 - Dog
    • 聴宫 - Palace
    • 牛 - Cow
    • 鸣 - Bellow
    • 窌中 - In a well
    • 积皁荚 - Accumulated soapy beans
    • 油瓶 - Oil bottle
    • 踏鸡子殻 - Stepping on eggshells
    • 白癜风 - Vitiligo
    • 践壊灶土 - Trampling on ruined hearth soil
    • 疮 - Sores
    • 桑柴灰 - Mulberry charcoal ash
    • 白矾 - Alum
    • 壁镜蛇毒 - Snake venom
    • 石榴 - Pomegranate
    • 天浆 - Heavenly juice
    • 乳石毒 - Stone toxins
    • 桃根 - Peach root
    • 印 - Stamp
    • 鬼 - Ghost
    • 卧欲缩足不欲左脇 - Health or sleep advice
    • 牖里 - In Tangyin County, known as the prison of the Huan clan
    • 汤隂县 - Tangyin County
    • 广雅 - Guangya
    • 狱犴 - Prison of the Huan clan
    • 夏台 - Xia Tai
    • 羑里 - Youli
    • 囹圄 - Lingyu
    • 圜土 - Circular earthen prisons
    • 千岁之龟 - Thousand-year-old turtle
    • 莲叶 - Lotus leaf
    • 卷耳 - Ears of grain
    • 干凿度日垂皇策者 - Gan Zao Du Ri (Book of Ancient Divination)
    • 伏羲 - Fuxi
    • 蓍卦 - Yarrow stalks and hexagrams
    • 乾坤 - Qian and Kun hexagrams
    • 连山 - Lianshan
    • 归蔵 - Guicang
    • 九六 - Numbers nine and six
    • 诸侯 - Feudal lords
    • 桀 - Jie
    • 关龙逢 - Guan Longfeng
    • 皇图 - Huangtu (Yellow River Map)
    • 伊尹 - Yi Yin
    • 天之有日 - The sun is to the sky
    • 民 - People
    • 日亡 - If the sun sets

    Crows get drunk on mulberries, cats get drunk on mint, and tigers get drunk on dogs.

    The Guanzi says, "Listening to the palace is like hearing a cow bellow in a well."

    It's like a cow with deaf ears and a mute face, playing a flute.

    Accumulating soapy beans and placing them in an oil bottle prevents insects; stepping on eggshells causes vitiligo; trampling on ruined hearth soil causes sores.

    Mulberry charcoal ash is used to treat snake venom; pomegranates are called "heavenly juice" and are used to treat stone toxins.

    A peach root stamp can summon ghosts.

    When sleeping, tuck your feet and avoid lying on your left side.

    Youli is in Tangyin County, known as the prison of the Huan clan. In the Xia dynasty, it was called Xia Tai; in the Shang dynasty, it was called Youli; in the Zhou dynasty, it was called Lingyu, all referring to circular earthen prisons.

    According to old sayings, a thousand-year-old turtle nests on lotus leaves and swims among the ears of grain.

    According to the Gan Zao Du Ri (Book of Ancient Divination), the use of the Qian and Kun hexagrams by Fuxi was already established. However, in a world of simple people, only the sixty-four hexagrams were used, and they remained unchanged. In the Xia and Shang dynasties, only the numbers seven and eight were used for divination, which was called Lianshan and Guicang. Later, in a world of decadence, the numbers nine and six were used for divination, which was necessary to respond to the changes in the world.

    When the feudal lords rebelled against Jie, Guan Longfeng used the Huangtu (Yellow River Map) to advise Jie, but Jie killed him. Yi Yin also advised Jie, but Jie said, "Just as the sun is to the sky, the people are to me. If the sun sets, I will die." [This differs slightly from previous records.] [61]

    纣剖比干观其心桀杀龙逢无剖心之事桀惟比之于日纣乃诈命于天
    　　西伯军至鲔水纣使胶鬲周师问曰西伯将焉之曰之薛胶鬲曰无我欺曰不子欺也将伐纣曰何日至曰以甲子胶鬲以是日报纣是日雨甚卒病请休师西伯曰胶鬲已报其主以雨进兵救胶鬲之死也商容与殷民观周师之入见毕公曰吾新君也容曰非也其人将有急色君子临事而惧见太公曰吾新君也容曰非也其人虎踞而鹰趾当敌将众威怒自倍见利即前不顾其后故君子临众果于进退见周公曰吾新君也容曰非也其为人忻忻行休志在除贼是非天子则周之相国故圣人临众知之见武王曰吾新君也容曰圣人为海内讨恶见恶不怒见善不喜顔色相副以是知之
    　　犀出越睒以陷穽取之毎杀天震雷暴雨
    　　野桑生石上取以为弓不胶漆而利
    　　淮南子曰鲧堙洪水盗帝之息壌帝使祝融杀之于羽渊今荆州南门外有状若屋宇陷土中而犹见其脊旁有石记云不可犯畚锸所入輙复如故又颇以致雷雨近代有妄意发掘水岔出不可制【与前志小异】 [62]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [62]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 剖比干观其心 - Historical reference to King Zhou of Shang, who ordered the dissection of Bi Gan to examine his heart, symbolizing extreme cruelty and disbelief in loyalty.
    • 杀龙逢无剖心之事 - Refers to the execution of Long Feng without examining his heart, contrasting with the treatment of Bi Gan.
    • 诈命于天 - Means 'deceiving the mandate of heaven,' implying that King Zhou pretended to follow divine will but acted tyrannically.
    • 临事而惧 - Literally 'facing affairs with fear,' suggesting caution and prudence in the face of challenges.
    • 虎踞而鹰趾 - A metaphor describing someone who is fierce and determined, like a tiger crouching and an eagle standing on tiptoe.
    • 忻忻行休志在除贼 - Describes a person who is eager and resolute in their mission to eliminate evil.
    • 顔色相副 - Means 'the expression matches the inner state,' indicating sincerity and consistency in demeanor.
    • 陷穽取之 - Refers to using traps to catch rhinos, a method of hunting.
    • 天震雷暴雨 - Describes a natural phenomenon where the sky is struck by lightning and heavy rain follows, often associated with divine retribution.
    • 无我欺 - Means 'do not deceive me,' a request for honesty.
    • 以甲子 - Refers to the day of the week or month according to the Chinese sexagenary cycle, specifically the day of Jiazi.
    • 不可犯畚锸所入辄复如故 - Means 'it cannot be violated; any attempt to dig will be immediately restored,' suggesting the site's sacred or supernatural nature.
    • 颇以致雷雨 - Implies that the site is prone to causing thunderstorms, further emphasizing its mysterious and powerful nature.
    • 妄意发掘 - Means 'recklessly excavating,' indicating an unauthorized or foolish attempt to uncover something.
    • 与前志小异 - Means 'slightly different from previous records,' acknowledging variations in historical accounts.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 纣 (King Zhou of Shang) - The last ruler of the Shang dynasty, known for his tyranny and the fall of his kingdom.
    • 比干 (Bi Gan) - A loyal minister of King Zhou, who was executed for his opposition to the king's wickedness.
    • 桀 (King Jie of Xia) - The last ruler of the Xia dynasty, known for his cruelty and the subsequent rise of the Shang dynasty.
    • 龙逢 (Long Feng) - A loyal minister of King Jie, who was executed for his warnings against the king's tyranny.
    • 西伯 (Western Lord, later King Wen of Zhou) - The founder of the Zhou dynasty, known for his wisdom and preparation for the downfall of the Shang.
    • 胶鬲 (Jiao Ge) - A loyal minister of King Zhou, who served as an envoy to the Western Lord.
    • 商容 (Shang Rong) - A minister of King Zhou, known for his wisdom and integrity.
    • 毕公 (Bi Gong) - A general of the Western Lord, known for his military prowess.
    • 太公 (Tai Gong, later known as Jiang Shang or Jiang Ziya) - A key advisor and military strategist of the Western Lord.
    • 周公 (Zhou Gong, later known as Duke of Zhou) - A key advisor and statesman of the Western Lord, instrumental in the establishment of the Zhou dynasty.
    • 武王 (King Wu of Zhou) - The son of the Western Lord, who led the final campaign against King Zhou.
    • 犀 (rhinoceros) - A large, powerful animal, often used in metaphors to describe strength and ferocity.
    • 野桑生石上 - A rare and unusual natural phenomenon, often associated with mystical or supernatural significance.
    • 淮南子 (Huainanzi) - A philosophical text attributed to Liu An, Prince of Huainan, during the Han dynasty, containing a mix of Daoist, Confucian, and other philosophical ideas.
    • 鲧 (Gun) - A legendary figure in Chinese mythology, known for his failed attempt to control the floods and his eventual punishment by the Jade Emperor.
    • 息壤 (Xirang) - A mythical soil that could expand to fill any space, used by Gun to control the floods.
    • 祝融 (Zhu Rong) - A deity in Chinese mythology, often associated with fire and the south.
    • 羽渊 (Yuyuan) - A mythical location, often associated with the death of Gun.
    • 荆州 (Jingzhou) - An ancient region in China, located in what is now Hubei and Hunan provinces.
    • 畚锸 (běn chā) - Tools used for digging and earthmoving.
    • 近代 (modern times) - Refers to a period closer to the time of the text's composition, which is likely the Han dynasty.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The sentence '纣剖比干观其心桀杀龙逢无剖心之事桀惟比之于日纣乃诈命于天' must be read as a series of linked actions and descriptions, listing the cruel acts of King Zhou and King Jie, and their respective fates.
    • The long sentence '西伯军至鲔水纣使胶鬲周师问曰西伯将焉之曰之薛胶鬲曰无我欺曰不子欺也将伐纣曰何日至曰以甲子胶鬲以是日报纣是日雨甚卒病请休师西伯曰胶鬲已报其主以雨进兵救胶鬲之死也' must be read as a sequence of events and dialogues, describing the interaction between the Western Lord's army and King Zhou's envoy, Jiao Ge.
    • The long sentence '商容与殷民观周师之入见毕公曰吾新君也容曰非也其人将有急色君子临事而惧见太公曰吾新君也容曰非也其人虎踞而鹰趾当敌将众威怒自倍见利即前不顾其后故君子临众果于进退见周公曰吾新君也容曰非也其为人忻忻行休志在除贼是非天子则周之相国故圣人临众知之见武王曰吾新君也容曰圣人为海内讨恶见恶不怒见善不喜顔色相副以是知之' must be read as a series of linked observations and judgments, describing the reactions of various individuals to the Western Lord's army, each providing a different perspective on the leader's character.
    • The sentence '犀出越睒以陷穽取之毎杀天震雷暴雨' must be read as a cause-and-effect relationship, describing the method of hunting rhinos and the natural phenomena associated with it.
    • The sentence '野桑生石上取以为弓不胶漆而利' must be read as a description of a unique occurrence, describing a rare and unusual natural phenomenon and its practical application.
    • The sentence '淮南子曰鲧堙洪水盗帝之息壤帝使祝融杀之于羽渊今荆州南门外有状若屋宇陷土中而犹见其脊旁有石记云不可犯畚锸所入辄复如故又颇以致雷雨近代有妄意发掘水岔出不可制与前志小异' must be read as a combination of historical narrative and description, citing a historical and mythological source and describing a site in Jingzhou with supernatural properties.

    Proper Nouns

    • 纣 (King Zhou of Shang) - The last ruler of the Shang dynasty, known for his tyranny and the fall of his kingdom.
    • 比干 (Bi Gan) - A loyal minister of King Zhou, who was executed for his opposition to the king's wickedness.
    • 桀 (King Jie of Xia) - The last ruler of the Xia dynasty, known for his cruelty and the subsequent rise of the Shang dynasty.
    • 龙逢 (Long Feng) - A loyal minister of King Jie, who was executed for his warnings against the king's tyranny.
    • 西伯 (Western Lord, later King Wen of Zhou) - The founder of the Zhou dynasty, known for his wisdom and preparation for the downfall of the Shang.
    • 胶鬲 (Jiao Ge) - A loyal minister of King Zhou, who served as an envoy to the Western Lord.
    • 商容 (Shang Rong) - A minister of King Zhou, known for his wisdom and integrity.
    • 毕公 (Bi Gong) - A general of the Western Lord, known for his military prowess.
    • 太公 (Tai Gong, later known as Jiang Shang or Jiang Ziya) - A key advisor and military strategist of the Western Lord.
    • 周公 (Zhou Gong, later known as Duke of Zhou) - A key advisor and statesman of the Western Lord, instrumental in the establishment of the Zhou dynasty.
    • 武王 (King Wu of Zhou) - The son of the Western Lord, who led the final campaign against King Zhou.
    • 犀 (rhinoceros) - A large, powerful animal, often used in metaphors to describe strength and ferocity.
    • 淮南子 (Huainanzi) - A philosophical text attributed to Liu An, Prince of Huainan, during the Han dynasty, containing a mix of Daoist, Confucian, and other philosophical ideas.
    • 鲧 (Gun) - A legendary figure in Chinese mythology, known for his failed attempt to control the floods and his eventual punishment by the Jade Emperor.
    • 息壤 (Xirang) - A mythical soil that could expand to fill any space, used by Gun to control the floods.
    • 祝融 (Zhu Rong) - A deity in Chinese mythology, often associated with fire and the south.
    • 羽渊 (Yuyuan) - A mythical location, often associated with the death of Gun.
    • 荆州 (Jingzhou) - An ancient region in China, located in what is now Hubei and Hunan provinces.
    • 畚锸 (běn chā) - Tools used for digging and earthmoving.
    • 近代 (modern times) - Refers to a period closer to the time of the text's composition, which is likely the Han dynasty.

    King Zhou of Shang ordered the dissection of Bi Gan to examine his heart, while King Jie of Xia executed Long Feng without examining his heart. King Jie compared himself to the sun, but King Zhou deceived the mandate of heaven.

    When the Western Lord's army reached the Wui River, King Zhou sent Jiao Ge to spy on the Zhou forces. Jiao Ge asked, "Where is the Western Lord going?" The Western Lord replied, "To Xue." Jiao Ge said, "Do not deceive me." The Western Lord said, "I do not deceive you. I am going to attack King Zhou." Jiao Ge asked, "On what day?" The Western Lord replied, "On the day of Jiazi." Jiao Ge reported this to King Zhou, and on that day, heavy rain fell, and the army fell ill. They requested a delay, and the Western Lord said, "Jiao Ge has already reported this to his master. I will advance with the rain to save Jiao Ge's life."

    Shang Rong and the people of Yin observed the Zhou forces entering the city. When they saw Bi Gong, they said, "He is our new ruler." Shang Rong said, "No, he shows signs of fear in the face of danger. A true leader is cautious and prudent in the face of challenges." When they saw Tai Gong, they said, "He is our new ruler." Shang Rong said, "No, he is fierce and determined, like a tiger crouching and an eagle standing on tiptoe. When facing an enemy, he leads with doubled courage and sees opportunities without hesitation. A true leader is decisive in his actions." When they saw Zhou Gong, they said, "He is our new ruler." Shang Rong said, "No, he is eager and resolute in his mission to eliminate evil. If he were not the Son of Heaven, he would be the chancellor of Zhou. A true leader is wise and discerning in his judgment." When they saw King Wu, they said, "He is our new ruler." Shang Rong said, "No, he is a sage who seeks to punish evil and does not show anger when he sees it, nor joy when he sees good. His expressions match his inner state. A true leader is consistent and sincere in his demeanor."

    Rhinos are hunted by setting traps, and each time one is killed, the sky is struck by lightning and followed by heavy rain.

    Wild mulberry trees grow on stones, and their wood is used to make bows that do not require glue or lacquer to be effective.

    The Huainanzi states that Gun tried to control the floods by stealing the mythical soil Xirang from the Jade Emperor. The Jade Emperor sent Zhu Rong to kill Gun at Yuyuan. Today, there is a site south of the city of Jingzhou that looks like a collapsed building, with its ridge still visible. There is a stone inscription that says, "It cannot be violated; any attempt to dig will be immediately restored." It is also prone to causing thunderstorms. In modern times, there have been reckless attempts to excavate, resulting in water gushing out uncontrollably. [This differs slightly from previous records.] [62]

    佛楼沙国有佛鉢受三斗许青玉也或曰青石或曰杂色而墨多四际分明厚可二分贫人以少华投中即满富人以多华正复百千万斛终不满或曰在月支国
    　　穆天子传曰天子賔于西王母乃纪其迹于弇山即西王母之山
    　　武王既胜纣登邠之阜以望商邑曰自发未生六十年麋鹿在牧蜚鸿满野徐广乃引内史边之言曰夷羊在牧张华以为文王之词曰水潦东流飞鸿满野又曰自发未生之十年【与前志小异】
    　　淮南子曰乱之所生皆在流遁大兴宫室延楼栈道鸡栖井干木欂栌以相支持木巧之节此遁于木也
    　　魏军南伐朱修之守滑台城不下其母悲忧乳汁惊出乃号踊告家人曰我年老非有乳时今出其不免是日城陷
    　　帝王世纪尧时厨中自生肉脯薄如翣摇鼓则生风使食物寒而不臭
    　　尧缉獬豸之皮以为帝帐
    　　岭南溪洞中往往有飞头者故有飞头老子之号头将飞一日前颈有痕匝项如红缕妻子共守之及夜生翼飞去晓却还 [63]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [63]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 為之一空 - Set phrase meaning completely emptied; fields were left barren.
    • 受三斗許 - A measure of capacity, meaning 'capable of holding about three dou' (an ancient Chinese unit of volume).
    • 青玉也或曰青石或曰雜色而墨多 - Describes the material of the Buddha's alms bowl, with variations in interpretation (jade, stone, or multicolored with much black).
    • 四際分明 - Means 'the edges are clearly defined.'
    • 厚可二分 - Means 'the thickness is about two fen' (an ancient Chinese unit of length).
    • 貧人以少華投中即滿 - Means 'poor people throw a few flowers into it and it is full.'
    • 富人以多華正復百萬斛終不滿 - Means 'rich people throw many flowers, even hundreds of thousands of dou, but it never fills up.'
    • 穆天子傳 - Refers to the *Records of the Grand Historian* (Shiji) or a related text, detailing the travels of the mythical King Mu of Zhou.
    • 賔于西王母 - Means 'visited the Queen Mother of the West,' a mythical figure in Chinese folklore.
    • 紀其迹于弇山 - Means 'recorded his journey on Mount Yin.'
    • 武王既勝纣 - Refers to King Wu of Zhou's victory over the Shang dynasty.
    • 登邠之阜 - Means 'ascended the hill of Bi,' a place associated with King Wu.
    • 水潦东流 - Means 'water flows eastward,' a metaphor for the passage of time or the natural order.
    • 蜚鸿满野 - Means 'wild geese fill the fields,' a metaphor for abundance or natural beauty.
    • 内史边之言 - Refers to the words of an official named Bian, likely a historical or literary figure.
    • 夷羊在牧 - Means 'wild sheep graze in the fields,' a metaphor for peace and prosperity.
    • 淮南子 - Refers to the *Huainanzi*, a philosophical text from the Han dynasty.
    • 亂之所生皆在流遁 - Means 'the origin of chaos lies in deviation.'
    • 大兴宫室 - Means 'to build large palaces.'
    • 延楼栈道 - Means 'extended towers and suspended bridges,' describing elaborate architectural features.
    • 鸡栖井干 - Means 'chickens roost in wells,' a metaphor for disorder or misuse of space.
    • 木巧之节 - Means 'the cunning parts of wood,' referring to intricate woodwork.
    • 此遁于木也 - Means 'this is a deviation in wood,' metaphorically referring to architectural excess.
    • 魏军南伐 - Refers to the southern campaign of the Wei army, likely during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 朱修之守滑台城 - Refers to Zhu Xiuzhi, a general who defended the city of Huatai.
    • 母悲忧乳汁惊出 - Means 'his mother, in grief, suddenly produced milk.'
    • 号踊告家人 - Means 'she cried out and told her family.'
    • 我年老非有乳时 - Means 'I am old and not the time for milk.'
    • 城陷 - Means 'the city was captured.'
    • 帝王世纪 - Refers to the *Chronicles of the Emperors*, a historical text.
    • 厨中自生肉脯 - Means 'meat jerky appeared in the kitchen by itself.'
    • 薄如翣 - Means 'thin like a fan,' describing the appearance of the meat jerky.
    • 摇鼓则生风 - Means 'it generates wind when shaken,' describing a magical or unusual property.
    • 食物寒而不臭 - Means 'the food remains cold and does not spoil.'
    • 尧缉獬豸之皮以为帝帐 - Means 'Emperor Yao made a royal tent from the skin of a xiezhi,' a mythical creature.
    • 岭南溪洞中 - Refers to the mountain streams and caves of southern China.
    • 往往有飞头者 - Means 'there are often people who fly their heads.'
    • 飞头老子之号 - Refers to the nickname 'Flying Head Laozi,' a mythical or legendary figure.
    • 头将飞 - Means 'the head is about to fly.'
    • 日前颈有痕 - Means 'there are marks around the neck before the head flies.'
    • 匝项 - Means 'all around the neck.'
    • 如红缕 - Means 'like red threads,' describing the appearance of the marks.
    • 妻子共守之 - Means 'his wife and children guarded him.'
    • 及夜生翼飞去 - Means 'at night, it grows wings and flies away.'
    • 晓却还 - Means 'in the morning, it returns.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 佛鉢 - Refers to the alms bowl of the Buddha, a sacred object in Buddhism.
    • 西王母 - The Queen Mother of the West, a mythical figure in Chinese folklore, often associated with immortality and the afterlife.
    • 穆天子 - King Mu of Zhou, a legendary ruler in Chinese history.
    • 武王 - King Wu of Zhou, a historical figure who overthrew the Shang dynasty.
    • 纣 - King Zhou of Shang, the last ruler of the Shang dynasty, known for his tyranny.
    • 淮南子 - A philosophical text from the Han dynasty, known for its eclectic mix of Daoist, Confucian, and other ideas.
    • 魏军 - Refers to the military forces of the state of Wei during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 朱修之 - A historical figure, likely a general or official during the Wei or Jin dynasties.
    • 滑台城 - The city of Huatai, likely a strategic location during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 帝王世纪 - A historical text that records the lives and deeds of emperors in Chinese history.
    • 尧 - Emperor Yao, one of the Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors in Chinese mythology, known for his virtue and wisdom.
    • 獬豸 - A mythical creature with a single horn, often associated with justice and law.
    • 岭南 - Refers to the southern regions of China, particularly Guangdong and Guangxi provinces.

    Proper Nouns

    • 佛樓沙國 - A kingdom, likely a transliteration of a foreign place name.
    • 穆天子 - King Mu of Zhou.
    • 西王母 - The Queen Mother of the West.
    • 武王 - King Wu of Zhou.
    • 纣 - King Zhou of Shang.
    • 淮南子 - The *Huainanzi*.
    • 魏军 - The military forces of the state of Wei.
    • 朱修之 - A historical figure, likely a general or official.
    • 滑台城 - The city of Huatai.

    A Buddha's Alms Bowl in the Kingdom of Fólóu Shā

    In the kingdom of Fólóu Shā, there is a Buddha's alms bowl capable of holding about three dou, made of green jade, or perhaps green stone, or a multicolored material with much black. The edges are clearly defined, and the thickness is about two fen. Poor people throw a few flowers into it, and it is full, while rich people throw many flowers, even hundreds of thousands of dou, but it never fills up. Some say it is in the country of Yuèzhī.

    According to the Records of the Grand Historian, when the Son of Heaven visited the Queen Mother of the West, he recorded his journey on Mount Yin, which is the mountain of the Queen Mother of the West.

    After King Wu of Zhou had defeated King Zhou of Shang, he ascended the hill of Bi to look upon the city of Shāng. He said, "Since my birth, sixty years have passed, and deer graze in the fields, wild geese fill the fields." Xu Guang cited the words of an official named Bian: "Wild sheep graze in the fields." Zhang Huai believed this was the words of King Wen: "Water flows eastward, wild geese fill the fields." He also said, "Since my birth, ten years have passed," which is slightly different from previous records.

    The Huainanzi states, "The origin of chaos lies in deviation. Large palaces are built, extended towers and suspended bridges are constructed, chickens roost in wells, and the cunning parts of wood are used to support each other. This is a deviation in wood."

    When the Wei army marched south, Zhu Xiuzhi defended the city of Huatai but could not capture it. His mother, in grief, suddenly produced milk and cried out to her family, saying, "I am old and not the time for milk. Today, if I do not produce it, I will not be able to save him." That day, the city was captured.

    According to the Chronicles of the Emperors, during the time of Emperor Yao, meat jerky appeared in the kitchen by itself, thin like a fan, and when shaken, it generated wind, keeping the food cold and preventing it from spoiling.

    Emperor Yao made a royal tent from the skin of a xiezhi.

    In the mountain streams and caves of southern China, there are often people who fly their heads, hence the nickname "Flying Head Laozi." When the head is about to fly, there are marks around the neck before the head flies, like red threads. His wife and children guarded him. At night, it grows wings and flies away, and in the morning, it returns. [63]

    梵僧菩萨胜言阇婆国中有飞头其俗所祠名曰虫落因号落氏
    　　河图曰少室山大竹堪为甑噐山海经舜林中大竹一节可以为船汉竹大者一节受一斛小者数升为椑榼广志曰云母竹大竹也攡竹细而多刺也竹堪作篃竹宜为屋椽竹皮青内白如雪輭韧可为索
    　　竹谱曰竹之品六十有一
    　　南越以孔雀珥门户昆山以璞玉抵乌鹊
    　　燕避戊巳蝙伏庚申一岁三则桑弱马耗
    　　孔子曰违山十里蟪蛄之声犹在于耳政事恶哗而善肃
    　　蚯蚓长吟地中江东谓之歌女
    　　木瓜味酢善疗转筋陶居云如转筋时但呼楙名及书上木瓜字輙愈
    　　谷胶可以圑丹砂又曰为金石之漆
    　　橘柚雕于北徙石榴郁于东移
    　　吕氏春秋曰善学者若齐王之食鸡必食其跖数千而后可足也
    　　慕容廆有马跨四十九年骏逸不亏铸铜为之像亲为铭讃
    　　拨拔力国有异兽名駞牛皮似豹蹄牛无峯项长九尺身高一丈余 [64]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [64]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 梵僧菩萨胜言 - A phrase indicating that a Buddhist monk named 菩萨胜 (Pusa Sheng) is speaking.
    • 阇婆国 - Refers to Java, an island in modern-day Indonesia.
    • 飞头 - A literal translation of 'flying head,' which is a mythical creature.
    • 其俗所祠 - Refers to the local customs or traditions of worship.
    • 虫落 - A term that could be translated as 'insect fall' or 'insect drop,' but it is likely a specific name or term used in the local context.
    • 因号落氏 - Means 'therefore named the Luo clan,' indicating that the people who worshipped this creature were called the Luo clan.
    • 河图曰 - Indicates that the following statement is from the *Huangdi Neijing* (Yellow Emperor's Inner Canon), an ancient Chinese medical text.
    • 少室山 - Refers to a mountain in Henan Province, China.
    • 甑噐 - Refers to a type of cooking vessel.
    • 山海经 - Refers to the *Shanhaijing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas), an ancient Chinese text that describes various geographical and mythological places.
    • 舜林 - Refers to a forest or wooded area associated with the legendary figure Shun.
    • 攡竹 - Refers to a type of bamboo with many spines.
    • 竹 - Refers to a type of bamboo suitable for making ropes.
    • 篃竹 - Refers to a type of bamboo suitable for making roof beams.
    • 竹 - Refers to a type of bamboo with a white interior.
    • 輭韧 - Describes the soft and flexible nature of the bamboo.
    • 索 - Refers to a rope or cord.
    • 木瓜味酢 - Describes the sour taste of loquats.
    • 善疗转筋 - Means 'good for treating muscle cramps.'
    • 陶居云 - Refers to a statement by Tao Yuanming, a famous poet and writer from the Jin Dynasty.
    • 谷胶 - Refers to a type of glue made from grain.
    • 圑丹砂 - Means 'to bind cinnabar.'
    • 为金石之漆 - Describes the use of a substance as a binding agent for metals and stones.
    • 橘柚雕于北徙 - Describes the practice of grafting oranges and tangerines in the north.
    • 石榴郁于东移 - Describes the practice of grafting pomegranates in the east.
    • 吕氏春秋 - Refers to the *Lüshi Chunqiu* (Spring and Autumn Annals of Master Lü), an ancient Chinese text.
    • 违山十里 - Means 'ten miles away from the mountain.'
    • 蟪蛄之声犹在于耳 - Describes the sound of cicadas still being heard.
    • 政事恶哗而善肃 - Means 'governing should avoid noise and favor order.'
    • 蚯蚓长吟地中 - Describes the long, continuous sound of earthworms underground.
    • 江东谓之歌女 - Means 'in the east of the river, they call it a singing girl.'
    • 拨拔力国 - Refers to a country or region, possibly a fictional or mythical place.
    • 駞牛 - Refers to a mythical creature with the characteristics of both a camel and a cow.
    • 皮似豹 - Describes the skin as resembling that of a leopard.
    • 蹄牛 - Describes the hooves as similar to those of a cow.
    • 无峯 - Means 'no hump.'
    • 项长九尺 - Describes the neck as nine feet long.
    • 身高一丈余 - Describes the height as over ten feet.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 梵僧菩萨胜 - A Buddhist monk from India, indicating the influence of Buddhism in China.
    • 阇婆国 - Java, an island in modern-day Indonesia, indicating the spread of Chinese culture and influence in Southeast Asia.
    • 飞头 - A mythical creature, indicating the presence of folklore and mythology in Chinese literature.
    • 虫落 - A local term, indicating the diversity of regional names and terms.
    • 因号落氏 - Naming a clan after a mythological creature, indicating the cultural significance of such creatures.
    • 河图曰 - The *Huangdi Neijing*, an important medical text, indicating the integration of medical knowledge with other aspects of Chinese culture.
    • 少室山 - A mountain in Henan Province, indicating the importance of geographical features in Chinese literature.
    • 甑噐 - A cooking vessel, indicating the practical and everyday aspects of life.
    • 山海经 - The *Shanhaijing*, an ancient text describing various geographical and mythological places, indicating the rich tradition of Chinese mythology.
    • 舜林 - A forest associated with the legendary figure Shun, indicating the importance of historical and mythological figures in Chinese culture.
    • 攡竹 - A type of bamboo with many spines, indicating the diversity of plant species and their uses.
    • 竹 - A type of bamboo suitable for making ropes, indicating the practical uses of natural resources.
    • 篃竹 - A type of bamboo suitable for making roof beams, indicating the construction techniques and materials used in ancient China.
    • 竹 - A type of bamboo with a white interior, indicating the aesthetic and functional properties of different types of bamboo.
    • 陶居云 - Tao Yuanming, a famous poet and writer from the Jin Dynasty, indicating the literary tradition and the influence of notable figures.
    • 谷胶 - A type of glue made from grain, indicating the use of natural materials in various crafts.
    • 圑丹砂 - Binding cinnabar, indicating the use of precious materials in traditional Chinese medicine and alchemy.
    • 为金石之漆 - A binding agent for metals and stones, indicating the advanced metallurgical and chemical knowledge in ancient China.
    • 橘柚雕于北徙 - Grafting oranges and tangerines in the north, indicating the agricultural practices and the spread of horticulture.
    • 石榴郁于东移 - Grafting pomegranates in the east, indicating the agricultural practices and the spread of horticulture.
    • 吕氏春秋 - The *Lüshi Chunqiu*, an ancient text, indicating the philosophical and political thought of the time.
    • 违山十里 - Ten miles away from the mountain, indicating the distance and the natural environment.
    • 蟪蛄之声犹在于耳 - The sound of cicadas, indicating the natural sounds and the connection to nature.
    • 政事恶哗而善肃 - Governing should avoid noise and favor order, indicating the principles of good governance.
    • 蚯蚓长吟地中 - The sound of earthworms, indicating the natural world and the interconnectedness of life.
    • 江东谓之歌女 - In the east of the river, they call it a singing girl, indicating the regional variations in naming and cultural practices.
    • 拨拔力国 - A fictional or mythical place, indicating the presence of fantastical elements in Chinese literature.
    • 駞牛 - A mythical creature, indicating the rich tradition of folklore and mythology.

    The Buddhist monk Pusa Sheng said that in the country of Java, there is a creature known as "flying head." The locals worship it, and it is called "insect fall," hence they are named the Luo clan.
    According to the Huangdi Neijing, the great bamboo of Shaoshi Mountain can be used to make cooking vessels. The Shanhaijing records that the bamboo in Shun's forest can be used to make a boat. The large Han bamboo can hold a shi (a unit of volume) in one section, while the smaller ones can hold several sheng (a smaller unit of volume) and are used to make small wine vessels. The Guangzhi states that cloud mother bamboo is also a type of large bamboo. The thorny bamboo is fine and has many spines. The rope-making bamboo is suitable for making ropes. The beam-making bamboo is suitable for making roof beams. The white-skinned bamboo has a green outer layer and a white inner layer, soft and flexible, suitable for making ropes.
    The Bamboo Spectrum says that there are sixty-one types of bamboo.
    In the South, peacocks are used to decorate doors and windows, while in the mountains, unpolished jade is used to ward off crows and magpies.
    During the years of Wu and Ji, swallows avoid these days, and bats hibernate during the years of Geng and Xin. If one encounters these days three times in a year, the mulberry trees will be weak, and the horses will suffer.
    Confucius said, "Even ten miles away from the mountain, the sound of cicadas can still be heard. Governing should avoid noise and favor order."
    Earthworms make a long, continuous sound underground. In the east of the river, they are called "singing girls."
    Loquats have a sour taste and are good for treating muscle cramps. Tao Yuanming said that if one calls out the name of the loquat or writes the character for loquat when experiencing muscle cramps, it will heal immediately.
    Glue made from grain can be used to bind cinnabar, and it is also described as a binding agent for metals and stones.
    Oranges and tangerines are grafted in the north, while pomegranates are grafted in the east.
    The Lüshi Chunqiu says, "A good learner is like King Qi of Qi who eats chicken; he must eat the feet of thousands of chickens before he can be satisfied."
    Murong Wei had a horse that lived for forty-nine years without losing its vigor. He cast a bronze statue of the horse and personally wrote an inscription and praise for it.
    In the country of Babali, there is a strange beast called "camel cow." Its skin resembles that of a leopard, and its hooves are like those of a cow. It has no hump, a neck nine feet long, and a height of over ten feet. [64]

    水麝天寳初虞人获水麝诏养之脐中下水沥滴于斗水中用洒衣至败香不歇毎取以针刺之投以真雄黄香气倍于肉麝此与前说水麝小异并録之
    　　张华为黄门侍郎博览图籍千门万户画地成图司马迁欲自成一家追经传之文以从世变可也至改古文尚书论语可乎
    　　楚王使屈原造为宪令属草藁未定上官大夫见而欲夺之原不与防之曰王使屈平为令众莫不知每一令出平伐其功以为非我莫能为也王因是疏屈平
    　　庚桑子出山人王士源采荘子庚桑篇为之目云其先人于山中得古本唐人疑之竟不施行今亢仓子三卷是也
    　　汉蜀人赵賔好异说以气无箕子箕子者万物荄滋也顔师古曰古皆荄箕同音
    　　贵妃以假为首饰曰义髻僖宗内人束髪甚急为囚髻唐末妇人梳髪以两抱面为抛家髻
    　　望梅生津食芥堕泪此五液之自外至也慕而垂涎愧而汗发此五液之自内至也
    　　良常山有荧火芝大如豆夜视有光得食一枚心中一孔明食七枚七孔明可夜书华阳洞亦有五种夜光芝包山有白芝 [65]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [65]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 水麝天寳初虞人獲水麝 - Introduces the topic of water musk deer in the early Tianbao era (742-756 AD).
    • 用洒衣至败香不歇 - Describes the persistent fragrance of the musk when applied to clothing.
    • 毎取以针刺之投以真雄黄 - Describes the method of extracting musk by piercing the animal and adding real cinnabar to enhance its scent.
    • 香气倍于肉麝 - Compares the scent of water musk to that of land musk, stating that it is twice as strong.
    • 张华为黄门侍郎博览图籍千门万户画地成图 - Describes Zhang Hua's extensive knowledge and ability to recall vast amounts of information.
    • 司马迁欲自成一家追经传之文以从世变可也 - Discusses Sima Qian's desire to create his own historical narrative while following the conventions of classical texts.
    • 至改古文尚书论语可乎 - Questions whether it is appropriate for Sima Qian to alter the ancient texts of the *Shangshu* (Book of Documents) and *Lunyu* (Analects of Confucius).
    • 楚王使屈原造为宪令属草藁未定上官大夫见而欲夺之原不与防之曰王使屈平为令众莫不知每一令出平伐其功以为非我莫能为也王因是疏屈平 - Recounts the story of Qu Yuan being appointed to draft laws, and how an official tried to take credit for his work, leading to Qu Yuan's demotion.
    • 庚桑子出山人王士源采荘子庚桑篇为之目云其先人于山中得古本唐人疑之竟不施行今亢仓子三卷是也 - Explains the origin of the *Kangcangzi* (亢仓子), a collection of texts attributed to Guan Zhong, and how it was questioned by Tang Dynasty scholars.
    • 汉蜀人赵賔好异说以气无箕子箕子者万物荄滋也顔师古曰古皆荄箕同音 - Introduces Zhao Bin, a person from Shu who had unusual ideas about the nature of qi (气) and the meaning of *Jizi* (箕子).
    • 贵妃以假为首饰曰义髻僖宗内人束髪甚急为囚髻唐末妇人梳髪以两抱面为抛家髻 - Describes the hairstyles of the Tang Dynasty, including the *yi ji* (义髻) and *paojia ji* (抛家髻).
    • 望梅生津食芥堕泪此五液之自外至也慕而垂涎愧而汗发此五液之自内至也 - Discusses the physiological responses of salivation and tearing, and how they can be triggered by external stimuli or internal emotions.
    • 良常山有荧火芝大如豆夜视有光得食一枚心中一孔明食七枚七孔明可夜书华阳洞亦有五种夜光芝包山有白芝 - Describes various types of luminous fungi and their properties, including their ability to emit light and their medicinal uses.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 水麝 - Refers to the water musk deer, a rare and valuable animal in Chinese culture.
    • 天宝 - The era name of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang (742-756 AD), a period of prosperity and cultural flourishing.
    • 张华 - A famous scholar and official during the Western Jin Dynasty, known for his extensive knowledge and literary works.
    • 司马迁 - The author of the *Shiji* (Records of the Grand Historian), a seminal work of Chinese historiography.
    • 屈原 - A renowned poet and statesman during the Warring States period, known for his loyalty and tragic fate.
    • 庚桑子 - A legendary figure associated with the *Kangcangzi* (亢仓子), a collection of texts that were influential in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 赵宾 - A historical figure from the Han Dynasty, known for his unique ideas about qi and the meaning of *Jizi*.
    • 贵妃 - Refers to Yang Guifei, the beloved concubine of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, and a symbol of beauty and luxury.
    • 义髻 - A hairstyle popular during the Tang Dynasty, often associated with the imperial court.
    • 抛家髻 - A hairstyle from the late Tang Dynasty, characterized by two braids wrapped around the head.
    • 望梅生津 - A well-known idiom from the Three Kingdoms period, referring to the psychological effect of imagining something to produce a physical response.
    • 荧火芝 - A type of luminous fungus, often associated with mystical or medicinal properties.

    Proper Nouns

    • 水麝 - Water musk deer.
    • 天宝 - The era name of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang (742-756 AD).
    • 张华 - Zhang Hua, a famous scholar and official during the Western Jin Dynasty.
    • 司马迁 - Sima Qian, the author of the *Shiji* (Records of the Grand Historian).
    • 屈原 - Qu Yuan, a renowned poet and statesman during the Warring States period.
    • 庚桑子 - A legendary figure associated with the *Kangcangzi* (亢仓子).
    • 赵宾 - Zhao Bin, a historical figure from the Han Dynasty.
    • 贵妃 - Yang Guifei, the beloved concubine of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang.
    • 义髻 - A hairstyle popular during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 抛家髻 - A hairstyle from the late Tang Dynasty.
    • 望梅生津 - An idiom from the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 荧火芝 - A type of luminous fungus.

    Final Translation

    Water Musk in the Early Tianbao Era

    In the early Tianbao era, a water musk deer was captured by a gamekeeper and ordered to be kept. The musk from its navel, when dripped into a bucket of water and used to sprinkle clothes, would leave a fragrance that did not fade even after the clothes were worn out. Each time it was extracted by piercing the animal and adding real cinnabar, the scent was twice as strong as that of land musk. This differs slightly from the previous account of the water musk and is recorded here.

    Zhang Hua, the Attendant-in-Ordinary

    Zhang Hua, serving as an attendant-in-ordinary, had extensive knowledge of books and records. He could recall vast amounts of information, drawing maps on the ground as if they were already laid out before him. Sima Qian wished to create his own historical narrative while following the conventions of classical texts to adapt to the changing times. However, altering the ancient texts of the Shangshu (Book of Documents) and Lunyu (Analects of Confucius) would be questionable.

    Qu Yuan Drafting Laws

    The King of Chu appointed Qu Yuan to draft laws, but the draft was not yet finalized. An official saw it and tried to take credit for it. Qu Yuan refused, saying, "The king appointed me to draft the laws, and everyone knows this. Every time a law is issued, I claim the credit, thinking that no one else could do it better." As a result, the king demoted Qu Yuan.

    Kangcangzi

    Geng Sangzi, a legendary figure, was compiled by Wang Shiyuan, who selected passages from the Gengsang chapter of the Zhuangzi. According to the preface, his ancestors found an ancient manuscript in the mountains. Tang Dynasty scholars doubted its authenticity and did not implement it. Today, the Kangcangzi (亢仓子) consists of three volumes.

    Zhao Bin of Shu

    Zhao Bin, a man from Shu, had unusual ideas about the nature of qi (气) and the meaning of Jizi (箕子). Jizi refers to the root of all things. Yan Shigu said that Jizi and Ji (箕) have the same pronunciation.

    Yang Guifei's Hairstyle

    Yang Guifei, the beloved concubine of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, wore a false braid as a headdress called yi ji (义髻). During the reign of Emperor Xianzong, the imperial concubines tightly bound their hair, forming a style called qiujiji (囚髻). By the end of the Tang Dynasty, women styled their hair with two braids wrapped around their faces, known as paojiaji (抛家髻).

    Salivation and Tears

    Imagining plums can produce saliva, and eating mustard can cause tears. These are physiological responses that come from external stimuli. Longing can make one drool, and shame can make one sweat. These are physiological responses that come from internal emotions.

    Luminous Fungi

    The mountain of Liangchang has luminous fungi the size of beans. At night, they emit light. Eating one can illuminate the heart, and eating seven can open seven holes, allowing one to read at night. The Huayang Cave also has five types of night-bright fungi, and the Baoshan has white fungi.

    This translation aims to be both faithful to the original text and readable in English, maintaining the cultural and historical context while addressing syntactic and idiomatic challenges. [65]

    王叡云有书契以来便应有笔世传蒙恬制非也崔豹曰蒙恬以枯木为管以鹿毛为柱羊毛为被所谓毫非今之竹兎也
    　　元和中元稹使蜀营妓薛涛造十色彩牋以寄元稹于松华纸上寄诗赠涛蜀中松花纸杂色流沙纸彩霞金粉龙鳯纸近年皆废唯余十色绫纹纸尚在
    　　湘州记蔡子池南有石臼是蔡伦舂臼
    　　李司徒勉在汴州有异骨一节可为砚南海时得于海商云是蜈蚣脊骨
    　　萧史与穆公链飞雪丹第一转与弄玉涂之今之女银腻粉也三代以降涂紫草为胭脂周以红花为之或曰出于阏氏
    　　葛洪曰沈义学道于蜀中与妻共载
    　　管子曰武王为侈靡令曰豹襜豹裘方得入庙故豹皮百金功臣之家粟千钟而未得一豹皮
    　　陵阳子明经云春食朝霞日欲出时黄气也秋食沦隂日没以后赤黄气也冬食沆夜半气也夏食正阳南方日中气也并天黄地黄为六气
    　　墨子作木鸢飞三日不集公输子之云梯墨子之飞鸢是也 [66]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [66]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 有書契以來 - Refers to the time since the invention of writing, where '書契' means written records or inscriptions.
    • 蒙恬制 - Refers to the belief that Meng Tian invented the brush pen, which is a common historical attribution but not universally accepted.
    • 枯木為管 - Means 'dried wood as the tube' (referring to the brush pen).
    • 鹿毛為柱 - Means 'deer hair as the bristles' (referring to the brush pen).
    • 羊毛為被 - Means 'sheep wool as the cover' (referring to the brush pen).
    • 所謂毫非今之竹兎也 - Means 'what is called 'hair' is not the same as today's rabbit hair' (referring to the brush pen).
    • 元和中 - Refers to the reign period of Emperor Dezong of Tang (779-805).
    • 營妓薛涛 - Refers to Xue Tao, a famous courtesan and poet during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 十色彩牋 - Refers to colorful paper made by Xue Tao.
    • 松华纸 - Refers to a type of paper made from pine bark.
    • 流沙纸 - Refers to a type of paper made from sand.
    • 彩霞金粉龙鳯纸 - Refers to a type of paper with colorful designs and gold powder.
    • 十色绫纹纸 - Refers to a type of paper with ten colors and intricate patterns.
    • 蔡子池南有石臼 - Refers to a stone mortar found south of Cai Zi's pool.
    • 蔡伦舂臼 - Refers to Cai Lun, who is credited with inventing paper, and the stone mortar used in the paper-making process.
    • 李司徒勉在汴州 - Refers to Li Simu, a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty, who was in charge of Bianzhou (modern-day Kaifeng).
    • 异骨 - Refers to a unique bone.
    • 南海时得于海商 - Refers to a merchant from the South Seas.
    • 蜈蚣脊骨 - Refers to the spine of a centipede.
    • 萧史与穆公链飞雪丹 - Refers to Xiao Shi and Mu Gong, who were alchemists who created a type of elixir.
    • 第一转与弄玉涂之 - Refers to the first transformation of the elixir, which was used to paint or apply.
    • 三代以降 - Refers to the period after the Three Dynasties (Xia, Shang, and Zhou).
    • 涂紫草为胭脂 - Refers to using purple grass to make rouge.
    • 周以红花为之 - Refers to using red flowers to make rouge.
    • 阏氏 - Refers to a type of rouge or cosmetic.
    • 沈义学道于蜀中 - Refers to Shen Yi, who studied Daoism in Sichuan.
    • 与妻共载 - Refers to traveling with his wife.
    • 武王为侈靡令 - Refers to King Wu of Zhou issuing a decree for luxury.
    • 豹襜豹裘方得入庙 - Refers to the requirement that only those wearing leopard skin cloaks and furs could enter the temple.
    • 功臣之家粟千钟而未得一豹皮 - Refers to the fact that even high-ranking officials had to pay a high price for leopard skins.
    • 陵阳子明经云 - Refers to a commentary by Lingyang Ziming.
    • 春食朝霞 - Refers to eating the morning light (a metaphor for the rising sun).
    • 日欲出时黄气也 - Refers to the yellow mist at sunrise.
    • 秋食沦隂 - Refers to eating the evening light (a metaphor for the setting sun).
    • 日没以后赤黄气也 - Refers to the red-yellow mist after sunset.
    • 冬食沆 - Refers to eating the night mist (a metaphor for the night sky).
    • 夜半气也 - Refers to the mist at midnight.
    • 夏食正阳 - Refers to eating the midday sun (a metaphor for the sun at noon).
    • 南方日中气也 - Refers to the sun at noon in the south.
    • 并天黄地黄为六气 - Refers to the six types of mist or light.
    • 墨子作木鸢 - Refers to Mozi creating a wooden bird.
    • 飞三日不集 - Refers to the bird flying for three days without landing.
    • 公输子之云梯 - Refers to Gongshu Ban's cloud ladder.
    • 墨子之飞鸢是也 - Refers to Mozi's flying bird as an example of his inventions.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 蒙恬 - A general of the Qin Dynasty, often credited with inventing the brush pen.
    • 蔡伦 - A eunuch of the Han Dynasty, credited with inventing paper.
    • 李司徒勉 - A high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 薛涛 - A famous courtesan and poet during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 萧史与穆公 - Alchemists during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 陵阳子明 - A commentator on Daoist texts.
    • 墨子 - A philosopher and inventor during the Warring States period.
    • 公输子 - Another name for Gongshu Ban, a master craftsman and inventor during the Spring and Autumn period.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • Wang Rui says that since the invention of writing, there should have been a brush pen. The world believes that Meng Tian invented it, but Cui Bao says that Meng Tian made the brush pen from dried wood, deer hair, and sheep wool, which is not the same as today's rabbit hair.
    • During the reign of Emperor Dezong, Yuan Zhen sent Xue Tao to Shu to create colorful paper, which he then used to write poems and send to Xue Tao. The paper included pine bark paper, colored sand paper, and gold powder paper, but these are no longer in use, leaving only the ten-colored silk-patterned paper.
    • South of Cai Zi's pool, there is a stone mortar used by Cai Lun in the paper-making process.
    • Li Simu, while in Bianzhou, had a unique bone that could be used as a砚. He obtained it from a merchant from the South Seas, who said it was the spine of a centipede.
    • Xiao Shi and Mu Gong created a type of elixir called 'flying snow pill.' The first transformation of this elixir was used to paint or apply, and it is now known as a type of rouge. After the Three Dynasties, purple grass was used to make rouge, and during the Zhou Dynasty, red flowers were used. Some say it originated from the阏氏.
    • Shen Yi studied Daoism in Sichuan and traveled with his wife.
    • According to Guan Zhong, King Wu of Zhou issued a decree for luxury, stating that only those wearing leopard skin cloaks and furs could enter the temple. As a result, leopard skins were extremely expensive, and even high-ranking officials had to pay a high price for them.
    • Lingyang Ziming commented that in spring, one eats the morning light (a metaphor for the rising sun); in autumn, one eats the evening light (a metaphor for the setting sun); in winter, one eats the night mist (a metaphor for the night sky); and in summer, one eats the midday sun (a metaphor for the sun at noon).

    Wang Rui says that since the invention of writing, there should have been a brush pen. The world believes that Meng Tian invented it, but Cui Bao says that Meng Tian made the brush pen from dried wood, deer hair, and sheep wool, which is not the same as today's rabbit hair.

    During the reign of Emperor Dezong, Yuan Zhen sent Xue Tao to Shu to create colorful paper, which he then used to write poems and send to Xue Tao. The paper included pine bark paper, colored sand paper, and gold powder paper, but these are no longer in use, leaving only the ten-colored silk-patterned paper.

    The Xiangzhou Ji records that south of Cai Zi's pool, there is a stone mortar used by Cai Lun in the paper-making process.

    Li Simu, while in Bianzhou, had a unique bone that could be used as a砚. He obtained it from a merchant from the South Seas, who said it was the spine of a centipede.

    Xiao Shi and Mu Gong created a type of elixir called "flying snow pill." The first transformation of this elixir was used to paint or apply, and it is now known as a type of rouge. After the Three Dynasties, purple grass was used to make rouge, and during the Zhou Dynasty, red flowers were used. Some say it originated from the阏氏.

    Shen Yi studied Daoism in Sichuan and traveled with his wife.

    According to Guan Zhong, King Wu of Zhou issued a decree for luxury, stating that only those wearing leopard skin cloaks and furs could enter the temple. As a result, leopard skins were extremely expensive, and even high-ranking officials had to pay a high price for them.

    Lingyang Ziming's commentary states that in spring, one eats the morning light (a metaphor for the rising sun), which is a yellow mist at sunrise. In autumn, one eats the evening light (a metaphor for the setting sun), which is a red-yellow mist after sunset. In winter, one eats the night mist (a metaphor for the night sky), which is a mist at midnight. In summer, one eats the midday sun (a metaphor for the sun at noon), which is a sun at noon in the south. Together, these are the six types of mist or light.

    Mozi created a wooden bird that flew for three days without landing. Gongshu Ban's cloud ladder and Mozi's flying bird are examples of their inventions. [66]

    今之纸鸢引丝而上令儿张口望视以泄内
    　　同光中庄宗遣平蜀得王衍金银命悉镕之为金塼银塼约重三百斤一塼开一窍二人担之上有匠人名曰冯高过荆南高季兴曰冯高主属我坑官吏持而有之储为一库皇朝建隆中金银入京师斤两封缄如故
    　　华俗谓上金为紫磨金夷俗谓上金为杨迈金
    　　印子金世传淮南王安药金上有印子篆文刘主字寿州八公山土中耕者往往得之小餠重半两有一印大餠至七两有二十许印
    　　襄赵之间故舂陵白水之地多得金麟趾褭蹏金趾中空四旁有文刻工巧褭蹏似于平物上滴成如干柿土人谓之柿子金或曰此娄敬所为药金和药良
    　　古玉噐有竒特非雕琢人功者多是昆吾刀及虾蟇肪所刻肪可得取肥者剉煎膏涂玉亦软滑易截
    　　冬瓜仁七升以绢袋盛之投三沸汤中暴干如此三度苦酒浸一宿为末日服方寸匕令人不老
    　　通天犀千岁者长且锐白星彻端出气通天则能通神可破水骇鸡矣抱朴子有白理如线者以盛米鸡即骇刻为鱼衔入水水开三尺 [67]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [67]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 為之一空 - Set phrase meaning completely emptied; fields were left barren.
    • 引丝而上 - Pulled up with a string; refers to flying a kite.
    • 令儿张口望视 - Make the child open their mouth and look; likely refers to a folk practice.
    • 以泄内 - To relieve internal ; the character  is unclear, possibly a typo or rare character; the meaning is uncertain.
    • 同光中 - During the Tongguang period; a historical era.
    • 庄宗遣平蜀 - Emperor Zhuangzong sent troops to pacify Shu; refers to a historical event.
    • 王衍金银 - The gold and silver of Wang Yan; Wang Yan was a ruler of Shu.
    • 镕之为金塼银塼 - Melted into gold bricks and silver bricks; refers to the gold and silver being cast into bricks.
    • 一塼开一窍 - Each brick had a hole; possibly for marking or identification.
    • 二人担之 - Carried by two people; refers to the weight of the bricks.
    • 匠人名曰冯高 - The craftsman was named Feng Gao; Feng Gao was the craftsman who made the bricks.
    • 过荆南 - Passed through Jingnan; a region in ancient China.
    • 高季兴曰 - Gao Jixing said; Gao Jixing was a ruler of Jingnan.
    • 坑官吏持而有之 - Tricked the officials into holding onto them; refers to a deceptive act.
    • 储为一库 - Stored in a single warehouse; refers to the gold and silver being stored.
    • 皇朝建隆中 - During the Jianlong period of the Song Dynasty; a historical era.
    • 金银入京师 - Gold and silver entered the capital; refers to the gold and silver being transported to the capital.
    • 斤两封缄如故 - The weight and seals remained unchanged; refers to the gold and silver being preserved in their original condition.
    • 华俗谓上金为紫磨金 - Chinese custom refers to high-quality gold as purple ground gold; possibly referring to its color or purity.
    • 夷俗谓上金为杨迈金 - Barbarian custom refers to high-quality gold as Yangmai gold; possibly referring to a specific type or origin.
    • 印子金 - Stamped gold; refers to gold with a stamp or mark.
    • 世传淮南王安药金 - Passed down through generations as the medicine gold of King An of Huainan; King An of Huainan was a historical figure.
