    • 印子篆文 - Stamped with seal script; refers to the stamp on the gold.
    • 刘主字 - Liu's character; possibly referring to a specific character or symbol.
    • 寿州八公山 - Baigong Mountain in Shouzhou; a specific location in ancient China.
    • 土中耕者往往得之 - Farmers often found them in the soil; refers to the discovery of the gold.
    • 小餠重半两 - Small pieces weighing half a tael; refers to the weight of the gold.
    • 大餠至七两 - Large pieces weighing up to seven taels; refers to the weight of the gold.
    • 有二十许印 - With about twenty stamps; refers to the number of stamps on the gold.
    • 襄赵之间 - Between Xiang and Zhao; refers to a region in ancient China.
    • 故舂陵白水之地 - The former Chongling Bai Water area; a specific location in ancient China.
    • 多得金麟趾褭蹏 - Often found golden deer hooves and horse hooves; refers to the discovery of gold artifacts.
    • 金趾中空 - The gold hooves were hollow; refers to the structure of the gold artifacts.
    • 四旁有文刻工巧 - With intricate carvings around the edges; refers to the craftsmanship of the gold artifacts.
    • 褭蹏似于平物上滴成如干柿 - The horse hooves were shaped like dried persimmons; refers to the appearance of the gold artifacts.
    • 土人谓之柿子金 - The locals called it persimmon gold; refers to the name given to the gold artifacts.
    • 或曰此娄敬所为药金和药良 - Some say it was the medicine gold made by Lou Jing, which was good for mixing with medicine; Lou Jing was a historical figure.
    • 古玉噐 - Ancient jade artifacts; refers to jade objects from ancient times.
    • 有竒特非雕琢人功者 - Those with unique features that were not carved by human hands; refers to jade with natural or unusual features.
    • 昆吾刀 - Kunwu knife; a type of ancient Chinese knife.
    • 虾蟇肪 - Toad fat; a term for a substance used in carving or polishing.
    • 可得取肥者剉煎膏涂玉 - Can be obtained by grinding and cooking the fat to make a paste and applying it to the jade; refers to a method of polishing jade.
    • 亦软滑易截 - Making it soft, smooth, and easy to cut; refers to the effect of the paste on the jade.
    • 冬瓜仁 - Winter melon seeds; refers to the seeds of a winter melon.
    • 以绢袋盛之 - Placed in a silk bag; refers to the method of preparation.
    • 投三沸汤中 - Put into boiling water three times; refers to the process of boiling.
    • 暴干 - Dried in the sun; refers to the drying process.
    • 如此三度 - Repeated three times; refers to the repetition of the process.
    • 苦酒浸一宿 - Soaked in vinegar overnight; refers to the soaking process.
    • 为末日服方寸匕 - Ground into powder and taken daily in a small measure; refers to the method of consumption.
    • 令人不老 - Makes one not age; refers to the supposed effect of the remedy.
    • 通天犀 - Sky-piercing rhinoceros horn; refers to a type of rhinoceros horn.
    • 千岁者长 - A thousand-year-old one is long; refers to the age and length of the rhinoceros horn.

    Translation:

    Kites, when flown with a string, are said to make children open their mouths and look up, possibly to relieve some internal discomfort, though the exact meaning of "内" is unclear.

    During the Tongguang period, Emperor Zhuangzong sent troops to pacify Shu and seized the gold and silver of Wang Yan. He ordered all of it to be melted into gold and silver bricks, each weighing about three hundred pounds, with a hole in each brick for identification. Two people were needed to carry each brick. The craftsman who made these bricks was named Feng Gao. When Feng Gao passed through Jingnan, Gao Jixing, the ruler of Jingnan, said, "Feng Gao, these bricks belong to me. I will trick the officials into holding onto them for me." The gold and silver were stored in a single warehouse. During the Jianlong period of the Song Dynasty, the gold and silver entered the capital, and their weight and seals remained unchanged.

    In Chinese custom, high-quality gold is called "purple ground gold," while in barbarian custom, it is called "Yangmai gold."

    Stamped gold, passed down through generations as the medicine gold of King An of Huainan, often bears seal script with Liu's character. It was found in the soil of Baigong Mountain in Shouzhou, with small pieces weighing half a tael and large pieces weighing up to seven taels, each with about twenty stamps.

    Between Xiang and Zhao, in the former Chongling Bai Water area, golden deer hooves and horse hooves were often found. The gold hooves were hollow, with intricate carvings around the edges, shaped like dried persimmons. The locals called it "persimmon gold," and some say it was the medicine gold made by Lou Jing, which was good for mixing with medicine.

    Ancient jade artifacts with unique features that were not carved by human hands were often made with the Kunwu knife or toad fat. Toad fat could be obtained by grinding and cooking it to make a paste and applying it to the jade, making it soft, smooth, and easy to cut.

    Seven liters of winter melon seeds were placed in a silk bag and boiled three times, then dried in the sun. This process was repeated three times, and the seeds were soaked in vinegar overnight. They were then ground into powder and taken daily in a small measure, said to make one not age.

    A thousand-year-old celestial rhinoceros is long and sharp, with a white star at its tip. It can emit a divine aura that can penetrate the sky, allowing it to communicate with the gods and break through water, frightening chickens. According to Baopu Zi, there are rhinoceroses with white lines like threads that, when filled with rice, can scare chickens. If carved into fish and placed in water, the water will open three feet wide. [67]

    勒毕国人长三寸有翼善言语戏笑因名语国饮丹露为浆丹露者日初出有露汁如珠也
    　　汉武习水战作昆明池钓鱼纶絶而去梦鱼求去其钩明日帝戏于池见鱼衔索为去之复游于池得珠一双
    　　东海员峤山有氷蚕长七寸有鳞角以霜雪覆之始为茧其色五防织为文锦入水不濡投火不燎
    　　石决明亦名九孔螺
    　　鱼子合猪肝食之杀人
    　　陈藏噐云蠏脚中髓及脑殻中黄并能续断絶筋骨碎之微内创中筋即连
    　　方言云桂林之中谓守宫能鸣者与蛤蚧相似
    　　蜘蛛网赘疣七日消烂屡有验
    　　柚似橙而大于橘石其实乳糖
    　　芎防不可乆服令人暴死
    　　藕与同食可以休粮
    　　淮口扬州出身梨苏合香一两酒一斗同煮极能调和五臓
    　　董蕖者婆罗门云阿魏苗根似白芷
    　　胡麻性与茯苓相宜即巨胜
    　　苦参为齿药使人伤肾腰重麸冷
    　　枣与棘相皆有刺
    　　粳粟米五谷中最硬得浆水易化仓粳米炊作干饭食之止痢 [68]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [68]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 勒畢國人長三寸有翼善言語戲笑因名語國 - Describes a mythical people from the country of Lebi, who are three inches tall, have wings, and are skilled in speaking and joking, hence the country is named 'Yuguo'.
    • 飲丹露為漿丹露者日初出有露汁如珠也 - Describes the people drinking 'Danlu' (a red dew) as a beverage, which is the dew that appears at sunrise and resembles pearls.
    • 石決明亦名九孔螺 - Identifies 'Shijueming' (a type of shellfish) as also known as 'Jiukongluo' (a nine-hole snail).
    • 魚子合豬肝食之殺人 - Warns that eating fish roe with pork liver can be fatal.
    • 陳藏噐云蠏脚中髓及腦殻中黄并能續斷絶筋骨碎之微内创中筋即連 - Explains that according to Chen Cangqi, the marrow in a crab's leg and the yellow substance in its shell can be used to heal broken bones and tendons when applied to the wound.
    • 方言云桂林之中謂守宮能鳴者與蛤蚧相似 - Notes that in the dialect of Guilin, the term 'Shou Gong' (a type of lizard) refers to a creature that can make sounds and resembles the 'Hajie' (a type of gecko).
    • 蜘蛛網贅疣七日消爛屢有驗 - Describes the use of spider webs to treat warts and boils, which often heal within seven days.
    • 柚似橙而大于橘石其實乳糖 - Compares the yuzu to an orange, stating it is larger than a tangerine and has a sweet, milky taste.
    • 芎防不可乆服令人暴死 - Warns that long-term use of 'Xiong Fang' (a type of herb) can lead to sudden death.
    • 藕與同食可以休糧 - Suggests that eating lotus root with yuzu can help one abstain from food.
    • 淮口揚州出身梨蘇合香一兩酒一斗同煮極能調和五臓 - Describes a recipe where one liang of Suhe Xiang (a type of incense) and one dou of wine are cooked together with a pear, which is said to harmonize the five organs.
    • 董蕖者婆羅門云阿魏苗根似白芷 - Identifies 'Dong Qu' (a type of plant) as similar to 'A Wei' (a type of plant) according to the Brahmins.
    • 胡麻性與茯苓相宜即巨勝 - States that 'Hu Ma' (sesame) is compatible with 'Fu Ling' (a type of fungus) and is also known as 'Ju Sheng' (a type of herb).
    • 苦參為齒藥使人傷腎腰重麸冷 - Notes that 'Ku Shen' (a type of herb) is used as a tooth remedy but can cause kidney damage and make the waist feel heavy and cold.
    • 棗與棘相皆有刺 - Compares the date and thorn, noting that both have thorns.
    • 粳粟米五谷中最硬得漿水易化倉粳米炊作干飯食之止痢 - Describes the hardness of 'Jing Suli' (a type of rice) and its ability to be easily softened with water, and how cooking it into dry rice can stop diarrhea.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 勒畢國 - A mythical country in Classical Chinese literature.
    • 丹露 - A mythical red dew that appears at sunrise.
    • 石決明 - A type of shellfish, also known as '九孔螺' (Jiukongluo).
    • 陳藏噐 - A historical figure or scholar mentioned in the text.
    • 方言 - Regional dialects and their unique terminology.
    • 桂林 - A region in China known for its unique dialect and flora.
    • 守宮 - A type of lizard or gecko.
    • 蛤蚧 - A type of gecko.
    • 董蕖 - A type of plant.
    • 婆羅門 - Refers to the Brahmins, a group of scholars or priests in ancient India.
    • 阿魏 - A type of plant.
    • 胡麻 - Sesame.
    • 茯苓 - A type of fungus.
    • 巨勝 - Another name for a type of herb.
    • 苦參 - A type of herb used as a tooth remedy.
    • 粳粟米 - A type of rice.
    • 漿水 - Water used to soften grains.
    • 倉粳米 - A type of rice stored in granaries.

    Proper Nouns

    • 勒畢國 - The country of Lebi.
    • 丹露 - The red dew.
    • 石決明 - Shijueming (a type of shellfish).
    • 陳藏噐 - Chen Cangqi.
    • 方言 - Regional dialects.
    • 桂林 - Guilin.
    • 守宮 - Shou Gong (a type of lizard).
    • 蛤蚧 - Hajie (a type of gecko).
    • 董蕖 - Dong Qu (a type of plant).
    • 婆羅門 - The Brahmins.
    • 阿魏 - A Wei (a type of plant).
    • 胡麻 - Hu Ma (sesame).
    • 茯苓 - Fu Ling (a type of fungus).
    • 巨勝 - Ju Sheng (a type of herb).
    • 苦參 - Ku Shen (a type of herb).
    • 粳粟米 - Jing Suli (a type of rice).
    • 漿水 - Water used to soften grains.
    • 倉粳米 - Jing Suli (a type of rice stored in granaries).

    The People of Lebi

    The people of Lebi are three inches tall, have wings, and are skilled in speaking and joking, hence the country is named "Yuguo." They drink Danlu (a red dew) as a beverage, which is the dew that appears at sunrise and resembles pearls.

    During the reign of Emperor Wu, he practiced water warfare and constructed the Kunming Pool. While fishing, his fishing line broke and he dreamed of a fish that pulled the hook away. The next day, when he played in the pool, he saw a fish holding a rope, which he took away. The fish then returned to the pool and brought him a pair of pearls.

    On the mountains of Yuanqiao in the East Sea, there are ice silkworms seven inches long with scales and horns. When covered with frost and snow, they begin to spin silk, and their cocoons are multicolored. The silk they weave is fine and does not get wet when submerged in water or burn when thrown into fire.

    Shijueming is also known as Jiukongluo (a nine-hole snail).

    Eating fish roe with pork liver can be fatal.

    According to Chen Cangqi, the marrow in a crab's leg and the yellow substance in its shell can be used to heal broken bones and tendons when applied to the wound.

    In the dialect of Guilin, the term "Shou Gong" (a type of lizard) refers to a creature that can make sounds and resembles the "Hajie" (a type of gecko).

    Spider webs can be used to treat warts and boils, which often heal within seven days.

    The yuzu resembles an orange but is larger than a tangerine and has a sweet, milky taste.

    Long-term use of Xiong Fang (a type of herb) can lead to sudden death.

    Eating lotus root with yuzu can help one abstain from food.

    A recipe where one liang of Suhe Xiang (a type of incense) and one dou of wine are cooked together with a pear is said to harmonize the five organs.

    According to the Brahmins, Dong Qu (a type of plant) is similar to A Wei (a type of plant).

    Hu Ma (sesame) is compatible with Fu Ling (a type of fungus) and is also known as Ju Sheng (a type of herb).

    Ku Shen (a type of herb) is used as a tooth remedy but can cause kidney damage and make the waist feel heavy and cold.

    Dates and thorns both have thorns.

    Jing Suli (a type of rice) is the hardest among the five grains and can be easily softened with water. Cooking it into dry rice can stop diarrhea. [68]

    采芸香叶置席下能去蚤虱子
    　　仙家谓醋为华池左味
    　　白髪须镊去消蜡防孔中即生黒者
    　　松脂杏仁枣肉茯苓等分食后服五十粒可不饥有黄连桔梗勿食猪肉有茯苓勿食酢物有细辛勿食生菜
    　　古无纯铜作镜者皆有锡杂之
    　　巴豆神仙食一枚即死鼠食三年重三十斤
    　　麸金是毒蛇屎
    　　征姓举事当忌亥日以火絶在亥
    　　巳日巳年不杀蛇
    　　段成式云神荼郁垒领万鬼
    　　疟鬼小不能病巨人故曰壮士不病疟晋人曰君子不病疟蜀人以痎疟为奴婢疟
    　　沈懐逺南越志曰珠有九品寸五分以上至寸八九分为大品有光彩一边小平似覆金者名珰珠珰珠之之次为走珠走珠之次为滑珠滑珠之次为磊螺珠磊螺珠之次为官雨珠官雨珠之次为税珠税珠之次为葱珠
    　　元封二年大秦献花蹄牛高六尺尾环绕角生四耳阿萨部多蜡虫鹿剖其肉重垒以石压之沥汁税波斯郁林等国草子醸于肉汁中经数日即变成酒饮之可醉

    　　续博物志卷十 [69]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [69]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 采芸香叶置席下能去蚤虱子 - Practical remedy using mugwort leaves to repel fleas and lice. '采' means 'to gather,' '芸香' refers to mugwort, and '蚤虱子' refers to fleas and lice.
    • 仙家谓醋为华池左味 - Taoist term where '醋' (vinegar) is referred to as '华池左味' (a term of the immortals), suggesting a mystical or alchemical significance.
    • 白髪须镊去消蜡防孔中即生黒者 - Method of hair removal and prevention of black hair growth in the pores. '镊去' means 'remove with tweezers,' and '消蜡防孔中即生黒者' suggests preventing black hair growth by removing wax.
    • 松脂杏仁枣肉茯苓等分食后服五十粒可不饥有黄连桔梗勿食猪肉有茯苓勿食酢物有细辛勿食生菜 - Detailed recipe for a medicinal concoction. '等分' means 'in equal parts,' and '食后服' means 'take after meals.' The warning against certain foods like pork, vinegar, and raw vegetables is also noted.
    • 古无纯铜作镜者皆有锡杂之 - Ancient practice of making mirrors with a mixture of copper and tin. '古无纯铜作镜者' means 'ancient mirrors were not made purely of copper.'
    • 巴豆神仙食一枚即死鼠食三年重三十斤 - Cautionary tale about the toxicity of the plant *Croton*. '神仙食一枚即死' means 'even immortals would die if they ate one seed,' and '鼠食三年重三十斤' means 'a rat eating it for three years would weigh thirty catties.'
    • 麸金是毒蛇屎 - Folk belief that '麸金' (a type of metal) is actually snake droppings.
    • 征姓举事当忌亥日以火绝在亥 - Superstition about the Zheng surname avoiding certain actions on the day of the pig (亥).
    • 巳日巳年不杀蛇 - Belief that one should not kill snakes on the day of the snake (巳) or in the year of the snake.
    • 段成式云神荼郁垒领万鬼 - Reference to the Taoist deities Shentu and Yulu who guard against evil spirits.
    • 疟鬼小不能病巨人故曰壮士不病疟晋人曰君子不病疟蜀人以痎疟为奴婢疟 - Description of malaria and its cultural perceptions. '疟鬼小不能病巨人' means 'the malaria spirit is small and cannot harm giants,' and '壮士不病疟' means 'heroes do not suffer from malaria.'
    • 沈懐逺南越志曰珠有九品寸五分以上至寸八九分为大品有光彩一边小平似覆金者名珰珠珰珠之之次为走珠走珠之次为滑珠滑珠之次为磊螺珠磊螺珠之次为官雨珠官雨珠之次为税珠税珠之次为葱珠 - Classification of pearls. '九品' means 'nine grades,' and '寸五分以上至寸八九分' specifies the size range. '珰珠' (dangzhu) is the highest grade, followed by '走珠' (zouzhu), '滑珠' (huazhu), etc.
    • 元封二年大秦献花蹄牛高六尺尾环绕角生四耳阿萨部多蜡虫鹿剖其肉重垒以石压之沥汁税波斯郁林等国草子醸于肉汁中经数日即变成酒饮之可醉 - Description of a rare animal and a method of making wine. '元封二年' is the year 109 BCE, and '大秦' refers to the Roman Empire. '花蹄牛' is a cow with spotted hooves, and '阿萨部多蜡虫鹿' is a type of insect used to make wax. '税波斯郁林等国草子' refers to herbs from Persia and other regions.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 仙家 - Refers to Taoist immortals or practitioners of Taoism.
    • 神荼郁垒 - Taoist deities who guard against evil spirits.
    • 巴豆 - A toxic plant used in traditional Chinese medicine.
    • 麸金 - A type of metal, often associated with alchemy.
    • 征姓 - A surname with specific cultural taboos.
    • 巳日巳年 - Days and years associated with the snake in the Chinese zodiac.
    • 南越 - An ancient region in southern China, now part of modern-day Guangdong and Guangxi provinces.
    • 元封二年 - The second year of the Yuanfeng era during the reign of Emperor Wu of Han (109 BCE).
    • 大秦 - A term used in ancient China to refer to the Roman Empire.
    • 花蹄牛 - A cow with spotted hooves, possibly a rare or mythical creature.
    • 阿萨部多蜡虫鹿 - A type of insect used to make wax, possibly a rare or exotic species.
    • 段成式 - A historical figure, possibly a writer or scholar.
    • 沈懐逺 - A historical figure, possibly a writer or scholar.

    Proper Nouns

    • 元封二年 - The second year of the Yuanfeng era during the reign of Emperor Wu of Han (109 BCE).
    • 大秦 - A term used in ancient China to refer to the Roman Empire.
    • 花蹄牛 - A cow with spotted hooves, possibly a rare or mythical creature.
    • 阿萨部多蜡虫鹿 - A type of insect used to make wax, possibly a rare or exotic species.
    • 段成式 - A historical figure, possibly a writer or scholar.
    • 沈懐逺 - A historical figure, possibly a writer or scholar.
    • 南越 - An ancient region in southern China, now part of modern-day Guangdong and Guangxi provinces.

    Final Translation

    Using Mugwort Leaves to Repel Fleas and Lice

    Place mugwort leaves under your mat to repel fleas and lice.

    Taoist Term for Vinegar

    The immortals refer to vinegar as "Hua Chi Zuo Wei."

    Removing White Hair and Preventing Black Hair Growth

    White hair must be removed with tweezers and wax must be applied to prevent black hair from growing in the pores.

    Medicinal Concoction

    Mix equal parts of pine resin, almond, jujube flesh, and poria, and take fifty grains after meals to avoid hunger. Avoid eating pork if you have yellow gentian or platycodon. If you have poria, avoid vinegar. If you have fine ginger, avoid raw vegetables.

    Ancient Mirrors

    Ancient mirrors were not made purely of copper; they all contained tin.

    Toxicity of Croton

    Even immortals would die if they ate one seed of croton. A rat eating it for three years would weigh thirty catties.

    Folk Belief About Metal

    Flour gold is actually snake droppings.

    Superstition About the Zheng Surname

    Those with the Zheng surname should avoid certain actions on the day of the pig (亥) and use fire to ward off bad luck on that day.

    Belief About Killing Snakes

    On the day of the snake (巳) or in the year of the snake, one should not kill snakes.

    Taoist Deities

    According to Duan Chengshi, the deities Shentu and Yulu lead ten thousand spirits.

    Malaria and Cultural Perceptions

    The malaria spirit is small and cannot harm giants, hence the saying that heroes do not suffer from malaria. The people of Jin say that gentlemen do not suffer from malaria. The people of Shu consider intermittent malaria to be a form of servant malaria.

    Classification of Pearls

    According to Shen Huaiyuan's Nan Yue Zhi, pearls are classified into nine grades. Those measuring more than five-tenths of an inch up to eight-tenths of an inch are the highest grade. They have a lustrous appearance and one side is slightly flat, resembling a golden cover. These are called dangzhu pearls. Below dangzhu pearls are zouzhu pearls, followed by hua pearls, leiguo pearls, guanyu pearls, shui pearls, and cong pearls.

    Rare Animal and Wine Making

    In the second year of the Yuanfeng era (109 BCE), the Roman Empire presented a cow with spotted hooves, six feet tall, with its tail wrapped around its horns, and four ears. The insect known as the "Asa Department Wax Deer" was used to make wax. Its meat was cut and layered with stones to press out the juice, which was then mixed with herbs from Persia and other regions and fermented for several days to become wine. Drinking this wine can intoxicate one.

    End of Continuation of Zuobi Zhi Volume Ten

    This translation aims to be both faithful to the original Classical Chinese and readable in English, while preserving the cultural and historical context. [69]

    采芸香叶置席下能去蚤虱子
    　　仙家谓醋为华池左味
    　　白髪须镊去消蜡防孔中即生黒者
    　　松脂杏仁枣肉茯苓等分食后服五十粒可不饥有黄连桔梗勿食猪肉有茯苓勿食酢物有细辛勿食生菜
    　　古无纯铜作镜者皆有锡杂之
    　　巴豆神仙食一枚即死鼠食三年重三十斤
    　　麸金是毒蛇屎
    　　征姓举事当忌亥日以火絶在亥
    　　巳日巳年不杀蛇
    　　段成式云神荼郁垒领万鬼
    　　疟鬼小不能病巨人故曰壮士不病疟晋人曰君子不病疟蜀人以痎疟为奴婢疟
    　　沈懐逺南越志曰珠有九品寸五分以上至寸八九分为大品有光彩一边小平似覆金者名珰珠珰珠之之次为走珠走珠之次为滑珠滑珠之次为磊螺珠磊螺珠之次为官雨珠官雨珠之次为税珠税珠之次为葱珠
    　　元封二年大秦献花蹄牛高六尺尾环绕角生四耳阿萨部多蜡虫鹿剖其肉重垒以石压之沥汁税波斯郁林等国草子醸于肉汁中经数日即变成酒饮之可醉

    　　续博物志卷十

    Final Translation

    Using Mugwort Leaves to Repel Fleas and Lice

    Place mugwort leaves under your mat to repel fleas and lice.

    Taoist Term for Vinegar

    The immortals refer to vinegar as "Hua Chi Zuo Wei."

    Removing White Hair and Preventing Black Hair Growth

    White hair must be removed with tweezers and wax must be applied to prevent black hair from growing in the pores.

    Medicinal Concoction

    Mix equal parts of pine resin, almond, jujube flesh, and poria, and take fifty grains after meals to avoid hunger. Avoid eating pork if you have yellow gentian or platycodon. If you have poria, avoid vinegar. If you have fine ginger, avoid raw vegetables.

    Ancient Mirrors

    Ancient mirrors were not made purely of copper; they all contained tin.

    Toxicity of Croton

    Even immortals would die if they ate one seed of croton. A rat eating it for three years would weigh thirty catties.

    Folk Belief About Metal

    Flour gold is actually snake droppings.

    Superstition About the Zheng Surname

    Those with the Zheng surname should avoid certain actions on the day of the pig (亥) and use fire to ward off bad luck on that day.

    Belief About Killing Snakes

    On the day of the snake (巳) or in the year of the snake, one should not kill snakes.

    Taoist Deities

    According to Duan Chengshi, the deities Shentu and Yulu lead ten thousand spirits.

    Malaria and Cultural Perceptions

    The malaria spirit is small and cannot harm giants, hence the saying that heroes do not suffer from malaria. The people of Jin say that gentlemen do not suffer from malaria. The people of Shu consider intermittent malaria to be a form of servant malaria.

    Classification of Pearls

    According to Shen Huaiyuan's Nan Yue Zhi, pearls are classified into nine grades. Those measuring more than five-tenths of an inch up to eight-tenths of an inch are the highest grade. They have a lustrous appearance and one side is slightly flat, resembling a golden cover. These are called dangzhu pearls. Below dangzhu pearls are zouzhu pearls, followed by hua pearls, leiguo pearls, guanyu pearls, shui pearls, and cong pearls.

    Rare Animal and Wine Making

    In the second year of the Yuanfeng era (109 BCE), the Roman Empire presented a cow with spotted hooves, six feet tall, with its tail wrapped around its horns, and four ears. The insect known as the "Asa Department Wax Deer" was used to make wax. Its meat was cut and layered with stones to press out the juice, which was then mixed with herbs from Persia and other regions and fermented for several days to become wine. Drinking this wine can intoxicate one.

    End of Continuation of Zuobi Zhi Volume Ten

    This translation aims to be both faithful to the original Classical Chinese and readable in English, while preserving the cultural and historical context.
